response:
The patient is a female with a recent history of left vertebral dissection without associated ischemia, for which she was taking aspirin 81 mg daily. She presented with transient right arm numbness and right neck pain. She had been healthy until about three weeks prior to admission, when she developed a stiff neck on the left side, a throbbing holocephalic headache, and unusual noises in her left ear. She also experienced lightheadedness upon standing. She had a CTA which showed left V3-4 dissection, but an MRI showed no infarct. She was given aspirin 81 mg daily and discharged. Since discharge, she had been doing well and had not missed any doses of her aspirin. She reported trying cocaine for the first time the night before admission. Upon waking, she experienced right arm numbness/tingling and right neck pain, which she described as feeling similar to her previous dissection. She also developed a posterior bioccipital throbbing headache. A CTA showed a right proximal V2 dissection.

Upon arrival at the hospital, she reported right neck and shoulder pain and a mild occipital headache. She denied changes in vision, dysphagia, changes in voice, double vision, dizziness, recurrence of numbness/tingling or weakness. She had no recent fevers or chills, or infectious symptoms. Her family history includes parents with alcohol and drug problems and a brother who had a seizure in the setting of a concussion. There is no family history of strokes, blood vessel problems, or connective tissue disease.

Physical examination revealed a hyperextensible left thumb and fingers. Neurological examination was normal. Blood tests showed an elevated white blood cell count (11.6*) and absolute neutrophil count (6.89*). An MRI of the head showed no acute infarct or intracranial hemorrhage, but there were two punctate FLAIR hyperintensities in the right frontal lobe and right postcentral gyrus, which were nonspecific and likely artifactual. A transthoracic echo report showed normal biventricular cavity sizes and regional/global biventricular systolic function, with no valvular pathology or pathologic flow identified.

The patient left against medical advice due to lack of child care at home, despite recommendations for further evaluation of her dissections with an MRA head/neck with fat sat. She was counseled against additional drug use and advised to present urgently to the ED if she experienced any additional neurological symptoms. The MRA of the head and neck was ordered as an outpatient to be completed prior to a neurology follow-up. The patient was discharged home with a diagnosis of vertebral artery dissection. Her discharge medications included aspirin 81 mg PO daily.

The patient, a female, was admitted due to chest pain and vaginal bleeding. She has a history of aortic aneurysm and fibroids. The chest pain was described as sudden onset substernal pressure, radiating to the left shoulder, and has been continuous for a few minutes. The patient also reported lightheadedness and dyspnea on exertion. The vaginal bleeding had been ongoing for 16 days, with the patient going through several pads per day. She had a D+C and IUD placement a few weeks prior to admission. The patient also reported occasional chest pain when she is anemic and loses a lot of blood.

The patient had pre-operative testing for elbow surgery, during which an abnormal EKG was found. An echo revealed a 4cm aortic aneurysm, which has been stable since its discovery. In the ED, the patient's Hct was 31.7, up from a previous 26. A CT chest scan showed a 4.1 cm diameter of the ascending aorta. The patient was seen by ob/gyn and vaginal bleeding was not thought to be an acute issue. She received morphine and zofran.

The patient's past medical history includes diabetes, dyslipidemia, hypertension, an enlarged aorta, fibroids, right and left elbow surgery, surgery for a herniated disc, appendectomy, and lap chole. She has no known cardiac history. Her family history includes hypertension and HLD in her mother, MI in her brother, and CHF in her sister, who died from the condition.

Physical examination revealed the patient to be morbidly obese, with a large neck, making it difficult to assess JVP. She had pressure ulcers on her buttocks, but no pus and a clean wound. Her cardiac, lung, abdomen, and extremities examination was normal. 

Lab results showed a mildly leftward axis, III TWI, PR 160, QRS <120, QTc 390 on EKG. A CT chest scan showed an enlarged ascending aorta measuring 4.1 x 4.1 cm. Pelvic ultrasound showed uterine fibroids and an appropriately positioned IUD. Lab results also showed RBC-3.70*, HGB-9.6*, HCT-31.5*, MCV-85, MCH-26.0*#, MCHC-30.5*, RDW-17.2*, and URINE RBC-106*, WBC-13*.

The patient was on provera and ferrous sulfate 65mg BID upon admission. She was discharged with a diagnosis of aortic aneurysm, chest pain NOS, and vaginal bleeding. Her mental status was clear and coherent, and she was alert and interactive. She was advised to follow up with her primary care doctor and gynecology team.

The male patient was admitted due to persistent lower chest and epigastric pain radiating to the back, despite normal blood pressure. The pain was associated with an intramural hematoma and penetrating aortic ulceration of the descending thoracic aorta. His past medical history includes hypertension, PTSD, and mood disorder. He has previously undergone lipoma removal and facial reconstruction following a motor vehicle accident. His mother had a myocardial infarction, but there is no family history of connective tissue disorders.

Upon admission, the patient was on Lisinopril 40 mg daily. His physical examination was largely unremarkable, with no signs of scleral icterus, edema, or wounds. His abdomen was tender with deep palpation in the lower-mid region, but no pulsatile mass was detected. His extremities were warm and well perfused, with palpable bilateral pedal pulses.

Abnormal lab results included RBC count of 3.82, Hemoglobin of 13.0, Hematocrit of 36.0, MCH of 33.9, MCHC of 36.0, PTT of 44.9, Glucose of 102, ALT of 79, AST of 94, and Calcium of 8.3. A CT angiogram revealed an intramural hematoma extending from the level of the left subclavian artery and terminating at the level of the renal arteries, with intramural ulcers at the level of the descending thoracic aorta. The descending thoracic aorta measured up to approximately 4.4 cm.

During his hospital stay, the patient underwent thoracic endograft repair for the penetrating thoracic aortic ulcer. Post-operatively, he was stable and his blood pressure was adequately controlled with oral medication. His diet was advanced appropriately and he tolerated it well. His intake and output were closely monitored, and electrolytes were routinely followed and repleted when necessary. He had a Foley catheter placed in the OR, which was removed without difficulty. His white blood count and fever curves were closely watched for signs of infection, but none were detected. His groin puncture sites healed well without hematoma or ecchymosis, and distal pulses remained intact. He was discharged home in a clear and coherent mental state, and was ambulatory independently. His discharge diagnosis was a penetrating descending thoracic aortic ulcer.

The patient, a male with a history of hypertension, PTSD, mood disorder, intramural hematoma, and penetrating ulceration of the descending thoracic aorta, was transferred from another facility after presenting with interscapular back pain characterized as sharp between the shoulder blades, causing difficulty breathing. His past surgical history includes lipoma removal and facial reconstruction following a motor vehicle accident. The patient's mother had a myocardial infarction, but there is no family history of connective tissue disorders.

Upon admission, the patient was on several medications including Lisinopril, Aspirin, Ibuprofen, Metoprolol Succinate XL, Gabapentin, HydrOXYzine, and Omeprazole. His physical examination was largely unremarkable, with mild tenderness to touch in the epigastrium just right of the midline.

Admission labs showed abnormal MCH at 33.0 and RDWSD at 51.7. A CTA revealed a new small irregularity or saccular outpouching of the anterior wall of the descending thoracic aorta, 3.5 cm above the level of the aortic stent graft. No external or periaortic rupture was noted. The visualized abdominal aorta near the diaphragmatic hiatus was aneurysmal, measuring 3.4 x 3.9 cm. There was no evidence of acute pulmonary embolism, and the heart was not enlarged. Emphysema and incidental calcified granulomas were noted in the lung, along with spinal degenerative changes.

During the hospital stay, serial CTAs showed interval resolution of much of the periaortic hematoma with aortic remodeling. The patient's ongoing pain was mild and largely positional. Blood pressures were well controlled and remained stable throughout his admission. No surgical intervention was recommended during this admission due to risks of paraplegia given the area of coverage and CT stability. The patient was discharged with a diagnosis of aortic aneurysm outpouching and was instructed to follow up with his primary care physician to monitor blood pressures with the goal to keep his systolic blood pressure less than 140.

The patient, a male of undisclosed age, was admitted due to complaints of back pain, fatigue, weakness, and palpitations. His medical history includes hypertension, Hepatitis C, a history of alcohol abuse, and a history of penetrating descending thoracic aortic ulcers. He had previously undergone thoracic endovascular aortic repair (TEVAR) and was transferred from another facility for further evaluation of his back pain. He reported that the pain began while he was at a grocery store and was accompanied by weakness. He denied experiencing fevers, chills, chest pain, shortness of breath, cough, numbness, or tingling in extremities. He also reported high blood pressure and heart rate at home. He had been previously admitted for similar back pain, which was not believed to be musculoskeletal in origin by orthopedic surgery. He was also briefly seen in the emergency department for a subdural hematoma, for which he was observed and sent home.

The patient's family history includes a mother who had a myocardial infarction, but no known connective tissue disorders. His physical examination was largely unremarkable, with the exception of diffuse tenderness. His laboratory results showed a prolonged PTT of 40.1, a low platelet count of 136, and a low RBC count of 4.48. His MCH was elevated at 33.7, and his RDWSD was also elevated at 52.6. His sodium level was low at 132, and his total CO2 was low at 20.

During his hospital stay, the patient's abdominal saccular aneurysm below the stent was found to be largely unchanged compared to previous imaging. A liver nodule was incidentally found on imaging. The leading diagnosis for his back pain was fibromyalgia or myofascial pain syndrome. No clear musculoskeletal or thoracic causes were identified. No arrhythmias were detected while he was on telemetry. The patient's weakness did not have any objective evidence, but the differential diagnosis included rheumatoid arthritis, systemic lupus erythematosus, polymyalgia rheumatica, polymyositis, fibromyalgia, and hypothyroidism. His CRP of 0.6 ruled out rheumatoid arthritis, polymyalgia rheumatica, and other vasculitides. His CK was within normal limits, ruling out muscle inflammatory diseases such as polymyositis. Imaging did not support spondyloarthritis, and there were no physical evidence for suspicion of systemic lupus erythematosus.

The patient's hepatic nodule was consistent with hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC), and he was recommended for a hepatology/transplant consult. He was started on amitriptyline for possible fibromyalgia. Further workup included TSH, lead, hepatitis B virus serology, and MRI to further characterize the liver lesion. The patient's liver lobe was indeed consistent with HCC. He has untreated hepatitis C and likely compensated cirrhosis given the chronicity of his hepatitis B and the appearance of his liver on CT. Hepatology consultation was inquired. Further workup included liver function tests and coagulation tests, along with tumor markers: AFP, CEA, CA, hepatitis C viral load, and an outpatient follow-up with a liver tumor clinic.

The patient's midscapular back pain and diffuse body pain, along with typical tender points, were consistent with myofascial pain. His myofascial pain syndrome is likely related to his joint hypermobility, which has caused repetitive, low-grade joint sprain. It is possible that the joint hyperlaxity is a manifestation of an undisclosed syndrome, especially in a patient with a history of aortic dissection. Management of his myofascial pain syndrome includes treatment of body pain, treatment of insomnia and anxiety, and physical exercise. The patient was recommended to follow up with these recommendations as an outpatient. Upon discharge, the patient was doing well, afebrile, and hemodynamically stable within normal limits.

The patient's medications upon admission included aspirin, gabapentin, lisinopril, metoprolol succinate XL, omeprazole, and hydroxyzine.

The patient is a middle-aged male with a history of untreated chronic Hepatitis C (genotype 3A, VL 256,000), alcohol-related cirrhosis complicated by varices and portal hypertensive gastropathy, and hepatocellular carcinoma post-RFA. He is currently undergoing a liver transplant workup. He also has a newly diagnosed HIV infection. His past medical history includes hypertension, PTSD, mood disorder, penetrating thoracic aortic ulcer of the descending thoracic aorta post-TEVAR, cervical disk herniation, gastrointestinal bleeding, possible COPD, and alcoholism. His surgical history includes lipoma removal from the left upper arm, facial reconstruction post-MVA, thoracic endograft repair for penetrating thoracic aortic ulcer of the descending thoracic aorta, and bilateral metacarpal injuries post-repair. His mother had a myocardial infarction, but there is no family history of connective tissue disorders.

The patient presented with abdominal pain and muscle weakness. He reported experiencing weakness, blurry vision, forgetfulness, headache, chest pain, and abdominal pain for about two days prior to admission. He also reported aches in all major muscle groups, but denied any fevers. His abdominal pain was epigastric and radiated to the right. He had one episode of diarrhea and felt feverish/flushed, but had no measured fever or chills. His workup revealed a markedly distended hydrops gallbladder (no stones seen), CBD dilated to 0.75 cm, and elevated lipase levels consistent with pancreatitis. He was treated with IV fluids and IV morphine and was initially NPO, then transitioned to a regular diet. He was seen by GI who recommended MRCP, and so the patient was transferred here for further management.

Upon admission, the patient's labs showed abnormal WBC, RBC, Hgb, Hct, MCV, MCH, MCHC, RDW, RDWSD, Plt Ct, Neuts, Lymphs, Monos, AbsLymp, AbsMono, PTT, Na, ALT, AST, LD(LDH), AlkPhos, TotBili, Albumin, and Calcium levels. His imaging results showed no acute process on CXR, unchanged lower thoracic aortic stent graft and suprarenal right lateral saccular AAA, persistent hepatic cirrhosis, persistent borderline splenomegaly with multiple calcified granulomas, and a hypodense posterior lateral R hepatic lobe segment 8 lesion that was larger in size than previously visualized on MRI of the abdomen, which was suspicious for ___. A repeat evaluation with multiphasic contrast-enhanced MRI of the abdomen was recommended. His abdominal ultrasound showed a R hepatic lobe segment 8 noncystic mass lesion, hydrops gallbladder with no evidence of gallstone or sonographic signs of acute cholecystitis, abnormally dilated CBD, AAA, and top normal splenic size. His discharge labs showed abnormal RBC, Hgb, Hct, MCV, MCH, RDW, RDWSD, Glucose, Na, HCO3, ALT, AST, AlkPhos, and TotBili levels.

The patient's medications upon admission were Lisinopril 20 mg PO daily and Metoprolol Succinate XL 50 mg PO BID. His discharge diagnoses included abdominal pain, dilated common bile duct without evidence of obstruction, alcoholic cirrhosis complicated by new hepatic encephalopathy and ascites, and chronic Hepatitis C infection. His mental status upon discharge was clear and coherent, and he was alert and interactive. He was ambulatory and independent. His discharge instructions included taking his new medications for hepatic encephalopathy, having regular bowel movements, following up with his primary care doctor for referrals for further treatment, and not driving due to his liver condition and propensity to become confused.

The patient is a male with a history of penetrating descending aortic ulcer, chronic Hepatitis C (untreated, genotype 3A, VL 256,000), alcohol-related cirrhosis complicated by varices/portal hypertensive gastropathy, hepatocellular carcinoma post radiofrequency ablation, and HIV. He presented with generalized weakness and fatigue, reportedly soon after receiving a meningococcal vaccine. He denies chest pain, dyspnea, nausea/vomiting, diarrhea, melena, rash, and fevers. Initial vitals were stable and physical examination was notable for the patient being awake and alert, no murmurs, clear lungs, and mild tenderness in the right upper quadrant of the abdomen. 

Lab results showed WBC 6.2, Hgb 15.9, Plt 104, AST 74, ALT 140, Alk ph 189, Tbili 2.1, Albumin 3.2, INR 1.5, and Lactate 1.8. Imaging revealed a cirrhotic appearing liver with redemonstration of a known RFA site in the right hepatic lobe, no evidence of portal vein thrombosis, and no intrahepatic biliary dilatation. The patient was not given any medication prior to transfer. 

The patient's past medical history includes hypertension, PTSD, mood disorder, cervical disk herniation, gastrointestinal bleeding, possible COPD, alcoholism, and chronic Hepatitis C. Past surgical history includes lipoma removal from the left upper arm, facial reconstruction post motor vehicle accident, thoracic endograft repair for penetrating thoracic aortic ulcer of the descending thoracic aorta, and bilateral metacarpal injuries post repair. The patient's mother had a myocardial infarction, but there is no family history of connective tissue disorder. 

Upon arrival to the floor, the patient confirmed the above history and reported worsening generalized weakness, mostly in his legs, along with numbness and tingling in his fingers and legs. He denied shortness of breath, but reported that it takes work to breathe. He also reported intermittent abdominal pain over the past month. 

Admission labs showed abnormal values for ALT (60), AST (107), Alk Phos (164), Total Bilirubin (2.2), Albumin (2.5), RBC (3.84), Hgb (13.6), Hct (38.4), MCV (100), MCH (35.4), RDW (16.0), and Platelet Count (104). Imaging showed a cirrhotic appearing liver with a known RFA site in the right hepatic lobe, no evidence of portal vein thrombosis, and no intrahepatic biliary dilatation. There was a subtle opacity in the right lung base raising the possibility of pneumonic infection. 

The patient's Lyme IgM was noted to be positive. Other tests including Sjo___'s antibodies, methylmalonic acid, copper, heavy metal screens, ACTH, and ACE were all within normal limits. The patient was diagnosed with Lyme disease and weakness. He was discharged home with instructions to take doxycycline 100 mg PO BID for ten days and to have outpatient physical therapy. His furosemide and spironolactone doses were decreased and metoprolol was held.

The patient, a male with a past medical history of Hepatitis C Virus (HCV) cirrhosis, Human Immunodeficiency Virus (HIV), and a history of radiofrequency ablation (RFA) for hepatocellular carcinoma, presented with acute on chronic abdominal pain. He reported worsening diffuse abdominal pain, bloating, increased foul-smelling burps, heartburn, and intolerance to heat. He was found to have leukocytosis and elevated liver function tests (LFTs) on outpatient labs. His past medical history also includes hypertension, post-traumatic stress disorder, mood disorder, thoracic aortic ulcer, cervical disk herniation, gastrointestinal bleeding, possible chronic obstructive pulmonary disease, alcoholism, and cirrhosis related to chronic Hepatitis C and alcohol. His surgical history includes lipoma removal, facial reconstruction following a motor vehicle accident, thoracic endograft repair, and bilateral metacarpal injuries repair. His family history includes a mother who had a myocardial infarction, but no connective tissue disorders.

Upon admission, the patient was on several medications including Calcium Carbonate, Descovy, Dolutegravir, Furosemide, Lactulose, Ondansetron, Oxycodone, Rifaximin, Spironolactone, Trazodone, Albuterol, Atorvastatin, Ipratropium-Albuterol, Metoprolol, Nicotine Patch, and Zinc Sulfate. He has allergies to NSAIDs, epidural injection, tramadol, and Tylenol.

During his hospital stay, the patient's labs showed elevated white blood cell count (15.0), red blood cell count (4.39), hemoglobin (15.7), mean corpuscular volume (103), mean corpuscular hemoglobin (35.8), red cell distribution width (17.3), platelet count (69), glucose (108), urea nitrogen (22), alanine aminotransferase (186), aspartate aminotransferase (217), alkaline phosphatase (267), total bilirubin (3.8), and lactate (1.8). His imaging showed no cholelithiasis or acute cholecystitis, cirrhotic liver morphology, and a patent portal vein. His gallbladder was mildly distended with surrounding pericholecystic fluid, and his liver showed cirrhotic morphology with an unchanged appearance of the ablation cavity in segment VII.

The patient was treated with IV Morphine Sulfate, IV Ampicillin-Sulbactam, PO/NG Rifaximin, and PO/NG Lactulose. Consultations with transplant surgery and hepatology determined no acute surgical issues based on ultrasound imaging and recommended admission to medicine.

The patient's hospital course was complicated by abdominal pain, transaminitis, left lower lobe consolidation, and shoulder pain. He was treated presumptively for acalculous cholecystitis with IV Unasyn transitioned to Vanc/Zosyn for a total of 8 days. He was discharged on 5 additional days of Augmentin to complete his course. His left lower lobe consolidation was thought to likely represent pneumonia for which he was already on Vanc/Zosyn. Azithromycin was also added for 5 days to cover atypicals. His shoulder pain was recommended for outpatient follow up. His HCV cirrhosis showed no evidence of acute decompensation, though he does have a history of hepatic encephalopathy and hepatocellular carcinoma. His HIV was well-controlled. 

Upon discharge, the patient was diagnosed with leukocytosis, abdominal pain, and HCV cirrhosis. His mental status was clear and coherent, and he was alert and interactive. He was advised to follow up with his primary doctor and the hepatology team.

The patient, a male with a history of Hepatitis C Virus (HCV) and alcohol-induced cirrhosis (Child B) complicated by Hepatocellular Carcinoma (HCC) post Radiofrequency Ablation (RFA), well-controlled HIV, Hypertension (HTN), Post Traumatic Stress Disorder (PTSD), Mood disorder, Penetrating thoracic aortic ulcer of the descending thoracic aorta post Thoracic Endovascular Aortic Repair (TEVAR), Cervical disk herniation, Gastrointestinal Bleeding (GIB), and possible Chronic Obstructive Pulmonary Disease (COPD), presented with lightheadedness, fatigue, and recurrent abdominal pain. He had a recent admission for diffuse abdominal pain and transaminitis consistent with acalculous cholecystitis and left lower lobe pneumonia. He was treated with antibiotics and discharged, but his abdominal pain returned and he was readmitted for IV diuresis and paracentesis. He also reported muscle cramps, subjective fevers, night sweats, intermittent chest palpitations, nausea without vomiting, and poor appetite. He had a nonbloody bowel movement and urinated earlier in the day. He had a recent admission for Lyme disease that was treated with doxycycline. He still has some tingling at the tip of his fingers bilaterally and neck stiffness. 

On physical examination, he was tender in the right upper quadrant (RUQ) and epigastrium without rebound or guarding. He became tachycardic and tachypneic upon palpation. Labs showed elevated AST/ALT/AlkPhos/Tbili and increased INR and PTT values, consistent with baseline. A RUQ with Doppler showed a distended gallbladder, but was negative for cholelithiasis, cholecystitis. Chest (PA and lateral x ray) showed an improved left lower lobe pneumonia. He received PO lactulose 30 cc, oxycodone 5 mg, and Zofran 4mg IV. A 20 gauge IV was placed in his right forearm. Cultures of blood, urine, and sputum were sent to be processed. EKG showed sinus rhythm, with no signs of ischemia and results consistent with prior EKG. 

His admission labs showed elevated RBC (4.24), MCV (105), MCH (36.1), RDW (17.2), RDWSD (67.8), Monos (15.7), AbsMono (1.13), K (5.9), HCO3 (20), ALT (148), AST (202), AlkPhos (247), TotBili (2.8), and Calcium (8.3). His Albumin was low (2.7). His urine showed few bacteria and small blood. His chest x-ray showed improved left lower lobe pneumonia. His RUQ ultrasound showed a distended gallbladder but without cholelithiasis, wall thickening, or other sonographic signs of acute cholecystitis. His abdominal ultrasound showed no ascites. His MRI of the brain and cervical spine showed no acute infarct, intracranial hemorrhage, or mass effect, and multilevel cervical spondylosis, most pronounced at C4-5. His bone scan showed a focal area of increased radiotracer uptake within the left distal acromion. His blood and urine cultures showed no growth. His mother has a history of Coronary Artery Disease (CAD). 

Upon admission, his medications included Atorvastatin, Calcium Carbonate, Descovy, Dolutegravir, Torsemide, Lactulose, Nicotine Patch, Ondansetron, Oxycodone, Rifaximin, Spironolactone, Trazodone, Zinc Sulfate, Albuterol Sulfate, Furosemide, and Ipratropium-Albuterol.

The patient, a male with a significant medical history including Hepatitis C Virus (HCV) and alcohol-induced cirrhosis (Child B), HIV, hypertension, PTSD, mood disorder, thoracic aortic ulcer, cervical disk herniation, gastrointestinal bleeding, and possible COPD, was admitted due to abdominal pain and volume overload. He has had multiple recent admissions for similar symptoms. The patient reported worsening abdominal pain over the last five days, lethargy, inability to sleep, and a 7-pound weight gain over two days. He denied any changes in diet or missed medication doses. 

Upon examination, the patient was tender to palpation throughout the abdomen, most notably in the epigastrium and around the umbilicus. Initial labs showed abnormal values for WBC (10.7), INR (1.5), potassium (5.1), ALT (100), AST (176), Alkphos (205), total bilirubin (3.3), and albumin (3.2). The patient was given oxycodone 5mg x2 for pain management. 

The patient has a history of acalculous cholecystitis and left lower lobe pneumonia, for which he was treated with various antibiotics. He was admitted 24 hours later for volume overload and underwent paracentesis with 0.9L fluid out. He was diuresed with IV lasix. On discharge, Lasix 10mg was stopped, and he was started on Torsemide 20mg, and Spironolactone increased from 25mg to 50mg. 

The patient's family history includes coronary artery disease in his mother. 

Upon discharge, labs showed abnormal values for RBC (3.56), hemoglobin (12.9), hematocrit (38.5), MCV (108), MCH (36.2), RDW (17.2), platelet count (86), PTT (66.4), ALT (100), AST (158), AlkPhos (160), and total bilirubin (3.0). 

The patient's abdominal pain was thought to be secondary to GERD and his PPI was increased to BID dosing. His volume overload was likely due to a suboptimal diuretic regimen. He received IV Lasix with improvement of weight and was euvolemic on exam. He was transitioned to lasix 40mg po. His INR increased from a baseline of 1.5 to 1.8 on admission, likely due to liver disease, but returned to 1.5 after administration of oral vitamin K. 

The patient's HIV is well controlled, with his most recent CD4 count being ___. He was continued on his home medications of Descovy and Dolutegravir. His HCV cirrhosis was managed with rifaxmin 550 BID and lactulose 30ml QID. His hepatitis C virus status is untreated, with genotype 3A and a viral load of 256,000. 

Upon discharge, the patient was instructed to weigh himself every morning, continue taking his medications as prescribed, and keep his appointments with his team of doctors. He was also advised to return to the hospital if he experienced fevers, chills, confusion, worsening abdominal pain, diarrhea, vomiting, or blood or black in his stool. His discharge medications included Furosemide 40 mg PO DAILY, Pantoprazole 40 mg PO Q12H, and his home medications.

The patient, a male with a history of Hepatitis C cirrhosis (Childs B), HIV (CD4 1106), hypertension, PTSD, mood disorder, penetrating thoracic aortic ulcer of the descending thoracic aorta post TEVAR, cervical disk herniation, GIB, and possible COPD, was admitted due to abdominal pain. He has had multiple recent admissions for abdominal pain with some imaging suggesting cholecystitis, but surgical teams have not considered him to have acute cholecystitis and no intervention was pursued. The patient also has a history of lipoma removal from the left upper arm, facial reconstruction post MVA, thoracic endograft repair for penetrating thoracic aortic ulcer of the descending thoracic aorta, and bilateral metacarpal injuries post repair. His family history includes CAD in his mother.

Upon admission, the patient was on several medications including albuterol sulfate, Atorvastatin, Calcium Carbonate, Descovy, Dolutegravir, Lactulose, Multivitamins, OxyCODONE, Rifaximin, ipratropium-albuterol, Ondansetron, Pantoprazole, Furosemide, and Spironolactone. His physical exam revealed severe tenderness to palpation diffusely, most notable in RUQ and RLQ. 

Abnormal lab results on admission included RBC-3.66*, MCV-109*, MCH-37.4*, RDW-17.2*, RDWSD-69.7*, Plt Ct-79*, Neuts-71.8*, AbsNeut-6.84*, PTT-41.4*, Glucose-101*, AnGap-8*, ALT-176*, AST-304*, AlkPhos-193*, TotBili-4.1*, and Albumin-2.6*. Imaging showed a cirrhotic liver with moderate perihepatic ascites, a distended gallbladder with wall thickening, and a patent portal vein. 

During his hospital stay, the patient's abdominal pain persisted and he experienced one day of axillary fevers. His discharge labs showed continued abnormalities including RBC-3.37*, Hgb-12.3*, Hct-36.3*, MCV-108*, MCH-36.5*, RDW-17.5*, RDWSD-70.4*, Plt Ct-81*, PTT-72.6*, Glucose-108*, Cl-109*, ALT-123*, AST-217*, AlkPhos-155*, TotBili-3.4*, Albumin-2.0*. 

The patient was discharged with a primary diagnosis of acute on chronic abdominal pain and secondary diagnoses of hepatocellular carcinoma, Hep C cirrhosis, HIV, hypertension, and known thoracic aorta ulcer. His discharge medications included Acetaminophen, Amitriptyline, Maalox/Diphenhydramine/Lidocaine, Polyethylene Glycol, Senna, OxyCODONE, albuterol sulfate, Atorvastatin, Calcium Carbonate, Descovy, Dolutegravir, Furosemide, ipratropium-albuterol, Lactulose, Multivitamins, Ondansetron, Pantoprazole, Rifaximin, Spironolactone, and TraZODone. The patient was advised to continue microwave RFA treatments and to return to the hospital if he experiences more severe pain that is not controlled by the medications or if he is unable to eat or has severe nausea and vomiting.

The patient is a male with a history of Hepatitis C cirrhosis (Childs B), HIV (CD4 1106), Hepatocellular Carcinoma (HCC) with rising AFP, hypertension, PTSD, mood disorder, penetrating thoracic aortic ulcer of the descending thoracic aorta post TEVAR, cervical disk herniation, gastrointestinal bleeding, and possible COPD. He has undergone several surgical procedures including lipoma removal from the left upper arm, facial reconstruction post motor vehicle accident, thoracic endograft repair for penetrating thoracic aortic ulcer of the descending thoracic aorta, and bilateral metacarpal injuries repair. 

The patient was admitted for severe post-procedure pain following a radiofrequency ablation of HCC. Prior to this, he had been experiencing right-sided abdominal pain and persistently elevated liver function tests. Imaging revealed gallbladder wall distension and patent portal, but subsequent HIDA scan and MRCP were unrevealing. A CTA of the liver showed interval recurrence of known HCC inferior to treatment segment in VII cavity, which was thought to be contributing to his pain. 

Upon admission, the patient reported ongoing abdominal pain, similar to prior abdominal pain but slightly sharper. He also reported chronic left shoulder pain and chronic lower extremity swelling. His physical exam revealed significant tenderness to touch in the epigastric, RUQ and RLQ regions of the abdomen, and mild asterixis on exam.

The patient, a male, was admitted to the Neurosurgery service after experiencing confusion and speech difficulties. He had recently undergone an elective left MCA aneurysm procedure and was discharged neurologically intact, aside from a baseline disconjugate gaze. His sister reported that he was unable to answer questions and would not stay on topic during a phone call, which prompted her to bring him to the ED. A CT/CTA of the head showed a left temporal-parietal intraparenchymal hemorrhage. The patient denied any falls, loss of consciousness, or numbness/tingling in arms or legs.

His past medical history includes biclonal gammopathy, AIHA, subarachnoid hemorrhage, sleep apnea, left eye strabismus, a possible cardiac abnormality in infancy, vitamin D deficiency, hypertension, and a congenital deformity of the foot. His family history includes two uncles with cerebral aneurysms, but no history of connective tissue disease.

Upon admission, his physical exam showed disconjugate gaze, right lateral nystagmus, and dysarthric speech with word-finding difficulties. His motor and sensory functions were intact. A CT/CTA head scan showed a clipped and coiled left parietal M4 segment middle cerebral artery aneurysm with expected postsurgical changes, a small parenchymal hemorrhage, and patent intracranial and neck vasculature. A CXR showed marked cardiomegaly with mild pulmonary vascular congestion. An MRI of the brain showed no evidence of acute infarct, but did show postsurgical changes, hemorrhage/residual postoperative blood products, and an unchanged right posterior nasal cavity and anterior nasopharynx polypoid lesion.

During his hospital stay, the patient experienced multiple episodes of self-limiting word-finding difficulties, which had resolved upon arrival. He denied any additional neurologic deficits. He was admitted to the ICU for close neurological monitoring and was started on high-dose Dexamethasone for post-operative cerebral edema. His urine was positive for Enterococcus, and he was treated with Ciprofloxacin. He remained clinically stable throughout hospitalization and was discharged home with close neurosurgical follow-up.

His medications upon admission included Tylenol, Bisacodyl, Furosemide, Lisinopril, Oxycodone, and Prednisone. His discharge diagnoses were cerebral edema and expressive aphasia. His mental status was clear and coherent, and he was alert and interactive. He was ambulatory and independent upon discharge.

The male patient was admitted due to weight gain and atrial fibrillation. He has a history of moderate-to-severe mitral regurgitation, moderate-to-severe aortic regurgitation, pulmonary hypertension, and recent brain coiling. He also has a history of hypertension, dyslipidemia, clipping of his left middle cerebral artery aneurysm, multiple myeloma, autoimmune hemolytic anemia, biclonal gammopathy, subarachnoid hemorrhage, sleep apnea, left eye strabismus, vitamin D deficiency, and congenital deformity of foot. He has a family history of cerebral aneurysms. 

During his stay, he underwent Afib cardioversion, right heart catheterization, and left heart catheterization. His physical examination revealed irregular heart rhythm, bibasilar crackles with mild expiratory wheezes, and no chest wall deformities, scoliosis or kyphosis. His discharge physical exam showed normal heart rhythm, clear lungs, and trace lower extremity edema.

His admission labs showed elevated proBNP (2486), WBC (10.3), RDW (15.7), RDWSD (48.8), NEUTS (78.8), LYMPHS (11.3), EOS (0.2), NUC RBCS (0.2), AbsNeut (8.10), AbsLymp (1.16), AbsMono (0.91), AbsEos (0.02), UREA N (41), and CREAT (1.5). His discharge labs showed elevated RDWSD (49.1), PTT (76.1), UreaN (43), Creat (1.4), ALT (57), and AST (45). 

His echocardiogram showed moderate to severe eccentric mitral regurgitation secondary to flail anterior and mitral valve prolapse, moderate to severe aortic regurgitation, and moderate to severe tricuspid regurgitation. His RUQ U/S showed multiple splenic hemangiomas. 

He was treated for atrial fibrillation with RVR, acute on chronic diastolic heart failure, moderate-to-severe mitral regurgitation and moderate-to-severe aortic regurgitation, abnormal LFTS, and exposure to scabies. He was also treated for autoimmune hemolytic anemia with prednisone. 

His medications upon admission were Lisinopril, Furosemide, PredniSONE, LeVETiracetam, and Aspirin. His discharge medications included Aspirin, LeVETiracetam, Lisinopril, PredniSONE, Metoprolol Succinate XL, Torsemide, Warfarin, and Ivermectin. 

His discharge diagnoses were acute on chronic diastolic heart failure, atrial fibrillation, moderate-to-severe mitral regurgitation, and moderate-to-severe aortic regurgitation.

The patient, a male of unspecified age, was admitted to the Cardiac Thoracic unit. He has a known history of Subarachnoid Hemorrhage (SAH) with a history of coiling of aneurysm and subsequent clipping, Autoimmune Hemolytic Anemia (AIHA), and mitral regurgitation. He presented with Congestive Heart Failure (CHF) and was evaluated and advised to recover and follow-up as an outpatient. He remained asymptomatic and returned to the clinic for clipping of cerebral aneurysm. He later presented with CHF and Atrial Fibrillation (AFib). He was cardioverted to Sinus Rhythm (SR) and diuresed. An echo showed severe Mitral Regurgitation (MR), Tricuspid Regurgitation (TR) and Aortic Insufficiency (AI) with preserved Ejection Fraction (EF). 

His past medical history includes hypertension, dyslipidemia, clipping of his left middle cerebral artery aneurysm, multiple myeloma post bone biopsy, Autoimmune Hemolytic Anemia on prednisone, biclonal gammopathy, sleep apnea, left eye strabismus, vitamin D deficiency, and congenital deformity of foot. His family history includes two uncles with cerebral aneurysms. 

During his hospital stay, he underwent mitral valve replacement with a size 31 mm mechanical valve and aortic valve replacement with a 23 mm mechanical valve. Post-operatively, he was transferred to the CVICU in stable condition for recovery and invasive monitoring. He was extubated, alert and oriented, and breathing comfortably. He was evaluated by the physical therapy service for assistance with strength and mobility. 

His physical exam showed strabismus of the left eye, facial twitching, skin tags on the left cheek, and upper and lower dentures. His blood tests showed abnormal Hct-31.8, RBC-3.29, Hgb-9.6, Hct-28.9, RDWSD-48.0, UreaN-27, RBC-3.46, Hgb-10.4, Hct-30.6, RBC-3.11, Hgb-9.3, Hct-27.5, WBC-10.6, RBC-3.07, Hgb-9.2, Hct-26.7, WBC-12.6, RBC-3.49, Hgb-10.3, Hct-30.9, and PTT-84.2. 

His medications upon admission included Levetiracetam, Lisinopril, Metoprolol Succinate, Prednisone, Torsemide, Warfarin, Aspirin, and Ferrous Sulfate. 

Upon discharge, his diagnosis included severe mitral regurgitation, aortic insufficiency, multiple myeloma post bone biopsy, Autoimmune Hemolytic Anemia on prednisone, biclonal gammopathy, subarachnoid hemorrhage post coiling, sleep apnea, left eye strabismus, vitamin D deficiency, hypertension, and congenital deformity of foot. His condition at discharge was alert and oriented, ambulating with a steady gait, sternal pain managed with oral analgesics, sternal incision healing well with mild erythema at distal pole and no drainage, and trace edema.

The male patient was admitted due to tamponade seen on a scheduled echocardiogram. His past medical history includes hypertension, dyslipidemia, clipping of his left middle cerebral artery aneurysm, multiple myeloma, autoimmune hemolytic anemia, biclonal gammopathy, subarachnoid hemorrhage, sleep apnea, left eye strabismus, vitamin D deficiency, and a congenital deformity of the foot. He has a family history of cerebral aneurysms in two uncles. 

Upon admission, the patient reported feeling well and denied chest pain, shortness of breath, leg swelling, abdominal pain, and lightheadedness. Initial vital signs were normal, but labs showed an INR of 5.0, WBC of 10.8, H/H of 12.1/38.4, K of 3.1, and lactate of 2.6. The patient was given 1L of normal saline. 

The patient underwent a pericardial drain and AVR procedures. An echocardiogram showed a large posterior effusion with tamponade physiology and 3+ aortic regurgitation. A TEE was planned to evaluate the aortic valve. 

Admission labs showed several abnormalities including WBC-10.8, Hgb-12.1, Hct-38.4, MCHC-31.5, RDW-15.9, RDWSD-47.3, Neuts-84.3, Lymphs-7.5, Eos-0.1, AbsNeut-9.08, AbsLymp-0.81, AbsMono-0.81, AbsEos-0.01, PTT-44.1, UreaN-26, Creat-1.3, K-3.1, pO2-324, pCO2-33, pH-7.53, and Lactate-2.6. 

Imaging studies showed a moderately dilated left atrium, markedly dilated right atrium, mild symmetric left ventricular hypertrophy, normal left ventricular cavity size, low normal left ventricular systolic function, normal right ventricular free wall thickness, normal right ventricular chamber size, abnormal septal motion/position, mildly dilated aortic arch, bileaflet aortic valve prosthesis, moderate to severe aortic regurgitation, bileaflet mitral valve prosthesis, mildly thickened tricuspid valve leaflets, moderate tricuspid regurgitation, borderline pulmonary artery systolic hypertension, moderate to large sized pericardial effusion, right ventricular diastolic collapse, and significant respiratory variation in mitral/tricuspid valve inflows. 

The patient's medications upon admission included amiodarone, aspirin, atorvastatin, levetiracetam, metoprolol tartrate, prednisone, ranitidine, and torsemide. 

During his hospital stay, the patient was treated for pericardial effusion/tamponade, acute kidney injury, atrial fibrillation, diastolic heart failure, autoimmune hemolytic anemia, subarachnoid hemorrhage post-aneurysm repair, hyperlipidemia, and GERD. He underwent a pericardial window procedure and was transferred to the telemetry floor for further recovery. By the time of discharge, the patient was ambulating freely, the wound was healing, and pain was controlled with oral analgesics.

The patient, a male with a history of hypertension, hyperlipidemia, mitral and aortic regurgitation post mitral valve replacement/aortic valve replacement, atrial fibrillation, diastolic heart failure, subarachnoid hemorrhage post coiling/clipping of his left middle cerebral artery aneurysm, biclonal gammopathy, autoimmune hemolytic anemia, sleep apnea, left eye strabismus, vitamin D deficiency, and congenital foot deformity, was admitted due to tamponade seen on echocardiogram. He presented with right lower quadrant/groin pain and was found to have an INR of concern for pericardial effusion. He denied chest pain, shortness of breath, back pain, fever, night sweats, chills, dizziness, headache, hematochezia, hematuria, and dysuria. 

During his stay, a pericardial drain was placed and removed, and he was found to have a large pericardial effusion with left ventricular collapse, right rectus sheath hematoma, and circumferential wall thickening of the ileocecal valve and distal ileum. His labs showed an INR >8, WBC 10.2, Hgb 10.8/Hct 38, Plt 339, Na+ 141/K+3.4/Cl- 92/CO2 33/Cr 1.5, EKG NSR 75 PR 216 (first degree block), PTT 41.9, INR 5.0, K 3.2, Mg 2.1, P 3, Ca 8.4, AST 44/ALT 11, T bili 1.2, Lipase 65, Albumin 3.0, Hgb 9.6/Hct 32.3, and Lactate 1.9. He was given potassium chloride, 2 units FFP, and Vit K 5 mg IV. 

His family history includes two uncles with cerebral aneurysms but no history of connective tissue disease. His medications upon admission included amiodarone, aspirin, levetiracetam, metoprolol tartrate, prednisone, ranitidine, torsemide, atorvastatin, docusate sodium, magnesium oxide, milk of magnesia, and oxycodone. 

During his hospital stay, the patient's pericardial effusion was drained, and he was placed on colchicine for pericarditis. His rectus sheath hematoma improved with vitamin K and FFP. He was maintained on a heparin drip due to his mechanical valves and INR reversal. His atrial fibrillation was managed with metoprolol and he was taken off amiodarone. His diastolic heart failure was managed by adjusting his torsemide dose due to acute kidney injury and increasing bicarbonate. His autoimmune hemolytic anemia was managed with continued prednisone, and his subarachnoid hemorrhage post aneurysm repair was managed with continued Keppra. His hyperlipidemia was managed with continued atorvastatin, and his GERD was managed with continued ranitidine. 

All cultures from his pericardial fluid were negative. His echocardiograms showed a large pericardial effusion with no signs of tamponade, and a small pericardial effusion with no signs of tamponade after the drain was placed. His right heart catheterization and intervention report showed a drop in RA pressure from 17 to 10 mm Hg after 120 cc of bloody fluid were removed and an additional 300 cc drained. His CO was 5.63 and CI was 2.52.

The patient, a male, was admitted to the Cardiac-Thoracic unit due to chest pain. His medical history includes a spontaneous pneumothorax, for which he underwent a right blebectomy and lobectomy, and a nonoperative distal radius fracture on the left side. During this admission, he was found to have a left apical pneumothorax, which was confirmed by a chest X-ray. A pleural pigtail catheter was placed to manage this condition. 

The patient's father may have had a pneumothorax at a young age. There is no family history of genetic connective tissue disorders. 

Upon admission, the patient's physical examination was generally normal, with the exception of a pectus excavatum and a left-sided pigtail in place. His discharge examination was also generally normal, with the exception of a left axillary incision site with a dressing in place to prevent leakage. 

Admission labs showed an elevated white blood cell count of 12.7 and a slightly elevated glucose level of 118. Discharge labs showed a normal white blood cell count and glucose level, but an elevated magnesium level of 2.3. 

Imaging on admission showed a small to moderate left-sided pneumothorax with no evidence of mediastinal shift. After the pigtail catheter was removed, imaging showed no evidence of a pneumothorax. 

During his hospital stay, the patient's pain was managed with oral pain medications and he received heparin for DVT prophylaxis. His incision was clean, dry, and intact without evidence of erythema or drainage. He remained hemodynamically stable throughout his hospital stay. 

The patient was not taking any medications prior to admission. His discharge diagnosis was spontaneous pneumothorax. He was discharged in a clear and coherent mental state, alert and interactive, and able to move independently.

The patient, a female with no significant past medical history, was admitted for intravenous heparin therapy due to a carotid dissection identified on an MRI/MRA. She reported a history of bronchitis with violent coughing and fatigue, as well as a left frontal headache a month prior to admission. Following the resolution of these symptoms, she developed pulsatile tinnitus in her right ear for three days, which then shifted to her left ear. This symptom persisted and was more noticeable when she was lying in bed at night, corresponding to her heartbeat. She had no recent neck pain, trauma, heavy lifting, chiropractic manipulations, hair appointments, etc. She frequently lifts her young children but reported no recent head or neck strain. She also reported no headache, visual changes, speech changes, dysphagia, facial droop, weakness, sensory changes, incoordination, bowel or bladder changes, or recent fevers, chills, shortness of breath, chest pain, palpitations, nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, or constipation.

Her past medical history includes myomectomies for fibroids, a cesarean section, a history of ectopic pregnancy, Asherman's syndrome, and a history of supraventricular tachycardia during pregnancy. Her family history includes her mother, who died with dementia and aortic sclerosis, and her father, who died of T cell leukemia and had hypertension.

Upon physical examination, she was awake, cooperative, and showed no signs of distress. Her vital signs were stable and within normal limits. Her neurological examination was normal, with no evidence of apraxia or neglect. Her cranial nerves were intact, and her motor and sensory functions were normal. Her deep tendon reflexes were normal, and her coordination was intact. Her gait was not tested.

Her admission labs showed an abnormal MCHC value of 35.5 and an abnormal RBC count of 15 in her urine. An MRI/MRA of her head revealed an abnormal appearance of the proximal intracranial portion of the left internal carotid artery, representing a focal dissection of this vessel. There was no occlusion, definite pseudoaneurysm, or evidence of embolic infarction in its vascular distribution. A CT angiogram of her head/neck confirmed the dissection and also revealed a small mucous retention cyst in the left maxillary sinus and a 4mm left thyroid lobe nodule.

During her hospital stay, she was initiated on IV heparin therapy and received her first dose of coumadin on her second hospital day. A CT angiogram was obtained to better visualize the extent of her carotid dissection and evaluate for the possibility of a pseudoaneurysm. The patient was informed of the small left thyroid lobe nodule and it was recommended that she should have an ultrasound of her thyroid done over the next weeks. She was discharged in a stable condition with instructions to follow up with her primary care physician within one week and to return to her PCP's office in two days for a follow-up INR check and titration of warfarin dose. She was also advised to return in three weeks for an outpatient follow-up CTA of head/neck to ensure that the aneurysm does not grow in size. Her medication upon admission was a daily multivitamin.

The patient is a left-handed woman who has a recent history of left internal carotid artery dissection and is currently on coumadin. She presented with a 15-second episode of dizziness. The onset of her symptoms began with a headache following a violent coughing episode. She subsequently developed pulsatile tinnitus and was discovered to have a dissection of the petrous portion of the left internal carotid artery. Initial treatment with heparin was transitioned to coumadin. Since her discharge, she has been doing well with only mild headaches responsive to tylenol and a decrease in the intensity of the pulsatile tinnitus. 

On the day of evaluation, she experienced a sudden onset of profound dizziness and a sensation of her head being in a vacuum. She denies vertigo and likens the dizziness sensation to lightheadedness. She recalls her heart pounding prior to symptom onset. The symptom was associated with anxiety and a sense of impending doom. It spontaneously resolved within 15 seconds. She denies chest discomfort prior to symptom onset. She has had decreased fluid intake and denies new medications other than the anticoagulants. 

Her past medical history includes dissection of petrous portion L ICA (attributed to coughing spells), myomectomies for fibroids, c section, ectopic pregnancy, Asherman's, and SVT during pregnancy. Her family history includes her mother who died with dementia and aortic disease, and her father who died of T cell leukemia and had hypertension. 

Upon admission, her physical exam was normal. Her neurologic examination was also normal. Her lab results showed an abnormal MCHC of 35.2 and 3 RBCs in her urine. Her CTA Head and Neck, CXR, and MRA and MRI brain results were normal except for the unchanged appearance of the left carotid dissection in the distal cervical region. 

During her hospital stay, she had several brief episodes of tachycardia seen on telemetry with HR in the 120's. No other cardiac arrhythmia was seen on either telemetry or EKG. The etiology of her dizziness is not clear. It was recommended that she undergo outpatient evaluation by a cardiologist. The dizziness was possibly anxiety-related. Her INR was subtherapeutic so she was restarted on a lovenox bridge. 

Upon admission, she was on coumadin and multivitamin. Her discharge diagnosis was dizziness. Her mental status was clear and coherent, she was alert and interactive, and she was ambulatory - independent with no neurologic deficits.

The patient, a female, was admitted for surgery following a right tibia fracture sustained while ice skating. This was an isolated injury and the patient denied any head strike or loss of consciousness, as well as any numbness or paresthesias. Her past medical history includes dissection of the petrous portion of the left internal carotid artery (attributed to coughing spells), myomectomies for fibroids, a cesarean section, an ectopic pregnancy, Asherman's syndrome, and supraventricular tachycardia during pregnancy. She has no known allergies or adverse drug reactions. Her family history is non-contributory.

Upon physical examination, the patient was in no acute distress and had unlabored breathing. Her abdomen was soft, non-tender, and non-distended. The incision was clean, dry, and intact with no erythema or discharge, and minimal ecchymosis. She was in an aircast boot on her right lower extremity. Her right lower extremity dorsalis pedis pulse was 2+ with distal digits warm and well perfused.

An X-ray of the tibia/fibula showed steps related to the fixation of a spiral fracture of the distal tibia, with placement of a tibial intra medullary rod with associated proximal and distal interlocking screws. Additional screws transfix a longitudinal fracture through the posterior aspect of the distal tibia, which extends to the tibiotalar articular surface. A fibular neck fracture was also noted.

During her hospital stay, the patient underwent surgery for a right tibia intramedullary nail, which she tolerated well. Post-surgery, she was initially given IV fluids and IV pain medications, and progressed to a regular diet and oral medications by post-operative day 1. She was given antibiotics and anticoagulation per routine. Her home medications, Simvastatin 20mg daily and ASA 81mg daily, were continued throughout her hospitalization. The operative splint was taken down on post-operative day 1 and she was transitioned to an aircast boot. Her hospital course was otherwise unremarkable.

At the time of discharge, the patient's pain was well controlled with oral medications, incisions were clean/dry/intact, and she was voiding/moving bowels spontaneously. She was non-weight bearing in the right lower extremity and was discharged on Lovenox for DVT prophylaxis. Her mental status was clear and coherent, and she was alert and interactive. Her activity status was independent ambulation. Her discharge diagnosis was a right tibia fracture.

The patient, a male, was admitted due to progressive left hip pain. After conservative management failed, he underwent a left total hip arthroplasty. His past medical history includes paroxysmal atrial fibrillation, dyslipidemia, likely obstructive sleep apnea, and osteoarthritis. He has no known allergies or adverse drug reactions. 

Upon admission, the patient was on several medications including Aspirin, Doxazosin, Fluticasone Propionate nasal spray, Hydrocodone-Acetaminophen for pain, Metoprolol Succinate XL, Finasteride, and Flecainide Acetate. 

The physical examination revealed that the patient was in no acute distress, with stable vital signs and well-controlled pain. The incision from the surgery was healing well with staples and scant serosanguinous drainage. The thigh was full but soft, with no calf tenderness. Neurologically, the patient was intact with no focal deficits. 

Lab results showed abnormal values for RBC (4.10, 3.81, 4.20), Hgb (13.4, 12.4, 13.4), Hct (39.1, 36.0, 39.6), MCH (32.6, 32.4), Glucose (109), and UreaN (32). 

Postoperative course was remarkable for sciatic pain, which was not affected by cyclobenzaprine but showed good effect with diazepam, lidoderm patch, and gabapentin. Pain was initially controlled with a PCA and then transitioned to oral pain medications. The patient received lovenox for DVT prophylaxis. The surgical dressing was changed and the incision was found to be clean and intact. The patient was seen daily by physical therapy and labs were checked throughout the hospital course. 

Upon discharge, the patient was diagnosed with left hip osteoarthritis. His mental status was clear and coherent, and he was alert and interactive. He was ambulatory but required assistance or aid. The patient was discharged to home with services in stable condition.

The male patient, with a past medical history of paroxysmal atrial fibrillation, dyslipidemia, likely obstructive sleep apnea, and osteoarthritis, was admitted due to recurrent episodes of dizziness and lightheadedness that had acutely worsened over the last 72 hours. The patient reported that these episodes had been increasing in frequency over the last month, occurring without any discernible triggers and lasting for minutes at a time. He denied syncope, chest pain, edema, and orthopnea throughout this entire period. He had been taking all his medications as prescribed, which included aspirin, doxazosin, finasteride, flecainide acetate, and metoprolol succinate. 

Upon admission, the patient's heart rate was notably low at 41 bpm. Initial treatment in the emergency department included 1 mg IV glucagon, Zofran, and 500 ml NS IVF, which improved his heart rate to the 50s. The patient was then admitted for further monitoring. 

The patient's family history includes his mother having a myocardial infarction and hypertension, and his father having a stroke. There were no early unexplained deaths or sudden cardiac death in the family. 

Physical examination upon admission and discharge showed mild bradycardia with no murmurs, gallops, or rubs. The patient was well developed, well nourished, and oriented. No significant skin lesions or rashes were noted. 

Abnormal lab results upon admission included glucose at 105, urea nitrogen at 30, mean corpuscular hemoglobin at 34.8, and platelet count at 144. Monocytes and eosinophils were also elevated at 14.4 and 8.6 respectively. 

Imaging studies showed increased infrahilar opacity in the right lung, concerning for pneumonia in the right clinical setting. There was also moderate elongation of the descending aorta and moderate retrocardiac atelectasis. 

During the hospital stay, the patient underwent placement of a dual chamber permanent pacemaker for sick sinus syndrome, which resolved his symptoms. Upon discharge, the patient was diagnosed with symptomatic bradycardia, sick sinus syndrome, and paroxysmal atrial fibrillation. His mental status was clear and coherent, and he was alert, interactive, and independently ambulatory.

The patient, a male, was admitted to the neurosurgery department with a chief complaint of headache. He was transferred from an outside hospital where a CT scan revealed a subarachnoid hemorrhage. The patient reported experiencing an electric shock sensation traveling up his spine to his head while at work, which was followed by a progressively severe headache, nausea, and vomiting. He was evaluated by his primary care physician who ordered a head CT at the ER, which confirmed the subarachnoid hemorrhage. The patient was then transferred for neurosurgical evaluation. 

The patient's past medical history includes cardiac stenting and coronary artery bypass grafting (CABG). His family history is significant for multiple cerebrovascular accidents (CVAs) in his sister, father, and mother. There is no family history of polycystic kidney disease or Ehlers Danlos syndrome. 

Upon physical examination, the patient was awake, alert, and cooperative. His vital signs were stable and his neurological examination was normal. An angiogram was performed as a major surgical or invasive procedure during his hospital stay. 

The patient's lab results showed abnormal values for RBC (3.73), HGB (12.4), HCT (34.8), MCH (33.2), MCHC (35.6), NEUTS (75.2), and LYMPHS (17.8). A head CT and CTA revealed hyperdensity anterior to the brainstem, a small degree of hydrocephalus, and no obvious aneurysm or arteriovenous malformation (AVM). 

During his hospital stay, the patient continued to experience headaches but remained neurologically stable. He developed a low-grade fever and blood cultures were sent, which were still pending at the time of discharge. An MRI of the C/T spine was negative for AVM but showed spinal stenosis. 

The patient's medications upon admission included lopressor, lipitor, ASA, lisinopril, fish oil, and a multivitamin. 

Upon discharge, the patient was neurologically stable. His discharge instructions included taking his pain medication as prescribed, limiting exercise to walking, increasing fluid and fiber intake, and avoiding anti-inflammatory medications unless directed by his doctor. He was also advised to contact his surgeon immediately if he experienced new onset of tremors or seizures, any confusion or change in mental status, any numbness, tingling, or weakness in his extremities, or pain or headache that was continually increasing or not relieved by pain medication.

The patient, a left-handed female, was admitted due to intermittent headaches and right neck and shoulder pain, similar to her previous episode of vertebral artery dissection. She has a past medical history of atraumatic right vertebral artery dissection. The patient reported that her symptoms began four days prior to admission, with no history of trauma or unusual activities. She initially managed her discomfort with NSAIDs (ibuprofen) but her pain progressed, leading to her decision to seek medical attention. She also experienced a transient episode of right eye blurring while working on the computer, which resolved spontaneously. The patient denied experiencing diplopia, dysarthria, dysphagia, lightheadedness, vertigo, tinnitus, hearing difficulty, difficulties producing or comprehending speech, focal weakness, numbness, parasthesiae, bowel or bladder incontinence or retention, and difficulty with gait. She also denied recent fever, chills, cough, shortness of breath, chest pain, tightness, palpitations, nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, constipation, abdominal pain, change in bowel or bladder habits, dysuria, arthralgias, myalgias, and rash.

The patient's family history is notable for a maternal grandfather with multiple strokes at an old age, but no known history of arterial dissection or connective tissue disease. She is allergic to codeine. Upon physical examination, the patient was found to be in mild discomfort, with tenderness along the right paraspinals, trapezi, and scapula, and very limited head turn to the right. Neurological examination was largely normal.

Lab results showed an abnormal RBC count of 3.64 and an abnormal Eosinophil count of 0.9. MRI and MRA of the brain and neck, as well as CT and CTA of the head and neck, showed no evidence of hemorrhage, edema, masses, infarction, stenosis, occlusion, or aneurysm formation. The patient's hospital course was uneventful, with no new dissection found on MRA. Her neck and shoulder pain was determined to be likely muscular in origin and was managed with valium and tizanidine. The patient was discharged home in a clear and coherent mental state, alert and interactive, and able to ambulate independently. Her discharge diagnosis included a history of atraumatic vertebral dissection and musculoskeletal neck and shoulder pain. The patient was advised to follow a heart-healthy diet, engage in daily exercise, and practice stress reduction techniques. She was instructed to follow up with her primary care physician in the next few weeks.

The patient, a white male, was admitted due to experiencing pain between his shoulder blades during exertion over the past several months. The pain was occasionally severe. A stress test was negative, but a CT scan revealed a thoracic aortic aneurysm. The patient is hypertensive and a smoker. He was started on Losartan. A connective tissue disorder was considered but ruled out. 

His past medical history includes hypercholesterolemia, thoracic aortic aneurysm, hypertension, and erectile dysfunction. His family history is significant for his grandfather who died from a ruptured aneurysm. 

Upon admission, the patient was on Losartan 50 mg daily, Toprol XL 50 mg daily, Cialis as needed, and Ambien 10 mg as needed at bedtime. 

The patient underwent a valve-sparing aortic root replacement procedure. Post-procedure, he was transferred to the CVICU in stable condition on Phenylephrine and Propofol drips. Cefazolin was used for surgical antibiotic prophylaxis. 

During his hospital stay, the patient developed a fever. C-difficile toxin was negative on two occasions and he reported frequent diarrhea episodes related to diet. This resolved with one Imodium tablet. Urinalysis was negative. White blood cell count remained normal and sternal incision showed no signs of infection. Fevers resolved. 

Abnormal lab results include RBC-2.90*, Hgb-9.4*, Hct-26.5*, MCH-32.5*, MCHC-35.6*, RBC-2.86*, Hgb-8.8*, Hct-25.4*, and K-3.2*, Glucose-110*, Na-132*, Cl-93*. 

Upon discharge, the patient was diagnosed with aortic root aneurysm, hypertension, hypercholesterolemia, and erectile dysfunction. He was alert and oriented, ambulating with a steady gait, and his sternal pain was managed with Dilaudid as needed.

The patient, a female of unspecified age, was admitted due to pleuritic pain. She has a history of intermittent chest tightness for the past year, which was found to be due to a moderate-sized right pneumothorax. The patient has had five episodes of chest discomfort, usually right-sided, accompanied by a feeling of tightness/muscle spasm, with pain upon inspiration occasionally associated with wheezing and dyspnea. The discomfort is typically on the front of the right chest, though it radiates to the back at times. She has no known allergies or adverse drug reactions.

Her past medical history includes left shoulder pain, irritable bowel syndrome, dysmenorrhea, female infertility, elevated prolactin level, visual changes, bilateral retinal lattice degeneration, and vitreous syneresis. Her family history includes her father having a stroke and her mother having a thyroid disorder.

Upon admission, she was not taking any preadmission medications. During her hospital stay, she underwent a right pleural chest tube placement and VATS right upper lobe and right lower lobe superior segment wedge resections, diaphragmatic biopsy with primary repair, and mechanical and chemical 0.5 g doxycycline pleurodesis.

Her physical exam revealed marked joint hyper-mobility of the wrist and hyper-extendable elbows. She was thin but not marfanoid appearing. Her vitals were stable and her pain was controlled with IV Tylenol and Dilaudid. Her chest tube drain put out a modest amount of tan, bloody fluid and had no air leak. Her post pull chest xray showed no pneumothorax. Her room air saturations were 95%.

Lab results showed WBC-7.2, RBC-4.07, HGB-12.1, HCT-36.3, MCV-89, MCH-29.7, MCHC-33.3, RDW-13.1, RDWSD-42.6, PLT COUNT-334, PTT-29.3, GLUCOSE-88, UREA N-14, CREAT-0.8, SODIUM-139, POTASSIUM-5.3, CHLORIDE-105, TOTAL CO2-23, ANION GAP-11. A CT scan of the chest revealed a moderate right pneumothorax with moderate right middle lobe atelectasis, with no underlying mechanism identified. There was no CT evidence for tension pneumothorax.

The patient was discharged with a diagnosis of spontaneous right pneumothorax. Her mental status was clear and coherent, and she was alert and interactive. She was ambulatory and independent. Her discharge medications included Acetaminophen, Cyclobenzaprine, Docusate Sodium, Milk of Magnesia, and OxyCODONE. She was instructed to continue using her incentive spirometer, check her incisions daily, and avoid ibuprofen for the next two weeks, among other instructions. She was scheduled for a follow-up appointment in the Thoracic Clinic in two weeks.

The patient, a female, was admitted due to shortness of breath. She has a history of left shoulder pain, irritable bowel syndrome, dysmenorrhea, female infertility, elevated prolactin level, visual changes, bilateral retinal lattice degeneration, and vitreous syneresis. Her family history includes her father having a stroke and her mother having a thyroid disorder. Upon admission, she was taking Cyclobenzaprine 10 mg PO TID as needed for spasms.

The patient had previously undergone VATS RUL and RLL wedge and mechanical and chemical pleurodesis for spontaneous pneumothorax, and biopsy of some pigmented diaphragmatic lesions. She presented with recurrent right basilar PTX. She experienced right-sided lower pleuritic type pain, worse on inspiration, and shortness of breath. Pathology from the VATS had shown fibroelastotic scarring and fibrin/hemorrhage suggestive of prior pneumothorax, with the diaphragmatic lesions consistent with hemosiderin deposition. There was some concern of pulmonary endometriosis given the temporal association with the patient's menses, however, this was not demonstrated on biopsy.

During her hospital stay, she underwent CT guided placement of right pleural pigtail catheter, intrapleural Doxycycline pleurodesis, and VATS pleurodesis, blebectomy, diaphragmatic repair. Post-procedure, her pain was managed with an epidural catheter and dPCA. Her diet was advanced to regular with no issues. Her PCA was discontinued on POD2 and her chest tubes were removed later that day. Repeat chest x-ray after chest tube removal revealed only a trace pneumothorax. Her epidural catheter was subsequently discontinued and she was transitioned to oral pain medications.

Abnormal lab results upon admission included MCHC at 31.6. Upon discharge, abnormal lab results included RBC at 3.71, Hgb at 11.0, MCHC at 31.9, Glucose at 105, and AnGap at 9. Radiographs showed a significant interval reduction in the right pneumothorax following the insertion of the right basal pigtail pleural drainage catheter. However, a repeat CXR revealed worsening of the pneumothorax which now had a fluid component.

The patient was discharged with a diagnosis of recurrent right pneumothorax. Her mental status was clear and coherent, and she was alert, interactive, and independently ambulatory.

The patient is a right-handed female who presented for further evaluation of recurrent nocturnal events. These episodes began a year ago, following a systemic, viral-like illness with low-grade fevers and malaise. The patient described waking from sleep with a sensation of muscle tension and 'discharging excess energy from her fingers.' She would then extend her back and extremities into the bed, with episodes typically lasting 3 seconds. She reported no auras, loss of consciousness, tongue biting, urinary incontinence, or postictal symptoms such as confusion. The longest episode-free interval was a 6-week period.

The patient's past medical history includes scoliosis, hyperlipidemia, mild aortic dilation, and sinus problems post-surgical repair of 'impaction'. She has undergone an MRI of her spine, which showed chronic spinal disease and scoliosis. An EEG, PSH, and brain MRI were reportedly normal. A sleep study was also conducted but was not diagnostic. The patient's family history includes a son with a seizure disorder, but no other neurological diseases were reported in the family.

Upon physical examination, the patient was found to be in no apparent distress, lying in bed comfortably. Her vital signs were stable and within normal limits. The neurological examination was largely unremarkable, with the patient being awake, alert, and oriented. Her motor and sensory functions were intact, and her gait was normal.

Lab results showed a slightly low RBC count at 4.04. All other lab results, including WBC, HGB, HCT, MCV, MCH, MCHC, RDW, PLT COUNT, GLUCOSE, UREA N, CREAT, SODIUM, POTASSIUM, CHLORIDE, TOTAL CO2, ANION GAP, CALCIUM, PHOSPHATE, MAGNESIUM, ALT(SGPT), AST(SGOT), and ALK PHOS, were within normal limits.

The patient was admitted to the epilepsy service for EEG LTM. Her interictal EEG showed intermittent left greater than right temporal/parietal theta slowing, as well as frequent left temporal/parietal sharp transients, likely epileptiform in nature. During an event, she had multiple myoclonic jerks, primarily of the right shoulder/arm, sometimes involving the entire body, and at times associated with dystonic posturing of the arms. The EEG during this event showed an arousal pattern. Given the stereotyped nature of the events and the abnormal interictal EEG, it was suspected that these events are ictal in origin.

Upon admission, the patient was on Lipitor 80 mg QHS and Metoprolol 6.25 mg BID. The patient was diagnosed with nocturnal frontal lobe epilepsy. Her mental status was clear and coherent, and she was alert and interactive. She was ambulatory and independent at the time of discharge.

The patient, a female with a history of nocturnal frontal lobe epilepsy, severe anxiety, depression, scoliosis, hyperlipidemia, mild aortic dilation, sinus problems post surgical repair of 'impaction', episcleritis, and nephrolithiasis, was admitted to the neurology service due to increased anxiety, sleep difficulties, and nocturnal events. She has had previous psychiatric admissions due to her severe anxiety and depression, which are possibly related to her epilepsy. Her symptoms began several years ago and were characterized by recurrent nocturnal events involving arousal from sleep, a poorly characterized energy sensation, core tightening, and bilateral tonic extension of the arms and legs lasting several seconds, as well as daytime right shoulder myoclonic jerks. 

Her EEG demonstrated left temporal/parietal slowing and frequent interictal epileptiform discharges in the same region. An MRI showed some nonspecific subcortical T2/FLAIR hyperintensities, but no concerning lesions. There were a couple small lesions in the left frontal lobe, one near the motor strip, that could potentially explain some of her symptoms; however, no definite source was present. She has undergone trials of carbamazepine and clonazepam, with no clear improvement in her symptoms, and is now off both. 

She had worsening and severe anxiety that ultimately led to inpatient psychiatric admission where she was started on Gabapentin (now at 200mg BID) and Nortriptyline 25mg BID, which initially seemed to help get her anxiety under good control. However, her anxiety worsened again. In terms of seizure control, her event frequency had decreased significantly, but she developed a new event that has been occurring nightly: sudden awakening from sleep, followed by irresistible urge to move/rock her legs back and forth. This is associated with intense sensation of fear and dread. 

During her hospital stay, she had several episodes of waking from sleep usually in the morning hours, with feeling anxious and variably being impelled to move her feet. There were no EEG changes associated with these episodes, which may last for an hour or so. After discussion with her outpatient neurologist, Lamotrigine 12.5mg po BID was started, without any rashes noted. Gabapentin was continued, and may be tapered as outpatient. 

Her family history includes a son with developmental disabilities and seizure disorder, and a maternal cousin with nocturnal events beginning with leg twitches. Her medications upon admission included Gabapentin 200mg TID, Fluvoxamine 200mg daily, Nortriptyline 25mg BID (tapering down), and Atorvastatin 80mg daily. Her lab results showed abnormal RBC count of 3.95 and MCH of 32.1, and Eosinophils of 5.5.

The patient, a male with no significant past medical history, presented with persistent and worsening neck pain, which began upon waking up. He also reported a change in tongue sensation, specifically an inability to feel cold when drinking from a water cooler. He was unsure if this sensation was localized to one side of the tongue or involved the entire tongue, and whether this sensation persisted to the present day. He had a history of a remote gymnastic-related neck injury more than a few years ago, which did not result in any neurological deficits but did cause severe neck pain. He also reported a recent hiking incident where he slipped slightly, but denied any significant fall or whiplash event. 

The patient's past surgical history includes hydrocele and varicocele repair, wisdom teeth removal, tonsillectomy, and hernia repair as an infant. He has no known allergies except for iodinated contrast media - IV dye. His family history is positive for diabetes and hypertension, but no connective tissue disease or genetic diseases involving stretchy skin or hypermobile joints were reported.

Upon physical examination, the patient was awake, cooperative, and showed no signs of distress. His vital signs were stable and within normal limits. His neurological examination was largely unremarkable, with normal mental status, cranial nerve function, motor function, sensory function, deep tendon reflexes, coordination, and gait. 

Laboratory results showed a hemoglobin level of 12.2, PTT of 28.5, and INR of 1.1. Both serum and urine drug screens were negative, and urinalysis was negative for infection. Radiologic data from an MRI/MRA of the head and neck showed no acute intracranial abnormality or acute infarct, and a normal MRA of the brain. However, there was a short segment of high signal surrounding the V3 segment of the left vertebral artery, suggestive of a focal dissection.

During his hospital stay, the patient was diagnosed with a left vertebral artery dissection, likely due to trauma. No brainstem or posterior circulation ischemia was seen. His tongue symptoms resolved and he was placed on a heparin drip in the emergency department, which was later transitioned to aspirin 81mg monotherapy. He was discharged in a clear and coherent mental state, alert and interactive, and able to ambulate independently. His discharge diagnosis was vertebral artery dissection.

The patient is a female with a history of depression since her early years. She has been on several medications, none of which have been effective. She has been under the care of various psychiatrists over the years. A few months ago, she was recommended Electroconvulsive Therapy (ECT) due to her refractory depression.
