She was stable upon discharge with her heart rate in the normal range. She was instructed to return to the ED if she experienced palpitations that did not resolve when taking her medications.

The patient, a female with a history of Asthma, Hypertension (HTN), sinus of valsalva aneurysm, and Supraventricular Tachycardia (SVT), presented with body aches, malaise, a non-productive cough, and sinus pressure. Her symptoms began two weeks prior to admission, improved after seven days, but then worsened again. She reported elevated temperatures up to 100 degrees, mild diarrhea, and a mild headache. She also reported using her asthma inhaler twice since the onset of her symptoms. 

Upon admission, she was afebrile with normal blood pressure and oxygen saturation. However, during her stay, she had one instance of fever with a temperature of 102 degrees and a drop in oxygen saturation to 91%. Lab results showed a white blood cell count of 3.3, normal Hemoglobin and Hematocrit, and platelets, and a normal chemistry panel with a creatinine of 0.7. Lactate was 1.2. She tested positive for Influenza A. 

She was initially prescribed Tamiflu, but she refused it due to stomach upset. She was then started on ceftriaxone/azithromycin and later transitioned to doxycycline for presumed pneumonia. This was based on her worsening symptoms and a chest x-ray that showed a retrocardiac opacity. Other medications she received included DuoNeb's, Tylenol, metoprolol succinate 50 mg, and IV fluids.

Her past medical history includes HTN, SVT, sinus of valsalva aneurysm (not seen on most recent 2 previous echos), uterine fibroids with menometrorrhagia, left breast cyst removal, and migraine headaches. Her family history is significant for hypertension and stroke, with her father and mother both deceased from stroke. She also has a paternal aunt with congestive heart failure.

Upon discharge, her white blood cell count was 3.6, Hemoglobin was 11.4, Hematocrit was 33.3, and MCV was 79. Her glucose level was 96. Her discharge diagnosis was Influenza A and Asthma. Her discharge medications included Hydrochlorothiazide 25 mg, Metoprolol Succinate XL 50 mg, ProAir HFA (albuterol sulfate) 90 mcg/actuation inhalation, and Vitamin D 1000 UNIT.

The patient, a female with a history of diabetes, was admitted for symptomatic cholecystectomy. She had been experiencing right upper quadrant pain for a month and was referred for surgery after an ultrasound at an outside hospital revealed gallstones. No other past medical or surgical history was noted. Her family history includes gallstones but is otherwise noncontributory.

Upon admission, the patient was on several medications including Lidocaine 5% Patch, MetFORMIN (Glucophage) 1000 mg, GlipiZIDE 10 mg, Cyanocobalamin 1000 mcg, Vitamin D, and Docusate Sodium 100 mg. Her physical examination was largely unremarkable with vitals within normal range and no signs of edema or labored breathing. She did exhibit tenderness around the port site incisions.

During her hospital stay, the patient underwent a laparoscopic cholecystectomy without complications. Post-surgery, she was stable, with well-controlled pain, and was able to tolerate a regular diet. She was ambulatory, voided without issues, and actively participated in her care plan. She received subcutaneous heparin and venodyne boots were used during her stay. At discharge, she was afebrile, with stable vitals, tolerating a regular diet, ambulating, voiding without assistance, and her pain was well controlled.

The patient was discharged with instructions for recovery at home, including activity restrictions, incision care, bowel management, pain management, and medication instructions. She was advised to follow up in the Acute Care Surgery clinic.

The patient is a male with a history of left temporal ganglioglioma, for which he underwent a subtotal resection. He has been experiencing symptomatic epilepsy with both focal dyscognitive and likely rapidly evolving generalized seizures. His first seizure occurred while at work, and he was found to have a left frontotemporal tumor. He has not received any further treatments like chemotherapy, radiation, or additional resections. His seizures include focal dyscognitive seizures characterized by lip smacking, confusion, and slurred speech, and generalized convulsions characterized by loss of consciousness, rhythmic shaking of all four extremities, and diffuse weakness. Triggers for his seizures include sleep deprivation and possibly alcohol.

The patient has been trialed on multiple anti-epileptic drugs (AEDs) with fair control after the surgery. His current AED regimen includes Phenobarbital, Lamotrigine XR, and Gabapentin. Previous AEDs that were ineffective include Levetiracetam, Pregabalin, and Lacosamide. He has had multiple emergency department visits for seizures, but no urgent hospitalizations, ICU stays, or intubations for seizures.

The patient denies any current difficulties producing or comprehending speech, focal weakness, numbness, parasthesiae, bowel or bladder incontinence or retention, and difficulty with gait. He also denies recent fever or chills, night sweats, weight loss or gain, cough, shortness of breath, chest pain or tightness, palpitations, nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, constipation, abdominal pain, change in bowel or bladder habits, dysuria, arthralgias, myalgias, and rash.

The patient's family history does not include epilepsy or other brain tumors, but does include lung cancer, breast cancer, and a father who underwent coronary artery bypass grafting. 

Upon physical examination, the patient was found to be awake, cooperative, and alert. He was able to relate history without difficulty and showed good knowledge of current events. His cranial nerves were intact, and he showed no deficits to light touch throughout. His coordination was normal, with no intention tremor, dysdiadochokinesia, or dysmetria noted.

Abnormal lab results include a glucose level of 103*, a phenytoin level of <0.6*, and a red blood cell count of 4.28*. His lamotrigine level was low at 1.2. 

During his hospital stay, the patient was admitted for long-term monitoring (LTM) seizure capture and medication titration. Three focal dyscognitive seizures were captured off AEDs, characterized by an initial flushed feeling, then unresponsiveness, lip smacking, lasting less than 2 minutes each. These seizures were captured on EEG with seizures from the left temporal region. His home AEDs of Phenobarbital and Gabapentin were stopped upon discharge, and his home lamotrigine XR 300mg QHS was increased to lamotrigine 200mg PO BID. He was also started on lacosamide 100mg PO BID. 

The patient was discharged with a diagnosis of seizures. His mental status was clear and coherent, and he was alert and interactive. He was ambulatory and independent.

The patient, a male, was admitted for long-term monitoring for medically refractory epilepsy and pre-surgical evaluation. He has a history of left frontotemporal ganglioglioma, which was surgically removed. Despite the surgery, the patient has continued to experience multiple seizure semiologies, including simple partial, complex partial, and secondary generalized seizures. He reports an average frequency of one seizure every two weeks, but can experience up to three in one week. He occasionally bites his tongue and often reports feeling sore following episodes. 

The patient's epilepsy risk factors include an abnormal pregnancy, CNS neoplasm, a neurosurgical procedure, alcohol abuse, and drug abuse. His current antiepileptic drug (AED) regimen includes 250mg lamotrigine twice daily, Vimpat twice daily, and 1mg Lorazepam as needed for prolonged seizure or cluster. He has previously tried Phenobarbital, Gabapentin, Levetiracetam, and Pregabalin, but these were stopped due to ineffectiveness. 

The patient denies any headache, loss of vision, blurred vision, diplopia, dysarthria, dysphagia, lightheadedness, vertigo, tinnitus or hearing difficulty. He also denies difficulties producing or comprehending speech, focal weakness, numbness, parasthesiae, bowel or bladder incontinence or retention, and difficulty with gait. 

His past medical history includes a left frontotemporal brain tumor, ganglioglioma, and symptomatic epilepsy. He takes Melatonin 3mg at bedtime for insomnia. There is no family history of epilepsy or other brain tumors, though there is a history of lung and breast cancer, and his father had a coronary artery bypass graft. 

Upon physical examination, the patient was awake, cooperative, and showed no signs of distress. His vitals were within normal range. Neurological examination showed he was alert, oriented, and able to relate history without difficulty. His language was fluent with intact repetition and comprehension. There were no paraphasic errors, and no evidence of apraxia or neglect. 

Lab results showed abnormal WBC at 10.4, RBC at 4.38, and glucose at 114. Imaging and tests showed decreased uptake in the left mid temporal lobe consistent with postsurgical changes. EEG results showed occasional left temporal spike and slow wave discharges, consistent with a prominent focus of increased cortical irritability in this region. 

During his hospital stay, the patient was monitored on cvEEG with seizure precautions. His medications were slowly weaned to off. He subsequently experienced several generalized tonic-clonic seizures, one of which required intervention with IV lorazepam as it had lasted over three minutes. Numerous seizures were captured on cvEEG. An interictal SPECT was performed, showing regional hypoperfusion in the area of his prior surgical resection. He was subsequently restarted on his prior home doses of lacosamide and levetiracetam, and was started newly on clobazam. 

The patient was discharged with a diagnosis of focal symptomatic epilepsy. His mental status was clear and coherent, and he was alert and interactive. He was instructed to continue his current medication regimen and to follow up with his epilepsy doctor.

The patient, a male of undisclosed age, was admitted for an elective left craniotomy for temporal lobectomy due to a history of seizures. His past medical history includes a left frontotemporal brain tumor, specifically a ganglioglioma, which had been partially resected previously. He also has symptomatic epilepsy. His non-AED medication includes Melatonin 3mg qhs for insomnia. Upon admission, he was taking Clobazam 10mg BID, Vimpat 200mg BID, Lamotrigine 250mg BID, and Ativan 1mg prn for seizures. 

The patient's family history does not include epilepsy or other brain tumors, but there is a history of lung and breast cancer, and his father had a coronary artery bypass graft. 

The patient underwent a left craniotomy for temporal lobectomy, which he tolerated well. Post-operative CT and MRI scans showed expected changes. He was stable and transferred to the floor on post-operative day (POD) #3. A head wrap was replaced on POD #4 due to a small fluid collection around the incision to aid healing. 

A neurology consult was obtained for assistance with management of antiepileptic medications post-operatively. The patient's primary epileptologist recommended no changes to his anti-epileptic medications while inpatient. 

Post-operatively, the patient's white blood cell count was 21.1, thought to be due to the steroids he received during the procedure. His CBC was checked daily and WBC trended down to 7.8 at discharge. On POD#4, his pain was well controlled, he was tolerating a PO diet, ambulating independently, and urinating without signs of retention. He was discharged home in stable condition. 

Upon discharge, the patient was clear and coherent, alert and interactive, and ambulatory - independent. His discharge diagnosis was epilepsy. His discharge instructions included keeping the incision dry, avoiding heavy lifting, running, climbing, or other strenuous exercise until his follow-up appointment, and not driving while taking any narcotic or sedating medication. He was also advised to avoid contact sports for 6 months. He was discharged on his home antiepileptic drugs and was instructed to follow up in the epilepsy clinic in a few weeks.

The patient is a male of undisclosed age with a history of a low-grade glioma, which was previously treated with resection. This procedure was complicated by refractory seizures despite adherence to antiepileptic drugs (AEDs), leading to a left temporal lobectomy. His AED regimen includes LACOSamide 200 mg twice daily, lamotrigine 250 mg twice daily, clobazam 10 mg twice daily, and lorazepam 1 mg as needed. He also takes melatonin 3mg at bedtime for insomnia. 

The patient was admitted due to a breakthrough seizure, which was atypical as it did not begin with an aura or start with a simple etiology followed by secondary generalization. This event was concerning given the patient's new symptomatic headache and known hygroma. Prior to admission, he had been experiencing a constant, worsening, piercing headache in his left temporal region for three days. 

Upon admission, his physical exam was largely unremarkable. Neurologically, he was alert and oriented, with fluent language, intact repetition and comprehension, and no evidence of apraxia or neglect. His cranial nerves were intact, and he demonstrated normal motor function, sensory function, and coordination. 

Lab results showed abnormal values for white blood cell count (11.1*), red blood cell count (4.03*), hemoglobin (12.7*), hematocrit (37.2*), absolute lymphocyte count (3.72*), absolute monocyte count (0.86*), and glucose (102*). 

MRI imaging revealed postoperative changes from the anterior left temporal lobectomy, an extracranial fluid collection with apparent connection to extra-axial space at the left middle cranial fossa, and worsened parenchymal abnormality in the anterior temporal lobe, extending into the subinsula, with cystic changes. These changes were thought to be the sequela of ischemia, with inflammatory or infectious processes considered less likely. 

During his hospital stay, the patient's breakthrough seizure was managed by increasing the dose of his clobazam from 10 mg twice daily to 10mg in the morning and 15 mg at night. He remained seizure-free without adverse effects or notable drowsiness on this increased regimen. 

The patient has no known allergies or adverse drug reactions. His family history is negative for epilepsy and brain tumors, but includes instances of lung and breast cancer, and his father underwent coronary artery bypass grafting at an unspecified age. 

Upon discharge, the patient was diagnosed with a breakthrough seizure and a subgaleal fluid collection. His mental status was clear and coherent, and he was alert, interactive, and independently ambulatory.

The male patient, with a history of refractory epilepsy, was admitted due to a large fluid collection under the skin overlying a prior craniotomy site. His past medical history includes left frontotemporal ganglioglioma post resection, grand mal and complex partial seizures, and left temporal lobectomy. He underwent a revision of the left temporal craniotomy incision and dural opening repair. The patient has no known allergies or adverse drug reactions. 

The patient's family history does not include epilepsy or other brain tumors, but there is a history of lung and breast cancer. His father underwent coronary artery bypass grafting and later passed away due to ALS. 

Upon discharge, the patient was alert and interactive, with clear and coherent mental status. His physical examination showed spontaneous eye opening, full orientation, and complex command following. His pupils were equal, round, and reactive to light, with full extraocular movements. His face was symmetric, tongue midline, and no pronator drift was observed. His speech was fluent, comprehension intact, and sensation intact to light touch and proprioception. His headwrap dressing was clean, dry, and intact. 

During his hospital stay, he underwent an elective revision and repair of dural opening/cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) leak. The procedure was uncomplicated, and post-operative CT showed normal post-operative changes and near total resolution of subgaleal fluid collection. He remained hemodynamically and neurologically stable, was ambulating, and tolerating oral intake prior to discharge. 

The patient was continued on his home antiepileptics and did not experience any seizures during this hospital admission. His medications upon admission included Clobazam 10mg in the morning and 20mg in the evening, Lacosamide 200mg twice daily, Lamotrigine 250mg twice daily, Lorazepam 1mg as needed for seizures, and Melatonin 10mg at bedtime as needed for insomnia. 

The patient was discharged with a diagnosis of subgaleal CSF collection and dural opening. His discharge instructions included care for his surgical site, activity restrictions, medication instructions, and symptoms to watch for that would require medical attention.

The patient, a male with a history of paranoid schizophrenia and chronic alcohol abuse, was admitted due to leg edema. He had a history of a PEA arrest and ICU course for septic shock due to pneumonia. He was reportedly living in poor conditions and was brought in by a social worker after being found with several open wounds on his legs, which were swollen and erythematous. He had been discharged from the hospital recently and was supposed to be taking antibiotics for leg cellulitis, but reportedly had not been doing so. He denied any pain, fever, chills, increased edema, other joint pain or rash. In the ED, he was given a dose of IV vancomycin and his vitals were stable.

His physical exam on admission showed brawny edema with mild erythema in both legs, skin flaking and changes consistent with a fungal infection, and wounds with eschar and prior evidence of bleeding near the left ankle. His discharge exam showed bilateral lower extremities bandaged in ACE wraps, 1+ pitting edema to thighs, no streaking erythema, and chronic appearing ulcers on the lateral aspect of both shins, with a moderate amount of purulent drainage.

Lab results showed a low RBC count of 3.31, low HGB of 9.8, low HCT of 30.7, low MCHC of 31.9, high RDWSD of 47.2, low lymphs of 18.4, and high monos of 13.5. Imaging studies showed no evidence of deep venous thrombosis in the visualized right or left lower extremity veins, severe soft tissue edema in the calves bilaterally, and all waveforms were triphasic bilaterally. There was no obvious reflux in either the proximal deep system or the saphenous veins.

During his hospital stay, the patient was treated for cellulitis and lower extremity ulcers with vancomycin, ciprofloxacin, metronidazole, and wound care for 3 days. He left against medical advice before completing a full course of antibiotics. He was also given thiamine, folate, and multivitamins for his history of alcohol abuse. He was not taking any preadmission medications. His family history was unremarkable, with no known medical problems. His discharge diagnoses included cellulitis, chronic venous stasis ulcers, chronic paranoid schizophrenia, and hypoxic brain injury.

The patient, a female, was admitted to the orthopedics service with a chief complaint of severe back and leg discomfort, including right-sided sciatica with pain radiating to the right lower leg. She described the sensation as a pinched nerve and reported pain with activity, at rest, and when sitting, standing, and bending. She denied any trauma and has been experiencing these symptoms for approximately one year. 

Her past medical history includes measles, chickenpox, arthritis, and an unspecified disease. She has undergone several surgical procedures in the past, including hand surgery, right shoulder surgery, tubal ligation, and a core biopsy. Her family history is significant for cancer, lung disease, neurologic disease, and arthritis.

During her hospital stay, she underwent an anterior/posterior lumbar decompression with fusion from L3 to S1. The procedure was completed without complications and she was transferred to the post-anesthesia care unit (PACU) in a stable condition. She returned to the operating room on the second hospital day for a scheduled L3-S1 decompression with posterior spinal interbody fusion (PSIF) as part of a staged 2-part procedure. This second surgery was also completed without complications and she was again transferred to the PACU in a stable condition.

Her postoperative course was notable for low hematocrit, for which she received a transfusion of packed red blood cells with good results. She experienced confusion on IV pain medication and was transitioned to oral pain medication when she was able to tolerate a regular diet. She was fitted with a lumbar brace for comfort and physical therapy was consulted for mobilization and ambulation. 

Her laboratory results showed several abnormal findings, including low red blood cell count (RBC), low hemoglobin (Hgb), low hematocrit (Hct), and high mean corpuscular hemoglobin (MCH). Her glucose levels were also elevated and she had low calcium and phosphorus levels.

Upon admission, she was taking several medications, including Carbidopa-Levodopa, Selegiline HCl, Comtan (entacapone), pramipexole, Amantadine, Gabapentin, Midodrine, Levothyroxine Sodium, and Atorvastatin.

She was discharged with a diagnosis of lumbar stenosis, spondylosis, disc degeneration, acute post-op blood loss anemia, and scoliosis. Her condition at discharge was good and she was transferred to an extended care facility.

The patient, a female under the Orthopaedics service, was admitted due to worsening back pain. She underwent XLIF T12-L2 and Posterior fusion T12-L2 procedures. The patient had a history of measles, chickenpox, arthritis, and an unspecified disease. She had previously undergone hand surgery, right shoulder surgery, tubal ligation, and a core biopsy. 

The patient's family history includes cancer, lung disease, neurologic disease, and arthritis. Upon physical examination, the patient was well developed, well nourished, and alert and oriented. She had limited range of motion in the lumbar spine due to pain but was ambulating well with the assistance of a walker and a TLSO brace for support. Gross motor examination revealed good strength in the bilateral lower extremities. Sensation was intact throughout all affected dermatomes. The lateral and posterior lumbar incisions were clean, dry, and intact without signs of infection. A Foley catheter was in place.

Lab results showed abnormal RBC count at 2.59, Hemoglobin at 8.3, and Hematocrit at 24.8. The patient was taken to the Operating Room for XLIF T12-L2 through an anterior lateral approach. The surgery was performed without complication and the patient was transferred to the PACU in a stable condition. On the second hospital day, she returned to the operating room for a posterior decompression and fusion scheduled as part of a staged 2-part procedure. The second surgery was also without complication and the patient was transferred to the PACU in a stable condition. Postoperative labs were grossly stable. 

The patient was on several medications upon admission including Amantadine, Atorvastatin, Carbidopa-Levodopa, Entacapone, Folic Acid, Gabapentin, Hydrocodone-Acetaminophen, Levothyroxine Sodium, Midodrine, Pramipexole, Selegiline HCl, Calcium Carbonate, Cyanocobalamin, and Multivitamins. 

The patient was diagnosed with spondylosis and stenosis upon discharge. She was alert, interactive, and ambulatory but required assistance or aid. The patient was discharged to an extended care facility.

The patient, a female, was admitted to the orthopedics service due to back pain. She had a history of measles, chickenpox, arthritis, and an unspecified disease. She had previously undergone hand surgery, right shoulder surgery, tubal ligation, and a core biopsy. The patient had a family history of cancer, lung disease, neurological disease, and arthritis.

Upon admission, the patient was well developed, well nourished, and alert. She had limited range of motion in the thoracolumbar spine due to pain but was able to ambulate with the assistance of a walker and a thoracolumbar sacral orthosis (TLSO) brace for support. Sensation was intact throughout all affected dermatomes and there was no clonus present. The thoracolumbar incision was clean, dry, and intact without signs of infection. The patient was voiding well without a foley catheter.

The patient had a T12 fracture following a fall, which was identified in radiographs taken at an outside hospital. She had previously undergone a T12-L2 fusion. After evaluation, it was determined that she would be a good candidate for a revision posterior fusion. The patient agreed to the plan and consent was obtained. She underwent a T10 to L1 fusion, open treatment of the T12 fracture, laminotomies of T12, T11, and T10, instrumentation from T10 to L1, removal of previous instrumentation at T12, and an autograft. The surgery was performed without complications and the patient was stable postoperatively.

Lab results showed a white blood cell count of 8.9, red blood cell count of 2.97, hemoglobin of 9.5, hematocrit of 27.1, mean corpuscular hemoglobin concentration of 35.1, all of which were marked as abnormal. The patient was afebrile with stable vital signs, comfortable on oral pain control, and tolerating a regular diet upon discharge.

The patient was discharged to extended care with clear and coherent mental status, alert and interactive level of consciousness, and ambulatory activity status requiring assistance or aid. She was instructed not to lift anything greater than 10 lbs for 2 weeks, to walk several times a day as part of her recovery, and to change positions every 45 minutes when sitting or standing. She was given a brace to wear for comfort when walking, and instructions for wound care and medication management. The discharge diagnosis was a T12 fracture.

The patient, a female under the care of Orthopaedics, was admitted due to bilateral lower extremity weakness and numbness. She also reported several episodes of urinary incontinence. She has a history of Parkinson's disease, back pain, measles, chickenpox, and arthritis. She has undergone several surgical procedures including L3-S1 fusion, XLIF and fusion, and a complete collapse T12 vert body fx. She also had a revision posterior laminectomy and fusion T10-L2. Other past surgical procedures include hand surgery, right shoulder surgery, tubal ligation, and a core biopsy. 

During this admission, she underwent multiple procedures including transpedicular decompression T10, fusion T4 to T12, revision of instrumentation T4 to T12, and multiple thoracic laminotomies. Post-surgery, she was found to be ambulating well with the assistance of a walker and a TLSO brace for support. Her gross motor examination showed improvement compared to before the surgery. Sensation was intact throughout all affected dermatomes and there was no clonus present. The thoracolumbar incision was clean, dry, and intact without erythema, edema, or drainage. A Foley catheter was in place.

Lab results showed abnormal values for RBC (2.86), Hgb (8.9), Hct (26.2), and RDW (16.2). An MRI revealed severe canal narrowing at T10 and increased cord signal T11-T12. Post-operative labs were stable and a hemovac drain that was placed at the time of surgery was removed on POD#2. 

The patient's family history includes cancer, lung disease, neurologic disease, and arthritis. Upon discharge, her diagnosis was T10 fracture and spinal stenosis. She was ambulatory but required assistance or aid and was sometimes confused. Her discharge disposition was extended care.

The patient, a female under the OBSTETRICS/GYNECOLOGY service, was admitted due to abdominal pain that started 5 days prior to admission. The pain was initially gradual and dull but became sharp and intermittent in the right lower quadrant the night before presentation. The patient also experienced associated nausea and vomiting, and anorexia. She has a history of ovarian torsion and believes the current pain feels similar. The patient has a past medical history of Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus, Asthma, Hypertension, Migraines, and Obesity. Her past surgical history includes Laparascopic appendectomy and cholecystectomy (both done for endometriosis), Laparascopic fulgaration of endometriosis twice, and Laparascopic left ovarian de-torsion/cystectomy. 

Upon physical examination, the patient was found to be in no acute distress, with regular heart rate and rhythm, clear lungs, and a soft, obese abdomen that was diffusely tender, maximally in the supra-pubic and right lower quadrant. There was no rebound or guarding, and active bowel sounds were noted. The patient was tender in both the right and left adnexa, but more so in the right adnexa. 

A pelvic ultrasound revealed an enlarged right adnexal cyst measuring 5.9 cm, a normal-sized left ovary with neither venous nor arterial waveforms identified, and a fibroid uterus with normal endometrium. Cultures were negative for Gonorrhea and Chlamydia. 

During her hospital stay, the patient underwent a diagnostic laparascopy which resulted in a right cystectomy for a simple cyst and endometrioma. Post-surgery, the patient's pain remained unchanged in terms of quality, location, and severity. She was actively requesting all her pain medicines around the clock and would also ask for more pain medicines in between dosages. She was receiving IV dilaudid and toradol. She also complained of nausea and reported vomiting. She found it difficult to eat anything by mouth for fear of vomiting. For her nausea, she was receiving IV zofran and phenergan. She was also placed on intravenous protonix. 

The patient's medications upon admission included Metoprolol, Metformin, Advair, Albuterol, Tompamax, Singulair 20, Imitrex, Toradol, Phenergan, and Percocet. Her blood sugars were followed while in hospital and she was placed on an insulin sliding scale while off her metformin. She was discharged on hospital day 4 with bearable pain, ambulating and voiding without difficulty. She was also set up with an outpatient chronic pain appointment. Her discharge diagnoses were Endometrioma and Chronic pain.

The patient, a female with a history of Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus, Asthma, Hypertension, Migraines, and Obesity, was admitted due to nausea and a two-day history of an unspecified condition. She had a prolonged post-operative course following a laparoscopic ovarian cystectomy due to nausea and another unspecified condition. She reported having a fever at home, but none was documented in the emergency department. 

The patient's surgical history includes a laparoscopic appendectomy and cholecystectomy, both performed due to endometriosis. She also underwent laparoscopic fulguration of endometriosis twice and a laparoscopic left ovarian de-torsion/cystectomy. 

Upon physical examination, the patient was tearful, with a temperature of 99.3, heart rate of 96, blood pressure of 127/90, respiratory rate of 17, and oxygen saturation of 100% on room air. Her abdomen was soft and tender to deep palpation on the right side of the umbilicus, with no rebound or guarding. Incision sites appeared clean, dry, and intact, and her abdomen was not distended. 

Lab results showed a glucose level of 194*, total CO2 of 19*, and lipase of 63*, all of which are abnormal. Her Mean Corpuscular Hemoglobin Concentration (MCHC) was 37.3*, and she had an elevated eosinophil count of 4.3*. 

During her hospital stay, the patient refused to attempt oral intake of unspecified medicine or antiemetics and expressed a desire to return home to take oral medications there. She was given Zofran and Phenergan, as well as one dose each of IV Dilaudid and Toradol, which resulted in some improvement. 

Upon admission, the patient was taking Metoprolol, Metformin, Advair, Albuterol, Tompamax, Singulair, Imitrex, Toradol, Phenergan, and Percocet. 

The patient was discharged with a diagnosis of abdominal pain, nausea, and vomiting. Her mental status was clear and coherent, and she was alert, interactive, and independently ambulatory. She was instructed to continue taking her unspecified medications and anti-nausea medications until her appointment with the unspecified clinic.

The patient is a female with a history of multiple cancers including stage II left breast cancer, cervical cancer, and Acute Lymphoblastic Leukemia (ALL) in her childhood. She has undergone lumpectomy, radiation therapy, and chemotherapy for her breast cancer, and is currently on Letrozole. She also had a prophylactic right lumpectomy and bilateral oophorectomy. She has a history of cervical cancer and has undergone a Loop Electrosurgical Excision Procedure (LEEP). She was treated for ALL with chemotherapy at a young age and again for recurrence. She also has a history of anxiety, left parotid pleomorphic adenoma with subsequent resection and left cheek cellulitis, migraines, and osteoporosis. 

The patient presented with a chief complaint of headache and generalized weakness. She has a long-standing history of headaches due to a stable meningioma. The headaches have been progressively worsening, and are associated with blurry vision and photophobia. She also reported generalized weakness, shortness of breath and chest tightness when climbing stairs, intermittent tingling in her feet, and new upper and lower back pain. She has chronic bilateral bone pain. 

Upon examination, she had right horizontal nystagmus, word finding difficulty, and slightly slurred speech. Her blood pressure dipped to 84/49 but improved to 108/64 after 1L NS. Her head CT showed no hemorrhage and a stable cranial vertex lesion, likely the meningioma. Her EKG was unchanged from prior and her chest X-ray was negative. Her alcohol level in the ED was 202. 

Her lab results showed a Chloride level of 110*, a Red Blood Cell count of 3.87*, and a Mean Corpuscular Hemoglobin of 32.7*. Her brain MRI showed a stable size of the meningioma. 

Her medications upon admission included Letrozole, Ativan, MS Contin, Oxycodone, Compazine, Topiramate, Zoledronic acid infusion, Vitamin C, Calcium + Vit D, and Rolaids. 

Her family history includes her mother with arthritis and her father with a hearing disorder. She has two healthy sisters and a brother, and no children. There is no family history of headaches. 

Conditions and symptoms that have been ruled out include meningitis due to lack of nuchal rigidity or systemic symptoms, and enlargement of the meningioma as the cause of the headache exacerbation. The altered mental status was likely due to alcohol intoxication. Her breast and cervical cancers are in remission. Her depression was likely exacerbated by the death of her aunt.

The patient, a female with a history of depression, two spontaneous abortions, two therapeutic abortions, and a single vaginal delivery, was admitted due to heavy vaginal bleeding. She was five weeks postpartum and had experienced normal vaginal bleeding until the week of admission, when she had three large gushes of blood. The first two instances were minor, but the third involved heavy, flowing blood and clots, causing her to feel dizzy and lightheaded. She was initially seen at an outside hospital where her hematocrit was found to be 22%. An ultrasound suggested retained products of conception. Her bleeding stabilized on its own and she was transferred for further management. It is noteworthy that after her vaginal delivery five weeks prior, she required manual removal of the placenta. Her hematocrit was 20.8% at the time of discharge from the hospital. She refused blood transfusion at that time and has not been taking her prescribed iron.

Upon admission, her medications included prenatal vitamins and ibuprofen. Her physical examination was largely unremarkable, with a small amount of dried blood on the perineum and a moderately stained pad. Her uterus was small, anteverted, mobile, and non-tender. The external os was approximately fingertip dilated, with no blood in the vault.

Lab results showed a glucose level of 95, urea nitrogen of 10, creatinine of 0.8, sodium of 139, potassium of 3.9, and chloride of 110 (marked with an asterisk as an abnormal finding). Her total CO2 was 22 and anion gap was 11. Her white blood cell count was 9.2 (marked with a hashtag), red blood cell count was 2.46 (marked with an asterisk), hemoglobin was 6.9 (marked with an asterisk), hematocrit was 20.5 (marked with an asterisk), and platelet count was 369. Her neutrophils were 61.8, monocytes were 5.6, eosinophils were 4.0, and basophils were 0.5. Her PTT was 23.4. A subsequent lab showed a red blood cell count of 2.27 (marked with an asterisk), hemoglobin of 6.2 (marked with an asterisk), and hematocrit of 19.3 (marked with an asterisk). Her platelet count was 354 and her hemoglobin was 6.3 (marked with an asterisk). A transvaginal ultrasound showed a hyperechoic heterogeneous endometrium measuring up to 2.2cm with increased color flow, most compatible with retained products of conception.

During her hospital stay, the patient underwent a dilation and curettage (D&C) procedure to remove the retained products of conception. The surgery was uneventful and she recovered well, being discharged four hours later in stable condition. She was afebrile, had minimal vaginal bleeding, was able to eat regular food, had adequate pain control with oral medications, and was ambulating and urinating without difficulty. Her discharge diagnosis included retained products of conception and the D&C procedure.

The patient, a male with a history of Osteogenesis Imperfecta and over 200 fractures, ADHD, and tobacco use, was admitted due to shortness of breath. He reported intermittent mid-back and shoulder pain, which worsened with certain movements, particularly raising his arms over his head. He also reported difficulty taking deep breaths, which he attributed to muscle issues rather than pain. Initial vitals in the ED showed tachypnea, and the patient was noted to have multiple deformities due to Osteogenesis Imperfecta. A CXR was inconclusive, showing atelectasis versus infiltrate, and an ECG showed Sinus tachycardia. A CT abdomen revealed a nonobstructive kidney stone. The patient was given 15mg IV toradol and IV morphine for pain, which he reported did not help, and levofloxacin 250mg IV for presumptive pneumonia.

Upon admission, the patient's physical exam showed a thin, small stature male in mild distress, breathing with the use of accessory and abdominal muscles. He had blue sclera, a large pectus excavatum, and multiple bone/muscle deformities due to Osteogenesis Imperfecta. His back was tender to palpation in the mid-back right lateral to the spine. 

Lab results on admission showed abnormal MCHC (30.8) and PTT (49.5), and elevated glucose (108). Discharge labs showed decreased RBC (3.82), Hgb (10.7), Hct (34.8), and MCHC (30.7), and decreased Creat (0.2). Other pertinent labs showed abnormal pO2 (60), pCO2 (47), pH (7.46), and calTCO2 (34). 

Imaging showed decreased aeration of the left lung, which could indicate atelectasis or pneumonia. A CTA of the chest showed no central pulmonary embolus, but multifocal atelectasis and small bilateral pleural effusions were noted. A CTU showed a 2mm non-obstructing right renal calculus and a calcified enlarged prostate. A lumbar/sacral Xray showed markedly abnormal, extremely osteopenic bones and abnormal morphology, consistent with Osteogenesis Imperfecta. A TTE showed normal left ventricular systolic function, but suggested a bicuspid aortic valve. 

During his hospital stay, the patient experienced respiratory distress and was unable to tolerate sitting up. He reported improved sensation of breathing when lying flat on his back. He was treated for community-acquired pneumonia with a 7-day course of levofloxacin. He also had an episode of Gauiac positive emesis and was found to have esophageal reflux. He was started on a PPI and his Hct remained stable over the last days of his hospital stay. He also complained of uncomfortable back pain throughout his hospital stay, which was managed but not completely resolved at the time of discharge. He was discharged on lidocaine patch and tramadol. 

The patient also required daily fleet enemas for constipation, which was likely worsened by the use of narcotics for his back pain. He was discharged on an aggressive bowel regimen including Miralax, senna, colace, lactulose as needed, and daily fleet enema as needed. He also had a 2mm non-obstructing kidney stone and hematuria on his UA, for which he was started on IV fluids on admission and given flomax. 

Upon discharge, the patient was prescribed home oxygen, Albuterol and ipratroprium nebulizer treatments, a bowel regimen of Miralax, senna and colace, fleet enemas as needed for constipation, Tramadol for pain control, Flomax, Lidocaine patch, and Omeprazole. His code status was changed to DNR/DNI during his admission. His discharge diagnoses were community-acquired pneumonia and Osteogenesis Imperfecta.

The patient, a male with a history of Osteogenesis Imperfecta and dwarfism, was admitted to the hospital following a fall from his wheelchair. He has had over 200 fractures in the past due to his condition. He also has a history of ADHD and tobacco use. There is no family history of CAD. 

Upon admission, the patient complained of left arm and leg pain, a headache, and some sternal pain. He was not on any blood thinning medications. His physical examination revealed ecchymosis with abrasion in the right periorbital region, tenderness to palpation over the right anterior sternum and over the midshaft forearm on the left, and a laceration to the right supraorbital region. His vital signs were normal. 

The patient's blood tests showed abnormal RBC counts of 4.39, 4.23, and 4.32, Hgb of 13.5, RDWSD of 53.1, 54.4, and 55.0, Neuts of 82.9, Lymphs of 8.0, Eos of 0.1, AbsNeut of 11.04, AbsLymp of 1.07, AbsMono of 1.08, AbsEos of 0.01, PTT of 43.9, Creat of 0.4 and 0.3, K of 5.4, and HCO3 of 17. 

Imaging revealed a complex fracture of the frontal bone, right lateral orbital wall and right orbital roof fracture, multiple severe hypodensities in the periventricular, subcortical, and deep white matter, pansinus mucosal thickening, mild anterolisthesis of C2 on C3, non-displaced fracture involving the mid shaft radius, multiple facial fractures, and a fracture involving the mandible. 

During his hospital stay, the patient underwent external fixation of bilateral mandible fractures including left body fracture and para-symphyseal mandibular fracture via reconstruction plate and screws. Post-operative course was stable and he was started on clear liquids and advanced to full liquids. His vital signs were stable and he was afebrile. He was voiding without difficulty. 

Upon discharge, the patient was diagnosed with left non-displaced midshaft radius fracture, multiple frontal bone fracture, right lateral, inf, sup orbital wall fracture, right orbital roof fracture, right sphenoid wing fracture, left mandibular body fracture acute, sphenoid bone, left middle cranial fossa, and right mandibular condyle fracture. 

The patient was on Concerta and Vit D upon admission. He was discharged home with service and was advised to follow jaw care instructions and to contact his doctor or nurse practitioner for any new or worsening symptoms.

The patient, a male, was admitted following a fall from his wheelchair at home, which resulted in a mild traumatic brain injury (TBI). He was found by his caretaker 24 hours after the fall. A CT scan of the torso revealed right rib fractures from the third to the sixth ribs. Further evaluation at the hospital showed a left parietal epidural hematoma. 

The patient's past medical history includes an appendectomy, arachnoid cyst, attentional problem, multiple falls with fractures, non-depressed skull fracture, osteogenesis imperfecta with over 200 fractures in the past, cerebellar hemisphere encephalomalacia, and ADHD. He also has a history of tobacco use. 

Upon admission, the patient was alert and oriented, with a Glasgow Coma Scale score of 15. His physical examination was largely normal, with intact sensation to light touch in all four extremities. 

During his hospital stay, the patient experienced hematemesis and was started on Pantaprazole. He also had guiac positive stools, but no interventions were recommended. His hemoglobin and hematocrit levels dropped, likely due to the hematemesis and guiac positive stools, but trended upwards by the time of discharge. 

The patient's medications upon admission included Concerta 54mg PO daily. His condition upon discharge was clear and coherent, and he was alert and interactive. He was able to get out of bed with assistance to a chair or wheelchair. 

The patient's discharge diagnoses included a left parietal epidural hematoma, rib fractures, and anemia. His hematoma was stable and he remained neurologically stable throughout his stay. His rib fractures were managed with acetaminophen and oxycodone for pain control.

The patient, a male with a history of osteogenesis imperfecta, dwarfism, and multiple falls, was admitted following a mechanical fall from his wheelchair. The fall resulted in bilateral rib fractures and a left femur fracture. The patient has a history of over 200 fractures due to his osteogenesis imperfecta. Other past medical history includes an appendectomy, arachnoid cyst, attentional problem, non-depressed skull fracture, and cerebellar hemisphere encephalomalacia. He also has a history of ADHD and tobacco use. There is no known family history of any conditions.

Upon admission, the patient appeared in mild discomfort. He was tachycardic and tachypneic with mild labored breathing, but no respiratory distress. His abdomen was soft, non-distended, and non-tender. There was no hip tenderness bilaterally, and his pelvis was stable. However, there was tenderness in his left knee. 

CT scans revealed a fracture of the distal left femur and cortical defects and step-off in the right lateral mid ribs and left mid and lower ribs, indicative of acute non-displaced fractures. No pneumothorax or evidence of pulmonary contusion was found, and there was no definite pelvic fracture. The patient's severe osteopenia was noted in the scans. 

Lab results showed a total CO2 level of 21 and an anion gap of 22. All other lab results were within normal ranges. 

The patient was evaluated by the orthopedic service, who determined that he was not a surgical candidate due to his diffuse osteopenia. They recommended ACE wrapping for bone healing, a method that has worked well for him in the past. His pain was much improved upon discharge, and he was otherwise stable. 

Upon admission, the patient was taking Senna and Docusate for constipation, Vitamin D, and Methylphenidate 54mg daily. 

The patient was discharged with a diagnosis of multiple rib fractures and left femur fracture. His mental status was clear and coherent, and he was alert and interactive. However, he required assistance or aid for ambulation. He was discharged home with service.

The patient, a right-handed female with no known previous medical history, was transferred from an outside hospital due to experiencing left-sided numbness. She reported having no known medical issues and had not seen a doctor for several years. Five days prior to her admission, she developed pain in her left heel that was sharp and radiated upwards. This was accompanied by a tingling sensation and a feeling of pins and needles from the heel up to the hip, posteriorly. The sensation persisted in her entire left leg. She denied any weakness or headaches. On the day of her admission, she woke up with numbness and tingling in her entire left arm and face, but reported no weakness and was still able to carry objects well. She also reported a one-minute headache around her head, non-throbbing, with no photo or phonophobia or nausea. 

Upon physical examination, the patient was found to be lying in bed, awake and alert, and cooperative with the exam. She reported hyperesthesia to cold sensation and pinprick on the left arm, circumferentially only. All other physical examination results were normal. 

Lab results showed abnormal values for RBC (4.09*), Hgb (11.4*), and Hct (34.2*). A second set of labs showed similar abnormal values for RBC (4.19*), Hgb (11.9*), and Hct (35.2*). 

Imaging results from an MRI showed no evidence of stroke or flow limiting stenosis. 

During her hospital stay, the patient's symptoms resolved and she had a normal exam at discharge. Her hgbA1c was 5.5 and her LDL was pending. She was not on any medications upon admission. 

The patient was discharged with a diagnosis of left-sided parasthesias with no found underlying organic etiology. Her mental status was intact, she was alert, awake, and oriented, with language intact, no apraxia, and no neglect. Her motor functions were normal with no deficits and her gait was narrow based and steady. 

The patient was advised to make all follow-up appointments and to contact her doctor or return to the nearest emergency room if she experiences any weakness or numbness on one side of her body with sudden onset.

He was recently hospitalized for neutropenic fever and prostatitis, and was discharged on ertapenem. He presented with abdominal pain, hoarse voice, sore throat, prolonged somnolence, weakness, and occasional confusion. His family history includes a mother with breast cancer and a father with lung cancer.

Upon admission, the patient's vital signs were stable and his physical exam was notable for perianal erythema. His lab results showed normal CBC, CHEM7, and lactate. However, his glucose was elevated at 119, and his ALT, AST, and LD(LDH) were also elevated at 147, 152, and 263 respectively. His CK(CPK) was also elevated at 26. His urine analysis was unremarkable.
