He was started on Nystatin swish and swallow and miconazole powder to the perianal area with improvement in symptoms. His neutropenia was resolved and thought to be viral in nature given the clinical course and normal bone marrow. HSV6 is a potential cause, but the PCR is pending at the time of discharge.

The patient's medications upon admission included Nexium for GERD, Lorazepam for nausea, Nystatin Cream for fungal dermatitis, Oxycodone for pain, Ertapenem for prostatitis, and Celebrex for pain. His discharge diagnosis included transaminitis and abdominal pain, with chronic prostatitis as a secondary diagnosis. His mental status was clear and coherent, and he was ambulatory and independent upon discharge.

The patient, a female with a history of type 1 diabetes, hypothyroidism, GERD, lupus, depression, portal hypertension, pancytopenia, elevated triglycerides, and chronic pancreatitis, was admitted due to pancreatitis and hyperlipidemia. She presented with epigastric pain radiating to her shoulders and back, accompanied by increased nausea, vomiting, and bloating. Lab results showed evidence of pancreatitis and hepatitis, with lipase at 3500 and AST at 700. A CT scan revealed worsening pancreatitis with necrosis of the pancreas body/tail, and an MRCP showed narrowing of the distal common bile duct. Despite being made NPO, placed on aztreonam, and given IVF rehydration, her condition did not improve. She developed a fever, her WBC count dropped from 7 to 2.4, and her H&H and platelet counts were also trending down. She was in moderate DKA with blood glucose in the 400s, which normalized after being put on an insulin drip. Aztreonam was stopped due to concerns of causing pancytopenia, and subcutaneous heparin was discontinued due to concerns of HIT. 

Abnormal lab results included PO2 at 41, urea nitrogen at 5, creatinine at 0.3, potassium at 5.6, total CO2 at 21, ALT at 284, AST at 343, alkaline phosphatase at 345, total bilirubin at 3.2, lipase at 183, albumin at 2.9, calcium at 7.8, phosphate at 1.6, triglycerides at 2259, WBC at 2.9, RBC at 2.81, hemoglobin at 7.6, hematocrit at 23.5, MCH at 37.3, neutrophils at 75.5, and platelet count at 54. 

During her hospital stay, the patient was treated with a dilaudid PCA, which was increased for better pain control and later transitioned to IV dilaudid PRN and then to PO Dilaudid. She was initially NPO for bowel rest due to acute pancreatitis, but was transitioned to clears and then to a regular low fat diet, which she tolerated well. Her respiratory status was monitored, and she was encouraged to use pulmonary toilet and IS. She was found to have a urinary tract infection and was discharged on Bactrim for three days. Her hemodynamics were stable, but she experienced pancytopenia and was evaluated by heme-onc. Her sugars and triglyceride levels were significantly elevated, and she was evaluated by specialists who gave recommendations for insulin sliding scale adjustments. 

Upon discharge, the patient was in stable condition, ambulating, voiding independently, and tolerating a regular low fat diet. Her discharge medications included HYDROmorphone (Dilaudid), Glargine, Lidocaine 5% Patch, Citalopram, Levothyroxine Sodium, Tricor, Atorvastatin, and Sulfameth/Trimethoprim DS. She was advised to follow up with her PCP within the week to discuss adjusting her blood sugar and cholesterol medications, and with her specialist regarding her high lipid levels.

The patient, a male, was admitted to the Orthopaedics service following a motorcycle accident near his home. He sustained an open fracture to his right tibia and fibula, which was confirmed by a portable x-ray at an outside hospital. He was unable to walk and was transferred to our facility via EMS. Prior to transfer, he was given 2 grams of Ancef. He has no known allergies except to Ceclor. 

Upon admission, his vital signs were stable and he was alert, though somnolent. His physical exam revealed two lacerations and an obvious deformity in his right lower leg. He had a 3 cm laceration on the anteromedial aspect of the mid tibia that probed to the bone, and a 1 cm laceration on the proximal anterolateral tibial that did not probe to bone. He had decreased ankle movement due to pain but was able to move all 5 toes and had intact sensation. His compartments were soft. 

His lab results showed a low hematocrit of 23.8, low RBC count of 3.27, low hemoglobin of 7.4, low MCV of 70, low MCH of 22.7, and high RDW of 18.8. 

During his hospital stay, he underwent irrigation and debridement (I&D) and open reduction and internal fixation (ORIF) of his right tibia and fibula. Postoperatively, his pain was managed with a PCA and he was transitioned to oral pain medications as tolerated. He was given perioperative antibiotics and Lovenox for DVT prophylaxis. His diet was advanced without complications and he made steady progress with physical therapy. 

Upon discharge, his vital signs were stable, he was afebrile, tolerating a regular diet, voiding spontaneously, and his pain was well controlled. His extremities were neurovascularly intact and all incisions were clean, dry, and intact without evidence of infection, hematoma, or seroma. He was discharged home in stable condition with detailed instructions for follow-up care. 

The patient has no known past medical history and was not on any medications upon admission. His family history is not known.

The patient, a male, was admitted to the Orthopaedic Trauma Service for repair of a nonunion in his right tibia and fibula. He had previously sustained an open tib-fib fracture which was treated with an intramedullary (IM) nail. However, he continued to experience persistent pain at the fracture site and along his fibula. The patient underwent a successful repair of the right tibia nonunion with an iliac crest autograft and an open reduction and internal fixation/repair of the right fibula nonunion with an autograft. Post-operatively, his pain was managed with a PCA and later transitioned to oral pain medications. He tolerated diet advancement without difficulty and made steady progress.

The patient's physical examination was largely unremarkable, with no signs of tenderness, deformity, erythema, edema, induration, or ecchymosis. His iliac crest bone graft site and right lower extremity incision were both clean, dry, and intact with no signs of infection. His right lower extremity was partial weight bearing.

Lab results indicated several abnormal findings including elevated WBC at 14.8, low Hgb at 11.3, low Hct at 35.4, low MCV at 67, low MCH at 21.4, high RDW at 19.4, and high glucose at 107. Additionally, his sodium levels were low at 131, chloride levels were low at 91, and bicarbonate levels were high at 33.

The patient had no known allergies except to Ceclor and Ancef. He was not on any medications upon admission. During his hospital stay, he received unspecified antibiotics and Lovenox for DVT prophylaxis. He was discharged in stable condition with instructions for wound care, weight-bearing, medication use, and follow-up care. His discharge diagnosis was nonunion of the right tibia and fibula.

The patient is a female with a past medical history of hypertension and glaucoma. She was admitted due to multiple pelvic fractures sustained from a mechanical fall at home. She reported severe right pelvis pain immediately after the fall but denied any head strike or loss of consciousness. Initial vital signs were stable and labs showed a white blood cell count of 15.5 and hemoglobin/hematocrit of 9.6/32.4. A plain film pelvis showed non-displaced fractures of inferior pubic rami, left superior pubic ramus, and a minimally displaced fracture of the right sacrum. The orthopedics team recommended pain control, weight-bearing as tolerated, and a follow-up in the Orthopedics Clinic in a week. The patient was admitted to Medicine for pain control.

During her stay, the patient denied any pain and reported that morphine worked well for her. She denied any fevers, chills, chest pain, abdominal pain, nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, constipation, and urinary symptoms. Her admission and discharge physical exams were largely unremarkable.

Lab results showed elevated WBC, RBC, Hgb, Hct, Neuts, Lymphs, Glucose, UreaN, Iron, calTIBC, and Ferritn. Imaging revealed non-displaced fractures of the inferior pubic rami bilaterally, non-displaced fracture of the left superior pubic ramus, minimally displaced fracture of the right sacrum, and a small amount of blood along the pelvic sidewalls, anterior to the bladder, and in the presacral space. There was also thickening of the cecum and adjacent lymphadenopathy, which was concerning for malignancy.

The patient's leukocytosis was resolving, likely due to the pelvic fracture. Her anemia was potentially due to acute blood loss from the pelvic fractures or iron-deficiency anemia from probable colon cancer. The patient's cecal thickening was very concerning for malignancy and she was recommended to have a colonoscopy for further evaluation. She elected to discuss this with her primary care physician as an outpatient.

The patient's hypertension was managed with her home medications of lisinopril and amiloride. Her glaucoma was managed with timolol and latanoprost. Upon discharge, the patient was started on standing Tylenol and Tramadol as needed for pain. She was advised to follow up with her primary care physician and orthopedics, and to have a CBC in a few days for resolving leukocytosis and hematocrit. The patient was discharged to an extended care facility for further rehabilitation.

The patient, a male, was admitted to the Vascular Surgical Service for evaluation of a restenotic left SFA stent. His medical history includes hypertension, type II diabetes mellitus, dyslipidemia, a positive PPD with a normal chest x-ray that was not treated, alcohol abuse, and peripheral vascular disease of the lower extremities. He had previously undergone left SFA angioplasty and stenting. His family history includes a father with hypertension and diabetes, but no history of neurologic disease, including stroke.

Upon admission, the patient was on several medications including amlodipine, plavix, gabapentin, glipizide, hydralazine, HCTZ, novolog, labetalol, lisinopril, metformin, mirtazapine, oxycodone, protonix, KCL, flomax, tylenol, aspirin, vit B12, colace, folate, and MVI. 

During his stay, the patient underwent LLE laser in-stent SFA stenosis & peroneal artery and PTA of native SFA, SFA stent stenosis and peroneal, without complications. Post-operatively, he was stable, NPO, on IV fluids, on bed rest with a condom catheter, and percocet for pain control. His diet was gradually advanced and he was given hydralazine for hypertension. 

Lab results revealed several abnormal findings including Hct-22.1, RBC-2.63, Hgb-7.9, Hct-25.9, MCV-99, MCHC-30.5, RDW-17.1, Neuts-73.1, UreaN-31, K-5.2, K-5.8, UreaN-32, and K-6.1. An elevated K level of 6.1 was managed with calcium gluconate and insulin with dextrose, bringing it down to 5.2. 

At the time of discharge, the patient was in good condition, afebrile with stable vital signs, tolerating a regular diet, ambulating, voiding without assistance, and his pain was well controlled. His discharge diagnosis was peripheral vascular disease.

The patient, a right-handed male with a history of hypertension, dyslipidemia, diabetes, cervical spondylosis with myelopathy, peripheral vascular disease, and alcohol abuse, was admitted due to progressively worsening weakness and numbness over the last 2 weeks. He first experienced difficulty standing about a year ago when he was hospitalized for vascular disease of the lower extremities. He underwent left SFA angioplasty and stenting at that time. His weakness was most pronounced in the distal lower extremities, but he was able to walk independently. Eight months ago, he began experiencing numbness in his feet that gradually progressed upwards to his hips. Six months ago, he was admitted to the hospital for hypertension and back pain. He underwent a lumbar puncture, the results of which are unclear. His daughter reported that he could no longer stand for prolonged periods after this hospitalization. He began to have worsening dyspnea on exertion, but denies dyspnea in the current interview. He has had no bowel or bladder dysfunction. He reports that his right leg is weaker than his left and has had difficulty gripping items in his hands. He has some lower back pain, but states that it has been 'improving.'

The patient's past medical history includes hypertension, type II diabetes mellitus, dyslipidemia, a positive PPD with a normal chest x-ray in the past that was not treated, alcohol abuse, and peripheral vascular disease of the lower extremities. He underwent left SFA angioplasty and stenting. His family history includes a father with hypertension and diabetes, but no history of neurologic disease, including stroke.

Upon admission, the patient was taking Lisinopril, Gabapentin, Plavix, HCTZ, Amlodipine, Toprol XL, and Metformin. His physical exam showed awake, alert, and cooperative behavior. He had a posterior cervical spine incision without erythema or drainage, and staples in place. He had left shoulder pain with PROM and decreased AROM in the left shoulder compared to the right. He had 2+ symmetric reflexes and WWP throughout.

Abnormal lab results included an ESR of 83, %HbA1c of 6.5, CRP of 14.0, calTIBC of 224, TRF of 172, RBC of 3.62, Hgb of 10.8, Hct of 30.9, and Glucose of 153. 

During his hospital stay, the patient underwent a complete neurologic evaluation, including an MRI of his cervical spine showing severe stenosis with cord signal changes and evidence of cord compression. He was cleared for surgical decompression and fusion, which was performed without complications. Post-operatively, he was transferred to the ortho spine service and medicine was consulted for management of his hypertension. He was started on Hydralazine PO in addition to Hydralazine IV for breakthrough with moderate control of his pressures. He was then transitioned to PO Labetolol for improved control. Post-operatively, he was noted to have some weakness of left wrist extension and left triceps. His hospital course was otherwise unremarkable. On the day of discharge, the patient was afebrile with stable vital signs, comfortable on oral pain control, and tolerating a regular diet. His discharge diagnosis was cervical myelopathy.

The patient, a male with a history of hypertension, type II diabetes mellitus, dyslipidemia, positive PPD with a normal chest x-ray in the past (not treated), alcohol abuse, and peripheral vascular disease of the lower extremities, was admitted due to bilateral heel ulcers. He had previously undergone left SFA angioplasty and stenting. The patient has been experiencing progressive lower extremity weakness as well as upper extremity weakness. An MRI showed evidence of cord compression, leading to cervical laminectomies involving C4 through C6 and fusion of the same levels. Post-surgery, the patient has been unable to regain the ability to walk and can only stand for five minutes at a time. His right upper strength has returned to normal, but his left upper extremity remains weak. 

Due to his preoperative weakness and prolonged postoperative recovery, the patient has developed bilateral heel decubiti. He has no symptoms of rest pain and no signs of local or systemic infection. The patient's son reported a left lower extremity arteriogram performed previously. The patient was referred for evaluation of bilateral heel ulcers and an angiogram was carried out to evaluate the arterial system of his lower extremities. 

During his hospital stay, the patient underwent an arteriogram of the right lower extremity, angioplasty of the right SFA, right anterior tibial artery, and peroneal artery balloon angioplasty. He remained hemodynamically stable throughout the procedure and tolerated it well. Post-operatively, he was transferred to the PACU for further stabilization and monitoring. 

The patient's lab results showed an elevated UreaN level at 23* and a low Hct level at 27.6*. His medications upon admission included Gabapentin, Hydralazine, Hydrochlorothiazide, Labetalol, Lisinopril, Mirtazapine, Oxycodone, Pantoprazole, Potassium Chloride, Tamsulosin, Folic Acid, Multivitamin, Senna, and Thiamine. 

The patient's family history includes a father with hypertension and diabetes, but no known history of neurologic disease, including stroke. Upon discharge, the patient was diagnosed with peripheral vascular disease, hypertension, DMII, and dyslipidemia. He was discharged to a rehabilitation facility in stable condition.

The patient, a male with a history of meningioma resection, transient ischemic attack, seizures, hypertension, hypercholesteremia, hypothyroidism, right spermatocele, congenital toe webbing, eye surgery, bilateral carpal tunnel disease, and a left knee replacement, was admitted for neurosurgical evaluation after presenting with subarachnoid hemorrhage (SAH) found on a CT scan. The patient was last seen acting normally at 7am, but was later found to be aphasic and was brought to the hospital. Neurosurgery determined that an intervention would not be helpful, and his CT imaging showed appropriate evolution of the bleed.

Upon admission, the patient was found to be expressive aphasic. His physical exam showed a temperature of 97.7, BP of 99/70, HR of 112, RR of 16, and O2 Sats of 95% on room air. His mental status was awake and alert, cooperative with exam, normal affect, but unable to test due to aphasia. His language showed dysarthria and aphasia. 

Lab results showed abnormal WBC count of 16.5, RBC count of 3.75, Hemoglobin of 11.9, Hematocrit of 33.3, MCHC of 35.9, Glucose of 128, Urea Nitrogen of 24, Calcium of 7.9, and Phenytoin of 26.4. Imaging showed extensive areas of intraparenchymal, subarachnoid, and subdural hemorrhages, mild mass effect on the left lateral ventricle, mild shift of the midline structures to the right side, and small retention cysts in the left maxillary sinus. There was no evidence of aneurysm, but extensive atherosclerotic disease was noted in the intracranial arteries. 

During his hospital stay, the patient developed a fever of 101-102 degrees and was treated with Ciprofloxacin for a urinary tract infection. His chest x-rays were unremarkable, but he had a persistent leukocytosis. Despite extensive work-up, the etiology of the fever and leukocytosis was not identified. The patient was also found to have transaminitis of unclear etiology, hyponatremia, and thyroid nodules. His medications upon admission included Aggrenox, Lipitor, HCTZ, Enalapril, Finasteride, Synthroid, Trileptal, and Dilantin. 

Upon discharge, the patient was neurologically and hemodynamically stable. His primary diagnosis was bilateral subarachnoid hemorrhage, left subdural hematoma, and fever. His secondary diagnosis was seizure disorder and hypertension. He was discharged to an extended care facility for further physical and occupational therapy. His discharge medications included Acetaminophen, Finasteride, Oxcarbazepine, Docusate Sodium, Multivitamin, Atenolol, Lansoprazole, Levothyroxine, Calcium Carbonate, Enalapril, Amlodipine, Insulin Glargine, and Insulin Sliding Scale. He was instructed to limit exercise to walking, not to take any anti-inflammatory medicines unless directed by his doctor, and to call his surgeon immediately if he experienced any new onset of tremors or seizures, any confusion, lethargy or change in mental status, any numbness, tingling, weakness in his extremities, pain or headache that is increasing, or not relieved by pain medication, or new onset of the loss of function, or decrease of function on one whole side of his body.

The patient, a right-handed male, was admitted to the neurology service with a chief complaint of code stroke. He has a history of left-sided hemorrhage while on Aggrenox, hypertension, hyperlipidemia, and a seizure disorder following tumor removal. The patient's wife reported that he woke up and was unable to speak, which she discovered when she heard him banging his head on the bathroom commode. The patient has no signs of tongue biting. The patient had a sudden inability to speak without focal weakness back in the past and was found to have multiple intracranial hemorrhages. He was evaluated for underlying vascular malformation which was negative. He has no residual weakness or sensory deficits but has very poor short-term memory and possible dysnomia since the stroke. The patient had a left fronto-temporal meningioma removed in the past and had a seizure afterwards. He is maintained on medication, the dosage of which is 450mg BID. 

The patient's past medical history includes meningioma resection, transient ischemic attack with no residual deficits, seizures attributed to prior brain surgery, hypertension, hypercholesteremia, hypothyroidism, left knee replacement, right spermatocele, congenital toe webbing, eye surgery, and carpal tunnel disease. His family history is non-contributory. 

Upon physical examination, the patient was awake and alert, but unable to say the month, year, or who the president is. He had brief bursts of spontaneous, fluent speech but appeared to have poor comprehension. His cranial nerves were intact, and his motor and sensory functions were normal. 

Abnormal lab results included a white blood cell count of 11.7*, red blood cell count of 4.29*, hemoglobin of 12.3*, and hematocrit of 36.2*. His MRI/MRA showed extensive encephalomalacia in the anterior/inferior frontal and anterior temporal lobes, likely related to previous trauma. There was also evidence of chronic blood products in the superior right precentral gyrus, small chronic infarctions in the left corona radiata and in the inferior right cerebellar hemisphere, and extensive atherosclerosis in the anterior circulation. 

During his hospital stay, the patient was evaluated for possible acute stroke vs seizure. His MRI and MRA did not show acute infarction, but there was some area of posterior superior parietal lobes in a symmetric signal abnormality seen on DWI. His symptoms were not felt to be due to stroke but were likely due to a seizure and post-ictal state. 

Upon admission, the patient was on Enalapril 10 BID, Amlodipine 2.5 daily, Levothyroxine 112mcg daily, Lipitor 20 daily, and a multivitamin. His discharge diagnosis included seizure, history of meningioma, and history of OCH/SAH/SDH. His condition upon discharge was stable, with limited speech and difficulty with 2 step commands.

The patient, a right-handed male, was admitted to the neurology service due to an episode of speech arrest and right-sided droop. His medical history includes a left-sided hemorrhage while on Aggrenox, hypertension, hyperlipidemia, and a seizure disorder following tumor removal. He also had a recent seizure with speech arrest. The patient has a history of a left fronto-temporal meningioma, which was removed, and he experienced a seizure afterwards. He is maintained on Trileptal for his seizure disorder. He also has a history of a CNS bleed, which resulted in multiple intracranial hemorrhages. Other past medical conditions include transient ischemic attack (TIA), hypertension, hypercholesteremia, hypothyroidism, right spermatocele, congenital toe webbing, eye surgery, and carpal tunnel disease. He had a left knee replacement and has been seizure-free for several years. 

During his admission, the patient was alert with no signs of meningismus or carotid bruits. His neurological examination showed speech arrest, but he was able to mobilize all four limbs symmetrically. His sensory examination was grossly intact. 

Lab results showed elevated levels of white blood cells (WBC-11.8*), red blood cells (RBC-4.46*), hemoglobin (Hgb-13.0*), hematocrit (Hct-37.0*), mean corpuscular hemoglobin concentration (MCHC-35.1*), glucose (Glucose-147*), urea nitrogen (UreaN-21* and 27*), and lactate (Lactate-2.3*). 

Imaging and EEG results showed no acute intracranial process but stable multifocal areas of encephalomalacia. EEGs showed abnormal findings such as rare isolated left anterior temporal sharps and sharp and slow wave complexes, persistent focal left anterior temporal slowing, and mildly encephalopathic background. 

The patient was initially suspected to have had a stroke due to the sudden onset of speech arrest, but this was ruled out as he rapidly improved and had a previous history of a similar episode diagnosed as a seizure. He was admitted for long-term monitoring of his seizures. His Trileptal dose was increased to 750 mg BID with close monitoring of his sodium and white cell count. 

Upon admission, the patient was on OCX 600 bid, LT4 112 mcg qd, Enalapril 10 bid, Amlodipine 2.5 mg qd, Lipitor 20 qhs, MVI, and calcium citrate. His family history was non-contributory.

The patient, a male with a history of left-sided hemorrhage while on aggrenox, hypertension, hyperlipidemia, and seizure disorder following tumor removal, was admitted after experiencing a seizure. His wife noticed the seizure, which was representative of his typical episodes that started after his stroke. The patient was in his usual state of health prior to the seizure. He was evaluated for possible right-sided weakness in the emergency department (ED) and showed dramatic improvement, although he was unsteady on his feet. Physical therapy assessment recommended rehab. The patient has been seizure-free for about a year and denies any recent infections, medication non-adherence or changes, or other precipitating factors. 

His past medical history includes strokes, seizures, meningioma removal, intracranial hemorrhage, hypertension, hypercholesteremia, hypothyroidism, right spermatocele, congenital toe webbing, eye surgery, bilateral carpal tunnel disease, and a left knee replacement. 

Upon admission, the patient was on enalapril, amlodipine, oxcarbazepine, Synthroid, Ca+VitD, multivitamins, Atorvastatin, and Levothyroxine. 

The physical exam showed the patient to be alert, able to say only a few words, and in no acute distress. He had a regular heart rate and rhythm, normal S1 + S2, no murmurs, rubs, or gallops. His abdomen was soft, non-tender, non-distended, with bowel sounds present, no rebound tenderness or guarding, and no organomegaly. Neurologically, he was alert, able to say partial sentences, and followed commands at times. He had a right facial droop. 

Admission labs showed abnormal WBC at 11.6, MCHC at 35.5, glucose at 125, and UreaN at 29. A head CT showed no acute intracranial process but did show sequelae of multiple areas of encephalomalacia in the bifrontal and temporal lobes. 

During his hospital stay, the patient was evaluated by neurology for a typical seizure and showed rapid improvement in the ED. A CT head scan was not concerning for an acute process. Infectious work-up did not reveal any concerning etiologies. The episodes were likely small breakthrough seizures. The patient also had microscopic hematuria, likely secondary to foley trauma, which resolved by discharge. His hypertension was managed with enalapril and amlodipine, his hypothyroidism with levothyroxine, and his hyperlipidemia with lipitor. 

Upon discharge, the patient was diagnosed with a seizure, history of stroke, hypertension, and hyperthyroidism. He was sometimes confused but alert and interactive. He was ambulatory but required assistance or aid.

The patient, a male quadriplegic with a history of a sacral decubitus ulcer, was advised to come in due to an MRI indicating osteomyelitis. He has a history of hypothyroidism, broken neck resulting in quadriplegia, gastroparesis, and low testosterone levels. He is on several medications including Methadone, ClonazePAM, amphetamine-dextroamphetamine, Propantheline Bromide, Diazepam, Collagenase Ointment, Testim, Docusate Sodium, Bisacodyl, and OxycoDONE. 

The patient's sacral decubitus ulcer had a wound culture that tested positive for MRSA. He had been on Bactrim for 10 days, completed two weeks prior to the current visit. He reported a reduced appetite over the last two weeks and weight loss. An MRI showed enhancing marrow edema involving the S3-S4 segments of the sacrum and the coccyx with posterior cortical disruption, raising concern for extension of infection into the epidural space. There was also non-mass-like enhancing edema in the subcutaneous soft tissues and gluteal muscles, consistent with the patient's known chronic coccygeal decubitus ulcer with chronic infection. 

Initial labs were remarkable for ESR 2, CRP: 1.5, lactate 0.9, Cr 0.4, K 3.9, WBC 6. The patient was given 1 gram IV vancomycin and Zosyn IV 4.5 g. Blood and urine cultures were sent. The sacral decubitus ulcer was probed to the bone. 

A bone biopsy was attempted but only an aspirate was obtained. Preliminary results on the aspirate showed gram stain negative, with final results pending. The patient was started on IV Vancomycin for presumed MRSA osteomyelitis. 

The patient's quadriplegia and chronic pain syndromes were stable, and he continued home medications. He had a foley catheter in place, with a clean urinary specimen. 

Abnormal lab results included RBC-4.48*#, Hgb-13.2*#, Hct-40.5#, RBC-4.25*, Hgb-12.8*, Hct-38.4*, Glucose-103*, and Creat-0.4*. 

The patient was discharged with a primary diagnosis of osteomyelitis and secondary diagnosis of quadriplegia, complicated by chronic pain. He was instructed to continue taking IV vancomycin and to get lab work done. He was also advised to follow up with the ID doctors.

The patient, a male with a history of paraplegia due to a broken neck, neurogenic bladder requiring intermittent catheterization, recurrent urinary tract infections (UTIs), hypothyroidism, gastroparesis, and low testosterone levels, presented with lower abdominal pain. He reported several days of cloudy and foul-smelling urine, poor appetite, and general malaise. He had a recent episode of sore throat treated with oral augmentin. He has no known family history of any diseases. 

Upon admission, his vitals were stable and physical examination was unremarkable. His initial labs showed a white blood cell count of 7.1, normal electrolytes, and urinalysis with large leukocytes, bacteria, and 39 white blood cells. No imaging was done. He was given ceftriaxone and 1L of normal saline. 

The patient's urine culture showed a growth of Enterobacter cloacae complex, which was resistant to several antibiotics including amoxicillin/clavulanic acid, cefazolin, cefoxitin, cefpodoxime, cefuroxime (oral and IV), ciprofloxacin, levofloxacin, nitrofurantoin, and trimethoprim/sulfamethoxazole. It was sensitive to ceftazidime, ceftriaxone, gentamicin, tetracycline, and tobramycin. 

His admission labs showed a glucose level of 129, and urinalysis showed urobilinogen of 2, RBC of 20, and WBC of 39. His discharge labs showed a decrease in RBC (4.04), hemoglobin (12.1), hematocrit (38.0), and MCHC (31.8). 

During his hospital stay, the patient experienced hypotension, which was attributed to autonomic dysfunction from his spinal cord injury. He also had mild transaminitis of unclear cause. His chronic issues included chronic pain, for which he was on diazepam, oxycodone, and methadone, neurogenic bladder, for which he was on propantheline bromide, and sleep disorder, for which he was on mirtazapine and amphetamine-dextroamphetamine. 

Upon discharge, the patient was diagnosed with a UTI, paraplegia, neurogenic bladder, and chronic pain. His discharge medications included amphetamine-dextroamphetamine, ascorbic acid, bisacodyl, clonazepam, diazepam, docusate sodium, fluticasone propionate nasal spray, methadone, mirtazapine, oxycodone, propantheline bromide, vitamin D, ceftriaxone, and testim. He was instructed to follow up with his primary care doctor in one week.

The male patient, with a history of paraplegia due to a C6 injury, gastroparesis, neurogenic bladder requiring intermittent catheterization, recurrent urinary tract infections (UTIs), chronic pain syndrome, polysubstance use disorder, dysthymic disorder, osteomyelitis, hyperlipidemia, peptic ulcer disease, vitamin D deficiency, and testicular hypofunction, presented to the emergency department (ED) with complaints of dysuria and fatigue. He reported fevers, malaise, and urine growing multi-drug resistant organisms. He had been previously treated with Augmentin for a UTI, but due to lack of improvement, was transitioned to ciprofloxacin. However, he only took three doses due to joint pain. His urine culture returned positive for Providencia rettgeri, E. coli, and K. pneumoniae, all highly resistant, but collectively all sensitive to ceftazidime. 

In the ED, his vital signs were stable and labs showed a slightly elevated potassium level (5.9) and a slightly decreased bicarbonate level (19). Urine studies showed large leukocytes, small blood, negative nitrites, 4 RBC, >182 WBC, few bacteria, and 1 epithelial cell. He was given IV ceftazidime 2g, PO clonazepam 0.5mg, and Oxycodone 15mg. 

His family history includes type 2 diabetes mellitus in his father and unspecified cancer in his mother. His medications upon admission included bisacodyl, oxycodone, methadone, clonazepam, mirtazapine, testosterone gel, amphetamine-dextroamphetamine XR, methenamine hippurate, fluticasone propionate nasal spray, docusate sodium, vitamin D, and ascorbic acid. 

During his hospital stay, he was found to have dilutional anemia, likely due to the large amount of IV fluids he received. His blood cultures were positive for gram-positive cocci in clusters in one aerobic bottle, but this was likely a contaminant as he had no signs of infection. His urine culture showed growth of Providencia rettgeri and Escherichia coli, both of which were susceptible to ertapenem. His discharge diagnosis was a complicated urinary tract infection.

The patient, a male, was admitted to the orthopedics service due to right leg pain and left ankle pain. He had been involved in a motor vehicle collision, which resulted in immediate pain in his right and left lower extremities and an inability to ambulate. He denied loss of consciousness or premonitory symptoms. His past medical history was unremarkable and he had no known allergies or adverse drug reactions. His family history was non-contributory.

Upon physical examination, the patient was alert and oriented, and in no acute distress. His right lower extremity was wrapped and in an air cast boot. He underwent several surgical procedures including intramedullary nailing for a right tibial shaft fracture, open reduction and internal fixation for a medial malleolar fracture, examination under anesthesia with external rotation stress, and closed treatment with manipulation for a fibular shaft fracture.

Imaging of the shoulder showed no fracture, dislocation, degenerative change, lytic or sclerotic bone lesion, radiopaque foreign body, or soft tissue calcifications. The visualized portions of the lungs were clear. Imaging of the tibia and fibula showed progressive placement of an intramedullary nail with proximal and distal transfixing screws in the tibia. Tibial length appeared to be restored and the fracture alignment appeared near anatomic. Additional screws had been placed at the medial malleolus. A comminuted fracture of the fibula was also noted.

Lab results showed elevated neutrophils (88.9*), lymphocytes (5.2*), and white blood cell count (17.4*). The patient's glucose level was also elevated (106*), as was his urea nitrogen level (24*). 

The patient was initially given IV fluids and IV pain medications, and progressed to a regular diet and oral medications by post-operative day 1. He was given perioperative antibiotics and anticoagulation per routine. His home medications, Albuterol sulfate and Omeprazole, were continued throughout his hospitalization. 

Upon discharge, the patient was afebrile with stable vital signs, his pain was well controlled with oral medications, his incisions were clean, dry, and intact, and he was voiding and moving bowels spontaneously. His discharge diagnoses were right tibial shaft and fibular shaft fracture, and right medial malleolar fracture. He was discharged home with home physical therapy services.

The patient is a female with a past medical history of Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus, Hypertension, Peripheral Vascular Disease, Hypothyroidism (post partial thyroidectomy, thyroid nodule), and a history of Bell's palsy. She has undergone a cholecystectomy and hysterectomy in the past. She presented with dizziness, nausea, vomiting, and diarrhea. She also experienced an allergic reaction to dye, resulting in transient hypotension. The patient has a history of multiple allergies including Penicillins, Sulfa, Ciprofloxacin, Mavik, Diovan, adhesive tape, aspirin, Nifedipine, Cyclobenzaprine, chlorthalidone, pseudocholesteine deficiency, simvastatin, Iodinated Contrast Media - IV Dye, Zofran, and ceftriaxone. 

The patient's symptoms began after arrival to the ED, and improved after admission. She was able to tolerate oral intake with some residual symptoms. The patient's symptoms likely originated from a viral gastroenteritis, possibly norovirus, contracted from her granddaughter who had a similar illness two days prior to presentation. The patient's dizziness resolved and the etiology of the dizziness is unclear, but it may have been a prodrome to her oncoming viral gastroenteritis illness. 

The patient's labs showed an elevated glucose level of 223, a low total CO2 of 17, a low calcium level of 7.0, a low phosphate level of 1.9, a low magnesium level of 1.5, a low RBC count of 3.97, a low HGB of 11.1, a low HCT of 33.7, and an elevated lactate level of 3.3. The patient's WBC count was initially elevated at 13.6 but trended down to 5.9. The patient became hypoNa to 131 after IVF. 

The patient's chest x-ray showed patchy opacity, likely atelectasis. A CT of the abdomen and pelvis showed a moderate amount of liquid stool without evidence of obstruction or intra-abdominal abscess, and a moderate-sized hiatal hernia. The patient had mild pruritis after administration of IV contrast, possibly a contrast reaction. 

The patient's family history includes her father having a myocardial infarction at an unspecified age, her mother having open heart surgery and heart cancer, her sister having a heart condition, and diabetes and thyroid disease on her mother's side of the family. 

Upon admission, the patient was on Chlorthalidone, cilostazol, Clopidogrel, Lantus Solostar, HumaLOG, Levothyroxine Sodium, and Lisinopril.

The patient is a female with a history of hypertension, hypothyroidism, type 2 diabetes, peripheral vascular disease, shoulder pain, back pain, knee pain, Bell's palsy, arthritis, smoking, hysterectomy with ovaries left in, thyroid nodule post right thyroid lobectomy and isthus excision, peripheral vascular disease post left hallux amputation and left SFA recanalization and stenting to treat left hallux gangrene, dermatofibroma, and abnormal pap smear. She has a significant family history of stroke in her paternal aunt, heart attack in her father, blood clots in her father and cousin, and her mother had heart cancer and melanoma of the eye and skin.

She presented to the clinic with asymptomatic hypotension and intermittent vomiting for the past three weeks. She was found to have a new microcytic anemia, increased creatinine levels, and hyponatremia. A CT scan revealed extensive retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy, a 4.2 cm retroperitoneal mass, an increased size of a 3.0 cm right adnexal soft tissue lesion, a new irregular lytic lesion in the right ischium/posterior right acetabulum, and numerous new pulmonary nodules in the bilateral lung bases. She also reported a 10lb weight loss, back and hip pain in her right sacral area, fatigue, chronic nausea/vomiting/diarrhea, and a chronic mild dry cough. She denied any fevers, chills, abdominal pain, chest pain, shortness of breath, swelling, or urinary symptoms.

She underwent an EBUS with biopsy. Her admission medications included cilostazol, clopidogrel, insulin glargine, insulin lispro, levothyroxine sodium, lisinopril, chlorthalidone, cyclobenzaprine, and tacrolimus. She was found to be orthostatic and diagnosed with secondary adrenal insufficiency, possibly due to pituitary compression. She was trialed on methylprednisolone, which she tolerated well. Her home lisinopril and chlorthalidone were held at discharge. She was discharged on methylprednisolone, cilostazol, clopidogrel, insulin lispro, insulin glargine, levothyroxine sodium, and tacrolimus. Her chlorthalidone, cyclobenzaprine, and lisinopril were held and she was advised not to restart them until instructed by her primary care doctor.

Despite recommendations for further hospital stay and workup, the patient left against medical advice. She was advised to follow up with hematology oncology, radiation oncology, and endocrinology as an outpatient. It was also recommended that she get an MRI brain for further evaluation of her intracranial masses.

The patient, a female with a history of Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus (T2DM), Peripheral Artery Disease (PAD) with multiple angioplasties and a left Superficial Femoral Artery (SFA) stent, and a metastatic neuroendocrine tumor with brain, lung, lymph node, and bone metastases, was admitted due to an infection in her left toe ulcer. She has previously undergone a left big toe amputation. The patient is on palliative everolimus and octreotide for her metastatic neuroendocrine tumor. 

Upon admission, the patient's labs showed abnormal values for RBC (3.88), Hemoglobin (10.2), Hematocrit (31.3), Mean Corpuscular Volume (MCV - 81), and Glucose (111). During her stay, a bedside debridement of the left foot ulcer was performed. The patient's foot films showed a status post amputation of the big toe at the level of the distal first metatarsal with no evidence of acute cortical destruction to suggest acute osteomyelitis radiographically. No definite soft tissue gas was seen, but a plantar calcaneal spur was noted. 

The patient's condition improved significantly after being administered vancomycin in the Emergency Department. The podiatry team did not recommend any operative intervention aside from the bedside debridement. The patient was advised to continue with oral antibiotics for a certain number of days. Due to her numerous drug allergies, the Infectious Disease team recommended linezolid. The patient was advised to follow up with podiatry in a week and to continue daily betadine dressings to the left toe. 

The patient's discharge labs showed persistently abnormal values for Hemoglobin (10.4), Hematocrit (31.9), MCV (80), Mean Corpuscular Hemoglobin (MCH - 25.9), and Glucose (134). Blood cultures were pending at the time of discharge and will need to be followed up. 

The patient's medication upon admission included cilostazol, clopidogrel, insulin lispro, levothyroxine sodium, lisinopril, tacrolimus, insulin glargine, methylprednisolone, diphenoxylate-atropine, everolimus, and loperamide. 

The patient was discharged with a diagnosis of foot ulcer and cellulitis. Her mental status was clear and coherent, and she was alert and interactive. However, she required assistance or aid for ambulation. She was advised to continue daily betadine dressings to her toe ulcer until her follow-up with podiatry.

The patient, a female with a history of metastatic neuro-endocrine (carcinoid) tumor, type 2 diabetes mellitus (T2DM) complicated by a recent toe ulcer/cellulitis, and multiple drug allergies, presented to the emergency department (ED) with malaise. She reported feeling extremely fatigued and had difficulty getting out of bed. She also reported a decrease in appetite and some loose stools that self-resolved. She denied experiencing headache, neck stiffness, cough, vomiting, abdominal pain, or dysuria. She was found to be hypotensive at an outpatient allergist visit and was referred to the ED. 

The patient's past medical history includes arthritis, Bell's palsy, cigarette smoking, gastrointestinal issues, hypertension, right axillary lymphadenopathy, thyroid nodule, peripheral vascular disease, back pain, dermatofibroma, leg pain, rash, and a history of chickenpox. She has undergone multiple surgeries including ulnar nerve reconstruction, multiple ear surgeries, tonsillectomy, partial hysterectomy, carpal tunnel syndrome surgery, ganglion removal, and cholecystectomy. 

The patient's family history includes heart disease, transient ischemic attack, eye loss, diabetes type I in her mother, myocardial infarction in her father, valve prolapse and hearing loss in her sister, sudden cardiac death in another relative, pneumonia and obesity in her daughter, fluctuating blood pressure and gout in her son, Crohn's disease and hearing problems in her granddaughters, myocardial infarction in a 3-week-old relative, and stroke in her aunt.

Upon admission, the patient's vitals were 98.1 115 110/62 10 95% RA. Her labs showed a WBC of 11.6, Hgb 11.5, plt 231, AST 55, Lipase 87, CK 118, Cr of 1.1, lactate 1.0, Mg 1.5. Her UA was negative for infection and her Trop was <0.01. Her CXR showed no acute process. Her CT head showed no acute intracranial process, a partially visualized right parietal mass with surrounding edema, and a previously seen enhancing right hypothalamic lesion. Her EKG showed sinus rhythm, low voltage, precordial leads, anteroseptal infarct, old, with no significant change compared to previous ECG. 

During her stay in the ED, the patient's Tmax increased to 102 and she became encephalopathic. She was broadened to bacterial meningitis dosing and admitted for further care. During the time that she was febrile, she was hypoxic to 88%RA, but her CXR was unchanged. 

The patient's medications upon admission included cilostazol, clopidogrel, diphenoxylate-atropine, levothyroxine sodium, lisinopril, loperamide, methylprednisolone, tacrolimus, hydrocerin, alprazolam, glargine, ondansetron ODT, and cetirizine. 

The patient's discharge diagnoses were cellulitis and diabetic foot ulcer. Her secondary diagnoses included adrenal insufficiency, hypertension, hypomagnesemia, type 2 diabetes, hypothyroidism, peripheral arterial disease, and metastatic carcinoid tumor.

The patient, a female with a history of type 2 diabetes, hypertension, thyroid nodule, arthritis, right axillary lymphadenopathy, history of chickenpox, and previous surgeries including cholecystectomy, hysterectomy, carpal tunnel surgery, and ulnar nerve reconstruction, was admitted due to a gangrenous left foot hallux. She reported dropping a dish on her foot several months prior, which led to darkened discoloration and worsening pain. She was given a one-week course of Clindamycin, which improved the erythema at the base of her left hallux. She denies any fever, chills, nausea, or vomiting. 

During her stay, she underwent a left digit amputation and a lower left extremity angioplasty. Her physical exam revealed a gangrenous left hallux without any bogginess or fluctuance, localized erythema at the base of the left hallux, and no tenderness to palpation. Radiology reports indicated severe end-stage osteoarthritic changes involving the first MTP joint, distal SFA versus popliteal disease on the left, and likely tibial disease. There was no radiographic evidence for osteomyelitis. 

Her lab results showed abnormal RDW and glucose levels on admission and discharge, and an abnormal Vancomycin level on discharge. Her home medications, including amlodipine, atorvastatin, lantus, levothyroxine, and lisinopril, were continued during her stay. 

Post-surgery, she remained afebrile with a normal WBC count and was able to tolerate regular oral intake without any problems. She was discharged with instructions to remain partial weight-bearing to her left heel in a surgical shoe, physical therapy, two weeks of post-operative antibiotics, and daily dressing changes. Her discharge diagnosis was a left gangrenous hallux.

The patient, a female, was admitted for elective angioplasty of her left lower extremity due to a non-healing ulcer on her left toe and progressive diffuse stenosis within a previously placed stent in her left lower extremity. The stenosis was associated with a drop in the left ankle-brachial index to 0.62. The patient underwent angioplasty of the left distal SFA stent in-stent stenosis and popliteal artery. Postoperatively, she was stable with no issues and her creatinine level was 0.7, which was at her baseline. She was discharged home in good condition with instructions to follow up in one month's time with a duplex of her left lower extremity.

Her past medical history includes type 2 diabetes, hypertension, a thyroid nodule, arthritis, right axillary lymphadenopathy, a history of chickenpox, and surgeries including cholecystectomy, hysterectomy, carpal tunnel surgery, and ulnar nerve reconstruction. She also has a history of right axillary pain and Bell's palsy. 

Upon admission, her medications included lantus 58 qam, lisinopril 40', levothyroxine 25' M through F, 50' on weekends, and chlorthalidone 12.5 every other day. She has allergies to penicillins, sulfa antibiotics, ciprofloxacin, Mavik, Diovan, adhesive tape, aspirin, nifedipine, cyclobenzaprine, chlorthalidone, and has a pseudocholesteine deficiency.

Her physical exam upon discharge showed normal vital signs, with no organomegaly and normal pulses. However, her blood glucose level was high at 330 and her chloride level was low at 95. 

The patient was discharged with a diagnosis of a left toe ulcer with distal SFA instent stenosis post stent and popliteal angioplasty. Her mental status was clear and coherent, and she was alert, interactive, and independently ambulatory.

The patient, a female, was admitted for bilateral lower extremity claudication. She underwent a percutaneous transluminal angioplasty (PTA) of bilateral popliteals. The procedure was well-tolerated and she was extubated and transferred to the PACU in stable condition. She remained in bedrest for two hours, her diet was advanced, and she was restarted on her aspirin and clopidogrel therapy. Overnight, she did well with no new events. She was discharged in stable condition.

Her past medical history includes type 2 diabetes, hypertension, thyroid nodule, arthritis, right axillary lymphadenopathy, history of chickenpox, and she has undergone cholecystectomy, hysterectomy, carpal tunnel surgery, and ulnar nerve reconstruction. She also has a history of right axillary pain and Bell's palsy. She has no known family history of any significant diseases.

On physical examination at the time of discharge, she was alert and oriented times three. Her vitals were stable with a temperature of 98.4, heart rate of 86, blood pressure of 134/88, respiratory rate of 20, and oxygen saturation of 99% on room air. Her cardiac examination revealed a regular rate and rhythm. Pulmonary examination was clear to auscultation bilaterally. Her abdomen was soft, non-tender, and non-distended. There was no evidence of hematoma or bruising in both groins and her pulses were palpable.

Her medications upon admission included Acetaminophen, Chlorthalidone, Clopidogrel, Docusate Sodium, Levothyroxine Sodium, Glargine, and Lisinopril. She also had a nicotine lozenge for nicotine withdrawal. 

The patient was discharged with a diagnosis of bilateral lower extremity claudication and diabetes. Her discharge condition was stable with an activity status of ambulatory - independent. Her level of consciousness was alert and interactive and her mental status was clear and coherent.

The patient, a male, was admitted with a chief complaint of a swollen left knee and a fever. He reported waking up with a markedly swollen and tender knee, which he could barely flex. He also reported a fever of 100.1 and 101.8 on two separate occasions. The patient had previously injured the same knee in a softball game but did not seek medical attention at the time. He had no recent trauma to the knee, no scrapes or broken skin, and no recent illnesses. He works in a job where he spends about 70% of his time at job sites but could not recall any recent injury to the knee. 

Upon presenting to the ED, the joint was aspirated and no organisms were seen. He was discharged with Keflex. However, the culture grew GPCs (speciation pending) and he was called to return to the ED. His knee was clinically improved with less pain and improved range of motion. He was evaluated by ortho in the ED who recommended Vanco IV for possible septic bursitis. 

The patient's past medical history includes kidney stones. He is adopted and therefore, his family history is unknown. His physical exam showed erythematous and warm left knee joint with mild discomfort on passive range of motion. 

Lab results showed abnormal values for WBC-2500*, HCT-4*, POLYS-66*, BANDS-1*, SED RATE-63*, and CRP-188.6*. The joint fluid culture showed MSSA. Knee films showed extensive soft tissue swelling, but no acute fracture or dislocation was identified. 

During his hospital stay, the patient was diagnosed with septic bursitis. He was initially treated with Keflex which resulted in resolution of erythema. He returned to the hospital once joint cultures had small growth MSSA. Ortho believed it to be septic bursitis and not septic arthritis given adequate range of motion. The patient was placed on IV vancomycin while awaiting sensitivities, with continued improvement on knee, and remaining afebrile. 

Upon admission, the patient was on Keflex. His discharge diagnosis was septic bursitis and his condition upon discharge was good.

The patient, a male of undisclosed age, was admitted due to lung nodules. He had developed a cough and a subsequent Chest CT showed a 5mm RML nodule. He has been followed yearly for this and in an undisclosed time, bilateral pulmonary nodules were documented. He underwent a CT guided biopsy of a LLL nodule which was non-diagnostic. He also had a CT of the abdomen and pelvis which showed some liver lesions and other unspecified lesions. The etiology of these lesions is unclear but the differential included infection, neoplasm, and vasculitis. He had a colonoscopy which was normal. His pulmonary symptoms include cough and some mid chest pressure when he feels more congestion. He denies shortness of breath, dyspnea on exertion, weight loss, recent upper respiratory infection/pneumonia, foreign travel, etc. He smoked for a short time about an undisclosed number of years ago but none since. He works every day in a family business and is exposed to lots of dust, sewerage, etc.

His past medical history includes kidney stones, RML lung nodule, and MRSA in the left knee and nose. He has also undergone bilateral hernia repairs. He has no known allergies or adverse drug reactions. His family history is unknown as he is adopted.

During his hospital stay, he underwent a VATS RML wedge resection x 2 and RLL wedge resection x 1. He recovered well post-surgery, with minimal chest tube output, good oxygenation on room air, stable vital signs, and independent ambulation. His chest X-ray showed no pneumothorax or consolidation. His pain was well controlled on oral pain meds. He was discharged home and will follow up in clinic in 2 weeks.

Upon admission, he was taking over-the-counter cholecalciferol (vitamin D3) and a daily multivitamin. His physical exam was largely unremarkable. However, his lab results showed an elevated glucose level of 127* and a slightly elevated creatinine level of 1.3*. His chest X-ray prior to chest tube pull showed no appreciable pneumothorax, right pleural drainage tube in place, no appreciable pleural effusion, moderately extensive atelectasis and biopsy artifact persist in the right mid and lower lung zones, and moderate atelectasis has developed on the left. Left and right upper lobes are clear. Cardiomediastinal silhouette is unremarkable.

The patient, a female with a past medical history of spontaneous abortion, hypothyroidism, Vitamin D deficiency, possible GERD, and vasovagal syncope in childhood, presented with progressive sensory changes. She had recently experienced a miscarriage and underwent a D&C procedure. Post-procedure, she noticed odd paresthesias to light touch over her lower legs and feet, which later extended to include her thighs, waist, and eventually ascended to nipple level. She reported no abnormality at rest and was only aware of the difference when she touched her skin. An urgent MRI scan was arranged which showed several enhancing lesions in the dorsal cord, as well as several lesions that did not enhance. 

Upon physical examination, the patient was alert and oriented, with normal muscle bulk and tone throughout. She reported an odd sensation to light touch, which included the legs and extended up the trunk to the level of ~T4 bilaterally. She believed that the right side was perhaps more affected. 

Admission labs showed an elevated absolute neutrophil count of 7.46 and a glucose level of 112. Cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) analysis showed 1 RBC. Imaging revealed multiple T2/FLAIR hyperintense lesions throughout the cervical and thoracic spinal cord, suggestive of demyelinating disease. There were also scattered white matter lesions in the bilateral cerebral hemispheres, as well as the right cerebellar hemisphere. 

During her hospital stay, the patient reported improvement in her symptoms. A lumbar puncture showed no evidence of infection. The presence of both enhancing and non-enhancing lesions on her MRI was highly suggestive of a neurological condition. The patient was diagnosed with multiple sclerosis (MS) using the McDonald criteria. 

Upon discharge, the patient's mental status was clear and coherent, and she was alert and interactive. Her neuro exam showed decreased light touch (mixed with dysesthesia) in the medial legs and bilateral torso. Her discharge medications included Levothyroxine Sodium 75 mcg PO DAILY. The patient was discharged home with a primary diagnosis of multiple sclerosis.

The patient, a female, was admitted following an accident where she was struck by an automobile while crossing the street. She lost consciousness and woke up in the ambulance. She was then transported to the hospital for further management. The patient has a history of hypertension and hyperlipidemia. She is allergic to Penicillins and Tetracycline. 

The primary complaint was due to the accident. She underwent an Open Reduction Internal Fixation (ORIF) procedure for a mandible fracture. Postoperatively, she was initially sleepy, likely due to the anesthesia and narcotics. She had difficulties navigating stairs, which was a concern as her apartment has 35 stairs. However, she was eventually cleared for home with services by Physical Therapy. 

Lab results showed abnormal glucose levels at 137, potassium at 3.0, total CO2 at 20, hematocrit at 35.3, mean corpuscular volume (MCV) at 80, and lymphocytes at 43.9. A head CT scan revealed no acute intracranial process, a left frontal/supra-orbital scalp soft tissue hematoma without skull fracture, and sinus disease. A mandibular series was conducted but the right mandibular ramus fracture was not clearly identified on the images provided due to the patient's dizziness. 

Upon discharge, the patient was hemodynamically stable, tolerating a regular diet, and her pain was adequately controlled. Her discharge diagnoses included pedestrian struck by auto, mandible fracture, lip and facial lacerations, and knee abrasions. She was given wire cutters and instructions on a full liquid and soft diet, and increasing her protein intake. She was advised to drink at least 6 cans daily of Boost Plus/Ensure Plus for proper nutrients and vitamins to aid in the healing process. She was also instructed to cut the wires on both corners of her teeth and go to the Emergency room immediately in case of an emergency such as shortness of breath, nausea, and/or vomiting.

The patient, a male, was admitted for an elective right total knee replacement due to progressive right knee pain with activity. His past medical history includes osteoarthritis, a tonsillectomy, knee arthroscopy, and prostate cancer treated with XRT. He had no medications upon admission. 

During his hospital stay, the patient underwent a successful right total knee replacement procedure without any complications. He was closely monitored throughout the procedure and remained hemodynamically stable. Post-operatively, he was transferred to the PACU and floor for further recovery. He remained hemodynamically stable and his pain was well controlled. However, he required a transfusion due to expected postoperative anemia. He made progress with physical therapy to improve his strength and mobility. 

Upon physical examination at discharge, the patient was afebrile with all vital signs stable. He was alert and oriented, with no acute distress. His right lower extremity was partially weight bearing. The incision was intact with no swelling, erythema, or drainage. The dressing was clean, dry, and intact. Extensor/flexor hallicus longus was intact, sensation was intact to light touch, neurovascular was intact distally, capillary refill was brisk, and 2+ pulses were present. 

The patient was discharged in a stable condition with a diagnosis of osteoarthritis. His discharge instructions included monitoring for any shortness of breath, new redness, increased swelling, pain, or drainage, or a temperature over 101. He was advised to bear weight on his right leg and use crutches for ambulation. He was also instructed to keep his incision/dressing clean and dry, and to continue his Lovenox injections as prescribed to help prevent blood clots.

The patient, a male, was admitted for an elective L4-S1 lumbar fusion due to lumbar spondylosis. His chief complaint upon admission was right leg pain. His past medical history includes Chol and RUL opacity on CT. The patient underwent a major surgical procedure, L4-S1 posterior lumbar interbody fusion with pedicle screws and bone marrow aspirate. Post-operatively, he was transferred to the floor from the PACU. His immediate post-op pain was managed with a PCA, which was later discontinued and replaced with oral agents. The patient was able to void independently after his Foley was discontinued. Physical therapy evaluated him and cleared him for home. 

Post-op x-rays showed proper alignment and no evidence of hardware complication. His surgical wound was covered with steri strips and was clean, dry, and intact. The patient's drain was discontinued and there were no issues with drainage. His Ancef was discontinued. He had a Tmax for 24 hrs of ___, a UA was ordered and IS was recommended. He was ambulating independently. The UA was negative and he was asked to call the office to follow up on the culture. 

Upon admission, the patient was taking Oxycodone PRN. His mental status upon discharge was clear and coherent, and he was alert and interactive. He was able to ambulate independently. The patient was discharged home with a diagnosis of lumbar spondylosis. His discharge instructions included wound care, activity restrictions, pain management, and signs of complications to watch for. He was also instructed to call the office for urine culture results and to continue using his Incentive spirometer.

The patient is a male of undisclosed age who was admitted due to severe back and leg pain that had been worsening over the course of 18 months. His pain was so severe that it was poorly controlled with IV opioids and was affecting his ability to work. He had previously tried conservative management for his pain with little relief. He was scheduled for surgical repair of a ruptured L4-L5 disc in the coming weeks. The patient denied having diabetes mellitus, intravenous drug use, recent back procedures, urinary incontinance, urinary retention, or fecal incontinance. His past medical history includes gastroesophageal reflux disease (GERD). He was not on any medications upon admission and has no known drug allergies. His social history includes rare alcohol consumption and smoking, but no illicit drug use.

Physical examination revealed the patient was alert and oriented, but in severe discomfort. Sensory and motor examinations were normal. His upper extremities were clean and intact with no signs of tenderness, deformity, erythema, edema, induration, or ecchymosis. His arms and forearm compartments were soft and he had no pain with passive motion. His radial pulses were 2+.

During his hospital stay, the patient was considered for surgery for his herniated lumbar disc. However, due to surgical emergencies, the operation was delayed. In the meantime, his pain was managed with IV decadron, and oral oxycontin and oxycodone. As the patient became comfortable and was able to ambulate on oral pain medications, he was discharged home. He was instructed to avoid heavy lifting and significant twisting or bending. His condition upon discharge was good. His discharge diagnosis was a herniated lumbar disc.

The patient is a female who was admitted due to worsening depressive symptoms and suicidal ideation. She has a history of depressive episodes but no previous psychiatric admissions or suicide attempts. She reported persistent thoughts of suicide over the last few weeks, along with symptoms of major depression such as loss of interest in activities, inability to see or invest in the future, lack of appetite, trouble concentrating, hopelessness, and a generalized feeling of being 'lost.' She also has a history of sexual trauma. The patient reported a three-month worsening of depressive symptoms, including anhedonia, poor concentration, low motivation, fatigue, diminished appetite, and suicidal ideation. She denied having any supports in the community and cited school and financial stressors as contributing to her worsening mood. 

The patient has a past medical history of asthma and Achilles tendonitis. She has no known drug allergies but is allergic to alcohol. She was not on any medications upon admission. She denies any family psychiatric history. 

Physical examination was largely unremarkable. Neurological examination was also normal. The patient was awake and alert, oriented, and able to register and recall words. Her speech was low volume with brief answers. Her mental status on discharge was improved, with a better mood congruent with a euthymic, reactive, mildly constricted affect. She denied suicidal ideation and demonstrated fair insight and good judgment.

Lab results showed an abnormal MCHC of 30.5 and an abnormal RDWSD of 47.1. Eosinophils were also slightly low at 0.7. All other lab results were within normal limits.

During her hospital stay, the patient was diagnosed with an unspecified depressive disorder. She was provided with individual and group therapy focusing on meditation, self-awareness, and coping skills. She was encouraged to participate in various groups and milieu therapy opportunities offered by the unit. She was also provided with over-the-counter analgesics for her Achilles tendonitis. 

Upon discharge, the patient was not at an acutely elevated risk of self-harm nor danger to others. She was discharged home with a diagnosis of unspecified depressive disorder. Her mental status was clear and coherent, and she was alert and interactive. She was in a good mood congruent with euthymic, reactive affect, and denied suicidal ideation. She was not prescribed any medications upon discharge.

The patient, a male with no significant past medical history, was referred for ERCP due to a biliary tumor. He was recently diagnosed with cholangiocarcinoma when he presented with jaundice and pruritis. He has a 3 cm complex hilar biliary stricture and has undergone two ERCPs. He was scheduled for a curative Whipple procedure, which was aborted due to the discovery of multiple peritoneal implants. This was a surprise to the patient and his family as previous CT scans did not show this. The patient has regained some weight since the placement of a plastic stent during ERCP. His initial jaundice has resolved. 

The patient underwent major surgical procedures including ERCP with metal stent, percutaneous cholecystostomy tube placement, and PICC placement. He reported pain at the site of the laparoscopy wounds and in his shoulder after the procedure. He also experienced a 7 lbs weight loss over a month and chest pain that resolves with Ativan. He also reported right shoulder pain post laparoscopy. 

The patient's family history includes his maternal grandfather and grandmother dying from heart disease. His uncle had diabetes and died of heart disease, and another uncle died of lung cancer. 

Upon admission, the patient was on OxycoDONE (Immediate Release) 5 mg PO Q4H for pain and Lorazepam 0.5 mg PO Q6H for anxiety. 

Abnormal lab results include WBC-16.8*, RBC-4.49*, MCH-32.1*, and AlkPhos-319*. The patient's ERCP revealed a complex tight 3cm hilar stricture. The right anterior and posterior intrahepatics were visualized, but the left intrahepatic duct could not be seen. A sphincterotomy was performed and two 8cm by 8mm uncovered metal stents were successfully placed in the right anterior and posterior intrahepatic ducts. 

CT scan showed diffuse intrahepatic biliary ductal dilation, central hepatic vascular stenosis and effacement with a central poorly defined hepatic lesion suggestive of cholangioCA/ Klatskin tumor. Blood culture revealed ESCHERICHIA COLI. 

The patient's hospital course was complicated by acute cholangitis and E. coli septicemia, as well as portal vein thrombus and evidence of liver infarct. He was started on Zosyn/Cipro and a PTC was placed. He was transitioned to Imipenem and his blood cultures were cleared. He was discharged on Meropenem 1g IV q8 for at least 2 weeks. He was also initiated on Lovenox 60mg SQ q12 hrs, indefinitely, but for at least several months. 

The patient's discharge diagnoses include metastatic cholangiocarcinoma, with metastases to peritoneum and liver, bile obstruction with acute cholangitis and E. coli septicemia, and portal vein thrombosis.

The patient, a male of unspecified age, was admitted due to increasing right upper quadrant pain. He has a history of cholangiocarcinoma, for which he has had multiple stents placed to relieve biliary obstruction. He has also undergone a laparotomy which revealed peritoneal metastases, ruling out a previous plan for a Whipple procedure. His MRI brain scan showed no acute intracranial abnormality and no enhancing mass. His cerebrospinal fluid studies showed a total protein of 100 and 111, and a glucose of 87. His ANCA, HIV antibody, and IgG were all negative. His complement C3 was 85 and C4 was 20. His discharge labs showed a white blood cell count of 4.6, red blood cell count of 3.74, hemoglobin of 11.4, hematocrit of 33.5, and platelet count of 124. His glucose was 116 and urea nitrogen was 31. 

The patient's mental status changes were initially thought to be due to steroid-induced psychosis with underlying neuropsychiatric issues. However, he also had a component of delirium given his lack of attention and fluctuating symptoms. His steroids were tapered down and he was started on seroquel with improvement in behavior, but not memory loss. His tremor was thought to be due to the steroids and was expected to improve with reduction in steroids. His hypertension was controlled and his hypothyroidism was managed with levothyroxine. He was discharged to an extended care facility with a diagnosis of steroid-induced psychosis and other encephalopathy of unclear etiology. He was instructed to follow up for outpatient neuropsychiatric testing.

The patient is a male with a history of COPD, prostate adenocarcinoma (treated with external beam treatment and Lupron), seropositive rheumatoid arthritis (on Methotrexate and sulfsalazine), degenerative joint disease in both shoulders (received a cortisol shot a month ago), schizophrenia (on risperidal and prozac), recurrent UTIs post prostate cancer radiation (on daily Macrobid), left lower lobe nodules (followed by Pulm and serial CTs), and GERD (on omeprazole). He presented with increased oxygen saturation requirement, despite not usually requiring O2. He reported fatigue but denied shortness of breath, fevers/chills, or increased cough. He also reported a 50lbs weight gain over the past year and occasional drenching night sweats over the past two months. 

Upon admission, the patient's vitals were Temp F98.6, BP118/58, HR96, R20, O2-sat 88-91% 4LNC. Physical examination revealed decreased breath sounds over the right lung fields, +rhonchi at b/l bases, +wheezes over b/l upper lung fields, no dullness to percussion, and mild clubbing in hands. 

Lab results showed abnormal WBC-11.2*, RBC-4.04*, Hgb-13.6*, Hct-39.4*, MCH-33.7*, Neuts-87.6*, Lymphs-6.0*, and Glucose-112*. Imaging revealed right lower lobe pneumonia and bibasilar atelectasis. A video swallow evaluation showed no evidence of aspiration during eating. 

The patient was initially thought to have hospital-acquired pneumonia (HAP) and was placed on broad coverage with cefepime, vancomycin, and levofloxacin. However, repeat CXR showed rapid resolution of infiltrate, suggesting transient mucus plugging with chronic cough/ oropharyngeal aspiration as the more likely cause. This was confirmed by a CTA which showed multifocal bilateral consolidations consistent with aspiration. 

The patient was treated for a COPD exacerbation with a five-day prednisone taper of 40mg daily, a seven-day course of levofloxacin, duonebs, and advair. His respiratory status improved and supplemental oxygen was weaned to 1 L. 

Other chronic issues include lung nodules and RLL infiltrate, rheumatoid arthritis, GERD, right shoulder pain, schizophrenia/depression, recurrent UTI, and a history of adenocarcinoma. 

Upon discharge, the patient's primary diagnoses were mucus plugging and COPD exacerbation, with secondary diagnoses of rheumatoid arthritis, schizophrenia, recurrent UTIs, history of prostate cancer, and GERD. His mental status was clear and coherent, and he was alert and interactive. He was able to get out of bed with assistance to a chair or wheelchair. His discharge medications included docusate sodium, finasteride, fluticasone-salmeterol, fluoxetine, risperidone, ranitidine HCl, sulfasalazine, guaifenesin, levofloxacin, methotrexate sodium, Claritin, Macrobid, folic acid, Vitamin D-3, and azelastine.

The patient is a male with a past medical history of prostate cancer, rheumatoid arthritis, schizophrenia, and emphysema. He presented with fever, cough, and dyspnea. He reported chills and dyspnea at his nursing home prior to admission. He was found to be febrile and hypoxic, so oxygen was started. He reported having sick contacts at his nursing home and a cough productive of yellow sputum. He also reported increasing pain from urination for the past two months, located in his penis and around his prostate. He denied hematuria but reported feeling nauseous without vomiting and a mild headache. 

In the ED, his vitals showed a temperature of 100.7, heart rate of 125, and oxygen saturation of 91% on 6L Nasal Cannula, which improved prior to admission. His chest X-ray showed a right lower lobe infiltrate and his EKG showed sinus tachycardia with no ischemia. His white blood cell count was elevated at 11.6 with 95% neutrophils and his lactate was 2.5. He was given 1L of normal saline, nebulizer treatments, levofloxacin 750, and vancomycin 1 gram and was admitted to the floor. 

His past medical history includes prostate adenocarcinoma, seropositive rheumatoid arthritis, COPD, schizophrenia, recurrent UTIs after prostate cancer radiation, left lower lobe nodules, and GERD. His family history includes diabetes mellitus but no cancers. 

Upon admission, his physical exam showed clear lungs with good air movement and crackles at the right base, a non-displaced PMI with tachycardia but regular rhythm, and no suprapubic tenderness. His extremities showed trace edema in both legs without redness or swelling, badly deformed hands and feet with ulnar deviations, and chronic bilateral elbow bursitis. 

His admission labs showed an elevated lactate of 2.1, glucose of 137, urea nitrogen of 22, white blood cell count of 11.6, red blood cell count of 3.97, MCV of 104, MCH of 35.6, and neutrophils of 95.2. His urine showed large leukocytes, 18 red blood cells, and few white blood cell clumps. His urine culture showed yeast and Corynebacterium species. His chest X-ray showed recurrent right upper and bilateral lower lobe pneumonia. 

During his hospital stay, he was diagnosed with bacterial pneumonia and was started on empiric antibiotics. His lactate trended down and his oxygen requirement resolved. He also had microscopic hematuria and bacteriuria but no UTI. His hypoxemia was attributed to his pneumonia in the setting of baseline emphysema. His rheumatoid arthritis was managed with tramadol and Tylenol for pain control. His prostate cancer is currently in remission with undetectable PSA levels. His hyperlipidemia was managed with aspirin and atorvastatin. His schizophrenia and depression were managed with fluoxetine and risperidone. His macular degeneration was managed with multivitamins and his GERD was managed with omeprazole. 

Upon discharge, his mental status was clear and coherent, and he was alert and interactive. He was able to get out of bed with assistance to a chair or wheelchair. His discharge diagnoses were pneumonia, rheumatoid arthritis, and COPD. His discharge instructions included completing a 5-day course of antibiotics and getting a repeat chest X-ray in 6 weeks to ensure his pneumonia has resolved. He was also instructed to take all his medications as prescribed and keep all follow-up appointments.

The patient, a male with a history of COPD, presented to the ED from a SNF with an increased cough, mucous production, and a fever of 100.6. He reported that his symptoms were similar to the pneumonia he was hospitalized for last year, but not as severe. He was given IV steroids, nebulizers, Vanco, and ZOsyn in the ED. His past medical history includes prostate adenocarcinoma, for which he underwent external beam treatment and Lupron a few years ago, seropositive rheumatoid arthritis, degenerative joint disease in both shoulders, schizophrenia, recurrent UTIs after prostate cancer radiation, left lower lobe nodules, and GERD. His family history includes diabetes mellitus, but no cancers.

Upon physical examination, the patient was found to have occasional diffuse expiratory wheezing, good air movement, and a cough triggered by coughing or exertion. His hands showed severe distortion from known rheumatoid arthritis. His vital signs were T: 97.9, BP: 142/83, HR: 94, RR: 20, O2sat: 94%/3L NC.

Lab results showed an elevated glucose level of 121, and abnormal RBC, HGB, HCT, MCV, MCH, NEUTS, and LYMPHS values. A chest X-ray indicated recurrent lower lobe pneumonia.

During his hospital stay, the patient's pneumonia and COPD exacerbation improved significantly with levofloxacin 750 mg, steroids, and an increase in guaifenison and nebulizer treatments. His rheumatoid arthritis, prostate cancer, hyperlipidemia, schizophrenia, depression, and GERD were managed with their respective medications.
