Upon admission, the patient was on a variety of medications including Risperidone, Tamsulosin, TraMADOL, Ipratropium Bromide Neb, Albuterol, Acapella, Atorvastatin, Senna, Fluticasone-Salmeterol Diskus, azelastine, Azo Cranberry + Probiotic, Docusate Sodium, Guaifenesin, Omeprazole, Methotrexate, Aspirin, BuPROPion, Acetaminophen, Finasteride, Fluoxetine, FoLIC Acid, Nitrofurantoin Monohyd, Polyethylene Glycol, Tiotropium Bromide, Cyanocobalamin, and Vitamin D.

The patient was discharged with a diagnosis of bacterial pneumonia and an acute exacerbation of COPD. His mental status was clear and coherent, and he was alert and interactive, but required assistance or aid for ambulation. He was discharged to an extended care facility.

The patient, a male with a history of COPD, schizophrenia, rheumatoid arthritis (RA), prostate cancer, and sleep apnea, was admitted due to progressive dyspnea and hypoxia. He has a history of recurrent pneumonias and was recently treated for pneumonia with azithromycin. The patient reported continued progression in dyspnea and increased oxygen requirement. He denied fevers/chills but reported a productive cough, poor appetite, and fatigue. He was sent in by his nurse practitioner due to dyspnea because a recent echocardiogram showed an ejection fraction (EF) of approximately 10% with a probable apical clot. 

Upon admission, the patient was given vancomycin, levofloxacin, methylprednisolone, and started on a heparin drip. A chest x-ray showed a large right-sided opacity. An EKG showed sinus rhythm with premature atrial contractions (PACs) and premature ventricular contractions (PVCs), left axis deviation, and ST depressions/T wave inversions in lateral leads. The patient was transferred to the floor due to sustained hypoxia in the setting of intense coughing and sputum production. He was given 40mg IV lasix and started on duonebs. 

The patient's past medical history includes hypertension, prostate adenocarcinoma, seropositive rheumatoid arthritis, COPD, schizophrenia, recurrent UTIs after prostate cancer radiation, left lower lobe nodules, and GERD. His family history does not include early myocardial infarction, arrhythmia, cardiomyopathies, or sudden cardiac death. 

Abnormal lab results include RBC count of 4.09, Hgb of 13.6, MCV of 110, MCH of 33.2, MCHC of 30.1, Neuts of 81.6, Lymphs of 9.3, Glucose of 130, HCO3 of 37, proBNP of 7623, and PTT of 150. The patient's arterial blood gas showed a pO2 of 68, pCO2 of 72, pH of 7.36, and calTCO2 of 42. 

The patient underwent coronary catheterization and thoracentesis. The coronary angiography showed normal coronary arteries. The patient's pleural effusions were tapped and appeared transudative, likely due to heart failure. The patient was diuresed to euvolemia and a chest tube was placed with adequate drainage and improvement in dyspnea. 

The patient was diagnosed with acute systolic heart failure, COPD, mucus plugging, hypoxia, and rheumatoid arthritis. He was started on metoprolol XL 50 QDay, furosemide 40 QDay, lisinopril 5mg QDay, and warfarin at 2mg. The patient was discharged to extended care.

The patient, a male with a history of Chronic Obstructive Pulmonary Disease (COPD) requiring oxygen, schizophrenia, Rheumatoid Arthritis (RA), prostate cancer, and sleep apnea, was admitted due to hypoxemic respiratory failure and shortness of breath. He had multiple previous admissions due to pneumonia. In the emergency department, he presented with shortness of breath and no urine output for three days. Initial vitals showed hypoxemia and a blood gas analysis revealed abnormal values. Lab results showed elevated BUN/Cr (68/1.7), WBC (12 with 96% Neutrophils), and lactate (4.5). Chest x-ray showed extensive consolidation in the right lung, indicative of pneumonia. He was given a dose of Vancomycin, Cefepime, and Levofloxacin, and was intubated due to worsening respiratory status. A bronchoscopy showed thick sputum.

The patient's past medical history includes hypertension, prostate adenocarcinoma (treated with external beam treatment and Lupron), seropositive rheumatoid arthritis (treated with Methotrexate and sulfsalazine), COPD, schizophrenia (treated with Abilify and Prozac), recurrent urinary tract infections (UTIs) after prostate cancer radiation (treated with Macrobid), left lower lobe nodules, and Gastroesophageal Reflux Disease (GERD, treated with Omeprazole). There is no family history of early myocardial infarction, arrhythmia, cardiomyopathies, or sudden cardiac death.

Upon admission, the patient was intubated and sedated. Physical examination revealed decreased breath sounds at bilateral bases, regular heart rate and rhythm, and a soft, non-distended abdomen. The patient was on multiple medications upon admission, including Acetaminophen, Aripiprazole, Aspirin, Bupropion, Cyanocobalamin, Docusate Sodium, Finasteride, Fluoxetine, Fluticasone-Salmeterol Diskus, Folic Acid, Guaifenesin ER, Ipratropium-Albuterol Neb, Lorazepam, Nitrofurantoin Monohyd, Omeprazole, Polyethylene Glycol, Senna, Tamsulosin, Tiotropium Bromide, Tramadol, Vitamin D, Warfarin, Acidophilus Probiotic, Azelastine, CertaVite with Lutein, Cranberry, Fleet Enema, Fluticasone Propionate NASAL, Loratadine, Methotrexate, Sulfazine EC, Metoprolol Succinate XL, Lisinopril, Furosemide, Atorvastatin, and Tadalafil.

During his hospital stay, the patient was found to be in septic shock with E.coli growing in sputum and urine. Despite maximal support, antibiotics, and therapy, the patient unfortunately passed away. His hospital course was complicated by hypoxemic respiratory failure due to pneumonia and possibly exacerbated by severe COPD and heart failure. He also had septic shock due to E.coli UTI and pneumonia. His renal function worsened, likely due to hypotension and Acute Tubular Necrosis (ATN), and he was started on Continuous Venovenous Hemofiltration (CVVH). He also had elevated INR and atrial fibrillation, likely due to chronic antibiotic use and multiple medication interactions. His INR was managed with IV Vitamin K and heparin.

The patient, a white male, was admitted for surgical procedures including excision of a scar, left inguinal hernia repair with mesh, umbilical hernia repair open, and incisional hernia repair with mesh x 2. These procedures were performed due to his history of open colectomy for diverticular disease. His past medical history includes diverticulitis. No family history was noted in the report. 

Upon admission, the patient was taking herbal supplements and valtrex. During his hospital stay, an epidural was placed for pain management, which was discontinued on post-operative day 1 and replaced with demerol PCA. The patient was initially kept on sips and then advanced to clears. His pain management was later switched to oral percocet. 

The patient's physical examination showed normal temperature, heart rate, blood pressure, respiratory rate, and oxygen saturation. His abdomen was soft, non-tender, and non-distended with positive bowel sounds. His abdominal dressings were clean, dry, and intact. No pertinent results were noted in the report. 

The patient experienced some nausea when his diet was progressed from sips to clears. His discharge diagnosis was open incisional hernia repair and his condition upon discharge was good. He was instructed to refrain from driving or operating machinery due to the drowsiness caused by his pain medications. He was also advised to contact his doctor or return to the emergency room if he experienced any new or worsening symptoms. 

The patient was advised to avoid heavy lifting for 6 weeks and to resume moderate exercise at his discretion, excluding abdominal exercises. He was instructed to shower only, avoid tub baths or swimming, and to cover any clear drainage from his incisions with clean, dry gauze. He was also advised to remove any remaining steri-strips a certain number of days after surgery and to contact the doctor if he experienced increased pain, swelling, redness, or drainage from the incision sites. A follow-up appointment with Dr. ___ was scheduled for a couple of weeks after discharge.

The patient, a male, was admitted for hernia repair. His past medical history includes diverticulitis. He had previously undergone a complex abdominal wall repair for what was believed to be an umbilical and right lower quadrant hernia extending suprapubic and an operation also in the left groin. He was repaired with two large polypropylene meshes, which were secured to the anterior abdominal wall. However, he returned with an increasing hernia, both in his umbilicus and recurring in his right lower quadrant. The patient reported that his original hernia recurred after lifting a heavy object several months after his colectomy for diverticulitis. His current situation was complicated by a recurrence of left lower quadrant pain and diagnosed with recurrent diverticulitis, for which he was on antibiotics and slowly improving.

During his stay, the patient underwent an incisional hernia repair with mesh and excision of left axilla skin. His postoperative course was uneventful, with pain initially managed with an epidural, which was discontinued on postoperative day one. His nutrition was advanced to regular by postoperative day four, and he was discharged with pain medication and a stool softener.

On physical examination, the patient was in no acute distress, alert and oriented. His abdomen was soft and nontender, with a reducible umbilical defect. He also appeared to have some protuberance in both inguinal areas, which was reducible. His musculoskeletal examination was normal, with good range of motion and strength in both upper and lower extremities.

Lab results showed an elevated glucose level of 108. No other abnormal lab results were reported. The patient had no known allergies except for pollen extracts and ragweed. He was not on any medications upon admission. His discharge condition was stable.

The patient, a male, was admitted to the plastic surgery service for a recurrent ventral hernia. He underwent a successful recurrent ventral hernia repair with mesh and component separation, as well as a panniculectomy. His past medical history includes diverticulitis. He has no known family history of any significant diseases. 

Upon admission, the patient was on the following medications: Acetaminophen 650 mg PO Q4H:PRN for mild pain, Atorvastatin 40 mg PO QPM, Levothyroxine Sodium 88 mcg PO DAILY, and LORazepam 0.5 mg PO Q4H:PRN for nausea and insomnia. 

Post-operatively, the patient was given Morphine PCA for pain control, which was effective. He was also given IV fluids until he was able to tolerate oral intake. His diet was advanced appropriately, but he developed a post-op ileus for 2 days. His diet was then restricted and slowly advanced. By the day prior to discharge, the patient was passing gas and having bowel movements. He was also started on a bowel regimen to encourage bowel movement. His Foley was removed on post-operative day 1. 

The patient's temperature was closely monitored for signs of infection post-operatively, but he remained afebrile throughout his stay. He received subcutaneous heparin during his stay and was encouraged to ambulate as early as possible. 

Upon discharge, the patient was doing well, afebrile with stable vital signs, tolerating a regular diet, ambulating, voiding without assistance, and his pain was well controlled. His discharge diagnosis was recurrent ventral hernia and redundant pannus. His mental status was clear and coherent, and he was alert and interactive. He was discharged home with instructions for personal care and activity restrictions.

The patient is a male with a history of recurrent hernias. He was admitted for a ventral hernia repair procedure, which was performed via suprapubic posterior component separation and mesh. The procedure was successful and the patient's postoperative course was uneventful. His pain was well managed and he was able to ambulate and tolerate a regular diet. The patient expressed a desire to return home. 

The patient has a known allergy to pollen extracts and ragweed. No medications upon admission were mentioned in the clinical note. 

The patient's discharge diagnosis was a ventral hernia. Upon discharge, his mental status was clear and coherent. He was instructed to leave the clear dressing on as discussed with his doctor. No abnormal symptoms or test results were mentioned in the clinical note. 

No family history was provided in the clinical note. No conditions or symptoms were ruled out as per the clinical note. The patient was discharged to his home and was scheduled for a follow-up appointment in the clinic.

The patient, a female with a history of hypercholesterolemia, allergic rhinitis, anxiety, and headaches, was admitted due to uterine prolapse and cystocele. She reported palpable prolapse that interfered with sitting and sexual intercourse, and was dissatisfied with her quality of life despite using a pessary during exercise. She denied any occult incontinence with the pessary. She also reported vaginal pressure and constipation, for which she uses Metamucil. She is sexually active and does not experience dyspareunia or vaginal dryness. 

Her past surgical history includes a pubovaginal sling with Gore-Tex nonperforating, cataracts, abdominoplasty, and a possible lap BTL. She is postmenopausal and denies post-menopausal bleeding. Her family history is unremarkable for breast, ovarian, or colon cancer.

During her stay, she underwent a robotic-assisted total laparoscopic hysterectomy, bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy, sacrocolpopexy, and cystoscopy. Her post-operative course was uncomplicated, with pain managed by IV dilaudid and toradol initially, then transitioned to oral oxycodone, ibuprofen, and acetaminophen. She was able to tolerate a regular diet, void spontaneously, and ambulate independently. 

Upon admission, she was on fluticasone, meclizine 12.5mg as needed for dizziness, simvastatin 20mg daily, and cetirizine as needed. No abnormal lab or test results were reported during her stay. She was discharged in stable condition with a diagnosis of pelvic organ prolapse.

The male patient, with a past medical history of chronic lower back pain and hypertension, presented for evaluation after an abnormal MRI. He reported experiencing severe headaches, described as 'the worst headache of his life,' for about a week, which he said improved with caffeine and aspirin. These headaches were bilateral, frontal, and associated with tinnitus. He denied vision changes, unilateral symptoms, neck pain, temporary vision loss, slurred vision, or difficulty with ambulation or ADLs. He also reported a productive cough, hoarse throat, and a globus sensation when swallowing. He denied rhinorrhea, fevers/chills, nausea/emesis, chest pain, shortness of breath, diarrhea/constipation, back pain, abdominal pain, and change in appetite. 

He had previously been diagnosed with a sinus infection by his primary care physician and was started on antibiotics. However, when his condition did not improve, he was re-evaluated and a non-contrast MRI was performed. The MRI raised concerns for bilateral carotid dissection, leading to his referral to the ED. A CTA of the head and neck in the ED confirmed the bilateral dissections, and vascular surgery was consulted for recommendations. 

The patient's physical exam on discharge was largely unremarkable, with no signs of Horner's syndrome, and his vitals were stable. His blood work showed abnormal RBC, Hgb, and Hct values on multiple occasions, as well as elevated glucose levels. His PTT was also consistently elevated. Imaging studies revealed bilateral internal carotid artery dissections, mild parenchymal volume loss, evidence of chronic small vessel ischemic disease, and a 1.5 cm hepatic lesion likely to be a hemangioma. 

During his hospital stay, the patient's headaches were well controlled with acetaminophen and ibuprofen. His blood pressure was initially controlled with hydralazine IV PRN and labetalol 100mg BID, and was later adequately controlled with a regimen of labetolol 100 mg and 12.5 mg of chlorthalidone. He was placed on a heparin drip that was then transitioned to xarelto for prophylaxis. 

Upon admission, the patient was on Atorvastatin 10mg, Aspirin 81mg, and Flonase. His discharge diagnosis was bilateral carotid arteries dissection. His mental status was clear and coherent, and he was alert, interactive, and ambulatory independently.

The male patient was admitted to the orthopedic service due to left knee pain. His medical history includes Obstructive Sleep Apnea (OSA), headaches, tonsillectomy, abdominal hernia repair, and right total hip arthroplasty. He has a family history of cancer and osteoporosis. Upon admission, he was taking Acetaminophen 325 mg for pain and Celecoxib 100 mg twice daily.

The patient underwent a total knee arthroplasty on his left knee due to osteoarthritis that had failed conservative management. The surgery was uncomplicated and the patient tolerated the procedure well. Postoperatively, he required a sciatic block for pain control in the Post Anesthesia Care Unit (PACU). His pain was initially managed with a Patient-Controlled Analgesia (PCA) pump, which was later transitioned to oral pain medications. He also received Lovenox for Deep Vein Thrombosis (DVT) prophylaxis. His surgical dressing was changed on the second postoperative day and the incision was found to be clean and intact without any signs of infection. 

The patient's lab results showed abnormal values for Red Blood Cell (RBC) count at 3.17 and 3.42, Hemoglobin (Hgb) at 10.4 and 11.4, Hematocrit (Hct) at 32.5 and 34.7, Mean Corpuscular Volume (MCV) at 102, Mean Corpuscular Hemoglobin (MCH) at 32.9 and 33.2, and Glucose at 120 and 111. 

By the time of discharge, the patient was tolerating a regular diet, was afebrile with stable vital signs, and his pain was adequately controlled on an oral regimen. His hematocrit was acceptable and the operative extremity was neurovascularly intact with a benign wound. He was discharged home in stable condition with the diagnosis of osteoarthritis in the left knee. His activity status was ambulatory but required assistance or aid.

The patient, a male, was admitted to Urology due to a right ureteral tumor identified as urothelial carcinoma. His chief complaints were high-grade superficial papillary urothelial carcinoma and gross hematuria. He underwent a cystoscopy, removal of right ureteral stent, right robotic-assisted laparoscopic nephrectomy with total ureterectomy and excision of bladder cuff, and instillation of intravesical chemotherapy. 

His past medical history includes a hernia repair with mesh, which resulted in pain and swelling in the testicle, leading to a subsequent varicocelectomy and epididymal surgery. He continues to experience pain and swelling of the testicle, which worsens with extended periods of sitting and exercise. He also reports swelling around the mesh. He has nocturia, which is unchanged, and denies significant feelings of incomplete emptying. Other past medical conditions include osteoarthritis, basal cell carcinoma, diverticulosis, Duputren's contracture, glucose intolerance, hypercholesterolemia, hypertension, obstructive sleep apnea, prostatic hypertrophy, and depression. His surgical history includes bilateral inguinal herniorrhaphy, scrotal varicocelectomy, tonsillectomy, total hip replacement on the right, and total left knee replacement. He has allergies to sulfasalazine and seasonal allergies.

His family history is positive for cancer and osteoporosis, but negative for nephrolithiasis, malignant hyperthermia, renal cell carcinoma, testicular carcinoma, prostate carcinoma, and bladder carcinoma. His father died of a suspected liver cancer.

Upon physical examination, his abdomen was soft and tender along incisions, with no reported calf pain to deep palpation. His incisions were clean, dry, and intact, with no evidence of hematoma collection or infection. 

Lab results showed abnormal values for RBC (3.55), Hgb (11.3), Hct (35.4), MCV (100), MCHC (31.9), and glucose (121), and Creat (1.4). 

Upon admission, his medications included Acetaminophen, Atorvastatin, Cephalexin, Diazepam, DULoxetine, Lisinopril, and Tamsulosin. His hospital course was unremarkable, and he was discharged in stable condition, eating well, ambulating independently, and with pain control on oral analgesics. He was given explicit instructions to follow-up in clinic in for trial of void in about one week.

The patient, a surgical tech, was admitted to the OB/GYN service for a recurrent rectocele, a condition she noticed a year after her initial posterior repair. She reported difficulty emptying her rectum, needing to splint, and straining to achieve full defecation. However, she denied constipation or hard stool. A defogram revealed a moderate anterior rectocele measuring approximately 4 cm with only partial emptying at the end of the study, normal contraction and relaxation of puborectalis muscle, and no paradoxical puborectalis function. She also reported some stress-type incontinence, leakage with coughing and sneezing, but found it not bothersome. No other pelvic floor issues were reported.

Her past medical history includes DVT and superficial thrombophlebitis after vein ablation surgery, kidney stones, GERD, hypercholesterolemia, disc herniation, low back pain, hypothyroidism, depression, and anxiety. She has undergone several surgeries including tonsillectomy, anterior disc fusion at C6-C7, right carpal tunnel release, laparoscopic cholecystectomy, laparoscopic appendectomy, posterior colporrhaphy, cesarean section, and laparoscopic supracervical hysterectomy for fibroids. She has had four pregnancies, two of which resulted in live births, one through vaginal delivery and one through cesarean section. The largest baby she delivered vaginally weighed 9.6 pounds. No forceps or vacuum were used.

The patient's family history includes a maternal grandmother with breast cancer and a brother with colon cancer. On physical examination, she was found to be well developed, well groomed, and overweight. Her abdomen was soft and nontender with scars from prior surgery. Her external genitalia was normal with no lesions or discharge. Her vagina revealed good support, and her cervix was normal. No mass or tenderness of the adnexal area was noted.

During her hospital stay, she underwent a posterior repair for recurrent rectocele and defactory dysfunction. Her post-operative course was uncomplicated, with pain controlled by IV Dilaudid/Toradol. On post-operative day 1, her urine output was adequate, she voided spontaneously, and her diet was advanced without difficulty. She was transitioned to PO codeine/ibuprofen/acetaminophen. She had pneumoboots in place due to her history of provoked DVT and was on O2 monitoring for her OSA. By post-operative day 1, she was tolerating a regular diet, voiding spontaneously, ambulating independently, and her pain was controlled with oral medications. She was then discharged home in stable condition with outpatient follow-up scheduled.

Upon admission, she was on Levothyroxine Sodium 75 mcg PO DAILY, Venlafaxine XR 225 mg PO DAILY, and Pantoprazole 40 mg PO Q12H.

The patient, a female, was admitted to the orthopedics service due to worsening neck and arm pain. After a thorough review of her medical history, physical examination, and radiographic studies, it was determined that she would be a good candidate for Anterior Cervical Discectomy and Fusion (ACDF) from C5-T1. The patient agreed to the plan and consent was obtained. 

Her past medical history includes deep vein thrombosis (DVT) and superficial thrombophlebitis following vein ablation surgery, kidney stones, gastroesophageal reflux disease (GERD), hypercholesterolemia, disc herniation, low back pain, hypothyroidism, depression, and anxiety. She has undergone several surgical procedures in the past, including tonsillectomy, anterior disc fusion at C6-C7, right carpal tunnel release, laparoscopic cholecystectomy, laparoscopic appendectomy, posterior colporrhaphy, cesarean section, and laparoscopic supracervical hysterectomy for fibroids. 

The patient's family history includes a maternal grandmother with breast cancer and a brother with colon cancer. 

On physical examination, the patient was well developed, well-nourished, and alert. Her cervical spine range of motion was somewhat limited due to pain. She was ambulating well without assistance and wearing an aspen collar for support. Gross motor examination revealed good strength in the bilateral upper extremities. Sensation was intact throughout all affected dermatomes. The anterior cervical incision was clean, dry, and intact without signs of infection. The patient was voiding well without a foley catheter and tolerating a po diet without complaints of dysphagia.

Lab results showed an elevated white blood cell count (WBC-10.4*) and a slightly low mean corpuscular hemoglobin concentration (MCHC-31.7*). 

The patient's surgery was performed without complications and she was transferred to the PACU in a stable condition. Postoperative pain was initially controlled with IV pain medication and she was transitioned to oral pain medication when she was able to swallow safely and tolerate a PO diet. Physical therapy was consulted for mobilization and an Aspen collar was provided for support and stability. 

Upon admission, the patient was taking Fenofibrate 160 mg PO daily, Multivitamins 1 TAB PO daily, Pantoprazole 40 mg PO Q12H, MethylPHENIDATE (Ritalin) 20 mg PO daily, Venlafaxine XR 225 mg PO daily, and Levothyroxine Sodium 75 mcg PO daily. 

The patient was discharged with a diagnosis of cervical disc degeneration and spondylosis. She was alert, interactive, and able to ambulate independently. She was instructed not to lift anything greater than 10 lbs for 2 weeks and to resume her normal home medications. She was also given additional medications to control her post-operative pain.

The patient, a female, was admitted due to a small bowel obstruction. She had been experiencing left upper quadrant abdominal pain for approximately 16 hours, along with nausea and dry heaving. She had a past surgical history of an appendectomy and hysterectomy. Her past medical history includes sarcoidosis, hypertension, type 2 diabetes (diet controlled), wet macular degeneration, irritable bowel syndrome (mostly diarrhea), and a history of diverticulitis. She has no family history of small bowel obstruction. 

Upon admission, her vitals were stable, but her abdomen was soft, moderately distended, and tender, particularly in the left upper quadrant. Her bowel sounds were hypoactive. Lab results showed a white blood cell count of 10 (82% PMNs). A CT scan of her abdomen with contrast suggested a mechanical small bowel obstruction in the mid to distal small bowel in the right lower quadrant, likely due to adhesions. There was also a small amount of adjacent free fluid and relative thickening of the wall of the involved bowel, raising concerns for possible small bowel ischemia. However, there was no evidence of perforation. 

During her hospital stay, she was treated conservatively with bowel rest, a nasogastric tube, and intravenous fluids. A small bowel follow-through showed contrast passing to the ascending colon. Her nasogastric tube was discontinued after she had a return of bowel function, with flatus and multiple bowel movements. She was able to tolerate a regular diet without abdominal distention or pain and was passing gas and having bowel movements. 

Her medications upon admission included losartan, pravastatin, ASA, niacin, calcium, melatonin, alendronate, and eylea injection. 

Abnormal lab results included a hematocrit of 45.7, glucose of 121, bicarbonate of 21, white blood cell count of 10.5, and RDWSD of 48.5. 

Upon discharge, she was without pain, nausea, or vomiting, and her abdomen was nontender and nondistended. Her mental status was clear and coherent, and she was alert and interactive. She was discharged home with instructions to manage constipation and to resume all regular home medications.

The patient, a female, was admitted to the orthopedics service with chief complaints of lower back and leg pain. She has a past medical history of depression and migraines, and has previously undergone a cyst removal procedure. She has no known family history of any significant medical conditions. She is allergic to amoxicillin and erythromycin base. 

Upon physical examination, the patient was found to be well developed, well nourished, and alert and oriented. She had limited range of motion in the lumbar spine due to pain, but was ambulating well with a lumbar corset brace for support. Gross motor examination revealed good strength in the bilateral lower extremities, and sensation was intact throughout all affected dermatomes. The anterior and posterior lumbar incisions were clean, dry, and intact without any signs of infection or inflammation. The patient was voiding well without a foley catheter.

The patient underwent an anterior and posterior L5-S1 fusion procedure due to her worsening back and leg pain. The procedure was performed without complications and the patient tolerated it well. Postoperative pain was managed with a dilaudid PCA and a bupivicaine epidural pain catheter. The patient remained NPO until bowel function returned, after which her diet was gradually advanced. The foley catheter was removed on the second postoperative day, and the patient was able to void. A hemovac drain that was placed at the time of surgery was also removed on the second postoperative day. A lumbar corset brace was fitted for comfort and support, and physical therapy was consulted for mobilization out of bed to ambulate.

Lab results revealed abnormal values for WBC (14.7), RBC (3.44), Hgb (10.2), Hct (31.4), and UreaN (5), and Calcium (8.3). However, the patient's postoperative labs were grossly stable. 

Upon discharge, the patient was diagnosed with lytic spondylolisthesis L5-S1. Her mental status was clear and coherent, and she was alert and interactive. She was ambulatory at the time of discharge. The patient was instructed not to lift anything greater than 10 lbs for 2 weeks, to walk for a certain amount of time each day, and to change positions every 45 minutes. She was also given instructions on how to care for her wound and when to call the office. She was advised to resume her normal home medications and was given additional medications to control her post-operative pain.

The patient is a female with a history of obesity, anxiety, migraines, cyclical nausea and vomiting, and chronic low back pain. She has undergone an Anterior Lumbar Interbody Fusion (ALIF) L5-S1 and posterior decompression and fusion L5-S1. She also had a tonsillectomy recently. The patient presented with worsening pain and difficulty swallowing, which started after her discharge from the tonsillectomy. She reported coughing up any oral intake and had liquid spewing out of her nose after attempted swallow with some coughing afterwards. She denied any significant sore throat or odynophagia limiting oral intake. She endorsed fever to 101 and self presented to the emergency department (ED).

In the ED, her vitals were recorded and she was given various medications including IVF 1000 mL NS, IV Acetaminophen IV 1000 mg, IV Ketorolac, PO Lidocaine Viscous 2%, IVF 20 mEq Potassium Chloride / 1000 mL D5NS, and IV Levofloxacin 750 mg. Her labs showed an elevated WBC count of 24.9 and normal Sodium and Creatinine levels. Her chest X-ray showed focal right midlung opacity with less well-defined hazy left mid to lower lung opacities. A CT scan of her neck with contrast showed no fluid collection or peritonsillar abscess and bilateral upper lobe regions of patchy consolidation worrisome for pneumonia.

The patient was consulted by the ENT for dysphagia. The scope was unremarkable for structural issue contributing to dysphagia. No urgent interventions were required. She was recommended for a PO challenge and if failed and needs admission, will need speech and swallow evaluation.

Her physical exam showed no significant findings. Her discharge exam was also unremarkable. Her lab results showed abnormal levels of glucose (104), sodium (129), potassium (9.3), chloride (93), anion gap (23), and potassium (6.2). Her WBC count was elevated at 24.9. Her neutrophils, lymphs, monos, and eos were also elevated. Her platelet count was 352.

The patient was diagnosed with dysphagia and pneumonia. She was treated for community-acquired pneumonia with Levaquin. Her anxiety and depression were managed with Ativan and she was advised to resume her sertraline on discharge. Her migraine was managed with Propranolol LA 80 mg PO DAILY on discharge.

Upon admission, the patient was on several medications including ALPRAZolam, Diazepam, Docusate Sodium, Morphine SR, Ogestrel, Omeprazole, OxycoDONE, Propranolol LA, and Senna. The patient was discharged home with clear and coherent mental status, alert and interactive level of consciousness, and independent ambulatory activity status. She was instructed to continue a diet of soft food with nectar thickened liquids and follow up with the swallowing team. She was also advised to continue antibiotics for one additional day for her pneumonia.

The patient, a female, was admitted to the Otolaryngology Head and Neck Surgery service due to a post-tonsillectomy hemorrhage. She had undergone a tonsillectomy 10 days prior and started experiencing high volume bleeding an hour before admission. The patient was able to protect her airway despite the ongoing bleeding. She had a recent admission for pneumonia with possible reactive airway disease. There is no known bleeding predisposition in the patient or her family. 

Her past medical history includes depression and migraines, and she has previously undergone a cyst removal. Her family history is noncontributory. On physical examination, the cauterized area of the glossotonsillar sulcus was dry with no signs of further bleeding. Her breathing was unlabored and she was alert and conversant.

Abnormal lab results included a glucose level of 113 and a white blood cell count of 12.4. The patient was taken to the operating room for control of the post-tonsillectomy hemorrhage, where appropriate hemostasis was achieved. Post-operation, she reported good pain control with oral oxycodone and acetaminophen, and was able to tolerate a soft diet. There were no signs of further bleeding.

Upon admission, there were no changes to her medications. Her discharge diagnosis was post-tonsillectomy bleeding. At the time of discharge, her mental status was at baseline and she was able to ambulate. She was instructed to rest, walk, use an ice pack on her throat, use a humidifier or vaporizer for comfort, drink plenty of fluids, and restart omeprazole following discharge. She was also given instructions for managing mild and strong pain. She was discharged home and was scheduled to follow up in the clinic within one week of discharge.

The patient, a female, was admitted due to right lower extremity ischemia and gangrene in her right toe. She had been experiencing worsening toe ulcerations and pain for over a week. Her past medical history includes type 2 diabetes, congestive heart failure, atrial fibrillation, and chronic obstructive pulmonary disease. She has allergies to Morphine and Ativan. 

During her hospital stay, she underwent several procedures. A diagnostic angiogram was performed on her right lower extremity. This was followed by a right femoral-below knee popliteal bypass with NRSVG. She also had her right toe amputated due to gangrene. 

Her hospital course was complicated by an elevated INR, which led to the rescheduling of her angiogram. She was given FFP to address this. Post-angiogram labs were stable. She was also prepped and consented for the OR in the morning. After her bypass surgery, she was placed on bedrest for the appropriate time and then transferred back to the 5 VICU. She underwent a right second toe amputation on POD5. 

Upon admission, she was on several medications including Coumadin, Celexa, Lisinopril, Verapamil, Neurontin, Metformin, Lasix, Amiodarone, and Lantus. 

Her discharge diagnoses included right lower extremity ischemia, right toe gangrene, anemia (acute on chronic, required blood transfusions), congestive heart failure (no documented EF, stable continued home dose Lasix), and a history of diabetes, congestive heart failure, atrial fibrillation, and chronic obstructive pulmonary disease. 

At the time of discharge, she was clear and coherent, alert and interactive, but required assistance or aid for ambulation. She was discharged to an extended care facility.

The patient, a female, was admitted due to changes in her mental status. She had recently undergone a right toe amputation and Fem Pop bypass and was recuperating in a nursing home where she was being treated for a chronic infection. During her stay, she experienced an acute episode of altered mental status, including momentary garbled speech. She was suspected to have either delirium post-infection or multiple transient ischemic attacks (TIAs). She was transferred to the hospital for further evaluation. 

Her past medical history includes peripheral vascular disease (PVD) post Fem Pop bypass and toe amputation, type 2 diabetes mellitus (DM2), congestive heart failure (CHF), atrial fibrillation (A-Fib), chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD), and bilateral carotid stenosis. She has allergies to Morphine and Ativan. Her medications upon admission included Coumadin, Celexa, Lisinopril, Verapamil, Neurontin, Metformin, Lasix, Amiodarone, and Lantus.

During her physical examination, necrotic changes were noted in her right lower extremity toes and a wound on her shin. Her left lower extremity had a blister-like lesion on the shin. Her lab results showed abnormal values for red blood cell count (3.08), hemoglobin (9.4), hematocrit (27.8), and RDW (16.0). Her bicarbonate level was also elevated (33). 

She underwent a left carotid endarterectomy due to concerns for TIAs in the setting of bilateral carotid stenosis. Post-procedure, she became unresponsive and needed to be intubated. She was transferred to the ICU and placed on a Neo drip to maintain her systolic blood pressure above 150 mmHg. She was extubated the following day and was alert, oriented, and neurologically intact. She received a blood transfusion due to a drop in her hematocrit level from 27 to 21.4 and was given Lasix. She also had episodes of wheezing, which resolved with Albuterol nebs. 

Her discharge diagnosis included bilateral carotid stenosis with TIAs, anemia (acute on chronic), and a history of PVD post Fem Pop bypass and toe amputation, DM2, CHF, A-Fib, and COPD. Her mental status upon discharge was clear and coherent, but she required assistance with ambulation.

The patient is a female with a significant medical history including a remote left subinsular stroke, atrial fibrillation (managed with coumadin), hypertension, hyperlipidemia, type II diabetes, peripheral vascular disease (PVD) with a history of right femoral-popliteal bypass and left carotid endarterectomy, chronic heart failure (CHF), chronic anemia, chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD), and a recent urinary tract infection (UTI) and acute renal failure. She has also undergone bilateral cataract surgery and a right second toe amputation. 

She presented with fluctuating difficulty in finding words for the past two days and dizziness. She denies any weakness, tremors, abnormal movements, or vision problems. She also reported shortness of breath and a productive cough in recent days. She had a head CT scan that showed no acute intracranial abnormality. Her INR was 2.6, CO2 of 31 with chloride of 97, creatinine of 1.2 with BUN of 25, and albumin of 3.1. Her WBC was 3.8, HCT 36, and PLT 374. Her BP was within normal limits. 

In the ER, her initial vital signs were stable. Her WBC was 9.2 with a normal differential and lactate of 1. Her urine analysis showed positive nitrates, WBC >50, and many bacteria. Urine culture was sent. Her BMP was within normal limits and INR was 2.5. ECG showed NSR without signs of ischemia. She received a total of 1 L NS. 

A stroke code was initially called but was cancelled when it was realized that her symptoms had been ongoing for two days. A CT head and neck did not show any new strokes. Neurology was consulted and suggested a possible watershed infarct, but was also concerned about a hypotensive episode. 

Her father died of a myocardial infarction and her mother died of pneumonia at a young age. There is no family history of cancer or stroke. 

Upon discharge, her primary diagnoses were UTI and hypotension. Her secondary diagnoses included atrial fibrillation, CHF, diabetes, hypertension, COPD, and PVD. She was alert and oriented, and able to ambulate short distances with assistance. She was instructed to continue her antibiotics and her coumadin dose was lowered to 1mg. When she has finished antibiotics, she can resume her home dose of 2mg daily.

The patient, a homeless male, was admitted due to a worsening productive cough and fever. He was initially observed in the ER, where an early left lower lobe pneumonia was detected on a chest X-ray. He was then given ceftriaxone/azithromycin and admitted to the floor. The patient reported a slowly worsening productive cough, myalgias, weakness, orthostasis, mild shortness of breath, and watery diarrhea. He denied abdominal pain, nausea/vomiting, and hemoptysis. His maximum temperature in the ER was 101.6. 

His past medical history includes Hepatitis C, which he could not tolerate interferon treatment for due to nausea a few years ago. He is being set up with a hepatologist by his primary care physician. He also had alcohol-related pancreatitis six months ago, since which he has significantly reduced his alcohol consumption. His family history is non-contributory.

Upon physical examination, his vital signs were stable and he was alert and oriented. His chest was clear to auscultation bilaterally, with no tactile fremitus or egophony. His abdomen was soft, non-tender, non-distended, with no masses or organomegaly. His extremities were warm, well perfused, with no cyanosis, clubbing, or edema. Neurologically, he was alert and oriented, with grossly normal findings.

Lab results showed an elevated white blood cell count of 14.4 and a decreased red blood cell count of 4.56. Neutrophils were elevated at 82.4 and lymphocytes were decreased at 12.1. Lipase was also elevated at 99. A chest X-ray showed opacity at the left lower lung, concerning for early pneumonia given the patient's clinical symptoms, and a granuloma at the right base.

The patient was diagnosed with community-acquired pneumonia, which improved with levofloxacin. He was afebrile and saturating well on room air prior to discharge. He was not on any medications upon admission.

The patient, a male with a history of alcohol-induced cirrhosis, presented with coffee ground emesis, maroon stools, and a significant drop in hemoglobin. He had previously quit alcohol and had a MELD score of 20. He had known esophageal varices that were recently banded. The patient was previously doing well at rehab until the day prior to admission when he began to feel generally unwell. On the morning of admission, he was noted to be very confused with an episode of coffee ground emesis and maroon stool. His hemoglobin was 5.7 on presentation (8.5 on prior discharge), 7.6 prior to transfer. His INR was 1.7, and creatinine was 2.2 (baseline 2.1). His ALT, AST, total bilirubin were within normal limits, but his albumin was 2.3. An upper endoscopy was nondiagnostic, and he received a total of 5 units of blood. The source of bleeding was unclear. He was transferred for further evaluation and consideration for a liver transplant.

Upon arrival to the MICU, the patient was awake, alert, and oriented. His abdomen was distended but not tender to palpation. His past medical history includes a first episode in this hospital when he was found at home in a stuporous state surrounded by bottles of vodka. Since then, he has had his varices ligated twice and had a paracentesis which showed a transudate and no evidence of malignancy. His family history includes a father who died from diabetes and a mother whose cause of death is unknown. He has two brothers and a sister, all of whom are living and well.

The patient's physical exam on admission and discharge was largely unremarkable, with the exception of a distended abdomen, spider angiomata on the anterior chest, and bilateral gynecomastia. His admission labs showed abnormal RBC, HGB, HCT, MCH, RDW, PTT, Urea N, Creat, Total CO2, Phosphate, and AST. His discharge labs showed abnormal WBC, RBC, Hgb, Hct, RDW, PTT, UreaN, Creat, Cl, HCO3, and Albumin.

During his hospital stay, the patient was started on PPI BID and ceftriaxone, and prepared for a scope the following morning. Upper scope revealed multiple gastric ulcers but no obvious brisk source of bleed. Flexible sigmoidoscopy was unremarkable. He was started on nadolol, continued on PPI and carafate. His home medications were resumed, with nadolol usage instead of atenolol, his IV proton pump inhibitor was transitioned to oral, antibiotics changed from ceftriaxone to ciprofloxacin to complete five day course. He was also maintained on rifaximin 550 PO BID and lactulose. He did not require product while inpatient.

The patient's medications on admission included Atenolol, Cyanocobalamin, Ferrous GLUCONATE, Furosemide, Isosorbide Mononitrate (Extended Release), Lactobacillus acidoph-L. bifid, Omeprazole, Rifaximin, and Spironolactone. His discharge diagnoses were gastrointestinal bleed (stable), encephalopathy (improved), and acute kidney injury on chronic kidney disease (resolved). His mental status was clear and coherent, and he was ambulatory but required assistance or aid.

The patient is a male with a history of stage IV Chronic Kidney Disease (CKD) and likely cirrhosis due to alcohol use, complicated by esophageal varices post banding, encephalopathy, and ascites. He was transferred for ongoing anemia and encephalopathy. The patient has been hospitalized four times in the past month for anemia and encephalopathy, receiving a total of 14 transfusions. During the most recent hospitalization, he received 2 units of packed red blood cells (pRBC) with hemoglobin at 7.2 upon transfer. His encephalopathy improved prior to transfer and he was placed back on regular dose lactulose of 30cc QID. 

Upon arrival, the patient denied any pain including abdominal pain, urinary symptoms, or dyspnea. He believes his confusion has improved. He denies any hematemesis or black, tarry stool. His last episode of black stool was a few months ago. His last alcoholic drink was in ___. 

The patient's father had diabetes and his mother had congestive heart failure. There is no family history of liver disease or GI malignancy. 

On physical examination, the patient was alert, oriented, and in no acute distress. He had good attention and memory, was able to count days of the week and year backwards, and able to recall home address and directions. He had mild asterixis. His sclera was anicteric, oropharynx clear, neck supple, and no lymphadenopathy was noted. He had a regular heart rate and rhythm, normal S1 + S2, and a systolic murmur best heard in the left sternal border. His lungs had crackles in the right base. His abdomen was soft, non-tender, distended, with bowel sounds present and a positive fluid shift. His extremities were warm, well perfused, with 2+ pulses, no clubbing, cyanosis or edema. His cranial nerves II-XII were intact, with normal strength in upper/lower extremities, grossly normal sensation, 2+ reflexes bilaterally, and gait deferred.

Abnormal lab results on admission included RBC-2.31*, Hgb-7.5*, Hct-21.8*, MCH-32.6*, RDW-16.2*, PTT-39.0*, UreaN-46*, Creat-2.4*, HCO3-21*, AST-54*, and Albumin-2.7*. Abnormal lab results on discharge included RBC-2.68*, Hgb-8.8*, Hct-25.0*, MCH-32.8*, MCHC-35.2*, RDW-17.0*, PTT-40.9*, UreaN-30*, Creat-2.1*, Na-129*, ALT-45*, AST-78*, calTIBC-192*, Ferritn-1788*, and TRF-148*. 

Imaging studies revealed grade II varices at the distal esophagus, fresh blood and clots in the antrum of the stomach, severe portal hypertensive gastropathy, and normal mucosa in the duodenum. A renal ultrasound showed normal size kidneys, a simple right renal cyst, and no hydronephrosis, stone, or suspicious solid mass. A chest X-ray showed clear lungs. A transthoracic echocardiogram (TTE) showed mild symmetric left ventricular hypertrophy with normal cavity size and regional/global systolic function, normal right ventricular chamber size and free wall motion, normal diameters of aorta at the sinus, ascending and arch levels, mildly thickened aortic valve leaflets with no aortic stenosis or regurgitation, structurally normal mitral valve with trivial mitral regurgitation and no mitral valve prolapse, normal estimated pulmonary artery systolic pressure, and no pericardial effusion. An MRI of the liver showed a cirrhotic appearing liver without suspicious focal lesion, portal hypertension with splenomegaly and portosystemic shunts, moderate abdominal ascites, large left and small right pleural effusions, cholelithiasis, and signal drop within the liver, spleen and bone marrow on in phase imaging, compatible with iron deposition and hemosiderosis. 

The patient underwent a Transjugular Intrahepatic Portosystemic Shunt (TIPS) procedure and embolization of the blood supply to the antrum due to refractory bleeding. He did not have recurrent clinical bleeding and his hemoglobin remained stable. His creatinine improved with albumin and with holding diuretics. His cirrhosis was managed with lactulose and rifaximin, and diuretics were initially held but restarted prior to discharge. His sodium levels were downtrending on discharge (129 from 132) so both lasix and aldactone were held on discharge. He had transient bradycardia to the ___ (isolated to the ___ which resolved without intervention. 

The patient's medications on admission included Furosemide 40 mg PO BID, Rifaximin 550 mg PO BID, Spironolactone 25 mg PO DAILY, Ciprofloxacin HCl 500 mg PO Q24H, Lactulose 30 mL PO Q6H:PRN HE, Nadolol 40 mg PO DAILY, Pantoprazole 40 mg PO Q12H, Cyanocobalamin 100 mcg PO DAILY, and Ferrous GLUCONATE 324 mg PO BID. His discharge medications included Cyanocobalamin 100 mcg PO DAILY, Pantoprazole 40 mg PO Q12H, Rifaximin 550 mg PO BID, Sucralfate 1 gm PO QID, and Lactulose 30 mL PO QID. 

The patient's primary diagnoses on discharge were acute blood loss anemia and portal gastropathy. His secondary diagnoses were alcoholic cirrhosis and acute kidney injury. The patient was discharged to extended care.

The patient, a male of undisclosed age, was admitted due to anemia. He has a medical history of stage IV chronic kidney disease (CKD) and cirrhosis, likely due to alcohol use. His cirrhosis has been complicated by esophageal varices, encephalopathy, and ascites. He has previously undergone banding for the esophageal varices and a Transjugular Intrahepatic Portosystemic Shunt (TIPS) procedure. During this admission, the patient underwent a TIPS upsizing. 

The patient presented with a hemoglobin level of 5.9 and hematocrit of 19.5, but reported no complaints. He denied experiencing black or bloody stools, chest pain, difficulty breathing, lightheadedness, abdominal pain, fever, nausea, or vomiting. He had a 5-point hematocrit drop over three days. He was recently admitted for ongoing anemia and encephalopathy and was found to have portal gastropathy. He required multiple transfusions and is being worked up for a liver transplant. 

Upon arrival to the floor, the patient reported feeling comfortable and denied recent fever, cough, headache, chest pain, shortness of breath, abdominal pain, nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, or hematochezia. He noted that his belly was slightly more swollen than usual in the last few weeks but was not tender. 

The patient's family history includes diabetes in his father and congestive heart failure in his mother. There is no family history of liver disease or gastrointestinal malignancy. 

On physical examination, the patient was alert, oriented, and in no acute distress. His abdomen was soft, non-tender, and mildly distended. His vitals were stable. 

Admission labs showed abnormal values for WBC (3.7), RBC (2.17), Hgb (7.5), Hct (21.5), MCV (99), MCH (34.5), RDW (16.6), PTT (45.4), UreaN (21), Creat (1.7), AST (55), Albumin (2.6), Phos (4.6), and Lactate (2.4). Discharge labs showed abnormal values for WBC (3.8), RBC (2.17), Hgb (7.2), Hct (20.9), MCH (33.1), RDW (16.9), PTT (43.2), UreaN (22), and Creat (1.9). 

Imaging showed a patent TIPS with questionable narrowing and turbulent flow at the distal TIPS, which may be a technical artifact due to suboptimal angulation. 

During his hospital stay, the patient was treated for acute blood loss anemia, chronic kidney disease, and cirrhosis. He received two units of pRBCs and was started on an octreotide drip, IV PPI, and ceftriaxone for SBP prophylaxis. His TIPS was successfully upsized. His hemoglobin was 7.2 on the morning of discharge, so he was transfused an additional unit of pRBC. 

The patient's medications upon admission included Cyanocobalamin, Pantoprazole, Rifaximin, Sucralfate, Lactulose, and Ferrous Gluconate.

The patient, a male with a past medical history of stage IV chronic kidney disease and cirrhosis likely due to alcohol use, was admitted due to recurrent anemia. His cirrhosis is complicated by esophageal varices post banding, hepatic encephalopathy, portal gastropathy, and ascites. He had previously undergone a transjugular intrahepatic portosystemic shunt (TIPS) procedure. During this admission, he underwent an esophagogastroduodenoscopy (EGD), a TIPS revision, and a diagnostic paracentesis. 

The patient's anemia was treated with Octreotide, a proton pump inhibitor (PPI), Ceftriaxone (CTX), and two units of packed red blood cells (pRBCs). Despite this, he was found to have anemia on routine labs during his rehabilitation stay. He reported no symptoms such as melena, hematochezia, hemetemesis, shortness of breath, light-headedness, dizziness, abdominal pain, or chest pain. 

His family history includes diabetes in his father and congestive heart failure in his mother. There is no known family history of liver disease or gastrointestinal malignancy. 

Upon admission, his vital signs were stable and physical examination was largely unremarkable, with the exception of mild bilateral ankle edema. His discharge physical examination was also largely unremarkable. 

Lab results on admission showed abnormalities including low white blood cell count (3.1), low red blood cell count (2.14), low hemoglobin (6.9), low hematocrit (20.2), high mean corpuscular hemoglobin (32.1), high red cell distribution width (17.3), high partial thromboplastin time (43.9), high blood urea nitrogen (24), high creatinine (1.7), low chloride (110), and high aspartate aminotransferase (46). Ascites fluid analysis showed elevated white blood cell count (26), polymorphonuclear leukocytes (15), lymphocytes (30), monocytes (29), and macrophages (26). 

Imaging showed a decrease in the portosystemic gradient from 16 to 11 mm Hg after angioplasty of the TIPS stent. EGD showed fresh blood and clots in the gastric antrum, a mosaic appearance of gastric mucosa compatible with portal hypertensive gastropathy, and two cords of grade I varices without high-risk stigmata. 

During his hospital stay, the patient received 1 unit of PRBCs on admission and an additional unit prior to discharge. His hematocrit at discharge was 24.2. His TIPS was revised and upsized to 10cm. He showed no signs of worsening encephalopathy and was continued on home lactulose/rifaxamin. His diagnostic paracentesis was negative for spontaneous bacterial peritonitis. His creatinine remained at baseline during his hospitalization. 

His medications upon admission included Cyanocobalamin, Ferrous Gluconate, Lactulose, Rifaximin, Sucralfate, and Pantoprazole. 

Upon discharge, his primary diagnoses were portal gastropathy and alcoholic cirrhosis post TIPS, with a secondary diagnosis of chronic kidney disease. His mental status was clear and coherent, and he was alert and interactive. He was ambulatory and independent. He was discharged to extended care.

The patient, a male of undisclosed age, was admitted due to anemia. He has a medical history of stage IV Chronic Kidney Disease (CKD) and cirrhosis, likely due to alcohol use. The cirrhosis has been complicated by esophageal varices, hepatic encephalopathy, portal gastropathy, and ascites. He has undergone banding for the varices and a Transjugular Intrahepatic Portosystemic Shunt (TIPS) procedure. The patient has been experiencing recurrent anemia, with a recent admission where he presented with a Hematocrit (Hct) of 20.2 and received 2 units of packed red blood cells (PRBC). An Esophagogastroduodenoscopy (EGD) showed fresh blood, portal gastropathy, and grade I varices. The patient's Hct at discharge was 24.2. 

Upon this admission, the patient was feeling well with no complaints of lightheadedness, dizziness, chest pain, palpitations, shortness of breath, abdominal pain, nausea, or any signs of gastrointestinal bleeding. He did note cognitive slowness and difficulty with word finding over the past 6 months. Initial labs showed a Hemoglobin/Hematocrit (h/h) of 7.0/20.6, platelets of 159, INR of 1.3, Creatinine of 2.0, and mildly elevated Aspartate Aminotransferase (AST) of 46. A rectal exam showed guaiac negative stool. An ultrasound of the right upper quadrant (RUQ) showed a patent TIPS and moderate ascites. 

The patient's family history includes diabetes in his father and congestive heart failure in his mother. There is no family history of liver disease or gastrointestinal malignancy. 

On admission, the patient's physical exam was largely unremarkable, with the exception of a distended abdomen and diffuse dry flaky skin on the forehead and chin. On discharge, the patient was alert and oriented, with a pedunculated papule with telangiectasias on the left nasal area. 

Lab results showed several abnormal findings including low White Blood Cell (WBC) count of 3.6, low Red Blood Cell (RBC) count of 2.09, low Hemoglobin (Hgb) of 7.0, low Hematocrit (Hct) of 20.6, high Mean Corpuscular Volume (MCV) of 99, high Mean Corpuscular Hemoglobin (MCH) of 33.5, high Red Cell Distribution Width Standard Deviation (RDWSD) of 52.5, high Monocytes of 21.5, low Eosinophils of 0.0, high Partial Thromboplastin Time (PTT) of 45.1, high Glucose of 123, high Urea Nitrogen of 27, high Creatinine of 2.0, low Chloride of 109, high AST of 46, and high Calcium of 10.5. 

During his hospital stay, the patient's anemia was investigated. His Hematocrit dropped 4 points, but no overt signs of bleeding were found. He received 2 units of PRBC, and his Hemoglobin increased from 7.0 to 8.0. An EGD did not reveal a source of bleeding. His haptoglobin was less than 5, LDH was within normal limits, and Total Bilirubin was trending down, suggesting that he was not hemolyzing. His reticulocyte count of 1.8 and reticulocyte index of 0.5% suggested a decreased production problem. 

The patient's cirrhosis was managed without signs of acute decompensation. He was afebrile, had no abdominal pain, and showed mild encephalopathy without asterixis. He continued his home medications of rifaxamin and lactulose, and received prophylaxis for Spontaneous Bacterial Peritonitis (SBP) with ceftriaxone. 

The patient also had hypercalcemia, with a corrected Calcium of 10.9 on discharge. He was asymptomatic, although he had a 6 month history of confusion. His Parathyroid Hormone (PTH) was low at 12, and his Thyroid Stimulating Hormone (TSH) was normal at 3.3. 

The patient was diagnosed with seborrheic dermatitis, with dry flaky skin at the nasal bridge and chin. He was started on topical ketoconazole. He also had a pedunculated papule with telangiectasias on his left nasal area, suggestive of basal cell carcinoma. 

The patient's medications on admission included ferrous gluconate, lactulose, pantoprazole, rifaximin, sucralfate, and vitamin D. 

Upon discharge, the patient was diagnosed with anemia, hypercalcemia, cirrhosis, CKD, seborrheic dermatitis, and basal cell carcinoma. He was advised to follow up with hematology for further workup of his anemia, and with dermatology for evaluation of his left nasal lesion and seborrheic dermatitis. He was also advised to continue his hepatology appointments and to follow up for further evaluation of transplant eligibility.

The patient, a middle-aged woman, was admitted due to abdominal pain, specifically epigastric pain that has been present for a month. The pain is sharp, located in the epigastric area, and is associated with nausea, vomiting, and alternating diarrhea/constipation. She has not had fever/chills, hemetemasis, dysphagia, or odynophagia. The pain does not subside with food and is often accompanied by reflux after eating. She also reports right lower quadrant pain, which was previously evaluated by an OB/GYN and diagnosed as 'pelvic congestion syndrome'. 

Her past medical history includes chronic migraines, anxiety/depression, hypertension, uterine fibroids, and arteriovenous malformation (AVM) complicated by multiple cerebrovascular accidents (CVA). Her family history includes hypertension in both parents, thyroid cancer in her father, and multiple AVMs. 

Upon admission, her vital signs were stable. Physical examination revealed tenderness to touch in the epigastrum, mild basilar crackles bilaterally, and small areas of petechial appearance on her skin. Neurological and psychological evaluations were normal. 

Lab results showed an elevated glucose level of 103, an elevated alkaline phosphatase level of 115, and elevated neutrophils at 49.0. A CT scan of the abdomen and pelvis showed no acute intra-abdominal process, markedly abnormal enhancement of the liver with focal rounded areas of hyperdensity, a 1.5 cm left adrenal adenoma, dilated pelvic and gonadal veins, a small amount of pelvic free fluid, and a fibroid uterus. A transvaginal pelvic ultrasound confirmed the presence of a fibroid uterus and dilated pelvic veins. 

The patient's medications upon admission included cilostazol, clonazepam, cyclobenzaprine, famotidine, hydrochlorothiazide, hydromorphone, medroxyprogesterone acetate, ondansetron, phenytoin sodium extended, loratadine, and polyethylene glycol. 

During her hospital stay, her abdominal pain was managed with a GI cocktail and narcotics. Her other chronic conditions, including AVM complicated by CVA, anxiety, hypertension, and pelvic congestion syndrome, were managed with her home medications. 

Upon discharge, her primary diagnosis was dyspepsia with GERD, and her secondary diagnoses included arteriovenous malformation with stroke x3, anxiety, and hypertension. Her mental status was clear and coherent, and she was alert, interactive, and independently ambulatory.

The patient, a female, was admitted due to acute on chronic left leg pain and erythema. She has a past medical history of chronic abdominal pain, attributed to constipation and left renal vein compression, anxiety, depression, and prior arteriovenous malformations (AVMs) post resection/radiation. She also has a history of chronic leg pain with a negative outpatient workup. The patient's leg pain started at the beginning of the year, with associated leg swelling and patchy erythema of the lower legs. Initial treatment for cellulitis with cephalexin did not improve her condition. A biopsy showed pigmented purpuric dermatitis. She was given a course of Keflex for possible early cellulitis. In the emergency department, she was given 1g vancomycin, 25mg IV Benadryl, 8mg Zofran, 1mg Dilaudid, and 40mEq KCl. She was admitted to medicine for cellulitis failing PO antibiotics. 

The patient's physical exam on admission showed that her left lower extremity was swollen compared to the right up to mid-shin, with confluent erythema and warmth from toes to mid shin. She reported pain with light touch over both legs but left greater than right. On discharge, her left lower extremity had swelling most prominent over the ankle, but up to mid shin. The edema was nonpitting. Confluent erythema was mostly around ankles, which per report is much improved from admission exam. Sensation was decreased compared to the right leg. 

Lab results on admission showed a glucose level of 106*, potassium level of 3.2*, and chloride level of 92*. A lower extremity ultrasound showed no evidence of deep venous thrombosis in the left lower extremity veins. 

The patient's chronic abdominal pain was attributed to left renal vein compression seen on MRI. She was prescribed hydromorphone 2mg QID:prn for abdominal pain. She was also continued on clonazepam for anxiety, phenytoin for seizure prophylaxis due to prior AVMs, hydrochlorothiazide for hypertension, senna and colace for constipation, and famotidine for GERD. 

The patient's family history includes hypertension in both parents. Her social history was not provided in the note. 

Upon discharge, the patient was prescribed ClonazePAM 0.5-1 mg PO TID, Famotidine 40 mg PO BID, Hydrochlorothiazide 25 mg PO DAILY, HYDROmorphone (Dilaudid) 2 mg PO QID:PRN abdominal and leg pain, Lidocaine 5% Ointment 1 Appl TP BID:PRN leg pain, Ondansetron 4 mg PO BID:PRN nausea, Phenytoin Sodium Extended 100 mg PO BID, Polyethylene Glycol 17 g PO QHS:PRN constipation, Cephalexin 500 mg PO Q12H, and Lactulose ___ mL PO DAILY:PRN constipation > 4 days. 

The primary diagnosis upon discharge was left lower extremity pain. The patient was discharged home with clear and coherent mental status, alert and interactive level of consciousness, and independent ambulatory activity status.

The patient, a male with a past medical history of ADHD and recurrent right ear abscess, was admitted due to facial cellulitis. He had been experiencing facial lesions for a week, accompanied by fever and chills. A day prior to admission, he had opened one of the lesions on his right zygomatic arch, which resulted in the expression of yellow, thin pus. This was followed by more intense fever, joint pains, and headache, leading to his father taking him for evaluation. At the time, he was found to be febrile and tachycardic, with a WBC count of 14.2 and lactate of 1.7. A physician suggested facial cellulitis, and a plastic surgeon attempted to aspirate the lesions but was unsuccessful. He was given IV Unasyn, 2L IVF, and discharged home on Augmentin.

On the day of admission, the patient woke up with chills, headaches, and fever, leading to a CT scan with contrast which identified facial cellulitis and a congenital variant of cavernous sinus flow without thrombosis. He was then admitted for overnight observation with IV antibiotics. Upon arrival, he was afebrile and feeling much improved.

The patient's physical exam upon admission showed an erythematous 2x2cm circular lesion on the right zygomatic arch, non-fluctuant, non-indurated, as well as 3 papules on the face. He also had several 1cm papules with white pus at the center on his neck and midline of back. His discharge exam showed similar findings.

Admission labs showed abnormal WBC of 11.1, RBC of 4.14, Hgb of 12.7, and Hct of 38.6. Discharge labs showed abnormal RBC of 4.06, Hgb of 12.6, Hct of 38.4, and Phos of 5.2. All blood cultures were negative for growth. A CT scan showed improvement in facial cellulitis and incomplete filling of the right cavernous sinus, believed to be a normal variant.

During his hospital stay, the patient improved on Vancomycin and Clindamycin, with less subjective symptoms and resolved leukocytosis. He was eventually transitioned to Bactrim and Cephalexin prior to discharge. He is to complete a 10-day course of oral antibiotics once discharged. 

The patient's medications upon admission were Fluoxetine 20 mg PO daily and Adderall (dextroamphetamine-amphetamine) 20 mg oral daily. His allergies include lobster, oysters, and dust. His family history is non-contributory. The patient was discharged in stable condition.

The patient, a male, was admitted for rectal cancer. His chief complaint was painless hematochezia, which had been ongoing for several years and was initially attributed to hemorrhoids. He also reported unintentional weight loss of 7 lbs over the past month, which he attributed to stress. He had never had a screening colonoscopy until recently, which revealed a rectal polyp 8 cm from the anal verge. The biopsy of the polyp was consistent with adenocarcinoma of the rectum. Follow-up CEA and staging scans were negative for metastatic disease. He also reported nocturia x3 at night and a progressively decreased urinary stream. 

The patient's past medical history includes rectal cancer, likely stage I, hypertension managed with lisinopril, hyperlipidemia managed with diet, and benign prostatic hyperplasia. His family history is unremarkable, with no known history of coronary artery disease or cancer. 

During his hospital stay, the patient underwent a robotic low anterior resection with a diverting ileostomy. Postoperatively, he developed urinary retention and fevers up to 101.7 on days 4 and 5. Blood and urine cultures were negative, and a CT scan of the abdomen and pelvis showed no infectious process. His hemoglobin was 40.7, creatinine 0.8, sodium 142, potassium 3.6, and chloride 104. 

Upon admission, the patient was on finasteride 5 mg daily, lisinopril 5 mg daily, and tamsulosin 0.4 mg at bedtime. 

Upon discharge, the patient was in good condition, ambulating independently, and alert and oriented times three. His abdomen was soft and appropriately tender, with a pink and well-perfused ileostomy. He was discharged home with a leg bag and a follow-up appointment at the clinic. His discharge diagnosis was rectal cancer.

The patient, a male with a history of rectal cancer, hypertension managed with lisinopril, hyperlipidemia controlled by diet, and benign prostatic hyperplasia, was admitted due to high urine output. He had previously undergone a low anterior resection (LAR) for his rectal cancer, which was likely stage I. His cancer was initially discovered after a colonoscopy was performed due to years of hematochezia. Follow-up colonoscopy showed no residual tumor, and a torso CT scan was negative for metastatic disease. A rectal MRI also showed no adenopathy or obvious residual disease. 

The patient reported high urine output over the last two days, with 5400 cc and 4000 cc respectively, along with ostomy output of 1400 cc and 1060 cc. He had good oral intake, with 3580 cc and 3620 cc on the respective days. He was asymptomatic, with no dizziness, lightheadedness, chills, sweats, abdominal pain, nausea, or vomiting. He had a slightly low systolic blood pressure of 98 (usually 100-110) and a temperature of 100.2 degrees. He was not using any dilaudid for pain, and required only 1 tylenol. 

Upon admission, his medications included tamsulosin, finasteride, lisinopril, and acetaminophen. His physical exam on discharge showed a temperature of 100.9, heart rate of 94, blood pressure of 120/53, and respiratory rate of 18. He was alert, oriented, and in no apparent distress. His oral mucosa was moist, lungs were clear to auscultation bilaterally, heart rhythm was regular with no murmurs, gallops, or rubs, and his abdomen was soft, nondistended, and minimally tender. His ostomy was pink and well perfused, with stool and gas in the bag. He had good skin turgor and no clubbing, cyanosis, or edema.

Lab results showed abnormal values for WBC (11.5), RBC (3.65), hemoglobin (11.7), hematocrit (33.6), glucose (131), and lactate (2.1). His electrolytes were normal and his urinalysis was negative. Urine studies revealed dilute urine. A renal consult was called and it was determined that his urine output was appropriate given his high level of oral fluid intake. He had low-grade fevers during his hospital stay. His foley catheter was removed on hospital day 2 and he voided spontaneously. A repeat infectious workup was significant only for coag negative staph in his initial urine sample, which was likely contaminated. 

The patient's family history is notable for a mother who was healthy into her later years and died of 'old age', a father who died young of unknown causes, and a healthy sister. There is no family history of coronary artery disease or cancer. 

The patient was discharged with a diagnosis of possible post-obstructive diuresis. His mental status was clear and coherent, and he was alert, interactive, and independently ambulatory.

The patient, a male of undisclosed age, was admitted to the surgery unit due to a prolapsed loop through his stoma. His chief complaint was discomfort at his prolapsed ostomy site, which had increased in size since his last clinic visit. He denied any changes in ostomy output, nausea, vomiting, fevers, chills, melanotic output or bright red blood from the stoma, or urinary symptoms. 

The patient has a history of rectal cancer, likely stage I, with no evidence of metastatic disease or residual tumor found in follow-up colonoscopies and imaging. He also has a history of hypertension, managed with lisinopril, hyperlipidemia controlled through diet, and benign prostatic hyperplasia. His family history is unremarkable, with no known history of coronary artery disease or cancer.

Upon admission, the patient underwent a takedown ileostomy with primary anastomosis. His post-operative course was complicated by a fever of 102.5, of unknown origin, and ileus, which resolved within a few days. He was initially kept NPO, but was slowly advanced to a regular diet, which he tolerated prior to discharge. He had return of bowel function with flatus and regular, formed bowel movements prior to discharge.

Abnormal lab results included low RBC (3.53, 3.23, 3.47), low Hgb (11.2, 9.9, 10.5), low Hct (31.1, 28.7, 31.2), high MCHC (35.9), high Neuts (75.7), low Lymphs (17.9), and high Glucose (122, 126). Additionally, the patient had low Na (131), low Calcium (8.3, 8.0), and low Phos (2.6).

Upon admission, the patient was taking tamsulosin 0.4mg, finasteride 5 mg, lisinopril 5 mg, and tylenol as needed. His hospital course was managed with Dilaudid IV/PCA for pain control, IV fluids until tolerating oral intake, and subcutaneous heparin for prophylaxis. He was discharged with a diagnosis of prolapse of loop ileostomy and intact colorectal anastomosis.

The patient, a female, was admitted due to perianal pain. She has a history of multiple perianal abscesses and has undergone incision and drainage (I&D) procedures in the past. She presented with a week-long history of perianal pain and subjective fevers. Despite being seen a week prior and started on antibiotics, her symptoms persisted and a small I&D was performed. However, her pain worsened and the perianal lesion increased in size. 

Upon physical examination, she was alert and oriented with no distress. Her vitals were stable, and her abdomen was soft, nondistended, and nontender. She had a large, extremely tender right fullness in the perianal area. 

Lab results showed an elevated white blood cell count at 19.4*, reduced red blood cell count at 4.13*, and a high neutrophil count at 92.1*. Her lymphocyte count was low at 5.3*, and her platelet count was high at 442*. 

A CT scan of the pelvis revealed a large right perianal abscess, approximately 7.3 x 3.5 x 3.1 cm in size, crossing the midline in two locations to involve the left ischioanal fossa. There was surrounding tissue induration but no subcutaneous air or clear rectal or supra-levator extension. 

She underwent an incision and drainage procedure under anesthesia, revealing a large right lateral perirectal abscess with no internal component seen on anoscopy. The old I&D site was extended, the area was explored and irrigated, and a wick was placed. 

Post-procedure, the patient developed a pruritic rash after consuming peanuts. She was treated with Benadryl, which alleviated her symptoms. She reported no shortness of breath or trouble swallowing. 

The patient has a known allergy to sulfa and peanuts. Her family history includes her mother having breast cancer. She was not on any medications upon admission. 

The patient was discharged with a diagnosis of perianal abscess and allergic rash. Her mental status was clear and coherent, and she was alert, interactive, and independently ambulatory. She was advised to avoid peanut products until further evaluation by her primary care physician.

The patient, a female, was admitted due to a perianal abscess. She has a history of multiple prior perianal abscesses, which have required multiple prior bedside incision and drainages (I&Ds) as well as an intraoperative drainage. She presented with worsening pain and swelling over the right perianal area. A CT scan of the pelvis with contrast revealed a predominantly right-sided perianal abscess in the ischioanal fossa, measuring 3.4 x 5.2 x 9.2 cm, which crosses midline to the left just anterior to the anal opening. The left-sided component is predominantly contained within the sphincter complex. 

During her hospital stay, she underwent a formal incision and drainage as well as placement of 2 setons. Post-operatively, she was transferred to the floor and was tolerating PO pain medications, PO antibiotics, and a regular diet. She was then discharged home in good condition. 

Upon admission, she was on clindamycin and oral contraceptive pills. She has an allergy to Sulfa (Sulfonamide Antibiotics). Her family history includes her mother having breast cancer. 

Her physical examination was largely unremarkable. She was in no acute distress, alert and oriented. Her cardiac and lung examinations were normal. Her abdomen was soft, non-distended, and non-tender to palpation. Setons were in place and not actively draining, with no surrounding erythema or induration. Her extremities were warm and well-perfused without edema. 

She has no personal or family history of inflammatory bowel disease, and denies any history of abdominal pain or frequent diarrhea.

The patient, a male with no known allergies or past medical history, presented to the ED with diffuse abdominal pain, localizing to the right lower quadrant. The pain was associated with nausea and vomiting. The patient had undergone a colonoscopy a day prior to the onset of the abdominal pain to evaluate for several months of diarrhea, abdominal cramping, tenesmus, and fecal urgency. The colonoscopy revealed colitis of the entire length of the colon and biopsies were taken, the results of which are pending. 

Upon physical examination at admission, the patient was alert and oriented, with vitals within normal range. The abdomen was soft, non-distended, but tender in the right lower quadrant, with no signs of peritonitis. 

Admission labs showed elevated white blood cell count (15.8), hemoglobin (13.5), neutrophils (77.8), lymphocytes (13.6), monocytes (7.3), eosinophils (0.6), absolute neutrophils (12.30), absolute monocytes (1.16), and C-reactive protein (15.1). Anion gap was also elevated (19).
