Her chronic pain was managed with a lower dose of gabapentin, along with her home doses of oxycodone-acetaminophen, tramadol, and acetaminophen. She was also found to have a shortened PR interval on ECG, and was advised to avoid sympathomimetics, specifically cocaine. Her laryngeal reflux was stable and managed with omeprazole.

The patient's bipolar disorder was managed with a switch from olanzapine to risperidone, which improved her emotional regulation, mood, and suicidal thoughts. She was also provided with a long-acting injectable formulation of risperidone. Her anxiety was relatively stable and managed with alprazolam. She reported a recent sexual assault, and was visited by a counseling team for support. The patient was discharged in a stable and improved condition, with no evidence of psychosis or suicidality. She was advised to continue taking her medications regularly, abstain from illicit substances, and follow up with her outpatient appointments.

The patient, a male, was admitted due to nausea and vomiting. He had undergone an exploratory laparotomy for bowel obstruction and was recovering well until he experienced these symptoms after a large meal. The patient vomited four times over two days, initially expelling partially digested food which later turned into clear-yellow fluid. Accompanying these symptoms were abdominal distension and pain, which he reported to have subsided after vomiting. He also reported passing a small bowel movement and a small amount of flatus.

The patient's past medical history includes sleep apnea, polyneuropathy which could be due to mild diabetes mellitus and B12 deficiency. He has undergone several surgical procedures in the past including sigmoid resection, tonsillectomy, appendectomy, finger surgery, rhinoplasty, rotator cuff surgery, and eye surgery to correct esotropia. His family history is negative for cancer.

Upon physical examination, the patient was awake, alert, and oriented. His abdomen was soft, mildly distended, and nontender. His incisions were healing well and the staples had been removed. There were no hernias or masses detected.

Lab results showed a neutrophil count of 71.3*, glucose level of 121*, urea nitrogen level of 30*, and creatinine level of 1.3*, all of which were abnormal. The patient's platelet count was 299, and his WBC, RBC, HGB, HCT, MCV, MCH, MCHC, RDW, sodium, potassium, chloride, total CO2, and anion gap were all within normal ranges.

During his hospital stay, the patient was given an NGT for decompression and was started on IV fluids for hydration. He began to pass gas and watery stool on the second and third day of his hospital stay. He was advanced to a regular diet on the third day and tolerated it well. His abdominal wound staples were also removed at that time. He was discharged home in stable condition with arrangements for prompt follow-up in clinic.

Upon admission, the patient was on several medications including azelastine, bupropion, citalopram, gabapentin, mirtazapine, cyanocobalamin, ibuprofen, oxycodone-acetaminophen, tamsulosin, zolpidem tartrate, zaleplon, mometasone, and docusate sodium.

The patient was diagnosed with post-operative ileus. He was instructed to stay hydrated due to diarrhea and to contact his surgeon or return to the emergency room if the diarrhea persists for more than a few days.

The patient, a male of undisclosed age, was admitted due to persistent diarrhea. His medical history includes sleep apnea, polyneuropathy (possibly due to mild diabetes mellitus and B12 deficiency), and a sigmoid resection performed several years ago. His surgical history includes tonsillectomy, appendectomy, finger surgery, rhinoplasty, rotator cuff surgery, and eye surgery to correct esotropia. The patient has no known allergies or adverse drug reactions. His family history is negative for cancer.

During this admission, the patient underwent an exploratory laparotomy with lysis of adhesions. He had been experiencing persistent diarrhea since his return from a trip abroad, where he had surgery for small bowel obstruction (SBO). A CT scan revealed multiple loops of dilated bowel and pneumatosis intestinalis. The patient denied any abdominal pain, nausea, vomiting, or fever. 

Physical examination upon discharge showed the patient was alert and oriented, with no signs of distress. His abdomen was soft and tender, with a midline abdominal incision covered with steri-strips. He had edema in both upper and lower extremities, more pronounced in the right upper extremity. 

Imaging results showed unchanged small bowel findings compared to a previous CT examination, with pneumatosis of small-bowel loops in the right upper quadrant and multiple dilated loops of small bowel. There was no evidence of mesenteric or portal venous gas, suggesting small bowel obstruction. There was also cholelithiasis without evidence of cholecystitis. A pathology report from a peritoneal nodule excision showed no malignancy. An ECG revealed a new right bundle-branch block. 

During his hospital stay, the patient experienced a period of disorientation, during which he became violent and required restraints and administration of IV haldol and lorazepam. His mental status gradually returned to baseline by the time of discharge. He also experienced hypotension on post-operative day 1, which was managed with a 1L bolus of normal saline in conjunction with IV albumin. A right-sided power PICC was placed for venous access. An upper extremity duplex series revealed a nonocclusive thrombus in the right basilic vein and possible chronic thrombus in the left brachial vein. 

The patient's medications upon admission included bupropion, citalopram, gabapentin, mirtazapine, and tamsulosin. Throughout his hospitalization, he had persistent diarrhea, for which he received intravenous metronidazole. Stool cultures were negative. He also had a positive urinalysis growing coag positive staph, which was treated with intravenous doxycycline and oral Macrobid. 

Upon discharge, the patient was diagnosed with recurrent partial small bowel obstruction. He was alert, interactive, and able to ambulate independently.

The patient, a male, was admitted due to recurrent episodes of small-bowel obstruction (SBO) over the past several years. His symptoms included intermittent abdominal distention and vomiting, with some episodes lasting approximately 3 days, but generally less than 24 hours. He has had multiple investigations including CT scans and an MR enteroscopy, which showed a slightly abnormal loop of small bowel in the left mid abdomen. However, attempts at visualization with push enteroscopy were unsuccessful. A capsule enterography was performed which showed no bleeding and no definitive abnormalities. The patient has not experienced significant weight loss.

His past medical history includes sleep apnea, polyneuropathy (possibly due to mild DM and B12 deficiency). He has also undergone several surgeries in the past, including sigmoid resection, tonsillectomy, appendectomy, finger surgery, rhinoplasty, rotator cuff surgery, and eye surgery to correct esotropia. His family history is negative for cancer.

During his stay, the patient underwent an exploratory laparotomy and lysis of adhesions. Post-operatively, he did well, with his diet advanced and tolerated without nausea or vomiting. He was passing flatus, his pain was controlled on oral pain medications, he was ambulating well, and his foley was taken out without problems.

Upon admission, the patient was on several medications including acetazolamide, bupropion, citalopram, gabapentin, mirtazapine, tamsulosin, azelastine nasal spray, zaleplon, zolpidem, mometasone (nasonex), cyanocobalamin ER, and a multivitamin.

Lab results showed a decrease in WBC from 9 to 6.6, a slight decrease in Hct from 40 to 39.4, and an increase in Plt from 198 to 224. Chemistry was within normal limits.

Upon discharge, the patient was diagnosed with adhesions. His mental status was clear and coherent, and he was alert, interactive, and independently ambulatory.

The patient, a male with a history of anxiety, was admitted due to Diabetic Ketoacidosis (DKA). He reported symptoms of nausea, vomiting, fatigue, malaise, polydipsia, and polyuria over the last two days. He denied any preceding illness but mentioned stress due to college finals. His family history includes a grandfather with adult onset diabetes. Upon admission, he was not on any medications.

During his stay, he was given 2 L of fluid, morphine, and Ativan, and was started on a bolus of 10 units IV insulin followed by a drip at 5 units/hr. A chest X-ray was performed which was reported as negative. His initial vitals in the ED were 98.9 116 114/63 16 100% RA and upon transfer to the MICU, his vitals were 97 92/50 18 100% RA. 

Abnormal lab results upon admission included PO2-117*, PCO2-26*, PH-7.18*, TOTAL CO2-10*, BASE XS--17, GLUCOSE-242*, TOTAL CO2-9*, ANION GAP-23*, CALCIUM-7.9*, PHOSPHATE-2.1*, PO2-47*, PCO2-21*, PH-7.11*, TOTAL CO2-7*, BASE XS--21, GLUCOSE-316*, CREAT-1.3*, CHLORIDE-111*, TOTAL CO2-5*, ANION GAP-25*, WBC-20.1*, NEUTS-83.6*, LYMPHS-11.2*, and URINE GRANULAR-13*. 

Upon discharge, abnormal lab results included WBC-3.8*, RBC-4.18*, Hgb-12.8*, Hct-35.9*, MCHC-35.6*, and Glucose-269*. 

The patient was diagnosed with DKA, evidenced by hyperglycemia, ketonuria, and anion gap metabolic acidosis. His A1c was 15.7%. He was started on insulin and his anion gap closed. He was also seen by a nurse educator who performed insulin teaching. On the day of discharge, his glucose levels were 179, 237, 257, 250. He continued to feel well and was tolerating oral intake without nausea or vomiting. 

He was also diagnosed with volume depletion secondary to aggressive IV fluid resuscitation, which normalized his creatinine levels. Pending labs including anti GAD and c-peptide will be followed by his outpatient provider. His CBC should be rechecked as an outpatient to ensure the cell lines are recovering. 

Upon discharge, the patient was alert, oriented, and in no acute distress. His mental status was clear and coherent, and he was ambulatory and independent. He was instructed to continue taking his prescribed insulin.

The male patient was admitted to the orthopedics service due to back pain following an incident where he jumped from a two-story balcony while intoxicated. He did not lose consciousness or hit his head during the incident. Upon examination, he was found to have a compression fracture of L3 with a 7 mm retropulsion into the spinal canal. He was neurovascularly intact and hemodynamically stable. The patient denied experiencing numbness, weakness, headache, visual changes, chest pain, shortness of breath, or neck pain. 

The patient underwent a major surgical procedure where a Thoracolumbosacral orthosis (TLSO) brace was fitted. He was kept on bed rest until the brace was fitted. He experienced urinary retention, for which a Foley catheter was placed and later removed once the brace was fitted and the patient was able to get out of bed and ambulate. He was voiding freely with control. The patient's initial pain was managed with medication and his diet was advanced as tolerated. Physical therapy was consulted for mobilization out of bed to ambulate. His hospital course was otherwise unremarkable. 

The patient has no known allergies or adverse drug reactions. His past medical history is unremarkable and he has no known family history of any conditions. Upon admission, the patient was on Sertraline 50 mg PO daily. 

Upon physical examination, the patient was mildly uncomfortable but alert and oriented. His cranial nerves II-XII were grossly intact. He had tenderness to the midline L spine. His sensory and motor functions were intact, as were his reflexes. Babinski sign was negative and there was no clonus. Perianal sensation was intact per patient and rectal tone was not tested as the patient denied stool incontinence. 

Upon discharge, the patient was diagnosed with an L3 burst fracture. His mental status was clear and coherent, and he was alert and interactive. He was ambulatory but required assistance or aid. He was discharged home with instructions on activity limitations, diet, brace usage, and medication management. He was also given instructions for follow-up appointments and when to seek immediate medical attention.

The patient, a female under the OBSTETRICS/GYNECOLOGY service, was admitted due to a shortening/dilated cervix. She was diagnosed with mono di twins during a routine visit and was noted to have a short cervix on ultrasound. She denies contractions, vaginal bleeding, loss of fluid, fevers/chills, recent illness, headaches, vision changes, chest pain, shortness of breath, palpitations, abdominal pain, and recent falls or abdominal trauma. She confirms active fetal movement. 

Her past medical history includes prenatal care with normal labs for Rh, Abs, Rub, RPR, HBsAg, HIV, and GBS. She tested negative for CF carrier screening and had normal Hgb electrophoresis. She had an elevated T21 risk (1:130) but declined follow-up testing. Her ultrasound showed normal AFI x 2, stomach and bladder, and normal MCA dopplers. She denies any abnormal Pap or cervical procedures, STIs, including HSV. She has no significant family history. 

Upon physical examination, she was alert and oriented, comfortable, with a soft, gravid, non-tender abdomen. No calf tenderness was noted. Her blood pressure was 138/74, and her temperature was 98.2°F. 

Lab results showed abnormal WBC at 13.3, RBC at 3.45, Hgb at 10.7, Hct at 32.4, and RDWSD at 52.2. Urine tests showed a pH of 6.0, Leuks-SM, Urobiln-2, and Ketone-10. All drug tests were negative. She tested negative for Chlamydia trachomatis, Neisseria gonorrhoeae, Group B Beta Strep, and bacterial vaginosis. A yeast vaginitis culture showed rare growth. 

During her hospital stay, she was treated with indomethacin for preterm contractions, which improved significantly. She had no further bleeding and fetal testing was reassuring. She completed a course of betamethasone. She remained clinically stable without any evidence of preterm labor. Her cervical exam was unchanged at the time of discharge. 

Upon admission, she was taking prenatal vitamins. Her discharge diagnosis was preterm contractions. She was alert, clear, coherent, and independently ambulatory at the time of discharge.

The patient is a male with a history of coronary artery disease (CAD) post stent placement, inguinal hernia, and thyroid cyst. He was admitted due to persistent back pain and urinary retention following a recent fall. He reported that the pain, described as 'like a shock', shoots down the side of his thigh to his knee. He was previously seen by his primary care physician (PCP) who prescribed Motrin and Flexiril. He was also given a 5-day course of Prednisone by an orthopedic doctor, which initially relieved his pain. However, the pain returned after the course was completed. He also reported a weak urinary stream and feeling like he was not fully emptying his bladder for about two weeks. 

Upon admission, the patient was found to have urinary retention, with 1600cc of urine released when a Foley catheter was placed. An MRI showed disc herniation. The patient's neuro exam was intact, with no saddle anesthesia, normal rectal tone, and no weakness. He continued to have urinary retention, failing voiding trials. However, he was able to void 400cc of urine on his own after the catheter was removed. 

The patient's lab results showed a hemoglobin level of 12.2* and a hematocrit level of 37.3*, both of which are abnormal. His urea nitrogen level was also abnormal at 21*. An MRI of the thoracic and lumbar spine showed a disc extrusion at L2-3, multilevel degenerative changes at other levels in the lumbar spine, and bilateral neural foraminal narrowing at L4-5 and L5-S1. 

The patient's medications upon admission included Simvastatin 80mg daily, baby aspirin, Plavix 75mg daily, Metoprolol 50mg daily, and Vicodin as needed for pain. 

The patient was evaluated by Orthopedics, who recommended no surgical intervention at this time. He was scheduled for an epidural steroid injection by the pain service. His Plavix was held for one week for the procedure, with his PCP's approval. He was discharged on Dilaudid as needed and Gabapentin for pain control. 

The patient's discharge diagnosis was L2-L3 disc herniation. He was alert and interactive upon discharge, and was able to ambulate independently.

The patient, a male with a history of hypertension and hyperlipidemia, was admitted to the cardiology service after presenting to the emergency room with exertional angina. He had a cardiac catheterization and intraaortic balloon pump placed and was then referred for coronary artery bypass grafting. The patient underwent coronary artery bypass grafting times four, with a left internal mammary artery to left anterior descending artery, reverse saphenous vein graft to obtuse marginal artery, reverse saphenous vein graft to ramus artery, and reverse saphenous vein graft to posterior descending artery. 

Post-operatively, the patient experienced several episodes of atrial fibrillation, which were treated with Beta blockade and amiodarone. He was also started on oral anticoagulation with coumadin. The patient was transfused two units of packed red blood cells for a hematocrit of 21%. He initially had some urinary retention and the foley catheter was re-inserted. After two days of treatment with Flomax, the foley was discontinued and the patient voided without difficulty. 

The patient's lab results showed abnormal glucose (114), urea nitrogen (24), creatinine (1.5), and HbA1c (6.0). His RBC count (3.85), hemoglobin (13.0), hematocrit (35.7), and MCH (33.7) were also abnormal. His blood glucose (131), urea nitrogen (50), and creatinine (1.4) were also elevated. 

The patient's echocardiography report showed normal left atrium and right atrium cavity sizes, normal left ventricular wall thickness, cavity size, and global systolic function, and normal right ventricular chamber size and free wall motion. The aortic valve leaflets were mildly thickened but aortic stenosis was not present. The mitral valve appeared structurally normal with mild mitral regurgitation. 

Upon admission, the patient was on toprol xl 100', lisinopril/hctz, asa 81', lovaza, simvastatin 40', and NTG prn. The patient's father had a myocardial infarction in his past. The patient was discharged to rehab in good condition.

The patient, a male, was admitted due to asymptomatic high-grade left carotid stenosis. This was discovered during a routine physical examination where a cervical bruit was found. A carotid ultrasound and CT angiogram were performed, which suggested a critical left carotid stenosis. The patient underwent a left carotid endarterectomy. He has no history of transient ischemic attack (TIA), amaurosis fugax, or stroke. However, he has a history of coronary artery disease and underwent a coronary artery bypass procedure last year due to anginal variant symptoms and significant three-vessel coronary artery disease. He also had a chronic left hemothorax, which required removal by thoracoscopy some months later.

His past medical history includes hypertension, hyperlipidemia, and a tonsillectomy. His father had a myocardial infarction. Upon physical examination, he appeared well with a heart rate of 79, blood pressure of 199/90, and respiratory rate of 19. A very loud left cervical bruit was noted, and carotid pulsations were strongly palpable. 

Lab results showed abnormal levels of RBC (3.03), Hgb (9.6), Hct (28.4), Glucose (162), UreaN (26), and Creat (1.4). A CTA showed a calcific atherosclerotic plaque and left carotid bifurcation with near occlusion of the lumen shortly after the bifurcation. A carotid duplex showed left carotid artery with a peak systolic velocity of 352 and an end-diastolic velocity of 118, putting in a ratio of 3.4, putting it in 80-99% range. The right side has minimal stenosis.

During his hospital stay, the patient developed a small hematoma overnight, which was determined to be stable and required no intervention. His medications upon admission included Lisinopril-Hydrochlorothiazide 20 mg-12.5 mg, Metoprolol Succinate 100mg, Simvastatin 40mg, and Aspirin 81mg. Upon discharge, his condition was stable. His discharge diagnosis was critical asymptomatic left carotid stenosis, status post left carotid endarterectomy.

The patient, a female, was admitted to the neurosurgery department due to back pain and leg pain that she has been experiencing since a car accident last year. The pain is located in her upper back and radiates to both legs, increasing with any activity and even standing. She has tried various forms of conservative management such as chiropractic treatment, acupuncture, LESI, etc., but none have been successful. She denies any bowel, bladder, or sexual issues. 

Her past medical history includes a motor vehicle accident, hypothyroidism, ACL surgery, and an appendectomy. She has no known family history of any significant medical conditions. She is allergic to amoxicillin and codeine. Upon admission, she was taking Levothyroxine 112mg every morning.

During her hospital stay, she underwent a T10-L2 posterior spinal fusion. The procedure was well-tolerated and she was transferred to the floor after satisfactory recovery from anesthesia. Initially, she was given IV fluids and IV pain medications, and progressed to a regular diet and oral medications by the second day post-operation. Pain control was initially an issue but by the third day post-operation, she was more comfortable. She was given antibiotics and anticoagulation as per routine. Her home medications were continued throughout the hospitalization. 

Upon discharge, her pain was well controlled with oral medications, her incisions were clean, dry, and intact, and she was voiding and moving bowels spontaneously. Her discharge diagnosis was T11-12 kyphosis. Her mental status was clear and coherent, and she was alert and interactive. She was ambulatory and independent. 

Her discharge medications included Acetaminophen 1000 mg PO Q6H, Bisacodyl 10 mg PO/PR DAILY, Cepacol (Sore Throat Lozenge) 1 LOZ PO Q4H:PRN sore throat, Diazepam 5 mg PO BID:PRN pain, Docusate Sodium 100 mg PO BID, OxyCODONE (Immediate Release) ___ mg PO Q4H:PRN Pain - Moderate, Senna 8.6 mg PO BID, and Levothyroxine Sodium 112 mcg PO DAILY.

The patient, a female with a history of hypertension, hyperlipidemia, depression, fibromyalgia, hypothyroidism, gastroesophageal reflux disease, diabetes mellitus, seasonal allergic rhinitis, restless leg syndrome, and Clostridium difficile infection, was admitted due to jaundice. She had noticed skin changes four days prior to admission and also reported abdominal distension and a significant weight loss of 30 pounds over two months. She had no fever, nausea, vomiting, or abdominal pain. She did report some loose stool, which she attributed to starting metformin for her newly diagnosed diabetes. 

During her stay, she underwent an ERCP with sphincterotomy and stent placement. A CT scan of her abdomen and pelvis revealed biliary tract dilation, mild pancreas duct dilation, pancreas glandular atrophy, a probable fibroid uterus, calcific aortic ASVD, hiatal hernia, and mild constipation. No large or bulky pancreas head mass or gallstones were seen. 

Her lab results showed several abnormal values: RBC count of 3.46, hemoglobin of 10.8, hematocrit of 33.9, glucose of 145, potassium of 3.1, ALT of 335, AST of 167, AlkPhos of 878, TotBili of 3.6, and magnesium of 1.4. Her total bilirubin was 6.5, direct bilirubin was 3.9, AST was 315, ALT was 512, and Alk Phos was 1033. 

The brushings from her common bile duct were positive for malignant cells, consistent with adenocarcinoma. A CT scan of her abdomen with and without IV contrast showed a biliary stent in the common hepatic duct, intrahepatic and extrahepatic biliary duct dilation, and enhancement of the intrahepatic bile duct walls. There were also prominent porta hepatis lymph nodes and a region of hypoattenuation in the uncinate process of the pancreas, measuring 16 mm, which was concerning for a pancreatic neoplasm. 

Her ERCP revealed a 1 cm tight, malignant appearing stricture in the distal common bile duct, and cytology samples were obtained. A 7 cm biliary stent was placed successfully across the stricture. 

Her medications upon admission included carisoprodol, amlodipine, irbesartan, atorvastatin, pramipexole, duloxetine, polyethylene glycol, levothyroxine, folic acid, vitamin D, acetylcysteine, omeprazole, lactobacillus acidophilus, saccharomyces boulardii, metformin, and acetaminophen. 

The patient was discharged with a primary diagnosis of bile duct obstruction and biliary stricture/pancreatic mass. Her mental status was clear and coherent, and she was alert and interactive. She was ambulatory and independent. Pending studies on discharge included biopsy results and a CT scan read.

The patient, a female, was admitted for a surgical procedure due to a diagnosis of ductal adenocarcinoma. She underwent several procedures including exploratory laparoscopy, laparoscopic biopsies of segment 4 and segment 3, exploratory laparotomy, pylorus-sparing radical pancreatoduodenectomy, end-to-side pancreaticojejunostomy, cholecystectomy with end-to-side ductal mucosa hepaticojejunostomy, placement of gold fiducials, antecolic duodenojejunostomy, and placement of a transgastric feeding jejunostomy tube. 

The patient's past medical history includes hypertension, hyperlipidemia, depression, fibromyalgia, hypothyroidism, gastroesophageal reflux disease, diabetes mellitus, seasonal allergic rhinitis, restless leg syndrome, and Clostridium difficile infection. She has no family history of early coronary artery disease or gastrointestinal cancer. 

Upon admission, the patient was taking several medications including polyethylene glycol, omeprazole, irbesartan, lactobacillus acidophilus, folic acid, metformin, pramipexole, carisoprodol, acetylcysteine, vitamin D, levothyroxine sodium, amlodipine, ondansetron, duloxetine, saccharomyces boulardii, and oxycodone. 

The patient's physical examination revealed a subcostal abdominal incision with a wound VAC dressing on to 120 mm Hg suction. Her vital signs were stable. Abnormal lab results included a low red blood cell count (3.14), low hemoglobin (9.5), low hematocrit (30.3), high glucose (243), and low calcium (8.0) and phosphorus (2.6) levels. 

The patient's stool tested positive for toxigenic C. difficile. A wound culture revealed sparse growth of Escherichia coli and gram-positive cocci. Pathology examination of the liver, lymph node, bile duct, and pancreatic duct showed no malignancy. However, the pancreas and duodenum showed moderately differentiated pancreatic adenocarcinoma. 

During her hospital stay, the patient developed wound infection and C. difficile colitis. She was treated with oral Flagyl, Keflex, and Vancomycin. Her blood sugar levels were monitored and managed with subcutaneous insulin. She was discharged home with a wound VAC in place and oral Vancomycin for treatment of C. difficile colitis. Her discharge diagnoses included ductal adenocarcinoma G2T3N0, chronic pancreatitis, wound infection, and C. difficile colitis.

The patient, a female, was admitted due to a wound infection. She has a history of pancreatic adenocarcinoma and had undergone a Whipple procedure, which was complicated by a wound infection. She had completed chemoradiation and presented with erythema of her abdominal wound. She noticed increased swelling on the right side of her incision, which later became red and started to drain fluid. The patient denied experiencing fevers, chills, and malaise. The infection cultures from her previous infection grew strep milleri sensitive to vanco and e.coli sensitive to meropenem and zosyn.

Her past medical history includes hypertension, hyperlipidemia, depression, fibromyalgia, hypothyroidism, gastroesophageal reflux disease, diabetes mellitus, seasonal allergic rhinitis, restless leg syndrome, and Cdiff. She has no family history of early coronary artery disease or gastrointestinal cancer.

Upon physical examination, her old subcostal incision was well healed, with the right lateral aspect open about 1.5 inches and packed with a moist-to-dry dressing. Erythema was significantly improved from prior examinations, with no drainage. Her vital signs were stable and she was afebrile.

Abnormal lab results included a red blood cell count of 3.11, hemoglobin of 9.3, hematocrit of 29.8, glucose of 183, urea nitrogen of 21, sodium of 129, bicarbonate of 21, and alkaline phosphatase of 184. 

During her hospital stay, a bedside incision and drainage procedure was performed to fully drain the collection from her wound. The wound was subsequently packed with a wet to dry dressing. She was started on vancomycin and her dressing was changed twice daily. She remained afebrile and hemodynamically stable throughout her hospitalization. Upon discharge, she was tolerating a regular diet, had normal bowel function, was ambulating, was voiding without difficulty, and was without pain.

Her medications upon admission included Avapro, Duloxetine, Folic Acid, Levothyroxine Sodium, Omeprazole, Polyethylene Glycol, Vitamin D, Metformin, Docusate Sodium, Hydromorphone, Florajen, Amlodipine, Acetaminophen, Mirapex, Florastor, NAC, Ondansetron, Vancomycin Oral Liquid, Morphine SR, needle, and Glargine.

The patient, a female with a significant past medical history of fibromyalgia, hypertension, depression, and pancreatic adenocarcinoma, was admitted due to fevers, chills, and tachycardia. She had previously undergone a Whipple procedure and chemotherapy for her pancreatic adenocarcinoma, which was complicated by a malignant right pleural effusion. She presented with a fever of 101.4, chills, general malaise, a non-productive cough, shortness of breath, and dyspnea on exertion. She also reported suprapubic tenderness, dysuria, and urgency for the past week. Her daughter noted progressive erythema surrounding the bandage site from the PleurX catheter placement. 

Initial lab results showed abnormal BUN/Cr, H/H 9.1/30.1, positive U/A (+ leuk, + bacteria, + WBC), WBC 7.1, and lactate 2.9. A CXR showed a small right pleural effusion with medial right lower lung opacity. The patient received 2L IVF NS, Vanc 1g, and Zosyn 4.5g. 

The patient's family history includes her mother who passed from lung cancer, her father who had a history of a cell tumor, and a maternal aunt who had a history of breast cancer. 

Physical examination on admission showed faint crackles at lung bases R>L, coarse breath sounds on the right side, a well-healed epigastric scar, a large reducible ventral hernia, and suprapubic tenderness. 

Abnormal lab results included RBC-3.00*, Hgb-9.3*, Hct-30.1*, MCV-101*, MCHC-30.9*, PTT-37.7*, Glucose-135*, Creat-1.2*, HCO3-21*, Lactate-2.9*, ALT-49*, AST-69*, AlkPhos-779*, ALT-43*, AST-69*, AlkPhos-858*, GGT-875*, WBC-3.9*, RBC-2.79*, Hgb-8.6*, Hct-27.7*, MCV-99*, Glucose-103*, HCO3-20*, ALT-36*, AST-49*, AlkPhos-762*, and Calcium-8.3*. 

Imaging showed a new 2.2 x 3.3 cm segment VIII hypodensity with interval increase in intrahepatic biliary dilatation, concerning for obstruction and possible disease recurrence. A small right pneumothorax, small to moderate left non-hemorrhagic pleural effusion, and increased intra-abdominal ascites were also noted. 

The patient was found to have E. Coli bacteremia of unclear source. She was initially covered broadly with Vanc/Meropenem. Blood culture from grew E. Coli sensitive to Meropenem. Vanc was discontinued following sensitivities. The patient most likely developed her acute blood stream infection from an abdominal source in the setting of recurrent pancreatic cancer, inflammation, and obstruction. 

The patient's chronic issues include chronic diarrhea in the setting of a history of C. Diff, fibromyalgia, hypertension, diabetes mellitus, hypothyroidism, and depression. Her medications upon admission included Ondansetron, irbesartan, Diphenoxylate-Atropine, Prochlorperazine, Benzonatate, Lorazepam, Oxazepam, MetFORMIN XR, carisoprodol, Creon, Amlodipine, Guaifenesin ER, HYDROmorphone, L.acidoph-B.lactis-B.longum, Omeprazole, Cetirizine, Duloxetine, Levothyroxine Sodium, Cyanocobalamin, FoLIC Acid, Glargine, and Insulin SC Sliding Scale using HUM Insulin.

The patient, a retired elementary school worker, was admitted with altered mental status (AMS), lethargy, and weakness. She has a history of insulin-dependent diabetes mellitus (IDDM), hypertension (HTN), fibromyalgia, depression, and metastatic pancreatic adenocarcinoma. She had previously undergone a Whipple procedure and chemotherapy with radiation therapy (chemoXRT) for her pancreatic cancer, which was complicated by malignant right pleural effusions. She had a pleurX catheter placement for the effusions. 

The patient's daughter reported that her mother had been very tired recently, with suppressed appetite, and had become increasingly lethargic, taking in less food and spending most of her time in bed. The patient's urine had been darker than usual, and she had been experiencing fevers, mainly at night. In the emergency department, she received 2L of normal saline, 1G of intravenous vancomycin, and 1G of meropenem. 

The patient's past oncology history includes presenting with symptoms of abdominal fullness and early satiety, worsening abdominal pain, nausea, and vomiting. She was found to have elevated liver function tests (LFTs) and a CT scan showed biliary ductal dilatation with mild pancreatic duct dilatation and pancreatic glandular atrophy. An ERCP procedure demonstrated a 1 cm malignant-appearing biliary stricture in the distal common bile duct, and a pathology report confirmed adenocarcinoma. 

The patient's family history includes her mother passing from lung cancer, her father having a history of a cell tumor, and a maternal aunt with a history of breast cancer. 

Upon physical examination, the patient was alert and oriented to person and place, with reduced breath sounds at the right base. Lab results showed elevated white blood cell count (15.3), low red blood cell count (3.17), low hemoglobin (9.4), low hematocrit (30.5), low mean corpuscular hemoglobin concentration (30.8), high red cell distribution width (16.3), high glucose (184), high urea nitrogen (37), high creatinine (1.4), low sodium (127), low bicarbonate (16), high alanine aminotransferase (47), high aspartate aminotransferase (63), and high alkaline phosphatase (1342). 

A CT scan showed new areas of hypoattenuation in the right hepatic lobe with central cystic necrotic areas, concerning for hepatic abscesses. There was also increasing abdominal ascites, stable portal venous thrombosis, and bilateral small pleural effusions. A right upper quadrant ultrasound showed an ill-defined hypoechoic focus in the right lateral lobe of the liver, concerning for inflammatory change, and a small to moderate amount of ascites. A chest x-ray showed a small right pleural effusion. 

During her hospital stay, the patient was diagnosed with polymicrobial sepsis and acute kidney injury. She was started on IV vancomycin, meropenem, and flagyl, which led to clinical improvement. A CT scan showed multiple extensive hepatic abscesses not amenable to drainage. The patient and her family elected not to undergo any procedures but to continue the antibiotic therapy with suppressive augmentin therapy while receiving palliative hospice care. 

The patient's medications upon admission included benzonatate, cetirizine, creon, duloxetine, guaifenesin ER, hydromorphone, levothyroxine sodium, omeprazole, ondansetron, oxazepam, carisoprodol, L.acidoph-B.lactis-B.longum, prochlorperazine, vancomycin oral liquid, metformin XR, and methylphenidate.

The patient, a female, was admitted due to pain and swelling of the right perineum. She had a history of a right Bartholin gland cyst, which was incised and drained years ago. Recently, she experienced swelling of the right labia and was prescribed Bactrim DS BID for a Bartholin gland abscess. However, her condition worsened, leading to an incision and drainage procedure and the placement of a Word's catheter. Post-procedure, she experienced significant bleeding and pain, which impaired her daily activities. 

Her obstetric history includes a spontaneous vaginal delivery complicated by cervical incompetence with rescue cerclage, and a cesarean section at 34 weeks due to preterm premature rupture of membranes, resulting in twins. Her gynecological history includes an abnormal Pap smear with no follow-up and a history of abnormal Pap smears post-LEEP. She denies any history of sexually transmitted infections. She has no chronic medical issues and her family history is not contributory.

Upon admission, her physical examination revealed an edematous, fluctuant, and erythematous right labia minora, which was spontaneously draining purulent fluid. A suture was in place at the inferior labia minora, effectively closing the incision site. No bleeding was noted. Her vital signs were stable.

Her lab results showed a mild leukocytosis and a drop in hematocrit from 32.3 to 29.0, likely due to blood loss from the initial incision. Other abnormal findings included a white blood cell count of 17.1, red blood cell count of 3.18, hemoglobin of 8.8, hematocrit of 29.0, MCHC of 30.3, RDWSD of 47.2, absolute neutrophil count of 12.10, absolute lymphocyte count of 3.78, absolute monocyte count of 1.11, eosinophils of 0.3, and a bicarbonate level of 21. Her urine was hazy in appearance with moderate blood, 30 protein, trace ketones, large leukocytes, 160 red blood cells, 103 white blood cells, and few bacteria.

During her hospital stay, she was switched from Bactrim to IV clindamycin and ampicillin-sulbactam. She underwent a successful incision and drainage in the operating room, after which she was transitioned to oral amoxicillin-clavulanate and clindamycin. Her pain was initially managed with ketorolac and hydromorphone, and later with oral acetaminophen, ibuprofen, and oxycodone. She was discharged in stable condition on the third day of her hospital stay.

The patient, a male with a history of HIV/AIDS (CD4 200), non-compliance with antiretroviral therapy, herpes, and syphilis, presented with a 10-day history of a gradually worsening left-sided posterior headache, daily left-sided epistaxis, and generalized fatigue. He also reported waking up with blood on his face and pillow from his nose, with episodes lasting up to 15 minutes. He has tried a humidifier without improvement. He also noted clusters of red rash on his side, boils in his groin, and denied having similar headache or symptoms previously. He has not been taking his antiretroviral medication due to denial and depression. He is sexually active with his HIV positive fiancée. He has no previous history of migraine or tension headache. His father has a history of alcoholism, but no other known family medical history was reported.

Upon admission, the patient was given 1G of acetaminophen and a nicotine patch. A lumbar puncture was performed, which showed 6 WBC, 89% lymphs, protein 38, glucose 32. Gram stain was negative. His urine analysis showed few bacteria, negative nitrate, and negative leukocytes. His chest X-ray showed no acute intrathoracic process and a CT scan of his head showed no acute intracranial process. His blood glucose level was slightly elevated at 122. His cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) showed an elevated RBC count at 415 and 32. His MRI showed minimal mucosal thickening in the right maxillary sinus and a small mucous retention cyst. 

The patient was admitted to medicine for further workup of his headache and to rule out cryptococcal meningitis. His CD4 count was 178, viral load and phenotype studies were pending at the time of discharge. His headache was not associated with significant leukocytosis or elevated protein levels to suggest bacterial or viral meningitis. His CSF cryptococcal antigen was negative. His MRI was negative for acute intracranial abnormality, significant for minimal right maxillary sinus inflammation. He was encouraged to trial standing Tylenol for his headache, with naproxen as needed. His epistaxis did not improve with humidifier use, and he was given nasal saline twice a day without occurrence of epistaxis. His skin rash was non-specific and erythematous, with no evidence of vesicles to suggest HSV or erythema, induration, or warmth to suggest cellulitis. His RPR was negative. He also had non-tender swelling in his groin that could suggest lymphadenopathy. His RPR was negative and urine gonorrhea and chlamydia were pending at the time of discharge. 

The patient was not taking any medications upon admission. He was discharged home and instructed to follow up with his primary care doctor. His mental status was clear and coherent, and he was alert and interactive. His activity status was independent and ambulatory.

The patient, a female with a history of cortical blindness from birth, was admitted following a right temporal hemorrhage. She was initially presented with a headache and speech difficulties, which were noticed by her mother over a phone call. The patient was taken to an outside hospital where a head CT scan revealed the hemorrhage. She was then transferred for further neurological workup. An MRI/MRA was performed to rule out arteriovenous malformation (AVM), but the results were inconclusive. An angiogram was recommended by the neurology service.

The patient's condition declined, leading to elective intubation. She was hyponatremic and exhibited left hemiparesis. A craniectomy was performed, after which her condition remained stable. Post-operative head CT showed a decrease in midline shift and stable right temporal hemorrhage with an evolving right middle cerebral artery (MCA) infarction. The patient was difficult to wean off the ventilator and was transferred to rehab with ventilator support.

The patient had a dermatologic reaction resulting in a linear IgA bullous eruption, leading to a diagnosis of vancomycin allergy. She also developed cellulitis in her right posterior thigh. Continuous EEG monitoring was ordered to assess for seizure activity, which showed some bifrontal activity. Her medication, Keppra, was increased to 1000mg BID. She was also trached and PEGed.

Lab results showed abnormal values for WBC, RBC, Hgb, Hct, MCH, RDW, Plt Ct, Neuts, Bands, Monos, Glucose, UreaN, Cl, %HbA1c, eAG, Cholest, LDLcalc, pO2, pCO2, and calTCO2. The patient was not on any medications upon admission. 

The patient's mother is living and healthy. There is no mention of any other family history. 

Upon discharge, the patient was diagnosed with right temporal hemorrhage, subfalcine herniation requiring right hemicraniectomy, right MCA infarct, left distal infarct, respiratory failure, dysphagia, cellulitis right posterior thigh, cortical blindness from birth, linear IgA bullous dermatoses, vancomycin reaction resulting in linear IgA bullous dermatosis, and a new vancomycin allergy. She was discharged to extended care.

The patient, a female known to the neurosurgery service, was admitted for an elective cranioplasty and replacement of her bone flap. This was due to a previous ruptured arteriovenous malformation (AVM) which required a craniectomy for control of intracranial pressure and cerebral edema. She had been discharged to a rehab facility for three months prior to this admission, where she showed improvements in daily functioning. She was moving her right side fully, but her left side remained paretic. Her past medical history includes cortical blindness from birth. She has allergies to Vancomycin and Cephalosporins. Her mother is living and healthy.

Upon physical examination, she was alert and oriented, followed commands, and had reactive pupils. She had rotary nystagmus at rest, symmetric face, midline tongue, and intact sensation to light touch in all extremities. Her right side and left lower extremity showed full strength, but her left upper extremity was paretic.

Her lab results showed an elevated glucose level of 116*, a high platelet count of 469*, and a high white blood cell count of 16.1*. Her hemoglobin was 13.1, and her hematocrit was 39. Her CT/CTA head pre-op showed stable herniation of the brain through the right hemicraniectomy defect, along with regional cystic encephalomalacia due to a prior large right MCA infarct. Post-op CT showed status post right cranioplasty with reduction of previous brain herniation, postoperative changes including a small amount of pneumocephalus and small foci of blood products in the right temporal lobe, and placement of ventriculostomy catheter via left frontal approach.

During her hospital stay, she underwent a right-sided cranioplasty and placement of a left frontal external ventricular drain (EVD), both of which she tolerated well. She was transferred to the ICU for further care post-operatively. Her intracranial pressure (ICP) was continuously monitored, and she had no complaints. Her neurological exam remained at her baseline. A subdural drain was placed at the time of her surgery and remained in place with mild bloody drainage. She developed a morbiliform rash, which was thought to be a reaction to Ancef. She was started on triamcinolone cream and sarna as needed.

Upon admission, she was on several medications including Amlodipine, Heparin (Porcine), Lansoprazole, Levetiracetam, Levothyroxine, Methenamine Hippurate, Bisacodyl, Cyanocobalamin (Vitamin B-12), Ferrous Sulfate, Multivitamin, and Saccharomyces Boulardii.

The patient, a female, was admitted due to left breast cancer which was discovered during an annual mammogram screening and confirmed with a stereotactic-guided core biopsy that showed invasive ductal carcinoma grade ___. The patient's HER-2/neu receptor was negative. She has no family history of breast or ovarian cancer. Her past medical history includes a ___ trimester TAB ___ years ago, relatively regular cycles, no history of abnormal paps or STDs, and stress-induced asthma (no intubations or hospitalizations). She has no past surgical history. 

During her stay, she underwent a skin-sparing left total mastectomy, left axillary sentinel node mapping and biopsy followed by a left transverse rectus abdominis musculocutaneous flap using ipsilateral rectus muscle. The patient tolerated these procedures well. Post-operation, the patient was able to get out of bed to the chair without difficulty and her pain was controlled with a morphine PCA. Her diet was advanced, which she tolerated well, and her pain medication was transitioned to oral medications. She was tolerating a regular diet, ambulation and her pain was controlled with oral pain medication. 

Upon admission, the patient was on albuterol and omeprazole. Her physical examination showed a rapid heart rate, regular rhythm, normal S1, S2, clear lungs, and a mildly distended abdomen that was appropriately tender to palpation. Her left breast flap was warm and well perfused. Her extremities were warm, well perfused, and showed no calf tenderness. 

The patient was discharged with a diagnosis of left breast cancer. Her mental status was clear and coherent, and she was alert and interactive. She was able to ambulate independently. Her discharge instructions included personal care instructions for her bilateral breast dressings and drain sites, activity restrictions, and medication instructions. She was advised to call the office immediately if she experienced any signs of infection, a large amount of bleeding from the incision(s) or drain(s), fever greater than 101.5 oF, or severe pain not relieved by her medication.

The patient, a male, was admitted to the neurosurgery service due to a two-week history of dizziness and confusion. A CT scan revealed a left occipital mass with surrounding vasogenic edema. The patient also reported a new rash along his right armpit. In the ER, he had a temperature of 100.7 and elevated blood pressure. His past medical history includes hypertension and an old retinal injury. There is no known history of cancer or tumors in his family.

Upon physical examination, the patient was found to have anisocoric pupils with the right larger than the left. His motor functions were intact, and he had sensation to light touch. His wound was clean, dry, and intact with staples in place. 

Lab results showed abnormal levels of WBC (17.0), RBC (4.22), Hct (39.5), MCH (32.5), RDWSD (49.7), PTT (24.0), Glucose (136), and UreaN (21). 

During his hospital stay, the patient underwent an MRI that confirmed the left occipital mass. He was started on Dexamethasone 4mg Q6. A CT torso was completed and showed liver cirrhosis and a possible liver mass. There was also a lucency at the right iliac crest, leading to a recommendation for a bone scan. The patient underwent a stereotactic biopsy, which was uncomplicated. Post-operative NCHCT showed expected post-operative changes. He was started on a 7-day course of Keppra for seizure prophylaxis. His Dexamethasone dose was adjusted to 4mg Q8 prior to discharge. 

A liver MRI revealed findings consistent with cirrhosis and portal hypertension with no evidence of a lesion. Labs were sent to assess liver function. The patient reported a rash below his right armpit down his flank, which was treated with Ketoconazole 2% cream BID x 4 weeks.

Upon admission, the patient was on HCTZ 25mg QD. His discharge diagnoses included a left occipital mass, cerebral edema, liver cirrhosis, and a rash. His mental status was clear and coherent, and he was alert and interactive. He was discharged home with service.

The male patient was admitted to the neurosurgery service due to an acute change in mental status. He had previously undergone a stereotactic brain biopsy, which revealed a grade 4 glioblastoma (GBM). His past medical history includes hypertension and an old retinal injury. There is no known history of cancer or brain tumors in his family.

Upon physical examination, the patient was found to be confused but awake, alert, and cooperative. He was oriented to person and place, but not date, and was unable to perform recall tasks. His language was limited and confused. His pupils were asymmetric, with the right pupil larger than the left. His extraocular movements were intact bilaterally without nystagmus, and facial strength and sensation were intact and symmetric. His motor function appeared normal, but he did not participate in a formal motor exam. There was a small palpable fluid collection at the site of his incision.

During his hospital stay, the patient presented with acute mental status changes. A CT scan of his head revealed an acute left subdural empyema and worsening cerebral edema. He underwent a left hemicraniectomy for decompression and evacuation of the empyema. His white blood cell count was elevated, suggesting a possible infection. He also required platelet transfusions to maintain a platelet goal of 50k.

There was suspicion of seizures, and EEG monitoring confirmed seizure activity. He was treated with Keppra and valproic acid. Despite this, he continued to have electrographic seizures. He was also intubated for surgery and remained so postoperatively, as he was unable to be weaned from pressure support.

The patient was diagnosed with a left subdural empyema, for which he was treated with vancomycin. Blood cultures showed gram-positive cocci in clusters. Palliative care was consulted, and the patient was made DNR. The family decided to pursue comfort measures, and the patient was extubated. He passed away with his family present at his bedside.

Upon admission, the patient was taking dexamethasone, famotidine, insulin, ketoconazole, and hydrochlorothiazide.

The patient, a female of undisclosed age, presented with severe pelvic pain. She has a recent history of diagnostic laparoscopy, diagnostic hysterescopy, and insertion of a Mirena IUD due to severe dysmenorrhea, dyspareunia, irregular menstrual cycles, and significant pelvic pain. No evidence of endometriosis or other pathology was found during surgery. She had previously left the ED before a full evaluation could be undertaken due to the long wait. She has been under significant stress at work and has agreed to seek psychiatric care for her strong anxiety but has not yet done so. 

Her past medical history includes GERD, asthma, fibromyalgia, and suspected anxiety disorder & PTSD. She has no known allergies or adverse drug reactions. Her family history is non-contributory. 

Upon physical examination, she was found to be in no acute distress, comfortable, and ambulatory. Her abdomen was soft, non-tender, with no rebound/guarding, and positive bowel sounds. She had minimal spotting on pad and was voiding spontaneously. Her extremities were non-tender and non-edematous. 

Her blood work showed a WBC of 8.9, RBC of 4.52, Hgb of 13.1, Hct of 39.8, MCV of 88, MCH of 28.9, MCHC of 32.9, RDW of 12.5, and Plt of undisclosed value. A transabdominal and transvaginal sonography was performed to assess IUD placement. The IUD was found to be satisfactorily placed within the endometrial cavity. A simple cyst measuring 5.6 x 3.6 x 3.1 cm was found in the right ovary. The left ovary appeared normal with follicular activity. There was no evidence of torsion or free fluid. 

During her hospital stay, her vital signs remained stable and her WBC was normal. An ultrasound showed a properly placed IUD and a simple, 5 cm right ovarian cyst. There was no evidence of torsion. Serial abdominal exams were benign. Multiple potential etiologies for pain were discussed with the patient, including onset of bleeding for the first time after recent surgery with IUD in place, simple cyst on right ovary, possible cyst rupture/leak leading to pain, and potential for microscopic disease. 

Upon admission, she was on omeprazole, naproxen, Percocet, and Mirena IUD. She was admitted overnight for serial abdominal exams to rule out acute intra-abdominal process. Her pain improved after IV toradol and she was able to rest comfortably overnight. In the morning, she was transitioned to PO naproxen and acetaminophen. Her pain continued to be well controlled. She was discharged home with close outpatient follow-up scheduled. Her discharge diagnosis was pelvic pain and right simple ovarian cyst.

The patient, a male with a medical history of hypertension (HTN), hyperlipidemia (HLD), type 2 diabetes mellitus (DM2), nephrolithiasis, lower back pain (LBP), and multiple sclerosis (MS), presented with a one-day history of fevers, chills, dysuria, and bilateral flank pain. He reported feeling like he was 'losing control' when urinating, experiencing pain and a sense of incomplete voiding. He also experienced abdominal pain, bilateral flank pain, and a fever. His primary care physician suspected a urinary tract infection (UTI), pyelonephritis, or prostatitis and sent him to the emergency department (ED). 

In the ED, his vital signs were recorded and he was found to have bilateral flank tenderness and a non-tender prostate. His labs showed a white blood cell (WBC) count of 23.4 (N:87.2), a hemoglobin (Hgb)/crit of 17.2/49.7, and a lactate level of 3.5. He was given two 1000 mL boluses of normal saline (NS), blood cultures were drawn, and he was administered acetaminophen and ceftriaxone. 

Upon admission, the patient reported continued nausea but denied vomiting. He had difficulty taking deep breaths without pain and experienced small, minimal but frequent attempts at urination with continued dysuria. He denied constipation/diarrhea. He also reported mild burning of his eye a few days prior to admission, which he said usually resolves on its own. 

His physical exam upon admission showed tenderness along the lower flanks, more on the right than the left. His discharge exam showed mild tenderness along the lower right flank. 

Lab results showed an elevated WBC count, neutrophils, lymphocytes, glucose, and lactate levels. His urine culture showed the presence of Escherichia coli. 

During his hospital stay, the patient's condition improved both clinically and on labs; his hemoconcentration resolved and his white count eventually downtrended. His urinary hesitancy and burning dysuria improved with the help of pyridium and tamsulosin. His hypertension was largely controlled during his inpatient stay. 

Upon admission, the patient was on MetFORMIN, GlyBURIDE, Losartan Potassium, Oxybutynin, and Lumigan.

The male patient, with a history of hypertension (HTN), hyperlipidemia (HLD), type 2 diabetes mellitus (DM2), nephrolithiasis, lower back pain (LBP), and multiple sclerosis (MS), was admitted due to fever. He had previously presented with abdominal pain, bilateral flank pain, fever, and urinary urgency. Initial concerns were urinary tract infection (UTI), pyelonephritis, or prostatitis. He was treated with antibiotics and discharged with a prescription for oral ciprofloxacin. Despite compliance with the medication, the patient continued to experience fevers, urinary urgency, and dysuria, leading to readmission. 

Upon readmission, the patient had a temperature of 101.4 and was given ceftriaxone and tylenol. Lab results indicated dehydration and a dirty urinalysis. The patient's mother has a history of HTN, but there is no family history of coronary artery disease, cerebrovascular accident, or diabetes. 

Abnormal lab results included glucose at 265*, sodium at 131*, chloride at 93*, lactate at 2.3*, neutrophils at 81.2*, lymphocytes at 11.0*, and urine red and white blood cells at 4* and 62* respectively. Urine culture showed over 100,000 organisms/ml of Escherichia Coli. Imaging revealed prostatitis with several fluid collections in the prostate, consistent with prostatic abscesses, splenomegaly, hepatic steatosis, and stable bibasilar pulmonary nodules. There was no evidence of pyelonephritis. 

The patient underwent ultrasound-guided drainage of the prostate abscess, with 2 cc of hemo-purulent fluid obtained and sent for microbiology analysis. The fluid showed no growth. The patient's hospital course involved treatment for prostate abscesses and poorly controlled diabetes. He was initially treated with IV ceftriaxone and underwent image-guided drainage of the largest abscess. He was transitioned to oral levofloxacin 24 hours prior to discharge. His diabetes was managed by resuming metformin and adding Glucotrol XL. His hypertension was managed by continuing half of his home dose of losartan due to lower blood pressures on admission, but he was transitioned back to his normal dose upon discharge. 

Upon admission, the patient was taking Lumigan (bimatoprost) 0.01 % ophthalmic hs, Ciprofloxacin HCl 500 mg PO Q12H, GlyBURIDE 2.5 mg PO BID, Losartan Potassium 100 mg PO DAILY, Tamsulosin 0.4 mg PO HS, and MetFORMIN XR (Glucophage XR) 1000 mg PO BID. The patient was discharged with a diagnosis of prostate abscess, secondary to diabetes mellitus and hypertension. His mental status was clear and coherent, and he was alert and interactive. He was ambulatory and independent upon discharge.

The patient is a male with a history of relapsing-remitting multiple sclerosis (RR-MS), insulin-dependent diabetes mellitus (IDDM), prostatic abscesses, and a recent dental abscess. He presented with a 10-day history of fever, chills, bilateral weakness and paresthesias in his legs and arms, and malaise. He had a dental abscess and molar extraction 9 days prior to admission and was prescribed oral amoxicillin, which he reportedly did not take as prescribed. He also reported a 2-week history of night sweats but no unintentional weight loss. His physical examination was notable for clear lungs bilaterally, normal heart sounds, a soft/non-tender abdomen, no CVA tenderness, warm and well-perfused extremities, and strength in all extremities.

Lab results showed an elevated WBC count of 13.8, and a glucose level of 110. Urine studies showed trace leukocytes, negative nitrites, 3 WBCs, no bacteria, and less than 1 epithelial. Chest X-ray demonstrated no acute cardiopulmonary abnormality. ECG showed a rate of 72bpm, sinus rhythm, left axis deviation, normal intervals, poor R wave progression, left ventricular hypertrophy (LVH), ST slurring in I/aVL, and T wave inversion in III/aVR. 

The patient was given IV Cefepime, IV Vancomycin, Pregabalin 150mg, Oxycodone 10mg, and Acetaminophen 1000mg. His condition improved in terms of strength and malaise. 

His past medical history includes four flares of RR-MS, neuropathy, urinary hesitancy, incomplete voiding, fatigue, snoring, witnessed apneic events, dragging of left foot at baseline, nephrolithiasis, prostatic abscess/prostatitis, resection of basal cell carcinomas and removal of a hemangioma from his lip, diabetes mellitus type II, hypertension, psoriasis, obstructive sleep apnea (OSA) on CPAP, and low back pain. His family history includes hypertension in his mother, with no history of coronary artery disease, cerebrovascular accident, or diabetes in the family.

Admission labs showed an elevated HbA1c of 7.5 and eAG of 169. Other abnormal findings included glucose of 172, WBC of 13.5, neutrophils of 75.9, lymphocytes of 11.7, absolute neutrophils of 10.20, and absolute monocytes of 1.00. Imaging findings showed no acute cardiopulmonary abnormality, no drainable fluid collections or foci of abnormal enhancement to suggest residual dental abscess, mild symmetric left ventricular hypertrophy with normal cavity size and regional/global biventricular systolic function, stable 1 mm nonobstructive calculus in lower pole of the right kidney, and small area of ground-glass opacity in the right lung base compatible with infectious etiology. Microbiology results showed no growth in urine and blood cultures. 

The patient was discharged with a primary diagnosis of community-acquired pneumonia and relapsing-remitting multiple sclerosis, and secondary diagnoses of type 2 diabetes mellitus, chronic pain, hypertension, and hyperlipidemia. His discharge medications included Cetirizine 10 mg, Levofloxacin 750 mg, Atorvastatin 10 mg, Ducodyl (bisacodyl) 5 mg, HYDROcodone-Acetaminophen (5mg-325mg), Glargine 26 Units, Losartan Potassium 100 mg, MetFORMIN (Glucophage) 500 mg, Pregabalin 150 mg, Triamcinolone Acetonide 0.1% Cream, Victoza 2-Pak (liraglutide) 1.2 mg, and Vitamin D 5000 UNIT.

The patient, a male with a history of relapsing-remitting multiple sclerosis, hypertension, hyperlipidemia, benign prostatic hyperplasia (BPH), insulin-dependent diabetes type II, diabetic peripheral neuropathy, obstructive sleep apnea (OSA), kidney stones, and a prior prostatic abscess, presented with a fever and dysuria. He had been experiencing these symptoms for three days, along with reddish-tinged urine, fatigue, flank pain, and burning during urination. He had been prescribed ciprofloxacin for acute complicated cystitis but continued to have fevers. He also reported a loss of appetite and a weight loss of 60 lbs. 

Upon admission, his vitals were stable, and he was alert and in no apparent distress. His physical exam was largely unremarkable, with no signs of distress or acute illness. However, he had decreased sensation to light touch in both lower extremities and a small area of erythema on his right second toe. 

Lab results on admission showed an elevated white blood cell count (27.6*), neutrophils (83.6*), and glucose (197*), along with decreased lymphocytes (5.6*), sodium (134*), chloride (94*), bicarbonate (21*), and an elevated anion gap (19*). His lactate level was also elevated at 2.3*. On discharge, his white blood cell count had decreased to 14.0*, and his glucose was still elevated at 183*. Other notable labs included an elevated C-reactive protein (276.6*) and prostate-specific antigen (PSA, 34.2*). 

Microbiology results showed 10,000-50,000 CFU/mL of Enterococcus faecalis in his urine. Imaging studies, including a chest X-ray and CT of the abdomen/pelvis, did not reveal any acute processes. However, the CT did show a focus of ground-glass opacity in the posterior right lower lobe of the lung, which was unchanged from previous imaging, and prostatomegaly. A prostate ultrasound showed an enlarged prostate consistent with BPH but no evidence of prostatic or periprostatic abscess or drainable fluid collection. 

During his hospital stay, the patient was diagnosed with sepsis, which resolved, and an Enterococcus urinary tract infection. His leukocytosis improved, and his diabetic neuropathy/neuropathic pain was managed. His type 2 diabetes mellitus required higher doses of Lantus insulin while inpatient due to the unavailability of Victoza. His hypertension was managed with Losartan, and his hyperphosphatemia improved without intervention. His constipation improved with PRN Bisacodyl, Senna, and Miralax, and his high-density lipoprotein (HDL) was managed with atorvastatin. His elevated INR was stable and considered a chronic problem. His depression was noted, and his wife planned to have him see a psychiatric nurse. His hyponatremia and anion gap metabolic acidosis resolved. 

Upon discharge, the patient was prescribed a 21-day course of Augmentin for his urinary tract infection, among other medications. He was instructed to follow up with his primary care doctor one week after discharge. He was also scheduled for home physical therapy to assist with walking. His discharge diagnoses included sepsis, Enterococcus urinary tract infection, leukocytosis, type 2 diabetes mellitus, hypertension, neuropathic pain, depression, and anion gap metabolic acidosis.

The patient, a middle-aged female, was admitted due to a severe headache, which she described as similar to her previous migraines but worse. She has a history of chronic migraines, chronic lower back pain, and endometriosis. She also reported experiencing muscle spasms and episodic upper back pain. The patient's mother is blind due to a rare genetic disorder. 

Upon admission, the patient was in distress and her vital signs were recorded as 98.2 76 ___ 98%. She was given Toradol, Reglan, Zofran, and Dilaudid in the ED, but her pain did not significantly improve. An hour after receiving Dilaudid 1mg IV, her pain was reduced and she was able to eat dinner. Her nausea was well controlled and she was not vomiting. 

The patient's medications upon admission included Acetaminophen-Codeine, Magnesium/Feverfew/Riboflavin, Naratriptan [Amerge], Ondansetron, and Zonisamide. She was also taking over-the-counter Lactobacillus Rhamnosus GG [Probiotic], Multivitamin-Iron-Folic Acid [Centrum Ultra Women's], and Omega-3 Fatty Acids-Fish Oil [Omega 3 Fish Oil].

Lab results showed abnormal values for Hct-34.9*, MCV-80*, MCHC-38.2*, RDW-15.7*, Neuts-85.7*, Lymphs-12.1*, Monos-1.6*, and Glucose-104*. The patient was found to have microcytic anemia, likely due to iron deficiency. 

During her hospital stay, the patient's headache was treated with a 6-day prednisone taper and Depakote 500mg IV x1. Within 12 hours of this therapy, her headache was gone and she was drastically improved and ready to go home. She was discharged with a diagnosis of intractable migraine headache. Her mental status was clear and coherent, and she was alert, interactive, and able to walk independently.

The patient, a female, was admitted due to post-procedural pain and nausea following a Y-90 ablation of cholangiocarcinoma. She was found to have a liver mass during a previous admission for urosepsis and renal dysfunction. The liver mass was confirmed to be cholangiocarcinoma through a liver MRI and ultrasound-guided targeted biopsy. The patient has a history of heavy alcohol consumption but has never been diagnosed with liver disease or cirrhosis. She tested negative for hepatitis C and B. She reported feeling generally well with no signs or symptoms from her disease. 

Her past medical history includes intracranial meningioma, bipolar disorder, depressive disorder, anxiety, alcoholism, benign positional vertigo, hypertension, left ventricular hypertrophy, chronic back pain, and osteoporosis. She has undergone several surgical procedures including tubal ligation, gastric bypass, total abdominal hysterectomy, and tonsillectomy. 

The patient's family history includes her mother being diagnosed with lung cancer and her father dying from gastric cancer and lung cancer. 

Upon admission, the patient was on several medications including Ondansetron, Lisinopril, Hydrocortisone cream, Lamotrigine, Lorazepam, Diphenhydramine, Ferrous Sulfate, Pantoprazole, Calcium Carbonate, Hydrochlorothiazide, Folic Acid, Pyridoxine, Riboflavin, Cyanocobalamin, Ascorbic Acid, Vitamin D, and Bupropion. 

The patient's physical examination revealed mild tenderness in the abdomen. Her lab results showed abnormal values for RBC, Hgb, Hct, MCV, MCH, RDWSD, AlkPhos, Calcium, and Phos. 

During her hospital stay, she underwent a Y-90 ablation for her cholangiocarcinoma. Post-procedure, she complained of severe abdominal pain, which was managed with IV dilaudid. By the time of discharge, she was tolerating a regular diet, mobile, and only required oral dilaudid for pain control. 

The patient was discharged with a diagnosis of cholangiocarcinoma post Y-90 ablation complicated by pain/post embolization syndrome. Her mental status was clear and coherent, and she was alert, interactive, and independently ambulatory. She was advised to follow up with her gastroenterologist and the tumor clinic for further management of her condition.

The patient is a female with a history of underlying liver disease, recent diagnosis of poorly differentiated cholangiocarcinoma, and recent treatment with Y90 radioembolization and portal vein embolization. She was admitted due to an episode of syncope. She reported feeling dizzy and lightheaded before blacking out and waking up on the floor. She had a laceration on her left chin, which was sutured. She had no chest pain, palpitations, or shortness of breath prior to the fall. She had been experiencing severe dehydration and lightheadedness due to a recent episode of colitis, which had resolved. She had also been experiencing poor appetite, abdominal pain when trying to eat, and significant weight loss.

Upon admission, her vitals were 98.6 91 138/64 24 96% RA. She appeared dehydrated and had right-sided abdominal tenderness. Labs showed WBC 4.4, H/H 7.7/23.9, Plt 191, Na 133, BUN/Cr ___, ALP 1103, albumin 2.6, lactate 1.0, INR 1.0, and bland UA. A CT scan showed diffuse anasarca with ascites and pleural effusions, as well as wall thickening of the cecal and descending colon. A CXR showed small bilateral pleural effusions. 

Her past medical history includes intracranial meningioma, bipolar disorder, depressive disorder, anxiety, alcoholism, BPPV, hypertension, left ventricular hypertrophy, chronic back pain, osteoporosis, and several surgeries including tubal ligation, RNY gastric bypass, total abdominal hysterectomy, and tonsillectomy. Her family history includes lung cancer in her mother and gastric cancer in her father.

Her medications upon admission included bupropion, lamotrigine, lorazepam, ondansetron, and acetaminophen. 

Abnormal lab results included WBC 2.9*, RBC 2.26*, Hgb 7.7*, Hct 24.0*, MCV 106*, MCH 34.1*, RDW 19.5*, RDWSD 75.2*, AlkPhos 1047*, GGT 306*, and Albumin 2.3*. Imaging showed diffuse anasarca, large ascites, bilateral moderate nonhemorrhagic pleural effusions, subcutaneous edema, wall thickening of the cecum and mid to distal descending colon, and a large known liver mass. 

Conditions and symptoms that have been ruled out include acute ischemia, hemorrhage, new masses, acute colitis, and intrathoracic metastasis. The patient's cancer progression or metastasis remains uncertain.

The patient, a female, was admitted due to weakness, malnutrition, and failure to thrive. She has a primary diagnosis of T1N0Mx cholangiocarcinoma and has undergone Y90 radioembolization and portal vein embolization. She reported an inability to eat, which has led to marked weakness and nearly complete failure to thrive. She also reported increasing abdominal bloating, nausea, and pain over the last several days. She has shortness of breath, but no cough or chest pain. She also reported some urinary incontinence due to inability to get to the commode. 

Her past medical history includes intracranial meningioma, bipolar disorder, depressive disorder, anxiety, prior alcoholism, benign paroxysmal positional vertigo, hypertension, left ventricular hypertrophy, chronic back pain, osteoporosis, tubal ligation, gastric RNY bypass, total abdominal hysterectomy, and tonsillectomy. She has a family history of lung cancer in her mother and gastric cancer in her father. 

Upon admission, she was on several medications including Acetaminophen, BuPROPion, LamoTRIgine, FoLIC Acid, HYDROmorphone, Multivitamins, Thiamine, Ondansetron, and LORazepam. 

Her lab results showed several abnormalities including low WBC, RBC, Hgb, Hct, MCV, MCH, and high RDW, RDWSD. Her AST, AlkPhos, Albumin, Calcium, Ferritn, VitB12, and TSH levels were also abnormal. Her imaging results showed bilateral layering pleural effusions, a large liver mass, and wall thickening of the cecum and mid to distal descending colon. 

During her hospital stay, she was diagnosed with severe protein-calorie malnutrition, anasarca with ascites and pleural effusions, frequent bowel movements, right upper quadrant pain, anemia, and depression/bipolar disorder. She was given 1u RBC for her low Hgb level. Her TPN was initiated and she was discharged with TPN. She was also given remeron for appetite stimulation and ritalin during daytime to augment mood. 

She was discharged to rehab for physical therapy. Her discharge diagnosis included failure to thrive, anasarca, severe protein-calorie malnutrition, T1N0Mx cholangiocarcinoma, and major depression.

The patient was admitted for a liver biopsy due to a 6.8 cm hepatic mass suggestive of cholangiocarcinoma. The patient had a history of intracranial meningioma, bipolar disorder, depressive disorder, anxiety, prior alcoholism, benign paroxysmal positional vertigo, hypertension, left ventricular hypertrophy, chronic back pain, osteoporosis, tubal ligation, gastric RNY bypass, total abdominal hysterectomy, and tonsillectomy. The patient's family history includes lung cancer in the mother and gastric cancer in the father.

During the hospital stay, the patient experienced complications including a left-sided pleural effusion, likely hepatic hydrothorax, causing significant dyspnea. This required chest tube placement. The patient also experienced significant pain, which was partially alleviated with the removal of the chest tube. Blood cultures on admission were positive for S. epidermidis, likely due to contamination from a PICC line.

The patient's past oncologic history includes urosepsis and renal dysfunction, during which a liver mass was discovered. A liver MRI showed a 6.8 cm right hepatic mass. An ultrasound-guided targeted biopsy of the liver mass was consistent with a poorly differentiated adenocarcinoma. The patient underwent Yttrium-90 radioembolization but declined neoadjuvant chemotherapy prior to planned surgical resection. The patient was admitted with syncope and weakness in the setting of recent colitis and dehydration. Noted to be anasarcic with new pleural effusions and ascites, ascitic fluid did not show malignant cells. Upcoming surgical resection was deferred due to nutritional status, weakness, and further evaluation with oncology.

Abnormal lab results include RBC-2.59*, Hgb-8.7*, Hct-26.5*, MCV-102*, MCH-33.6*, RDW-16.5*, RDWSD-61.1*, UreaN-25*, AST-62*, AlkPhos-1067*, TotProt-5.2*, Albumin-2.1*, and Calcium-7.5*. Cytology results were negative for malignant cells in both peritoneal and pleural fluid. Liver biopsy showed preserved architecture and features suggestive of nodular regenerative hyperplasia, minimal steatosis, minimal portal and lobular mononuclear inflammation, sinusoidal and focal mild portal fibrosis, and minimal iron deposition in hepatocytes. Microbiology results showed Staphylococcus epidermidis in the blood culture, resistant to Clindamycin, Erythromycin, Levofloxacin, and Oxacillin, but sensitive to Gentamicin, Rifampin, and Vancomycin. No growth was found in the pleural fluid culture.

The patient's medications upon admission included Bupropion, Ipratropium-Albuterol Neb, Acetaminophen, Hydromorphone, Lamotrigine, Lorazepam, Methylphenidate, Mirtazapine, and Loperamide. The patient was allergic to Celebrex, Norflex, Tylenol-Codeine #3, and nitroglycerin.

The patient, a female, was admitted for surgery due to cholangiocarcinoma. She underwent a diagnostic laparoscopy, right hepatic trisegmentectomy, and intraoperative cholangiography. The patient had previously been treated for intrahepatic cholangiocarcinoma with Y90 embolization and right portal vein embolization. However, surgery was initially deferred due to liver decompensation and concerns for malnutrition. Her condition improved and she was deemed suitable for resection. Her last staging MR showed no evidence of extrahepatic disease and the tumor was mainly confined to the right lobe of the liver with some encroachment on segment 4. 

The patient's past medical history includes intracranial meningioma, bipolar disorder, depressive disorder, anxiety, prior alcoholism, benign paroxysmal positional vertigo, hypertension, left ventricular hypertrophy, chronic back pain, osteoporosis, tubal ligation, gastric RNY bypass, total abdominal hysterectomy, and tonsillectomy. She has a family history of lung cancer in her mother and gastric cancer in her father.

Upon admission, the patient was on several medications including bupropion, dronabinol, folic acid, hydromorphone, lamotrigine, Viokace, sodium bicarbonate, lorazepam, acetaminophen, vitamin D, loperamide, and milk of magnesia. 

During her hospital stay, she required a unit of prbc for hct of 24.5 that increased to 27.7 after the transfusion. She reported pain post-operation and was lethargic and mildly confused on postop day. She developed dysuria and a urine culture grew > 100,000 Klebsiella, sensitive to Bactrim. She was hypertensive with SBP in the 170s and was given hydralazine. Her LFTs initially increased as expected and bilirubin was as high as 3.7 ALT and AST increased following the second surgery to 1287 and 2331 respectively. These were decreasing daily, as well as the bilirubin slowly decreasing, and down to 3 by the day of discharge. 

The patient was having difficulty with maintaining oral intake due to no interest in food, lack of appetite. A feeding tube was inserted and she was started on tube feeds. She was evaluated by physical therapy who recommended she go to rehab in the post-op period. Her JP drain was removed prior to discharge. Her home regimen of medications was restarted, Ativan dose was decreased due to the liver surgery, and narcotic pain medication was stopped to help with mental status. 

Abnormal lab results include WBC-10.3*#, RBC-2.66*#, Hgb-8.8*#, Hct-26.9*#, MCV-101*, MCH-33.1*, MCHC-32.7, RDWSD-54.5*, Glucose-151*, Cl-109*, HCO3-21*, Calcium-8.0*, RBC-2.87*, Hgb-9.2*, Hct-26.7*, MCH-32.1*, RDW-18.4*, RDWSD-59.9*, Glucose-108*, Na-131*, ALT-151*, AST-72*, AlkPhos-683*, TotBili-3.0*, Albumin-2.9*, and Calcium-8.2*.
