Upon discharge, the patient was diagnosed with cholangiocarcinoma, UTI, bipolar disorder, and moderate malnutrition. She was clear and coherent, alert and interactive, but required assistance or aid for ambulation. She was discharged to a rehab facility.

The patient is a female known to our service for a history of intrahepatic cholangiocarcinoma. She has undergone Y90 embolization, right portal vein embolization, and trisegmentectomy. She was last seen in the outpatient clinic where she reported an improved appetite and denied any abdominal pain, nausea, vomiting, or fevers. A follow-up MRCP demonstrated a 9.9 cm postoperative collection with a small 1.4 cm component along its posterior aspect, likely seroma versus biloma. She was admitted for percutaneous drainage.

Her past medical history includes intracranial meningioma, bipolar disorder, depressive disorder, anxiety, prior alcoholism, benign paroxysmal positional vertigo, hypertension, left ventricular hypertrophy, chronic back pain, osteoporosis, tubal ligation, gastric RNY bypass, total abdominal hysterectomy, and tonsillectomy. Her family history includes lung cancer in her mother and gastric cancer in her father.

Upon admission, her labs showed abnormal RBC count (2.99), Hgb (9.9), Hct (30.6), MCV (102), MCH (33.1), RDW (15.6), RDWSD (58.7), Glucose (112), ALT (61), AST (61), and AlkPhos (1436). At discharge, her labs showed abnormal RBC count (3.12), Hgb (10.2), MCH (32.7), RDWSD (49.8), UreaN (4), AlkPhos (1011), and Calcium (8.3).

During her stay, she underwent ultrasound-guided drainage of the collection along the resection margin, ERCP, and EUS Directed transgastric ERCP with stent placement. The ERCP was terminated due to difficulty in cannulating the CBD due to her history of Roux-en-Y gastric bypass. The EUS Directed transgastric ERCP revealed a high-grade bile leak, and an 8mm x 60mm uncovered Wallflex metal stent was successfully placed in the common bile duct. She experienced significant abdominal pain following the procedure, and her lipase was found to be 5395. She was kept NPO and on IV fluids for the next two days, and her pain resolved and lipase returned to normal.

Her medications upon admission included bupropion, dronabinol, folic acid, hydromorphone, lamotrigine, and lorazepam. A CT scan prior to discharge showed a significant decrease in the size of the fluid collection along the trisegmentectomy margin, and the CBD stent was in place. She was discharged to home with assistance for the pigtail catheter flushing, monitoring, and dressing changes.

The patient, a female with a history of intrahepatic cholangiocarcinoma, intracranial meningioma, bipolar disorder, depressive disorder, anxiety, prior alcoholism, benign paroxysmal positional vertigo, hypertension, left ventricular hypertrophy, chronic back pain, osteoporosis, gastric RNY bypass, total abdominal hysterectomy, and tonsillectomy, was admitted due to a bile leak. She had previously undergone a right trisegmentectomy and had been discharged with a drain in place. However, she presented with several days of malaise and increased drain output, along with symptoms of poor appetite, fatigue, pain around the drain, weight loss, shortness of breath, subjective fevers, and dizziness when standing.

During her hospital stay, she underwent several procedures including the placement of a biliary drain, a Dobhoff tube, and multiple upsizing of the PTBD due to leakage. Her lab results showed several abnormalities including elevated WBC, RBC, MCH, RDWSD, Plt Ct, Neuts, Lymphs, Monos, Eos, UreaN, Creat, Na, K, Cl, HCO3, AnGap, Calcium, AST, AlkPhos, and Lipase. Her bile culture showed moderate growth of gram-negative rods and sparse growth of Clostridium perfringens. 

The patient's hospital course was complicated by persistent nausea and vomiting, poor appetite, and high drain output. She was treated with IV fluids, Vancomycin, Zosyn, Zofran, and supplements. Her LFTs showed a decrease in alk phos from 1000 on admission to 600s. She also had a dobhoff tube placed and jevity tf started with a goal rate of 45cc/hour continuous. Despite these interventions, she continued to have drainage around the PTBD insertion site. 

The patient also experienced increased depression due to her prolonged hospital stay and multiple procedures. She was started on Remeron 7.5mg at HS with improvement in mood and sleep. She also fell out of bed without sustaining any injury but experienced slight dizziness with shaky legs and felt her legs buckle. Her pain was managed with Tylenol, Neurontin, and Fentanyl patch with break thru oral dilaudid. 

Her family history includes lung cancer in her mother and gastric cancer in her father. Her medications upon admission included bupropion, dronabinol, folic acid, hydromorphone, lamotrigine, and lorazepam.

The patient, a female, was admitted due to weakness and increased PTBD output. She has a history of intrahepatic cholangiocarcinoma, for which she underwent a right trisegmentectomy. She was recently admitted for increasing drain output and continual leakage around the PTBD drain, which required upsizing. She now reports worsening fatigue, nausea, decreased oral intake, weakness, dizziness with standing, and increased drainage from around her PTBD site. She denies fevers/chills, chest pain/shortness of breath, jaundice/pruritis, and dysuria.

Her past medical history includes intracranial meningioma, bipolar disorder, depressive disorder, anxiety, prior alcoholism, benign paroxysmal positional vertigo, hypertension, left ventricular hypertrophy, chronic back pain, osteoporosis, tubal ligation, gastric RNY bypass, total abdominal hysterectomy, and tonsillectomy. Her family history includes lung cancer in her mother and gastric cancer in her father.

During her stay, a feeding tube was placed due to severe malnutrition. However, it inadvertently came out and was replaced the following day. She was on Unasyn during this admission with the drain manipulations. She remained afebrile and her urine and blood cultures showed no growth after 5 days.

Her medications upon admission included bupropion, dronabinol, lamotrigine, lorazepam, docusate sodium, pantoprazole, polyethylene glycol, senna, ursodiol, acetaminophen, gabapentin, mirtazapine, amoxicillin-clavulanic acid, amlodipine, and hydromorphone.

Abnormal lab results on admission included WBC-3.6, RBC-2.84, Hgb-9.8, Hct-29.6, MCV-104, MCH-34.5, RDWSD-57.6, AST-46, and AlkPhos-1037. At discharge, abnormal lab results included WBC-2.5, RBC-2.53, Hgb-8.5, Hct-26.7, MCV-106, MCH-33.6, MCHC-31.8, RDWSD-55.2, Glucose-107, AlkPhos-654, Calcium-8.3, and Phos-4.7.

Her discharge diagnoses were left biliary stenosis, continued short-segment stenosis along the third portion of the duodenum, and severe malnutrition. She was discharged home with service.

The patient, a female with a history of intrahepatic cholangiocarcinoma, was admitted due to bile leakage around her percutaneous transhepatic biliary drainage (PTBD) site and weakness. She had previously undergone a right trisegmentectomy, which was complicated by a bile leak from the cut liver edge, requiring endoscopic retrograde cholangiopancreatography (ERCP) stenting and percutaneous drainage. She had been recently hospitalized for increased drain output and continual leakage around the PTBD drain, which required upsizing. She reported worsening fatigue, nausea, decreased oral intake, weakness, dizziness with standing, and increased bilious drainage from around her PTBD site. 

Her past medical history includes intracranial meningioma, bipolar disorder, depressive disorder, anxiety, prior alcoholism, benign paroxysmal positional vertigo, hypertension, left ventricular hypertrophy, chronic back pain, osteoporosis, tubal ligation, gastric RNY bypass, total abdominal hysterectomy, and tonsillectomy. She has a family history of lung cancer in her mother and gastric cancer in her father.

Upon physical examination, she was found to be tender to palpation at the midline PTBD drain site with minimal surrounding erythema. The dressing was saturated with clear bilious output. She was discharged in stable condition with a diagnosis of bile leak and severe malnutrition.

The patient, a female, was admitted due to weakness and leakage around her percutaneous transhepatic biliary drainage (PTBD) site. She has a history of intrahepatic cholangiocarcinoma, for which she underwent a right trisegmentectomy. This was complicated by a bile leak from the cut liver edge, requiring ERCP/stenting and percutaneous drainage. She has had multiple admissions for increasing drain output and has had her PTBD replaced three times. She has been on tube feeds and taking Augmentin since her last discharge. She denies experiencing fevers, chills, chest pain, shortness of breath, jaundice, pruritis, or dysuria.

Her past medical history includes intracranial meningioma, bipolar disorder, depressive disorder, anxiety, prior alcoholism, benign paroxysmal positional vertigo, hypertension, left ventricular hypertrophy, chronic back pain, osteoporosis, gastric RNY bypass, total abdominal hysterectomy, and tonsillectomy. She has also undergone multiple ERCP/PTBD placements and exchanges. Her family history includes lung cancer in her mother and gastric cancer in her father.

Upon physical examination, she appeared tired and had tenderness to palpation at the midline PTBD drain site with minimal surrounding erythema. Her dressing was saturated with clear bilious output. Her subhepatic drain site was clean, dry, and intact with minimal thick cloudy output. She had no rebound or guarding and no palpable masses. Her vitals were stable.

Her admission labs showed abnormal RBC, Hgb, Hct, MCV, MCH, MCHC, RDWSD, and glucose levels. Her AlkPhos was also elevated. Her discharge labs showed similar abnormalities, with a decrease in her Hgb level.

During her stay, she underwent a cholangiogram which showed a decompressed biliary tree and no evidence of a bile leak when the percutaneous biliary drain was injected. However, a small persistent leak was noted upon injection of the subhepatic drain. She had successful exchanges of her PTBD catheters. Despite this, she continued to have significant drainage around the PTBD, and wound/ostomy was called for assistance with placement of an ostomy device around the PTBD for skin preservation. This started to leak under the appliance within about 12 hours.

Her medications upon admission included Acetaminophen, Multivitamins W/minerals, amLODIPine, BuPROPion, Docusate Sodium, Dronabinol, Gabapentin, HYDROmorphone, LamoTRIgine, LORazepam, Mirtazapine, Pantoprazole, Polyethylene Glycol, Senna, and Ursodiol.

The patient, a female with a history of intrahepatic cholangiocarcinoma, was admitted due to a persistent bile leak following a right trisegmentectomy. The bile leak had resulted in dehydration and difficulty with nutrition. She reported constant pain from her drain and frequent leaking of bile around the right drain and the percutaneous transhepatic biliary drainage (PTBD). She denied experiencing fever, chills, shortness of breath, nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, or dysuria.

Her past medical history includes intrahepatic cholangiocarcinoma, intracranial meningioma, bipolar disorder, depressive disorder, anxiety, prior alcoholism, benign paroxysmal positional vertigo, hypertension, left ventricular hypertrophy, chronic back pain, osteoporosis, gastric RNY bypass, total abdominal hysterectomy, and tonsillectomy. She has also undergone multiple ERCP/ PTBD placements and exchanges. Her family history includes lung cancer in her mother and gastric cancer in her father.

During her hospital stay, she underwent an exploratory laparotomy with extensive lysis of adhesions, Roux-en-Y hepaticojejunostomy to intrahepatic bile duct, removal of previous pigtail drain and percutaneous biliary drain, and placement of a new Silastic transhepatic biliary drain. Postoperatively, she experienced low urine output and hypotension, which improved with IV fluid and IV Albumin administration. She also experienced a decrease in hematocrit, which was managed with PRBC transfusion.

Her lab results showed an increase in liver function tests, with total bilirubin reaching up to 6.3. She also tested positive for Enterobacter Cloacae in her blood cultures and Clostridium Difficile in her stool. Her blood work also showed abnormal values for RBC (2.56*), Hgb (8.1*), Hct (24.8*), RDW (21.2*), RDWSD (73.1*), Glucose (112*), UreaN (14), and HCO3 (27). Her ALT, AST, LD(LDH), AlkPhos, and TotBili were also elevated at various points during her stay.

She was treated with Ciprofloxacin and Flagyl for the Enterobacter Cloacae bacteremia, and oral Vancomycin for the C.diff infection. She also developed myoclonus and choreiform-like leg movements, which were managed by decreasing her Gabapentin and Dilaudid doses. Her discharge diagnoses included bile leak, malnutrition, incision cellulitis, C.diff, Enterobacter cloacae bacteremia, depression, and resolved myoclonus.

Upon admission, her medications included amLODIPine, Amoxicillin-Clavulanic Acid, BuPROPion XL, FoLIC Acid, Gabapentin, HYDROmorphone (Dilaudid), LamoTRIgine, LORazepam, Mirtazapine, Ursodiol, Acetaminophen, Vitamin D, Multivitamins W/minerals, Polyethylene Glycol, and Pantoprazole.

The patient, a female, was admitted due to fevers and bile leakage from her midline incision. She has a history of intrahepatic cholangiocarcinoma, which was treated with Y-90 embolization and portal vein embolization, followed by right liver trisegmentectomy. She experienced a persistent bile leak, which required several months of percutaneous treatment and ultimately surgical intervention. She underwent a Roux-en-Y jejunostomy to an exposed segment bile duct. Her post-operative course was marked by C. difficile infection, Enterobacter cloacae bacteremia, malnutrition, and incision cellulitis. She was discharged on antibiotics (ciprofloxacin), which she has since completed. Her bile drain was capped and tube feeds were discontinued. 

She presented with ongoing bile drainage from the drain site, swelling of the anterior portion of her midline incision, associated erythema and warmth, fevers at home, and generalized malaise. She reported poor oral intake due to feeling unwell, but denied nausea/emesis. She reported ongoing non-bloody diarrhea and was completing a course of oral antibiotics for C.diff. She denied chest pain, shortness of breath, dysuria, or urinary frequency. 

Her past medical history includes intracranial meningioma, bipolar disorder, depressive disorder, anxiety, prior alcoholism, benign paroxysmal positional vertigo, hypertension, left ventricular hypertrophy, chronic back pain, osteoporosis, tubal ligation, gastric RNY bypass, total abdominal hysterectomy, tonsillectomy, diagnostic laparoscopy, right hepatic trisegmentectomy, intraoperative cholangiography with takeback for hemorrhage, and multiple ERCP/ PTBD placement and exchanges. Her family history includes lung cancer in her mother and gastric cancer in her father.

Upon admission, her vitals were T101.8 HR116 BP128/80 RR18 96% RA. Her physical exam showed erythema, fluctuance with central dehiscence and active drainage of bile from her midline incision, and bile draining from JP site left lateral to midline incision, staining skin. Her labs on admission showed abnormal WBC, RBC, Hgb, Hct, MCV, MCH, RDWSD, Na, Cl, ALT, AST, AlkPhos, TotBili, DirBili, and Albumin. A CT scan showed a 5.2 x 1.6 x 6.6 cm collection of gas and bile in the anterior abdominal wall, which appears contiguous with the entry site/tract of the previously removed percutaneous biliary drain, which may represent the source for bile leak. The liver did not show evidence of biliary dilatation.

Her medications upon admission included bupropion, ciprofloxacin, furosemide, gabapentin, hydromorphone, lamotrigine, lorazepam, mirtazapine, pantoprazole, spironolactone, ursodiol, vancomycin oral liquid, acetaminophen, vitamin D, and multivitamins with minerals. 

She was discharged with a diagnosis of wound dehiscence and wound infection. Her mental status was clear and coherent, and she was alert and interactive. She was instructed to call her doctor for any concerning symptoms, to shower but not apply lotions or powders to the incision areas, to cover the incision with a dry gauze and change as needed, not to lift more than 10 pounds, and not to drive if taking narcotic pain medication.

The patient, a female with a history of intrahepatic cholangiocarcinoma, was admitted due to drainage from the sub-xiphoid area. She had previously undergone Y-90 embolization, portal vein embolization, and R liver trisegmentectomy, and had been dealing with a persistent bile leak. She also had microwave ablation of metastatic lesions in her remnant liver. She developed skin breakdown at the subxiphoid puncture site with bilious drainage over the past 6 weeks, which was consistent with a biliocutaneous fistula. She reported tenderness around the wound and some yellow/green drainage on the gauze sponge, but denied fever, chills, abdominal pain, nausea, vomiting, or diarrhea.

Her past medical history includes intracranial meningioma, bipolar disorder, depressive disorder, anxiety, prior alcoholism, benign paroxysmal positional vertigo, hypertension, left ventricular hypertrophy, chronic back pain, osteoporosis, tubal ligation, gastric RNY bypass, total abdominal hysterectomy, and tonsillectomy. She has also undergone diagnostic laparoscopy, right hepatic trisegmentectomy, intraoperative cholangiography with takeback for hemorrhage, and multiple ERCP/ PTBD placement and exchanges. Her family history includes lung cancer in her mother and gastric cancer in her father.

Upon admission, her medications included amlodipine, bupropion, folic acid, furosemide, hydromorphone, mirtazapine, ursodiol, vitamin D, and multivitamins with minerals. Her physical exam revealed a quarter-sized opening at the superior aspect of her midline incision with yellow/green drainage on gauze sponge, mildly tender to palpation around the wound, with some skin irritation surrounding.

Lab results showed abnormal RBC, Hgb, Hct, MCV, MCH, RDW, RDWSD, and glucose levels. Her AlkPhos and Calcium levels were also abnormal. A swab from her abdominal wound showed 1+ polymorphonuclear leukocytes and gram-positive cocci, with sparse growth of Enterococcus sp. 

During her hospital stay, she was started on IV Vancomycin. Her drainage became less bilious and the PTBD was cancelled. A wound culture was obtained that isolated sparse enterococcus felt to be a contaminant. A VAC dressing was placed that was removed the next day as the drainage was minimal and purulent that was too thick for the VAC. Drainage was thick/very light yellow/green. Normal saline wet to dry dressing changes were done. She declined tube feeds and preferred to try to eat as well possible and drink the Ensure Clears. MVI with minerals twice daily was started.

Upon discharge, her diagnosis included an enterocutaneous fistula, cholangio CA, and malnutrition. She was discharged to home in stable condition.

The patient, a female with a history of intrahepatic cholangiocarcinoma, was admitted due to shortness of breath. She had previously undergone Y-90 embolization, portal vein embolization, and right liver trisegmentectomy, and had been managing a biliocutaneous fistula of the subxiphiod region at home. She also reported recent diarrhea and loss of appetite. Her past medical history includes intracranial meningioma, bipolar disorder, depressive disorder, anxiety, prior alcoholism, benign paroxysmal positional vertigo, hypertension, left ventricular hypertrophy, chronic back pain, osteoporosis, gastric RNY bypass, total abdominal hysterectomy, and tonsillectomy. She has a family history of lung cancer in her mother and gastric cancer in her father.

Upon admission, the patient's physical exam revealed clear breath sounds in the left chest, absent breath sounds in the right hemithorax, and tenderness around the biliary cutaneous fistula. Her vitals were stable, and she was alert and oriented. Her medications upon admission included amlodipine, bupropion, folic acid, furosemide, mirtazapine, hydromorphone, ursodiol, vitamin D, and multivitamins with minerals.

During her hospital stay, the patient underwent a right thoracentesis, which improved her shortness of breath. Her blood tests showed abnormal values for RBC (2.75*), Hgb (10.1*), Hct (29.4*), MCV (107*), MCH (36.7*), RDWSD (60.2*), ALT (69*), AST (124*), AlkPhos (643*), TotBili (2.0*), and TotProt (6.0*). The patient was discharged in her typical state of health with a diagnosis of right pleural effusion, right diaphragmatic hernia, and cholangio carcinoma. She was instructed to follow up with thoracic surgery regarding the possible repair of her diaphragmatic hernia.

The patient, a female, was admitted for a diaphragmatic hernia. She has a history of cholangiocarcinoma, which was treated with Y90 embolization, portal vein embolization, and right trisegmentectomy. She had recurrent disease in the remnant liver treated with microwave ablation, which was complicated by biliary cutaneous fistula that has since healed. She underwent a right hepatectomy followed by complications that required additional abdominal surgery. On serial imaging, she was noted to have a large hernia with the majority of the small bowel and colon, within her right chest. She presented with a symptomatic right diaphragmatic hernia with associated shortness of breath on mild exertion and lying flat. She underwent a right thoracotomy, repair of diaphragmatic hernia with mesh.

Her past medical history includes intracranial meningioma, bipolar disorder, depressive disorder, anxiety, prior alcoholism, benign paroxysmal positional vertigo, hypertension, left ventricular hypertrophy, chronic back pain, osteoporosis, tubal ligation, gastric RNY bypass, total abdominal hysterectomy, and tonsillectomy. She has a history of multiple ERCP/ PTBD placement and exchanges. She has a family history of lung cancer in her mother and gastric cancer in her father.

Upon admission, she was on amLODIPine, BuPROPion, Calcium Carbonate, Furosemide, Omeprazole, Ursodiol, Mirtazapine, and Meclizine. Her physical exam showed no abnormalities. However, her lab results showed abnormal levels of WBC-3.2*, RBC-3.20*, Hgb-10.5*, Hct-32.6*, MCV-102*, MCH-32.8*, MCHC-32.2, RDWSD-47.9*, Plt Ct-94*, and AlkPhos-360*, and Phos-2.3*.

During her hospital stay, she had no nausea or vomiting, and her pain was well controlled. She experienced intermittent bouts of tachycardia when out of bed ambulating but remained asymptomatic without chest pain, shortness of breath or palpitations. EKG was within normal limits and CXR was stable with small bilateral pleural effusions. She was discharged in stable condition - pain controlled, tolerating diet, passing gas and was instructed to follow-up in clinic for her scheduled postoperative visit.

The patient, a male, was admitted to the orthopaedics service due to an infection in his right distal tibia. His chief complaint was an infection in the right distal tibia. He had previously suffered a right open distal tibia fracture after falling off a platform. Post-operatively, a wound developed on the lateral aspect of his ankle, which has persisted. He reported to the emergency department due to increasing pain and fever/chills over the last few days. 

During his hospital stay, he underwent two Incision and Drainage (I&D) procedures with Vacuum-Assisted Closure (VAC) placement for his right distal tibia fracture. A Peripherally Inserted Central Catheter (PICC) line was also placed for long-term antibiotics. His physical examination revealed a 1 cm wound over the lateral malleolus with necrotic tissue that probes to bone, surrounded by erythema extending above with blisters. His range of motion was limited to plantar flexion at the ankle. 

His lab results showed several abnormal findings including decreased Red Blood Cell (RBC) count, Hemoglobin (Hgb), Hematocrit (Hct), Mean Corpuscular Hemoglobin (MCH), and Mean Corpuscular Hemoglobin Concentration (MCHC). His White Blood Cell (WBC) count was also elevated, indicating a possible infection. His neutrophil and lymphocyte counts were also elevated. 

During his hospital stay, he was initially started on vancomycin until cultures from the operating room grew Methicillin-Sensitive Staphylococcus Aureus (MSSA). The vancomycin was then stopped and nafcillin was started. He was also evaluated by the infectious disease service for infection management. His hospital stay was otherwise uneventful with his lab data and vital signs within normal limits. 

Upon admission, the patient denied taking any medications. His discharge diagnosis was an infected distal tibia pilon fracture in the right lower extremity. His mental status was clear and coherent, but he required assistance or aid for ambulation. His discharge instructions included wound care, activity restrictions, dietary instructions, medication instructions, and physical therapy. He was advised to follow up with his primary orthopaedic surgeon.

The patient, a middle-aged male, was admitted due to bilateral lower extremity weakness, gait instability, balance difficulties, and right upper extremity weakness and loss of coordination. He reported progressive difficulties with gait/balance and intermittent bilateral lower extremity weakness and buckling over the past few weeks, leading to frequent falls. He also experienced increased right upper extremity weakness and loss of coordination with his dominant right hand, causing an inability to write or use his hand for fine motor activities such as typing. He described his lower extremity weakness as unpredictable and intermittent, causing frequent loss of balance. He also reported some recently developing parasthesias in his left palm. He denied bowel/bladder incontinence or saddle anesthesia, although he noted increased urinary urgency. 

The patient underwent a C4-7 Anterior Cervical Discectomy and Fusion (ACDF) procedure due to the progression of his neurologic symptoms and high risk of paralysis or severe cervical spinal cord injury if he continued to sustain frequent falls. His past medical history includes lumbar radiculopathy status post L4-5 fusion. He works as a professional but has been unable to work for the past 2 weeks due to his progressive neurologic symptoms. He denies tobacco, alcohol, and illicit drug use. 

On physical examination, the patient was found to be mildly hyper-reflexic in the bilateral lower extremities. His lab results showed abnormal values for WBC (13.5), RBC (4.36), Hgb (13.3), Hct (37.9), and Glucose (132). 

During his hospital stay, the patient was taken to the Operating Room for the above procedure. The surgery was without complication and the patient was transferred to the PACU in a stable condition. His initial postoperative pain was controlled with oral and IV pain medication. His diet was advanced as tolerated. Physical therapy and Occupational therapy were consulted for mobilization out of bed to ambulate and activities of daily living. 

Upon admission, the patient was on Gabapentin, Motrin, and Tylenol. His discharge diagnoses included cervical disc herniation, cervical stenosis, and cervical myelopathy. His mental status was clear and coherent, and he was alert and interactive. However, he required assistance or aid for ambulation.

The male patient was admitted due to general malaise and weakness. He had recently been admitted for myelopathy, with an MRI showing C4-5 disc protrusion with canal compromise, cord impingement, and edema on T2 sequences, which was likely indicative of myelomalacia. There were also less severe cord contour irregularities noted from moderate disc bulges at C5-6 and C6-7. He underwent a C4-7 Anterior Cervical Decompression and Fusion (ACDF) and was discharged home. However, he presented again with difficulty ambulating due to bilateral lower extremity knee buckling/weakness and feelings of dizziness/lightheadedness when standing up. He also reported not having a bowel movement in 5 days despite taking stool softeners and some nausea which limited his oral intake. He denied chest pain, shortness of breath, difficulty breathing or swallowing, and any issues with his incision site. 

His past medical history includes lumbar radiculopathy status post L4-5 fusion. He works as a professional but has not been able to work for the past 2 weeks due to his progressive neurologic symptoms. He denies tobacco, alcohol, and illicit drug use. 

Upon physical examination, he appeared well and comfortable. His upper and lower extremities showed some strength limitations. His incision was open to air and clean/dry/intact. 

Lab results showed abnormal values for WBC (10.2), RBC (4.07), Hgb (12.4), Hct (36.3), Lymphs (18.5), AbsNeut (8.22), AbsMono (1.11), and Glucose (102). 

During his hospital stay, he was managed medically with IV fluids and physical therapy for evaluation to return home. His diet was advanced as tolerated and he was tolerating oral thin liquids and foods well. He reported feeling much better with good strength and ability to mobilize well with the walker without weakness or instability. 

Upon admission, he was on Gabapentin, Motrin, and Tylenol. His discharge diagnosis was dehydration. His mental status was clear and coherent, and he was alert and interactive, but required assistance or aid for ambulation. He was discharged home with service.

The patient, a male, was admitted to the Orthopaedics service with the chief complaint of lumbar radiculopathy. An MRI obtained in the office showed spinal stenosis at the L4 level. The patient's past medical history includes lumbar radiculopathy status post L4-5 fusion, as reported by the patient. The patient has an allergy to docusate. 

A major surgical procedure, an L4 laminectomy, was performed without complications. Postoperative pain was managed with oral and IV pain medication. The patient's diet was advanced as tolerated. Physical therapy and Occupational therapy were consulted for mobilization out of bed to ambulate and perform activities of daily living. The patient's hospital course was unremarkable. On the day of discharge, the patient was afebrile with stable vital signs, comfortable on oral pain control, and tolerating a regular diet. 

Upon discharge, the patient was diagnosed as status post lumbar L4 laminectomy. The patient's mental status was clear and coherent, and he was alert and interactive. However, he required the assistance of a walker for ambulation. 

The patient's discharge medications included Acetaminophen 1000 mg PO Q8H, Morphine SR 15 mg PO Q12H, and OxyCODONE (Immediate Release) as needed for pain. 

The patient was discharged home with service. His discharge instructions included not lifting anything greater than 10 lbs for 2 weeks, walking for recovery, limiting lifting, eating a normal healthy diet, wearing a brace when walking, and wound care instructions. He was also instructed to resume his normal home medications and was given additional medications to control his pain. 

The patient was advised to follow up in 2 weeks for an incision check, baseline X-rays, and possibly the start of physical therapy. He was also advised to call the office if he had a fever over 101.5 degrees Fahrenheit and/or drainage from his wound. 

The patient's physical therapy included weight bearing as tolerated and using a walker for ambulation. He was instructed to follow discharge instructions for wound care.

The patient, a female, was admitted to the orthopedics service due to severe progressive difficulty with walking. This was found to be related to spondylolisthesis with severe stenosis at both L3-L4 and L4-L5. She elected to undergo surgical treatment for this condition. The major surgical procedure performed was an L3-5 lami and instrumented fusion. 

Her past medical history includes diabetes mellitus, asthma, hypertension, and a rotator cuff condition. She has an allergy to penicillins. Upon admission, she was on several medications including Losartan Potassium, Memantine, Furosemide, Donepezil, Omeprazole, Potassium Chloride, and Aspirin.

The physical examination revealed normal respiratory effort, regular heart rate and rhythm, and a dressing that was clean, dry, and intact. Sensory and motor exams were within normal limits. However, her blood work showed several abnormal results including elevated white blood cell counts (WBC-10.8*, 14.0*, 10.4*), low red blood cell counts (RBC-3.09*, 3.25*, 3.40*), low hemoglobin (Hgb-9.0*, 9.3*, 9.9*), and low hematocrit (Hct-25.4*, 26.6*, 28.4*). Her glucose levels were also elevated (Glucose-136*, 110*), and she had low calcium (Calcium-8.3*) and phosphorus (Phos-2.5*) levels. Her urine test showed the presence of protein (Protein-30*) and elevated white blood cells (WBC-16*), with few bacteria (Bacteri-FEW*).

During her hospital stay, she underwent surgery without complications and was transferred to the PACU in a stable condition. Postoperative pain was managed with oral and IV pain medication. Her diet was advanced as tolerated and her Foley was removed on postoperative day 2. Physical therapy and occupational therapy were consulted for mobilization and activities of daily living. Her hospital course was unremarkable and she was discharged in a stable condition, requiring assistance for ambulation.

Upon discharge, her diagnosis was spondylolisthesis with severe stenosis at L3-L4 and L4-L5. She was discharged to an extended care facility. Her discharge instructions included activity restrictions, wound care instructions, medication management, and follow-up appointments.

The patient, a female, was admitted for an incisional hernia. She had previously undergone a bowel resection due to diverticulitis and was now presenting with increased abdominal pain in the middle of her incision, accompanied by a small bulge. She also reported experiencing nausea and vomiting. A CT scan at an outside hospital revealed a small loop of bowel within the incisional hernia. Her pain and nausea/vomiting symptoms have since resolved, but she still experiences occasional discomfort at the site. 

The patient's past medical history includes fibroadenoma of the right breast, diverticulitis with a subsequent left colectomy, colonic polyps (with a negative colonoscopy), angioedema potentially due to Flagyl or ACE inhibitors, hypertension, drug allergies, left hip pain, and a latex allergy. Her family history includes colon cancer in her father.

During her hospital stay, the patient underwent a laparoscopic incisional hernia repair with mesh and lysis of adhesions. Post-procedure, she was kept NPO with IV fluids overnight. Her diet was advanced in the morning and she was able to tolerate a regular diet. Her pain was well controlled with oral pain medications and she was ambulating. 

Upon admission, the patient was taking Hydrochlorothiazide (HCTZ) 25mg daily and Spironolactone 100mg daily. No abnormal lab or test results were reported. The patient was discharged in good condition with instructions to return to the ER if she experienced vomiting, shaking chills, fever greater than 101.5 (F) degrees or 38 (C) degrees, increased redness, swelling or discharge from her incisions, chest pain, shortness of breath, or any other troubling symptoms. She was also advised to resume all regular home medications and take any new medications as ordered.

The patient, a female, was admitted for an elective ileostomy takedown. She has a history of chronic ulcerative colitis, which has not responded to medical therapy, and endometriosis. She was presented with various surgical options, including panproctocolectomy with permanent ileostomy to ileoanalp pouch, but a single stage operation was deemed unwise due to her interest in childbearing. 

Upon admission, the patient was on oral contraceptive pills. During her hospital stay, she underwent the ileostomy takedown procedure. Post-operation, she remained NPO, and her pain was managed with a striker pain pump sq and a PCA pump. A foley catheter was initially removed but had to be replaced due to failure to void. Over the course of her stay, her diet was gradually advanced from sips to a regular diet, and her pain was managed with oral medication. 

Her physical examination upon discharge showed no acute distress, with stable vital signs, regular heart rate and rhythm, clear lungs, and a soft, non-tender, non-distended abdomen. Her wound was clean, dry, and intact. 

Lab results showed abnormal RBC count at 4.16*, Hemoglobin at 11.3*, Hematocrit at 34.4*, and Urea Nitrogen at 3*. Other lab results, including WBC, Glucose, Creatinine, Sodium, Potassium, Chloride, Bicarbonate, and Anion Gap, were within normal ranges. 

The patient was discharged with a diagnosis of postoperative ileostomy takedown, ulcerative colitis, and prior total colectomy with ileal-anal pouch. Her condition upon discharge was good. She was instructed to contact her doctor or return to the ER for any significant changes in her symptoms or any new symptoms that concern her, including chest pain, vomiting, dehydration, blood in vomit or bowel movement, yellowing of skin or eyes, worsening pain, fever, or any serious change in symptoms. She was also given instructions for incision care. Her family history was non-contributory.

The patient, a female with a history of Ulcerative Colitis (UC) and a total proctocolectomy with J pouch, presented with abdominal pain, nausea, and vomiting. She had a history of three prior partial obstructions, one of which required hospitalization. She was also recently post In Vitro Fertilization (IVF) implantation. Upon admission, her abdominal pain was still present but less intense, and her abdominal distention had improved. She had passed a small amount of liquid stool since being in the emergency department (ED).

Her past surgical history includes a J pouch, tonsillectomy, and foot surgeries. She has no significant family history. She is allergic to Cipro. Her medications upon admission included Estradiol 2 mg PO TID, Aspirin EC 81 mg PO DAILY, and Crinone (proGESTerone micronized) 8% vaginal BID.

Physical examination revealed a mildly distended and tender abdomen, particularly in the suprapubic area. There was no rebound or guarding. Her vitals were stable. An MRI revealed distended small bowel loops up to 4 cm leading to a transition point in the pelvis, consistent with obstruction.

Abnormal lab results included a decrease in RBC (3.79), Hgb (11.1), and Hct (32.9), an increase in WBC (13.2, 16.3), Neuts (75.9, 88.6), AbsNeut (10.01, 14.45), Glucose (104, 115, 111), UreaN (1, 2, 5), and AnGap (8, 9, 19), and a decrease in Lymphs (16.1, 7.2), Monos (3.2), Eos (1.0, 0.0), AbsLymp (2.12, 1.18), AbsMono (0.53), AbsEos (0.00), HCO3 (21), K (3.4), Calcium (8.2, 8.0, 8.2), Phos (2.1, 1.2), and Mg (1.5).

During her hospital stay, she was managed with bowel rest, nasogastric tube, intravenous fluids, and serial abdominal exams. Her pain was controlled with Tylenol and IV morphine. She was closely monitored for signs of infection, bleeding, and cardiovascular and pulmonary stability. Her nasogastric tube was removed once output was low, and she was slowly advanced to a regular diet. She was voiding without difficulty and her HCG levels were monitored. She had no post-surgical complications or social issues causing a delay in discharge. She was discharged with a diagnosis of small bowel obstruction, tolerating a regular diet, passing flatus, voiding, and ambulating independently.

The patient is a female of undisclosed age who is currently seven weeks pregnant. She has a past medical history of ulcerative colitis and has undergone a total proctocolectomy with ileoanal J-pouch. She was previously complicated by small bowel obstruction. She was admitted due to several days of abdominal bloating, vomiting, and absence of bowel movements, which spontaneously resolved. She was able to tolerate diet without issue and was discharged home.

Upon admission, her labs showed an elevated WBC count of 18.4, RBC of 5.32, Neuts of 85.6, Lymphs of 8.1, Monos of 5.0, Eos of 0.4, AbsNeut of 15.77, AbsMono of 0.92, and Glucose of 101. Her urine culture showed no growth. An MRI of her abdomen and pelvis without contrast showed borderline dilated small bowel loops containing fluid and some fecal material tapering toward the J-pouch without a transition point to suggest obstruction. Findings could reflect an ileus. A short segment of narrowing near the prior obstruction could reflect peristalsis or a small stricture. Trace abdominopelvic ascites were also noted. An intrauterine pregnancy was better assessed on the same day pelvic ultrasound, which showed a single live intrauterine pregnancy with size equal to date and a small subchronic hemorrhage.

During her hospital stay, she presented with several days of abdominal pain, nausea/vomiting, similar to prior episodes of small bowel obstruction. However, her symptoms completely resolved in the ED and immediately following admission, she was able to tolerate the advancement of diet over 24 hours without issue. She was passing flatus and having bowel movements. Her WBC count improved throughout her admission, with a discharge WBC of 12.4. She had recent outpatient treatment for a UTI with Augmentin. Her urine culture subsequently returned with no growth. She continued prenatal vitamins, prednisone 5mg daily, endometrin 1000mg BID, estradiol patch 0.2mg every other day, and IM progesterone 50mg daily.

She also has chronic GERD and allergies, for which she takes famotidine 20mg BID and loratadine 10mg daily:PRN respectively. Her medications upon admission included loratadine 10 mg PO DAILY:PRN allergies, PredniSONE 5 mg PO DAILY, proGESTerone 50 mg/mL injection 1ml daily, Famotidine 20 mg PO BID, Prenatal Vitamins 1 TAB PO DAILY, Endometrin (proGESTerone micronized) 100 mg vaginal BID, and estradioL 0.1 mg/24 hr transdermal Two patches every other day.

The patient, a female, was admitted due to right leg pain following a fall in a snow bank while cleaning her car. She reported being unable to move her leg due to the pain. She has a history of right total knee arthroplasty and was found to have a right distal femur fracture above the right total knee after the fall. She was placed in a knee immobilizer. She also reported that she was scheduled for left hip surgery the following day. She denied any numbness, tingling, or pain elsewhere. 

Her past medical history includes right knee arthroplasty, Type II Diabetes Mellitus managed with insulin, gastric bypass with post abdominoplasty, left hip surgery, anemia, arthritis, history of amnesia, transient ischemic attack managed with Aspirin 325mg, hypertension, hyperlipidemia, depression, gastroesophageal reflux disease, and a heart murmur. She denies smoking or alcohol use.

Upon physical examination, her right lower extremity was dressed and in a brace. She had +2 pulses distally. She underwent a right distal femur open reduction internal fixation, which she tolerated well. Post-operation, she was given IV fluids and IV pain medications, which were later transitioned to a regular diet and oral medications. Her home medications were continued throughout her hospital stay. 

Her medications upon admission included Aspirin 325 mg, Metformin 1000 mg, Lisinopril 2.5 mg, and Bupropion XL 300 mg. 

She was discharged with a diagnosis of right periprosthetic distal femur fracture. At the time of discharge, her pain was well controlled with oral medications, her incisions were clean, dry, and intact, and she was voiding and moving bowels spontaneously. She was advised to bear weight on the right lower extremity and was discharged on Lovenox for deep vein thrombosis prophylaxis. She was also given written instructions concerning precautionary instructions and the appropriate follow-up care.

The patient is a female with a history of endometriosis, for which she underwent a total abdominal hysterectomy and left oophorectomy. She also has a history of colon polyps, irritable bowel syndrome (IBS), fibroids, depression, chronic bright red blood per rectum (BRBPR) with diverticulosis and polyps on colonoscopy, and a history of an eating disorder. She carries the HbS trait. She presented with lower abdominal pain, nausea, and vomiting (N/V) and diarrhea (D). The abdominal pain was similar to her typical pain with endometriosis but was more severe, requiring her to take more naproxen. She also reported having bloody stools since her teen years and is undergoing a gastrointestinal evaluation. She had a left chest ache that self-resolved and has not recurred. 

In the ER, her vitals were stable and her EKG and chest X-ray were unremarkable. However, a CT scan of her abdomen and pelvis showed an increase in the size of her right adnexal and right gluteus lipoma. Her urine analysis was consistent with contamination. Her complete blood count (CBC) was notable for a white blood cell count of 1.3 and an absolute neutrophil count (ANC) of 700 with no prior history of leukopenia or neutropenia. She was given cefepime and morphine for pain, which improved her condition. She also received 2 L of normal saline intravenous fluids and was admitted to Medicine. 

Her lab results on admission showed an abnormal white blood cell count of 1.3 and her vitamin B12 level was 1093. On discharge, her lab results showed an abnormal white blood cell count of 2.7, hemoglobin of 11.9, hematocrit of 35.3, neutrophils of 37, lymphocytes of 43, eosinophils of 5, and atypical cells of 9. Her stool culture results were negative for salmonella, shigella, campylobacter, yersinia, E.coli 0157:H7, and clostridium difficile toxin A & B. Her viral culture showed no virus isolated so far. 

Imaging showed a 5x4 cm right adnexal cyst and a left gluteal lipomatous lesion with internal septations that had increased in size, measuring 5.8 x 2.8 cm. Her upper EGD showed normal mucosa in the stomach with a biopsy showing chronic and mild active gastritis and organisms consistent with H. pylori. Her colonoscopy showed semi-solid and liquid stool scattered in the whole colon, diverticulosis of the sigmoid colon, and otherwise normal colonoscopy to the cecum. 

During her hospital stay, her abdominal pain was controlled with oxycodone and protonix was started. Her nausea, vomiting, and diarrhea resolved without intervention. She was found to be leukopenic with moderate neutropenia, most likely due to a viral infection. Her anemia was stable and her iron studies were normal. She was instructed to follow up for a CBC check to ensure improvement in WBC as she cleared the viral infection. She was also instructed to follow up with her primary care physician and gynecologist for a repeat pelvic ultrasound in 6 weeks for her adnexal cyst and for further imaging and possible biopsy of her lipoma. Her depression was managed with continued citalopram and trazodone. 

Her medications upon admission included citalopram 10mg, trazodone 25mg at bedtime, Vicodin as needed, and Naproxen twice daily as needed. She has allergies to penicillin, compazine, tigan, azithromycin, and percocet. 

Her family history includes no inflammatory bowel disease, an uncle who died of skin cancer, another uncle with end-stage renal disease, and a family history of diabetes mellitus.

The patient, a female under the orthopaedics service, was admitted due to a mass in her left hip area, which was incidentally found during a prior workup and has been enlarging over several years. The mass was presumed to be a lipoma. She underwent a major surgical procedure for the excision of the mass. The final pathology results are still pending.

Her past medical history includes fibroids and endometriosis, for which she underwent myomectomy, hysterectomy, and left oophorectomy. She also has a history of depression, irritable bowel syndrome (IBS), chronic bright red blood per rectum (BRBPR) with diverticulosis and polyps found on colonoscopy, an eating disorder, and hemoglobin S (HbS) trait.

The patient has allergies to penicillins, Compazine, Tigan, Azithromycin, and Percocet. Upon admission, she was on Pantoprazole, Trazadone, and Citalopram.

Her family history is significant for an uncle who died of skin cancer, another uncle with end-stage renal disease (ESRD), and a family history of diabetes mellitus (DM).

During her hospital stay, she tolerated the surgical procedure well and her post-operative pain was managed with oral analgesics. She was eager to be discharged home on post-operative day 1. Her physical examination showed a dressing on her left lateral hip, proximal to the left greater trochanter. She was afebrile with stable vital signs and was distally neurovascularly intact. 

Upon discharge, her diagnosis was a presumed lipoma, pending pathology. She was advised to bear weight as tolerated and use walking aids as needed. Her discharge instructions included leaving the dressing on until a specified date, then leaving the wound open to air, leaving steri-strips in place, and not soaking in a tub while showering. She was instructed to call for a follow-up appointment with her doctor.

The patient, a female with a history of endometriosis and chronic pelvic pain, presented to the emergency department with severe pelvic pain that had been worsening over the past three days. The pain was not relieved with Vicodin, a medication she uses at home for pain management. She reported mild nausea, decreased appetite due to pain, painful bladder symptoms when her bladder is full, incontinence, and chronic constipation. She denied having fever, chills, hematuria, dysuria, or abnormal vaginal discharge. 

In the past, she had been treated with Progestin and oral contraceptive pills (OCPs) for her endometriosis, but these treatments led to significant depression. She has never been treated with Lupron. She has been evaluated by both urology and GI in the past. Her past medical history includes depression, irritable bowel syndrome (IBS), an eating disorder, and a history of bright red blood per rectum (BRBPR) with diverticulosis and polyps on colonoscopy. She has also undergone a supracervical hysterectomy (SCH) and left salpingo-oophorectomy (LSO) for endometriosis. 

Upon admission, she was on Vicodin, Citalopram 10mg, and Trazodone 25mg qhs. Her physical examination revealed diffuse lower abdominal tenderness, moderate to palpation, with no rebound or guarding, and no costovertebral angle tenderness (CVAT). Her pelvic exam was deferred at her request. Her lab results were all within normal limits. A pelvic ultrasound showed a normal-appearing right ovary with arterial and venous waveforms and normal-appearing follicular activity.

During her hospital stay, she was admitted to the GYN service for IV pain medication and serial exams. Her severe pelvic pain was thought to be an exacerbation of her chronic pain likely due to extensive endometriosis. She was initially on IV toradol/dilaudid, then transitioned to oral pain medications on hospital day 2. She was also started on gabapentin and tizanidine for her chronic pain. 

Her family history was noncontributory. She was discharged on hospital day 3 in stable condition, with her pain moderately controlled on oral medications. She had close follow-up arranged prior to discharge and verbally agreed to a pain contract. Her discharge diagnoses were endometriosis and acute on chronic pelvic pain.

The patient, a female with a history of endometriosis and chronic pelvic pain, presented to the ED with an exacerbation of her usual pain. She has a past medical history of depression, IBS, an eating disorder, and a history of bright red blood per rectum with diverticulosis and polyps found on colonoscopy. She has had previous surgical history of SCH and LSO, abdominal MMY, and LTCS. She has no significant family history. 

The patient's chief complaint was severe pelvic pain. She denied any new symptoms or fevers and chills. She has been on a pain control regimen, including Lupron, as per the Chronic Pain Service. She had a similar admission for pain control in the past. During her current visit, she received oral Dilaudid in the ED, which improved her condition. However, she had an episode of emesis and returned to the ED. After receiving Zofran and Tylenol, she felt better but requested admission for observation due to anxiety about managing her symptoms at home. 

On physical examination, her vitals were normal and her abdomen showed mild tenderness in the lower quadrants. No imaging was performed as her vital signs and exam were not worrisome for an acute process. Her labs showed a WBC count of 3.3*, RBC count of 4.14*, HCT of 35.4*, MCHC of 35.1*, NEUTS of 36.9*, and LYMPHS of 55.0*. Urine UCG and UA were negative. 

During her hospital stay, her pain decreased with medication and there was no evidence of infection or acute abdominal process. She received her scheduled dose of Lupron while in the hospital. 

Upon admission, she was on several medications including Buspirone, Citalopram, Famotidine, Gabapentin, Hydrocodone-Acetaminophen, Hydromorphone, Ibuprofen, Leuprolide, Omeprazole, Tizanidine, and Trazodone. 

She was discharged with a diagnosis of chronic pelvic pain and endometriosis. Her mental status was clear and coherent, and she was alert, interactive, and independently ambulatory. She was instructed to call or return to the hospital if she experienced increased pain, nausea and vomiting, fevers, chills, or had any other concerns.

The patient, a female under the OBSTETRICS/GYNECOLOGY service, was admitted due to pelvic pain. She has a history of interstitial cystitis, endometriosis, and chronic pelvic pain. The patient described the pain as an acute exacerbation of her chronic pain, which is midline and suprapubic. She reported that her pain was not responsive to Vicodin, relaxation exercises, and warm compresses, and persisted despite her usual management of Elmiron, gabapentin, and tizanidine. She recently stopped taking Lupron due to decreased libido and believes this may be causing her pain. She also reported increased intercourse, which she believes triggers her pain. 

Her past medical history includes depression, irritable bowel syndrome, a history of eating disorder, a history of bright red blood per rectum with diverticulosis and polyps on colonoscopy, leukopenia, and interstitial cystitis. She has undergone a supracervical hysterectomy, left salpingo oopherectomy, abdominal myomectomy, and C-section. She has been on Lupron since an unspecified date and has no history of sexually transmitted infection. She had an ASCUS pap in the past and underwent a laparoscopic supracervical hysterectomy and left salpingo-oophorectomy with transcervical morcellation.

Upon admission, her physical exam showed no acute distress, a soft abdomen with diffuse tenderness to palpation, no rebound/guarding, no masses, and non-tender extremities. Her discharge physical exam was similar, with mild tenderness to palpation in lower quadrants and back.

Her lab results showed abnormal values for WBC (2.8* and 3.6*), RBC (3.89* and 4.08*), Hgb (11.4*), Hct (33.9*), and Cl (110*). Urine culture results were negative for a urinary tract infection.

During her hospital stay, she was continued on her home pain regimen including Vicodin, gabapentin, tizanidine, and elmiron. She was also started on pyridium and given IV Toradol and Dilaudid for breakthrough pain. She was advised to start a continuous OCP to manage pain secondary to endometriosis and was started on Yaz. She was discharged home in stable condition with adequate pain control on oral medication.

Her medications upon admission included Citalopram, Gabapentin, Hydrocodone-Acetaminophen, Leuprolide, Omeprazole, Pentosan Polysulfate Sodium, Trazodone, and Calcium Carbonate-Vitamin D3. 

The patient was discharged with a diagnosis of acute pain, endometriosis, and interstitial cystitis. Her mental status was clear and coherent, and she was alert, interactive, and independently ambulatory.

The patient is a female with a history of interstitial cystitis (IC), endometriosis, and irritable bowel syndrome (IBS). She had undergone an interstim stage II procedure. She presented to the emergency department with pelvic pain due to an IC flare. The patient reported that her pain initially improved after the interstim was placed, but she experienced severe pain from bladder spasms and a strong urge to void the day before her presentation. Her usual medication regimen, including Tylenol #3, did not significantly relieve her pain. She also adjusted her interstim settings without any effect. She denied any worsening discomfort at the surgical site and reported only soreness. She also reported not taking her oral contraceptive pills for her endometriosis due to nausea.

Upon admission, the patient appeared uncomfortable and was intermittently writhing in bed during what she described as spasms. Her abdomen was soft and tender suprapubically, with no rebound or guarding. Her urine analysis showed positive nitrites and bacteria. However, a repeat urine analysis was negative for these. 

During her hospital stay, her pain was controlled with dilaudid, toradol, and vicodin. She has previously responded well to Lupron, but it was discontinued due to decreased libido. She has also tried several oral contraceptive pills, which caused depressive symptoms and nausea/vomiting. After a discussion, she decided to return to Lupron for control of her presumed endometriosis pain. She was also prescribed estrogen add-back therapy in the form of a patch. She was treated with a 3-day course of macrobid due to the initial urine analysis results. 

Her medications upon admission included gabapentin 300 TID, celexa, elmiron, tylenol #3 as needed, and trazodone. She was initially on vicodin for pain control but was switched to oral dilaudid due to nausea/emesis. By hospital day 3, her condition had improved and she was discharged in good condition. 

The patient's family history was non-contributory. Her discharge diagnoses were endometriosis and interstitial cystitis.

The patient, a female with a history of endometriosis and interstitial cystitis, was admitted due to worsening pelvic and vaginal pain over the last three days. She has been managing her chronic pain with Tylenol with codeine, gabapentin, elmeron, and tizanidine. She also takes Lupron for her endometriosis, with her last dose given during a recent hospitalization. She reported feeling nauseous and having occasional vomiting, as well as dysuria. She denied any fever, chills, chest pain, shortness of breath, diarrhea, or constipation. It is important to note that she has had multiple admissions for this pain in the past.

Her past medical history includes depression, irritable bowel syndrome, gastritis, leukopenia, and endometriosis. She has a gynecological history of endometriosis, for which she has been on Lupron, and an ASCUS pap, which was never followed up for colposcopy. She underwent a laparoscopic supracervical hysterectomy and left salpingo-oophorectomy with transcervical morcellation. She has no history of sexually transmitted infections and is sexually active with her husband. Her obstetric history includes one spontaneous vaginal delivery, one cesarean section, three therapeutic abortions, and one spontaneous abortion. Her surgical history includes a cesarean section, myomectomy, laparoscopic supracervical hysterectomy and LSO, and InterStim stage II sacral nerve implant.

Upon physical examination, she was found to be in no acute distress, with a regular heart rate and rhythm, clear lungs, and a soft abdomen that was diffusely tender on palpation with voluntary guarding. Her extremities were non-tender, and her pelvic exam revealed normal appearing external genitalia, vaginal mucosa, and cervix without lesions or discharge.

Her urine tests showed a dark amber, hazy appearance with a positive nitrite and a urobilinogen level of 2*, which is abnormal. 

During her hospital stay, she was managed for pain in the setting of endometriosis and interstitial cystitis. She was initially given IV dilaudid overnight and pyridium. Chronic pain was consulted for further recommendations for outpatient pain regimen. She was started on notryptline, lidocaine patches, and tramadol, and was subsequently transitioned to PO dilaudid. This pain regimen adequately controlled her pain for 24 hours. 

Upon admission, she was on gabapentin, tylenol#3, tizanidine, elmiron, colace, and omeprazole. Her family history is non-contributory.

The patient, a female with a history of endometriosis and interstitial cystitis, was admitted due to worsening chronic pelvic pain. She described the pain as radiating from her lower abdominal quadrants to her lower back and groin, and it was exacerbated by activity. The pain was similar to her usual interstitial cystitis pain exacerbations. She also reported chronic bowel and bladder incontinence. She had been previously hospitalized for pelvic pain and was treated with Lupron (22.5 mg), which she did not find effective. Her current medications included elmiron, tylenol with codeine, gabapentin, and tramadol for pain management.

Her past medical history included depression, irritable bowel syndrome, gastritis, leukopenia, and a gynecological history of endometriosis, no sexually transmitted infections, an ASCUS pap that was never followed up for colposcopy, and a laparoscopic supracervical hysterectomy and left salpingo-oophorectomy with transcervical morcellation. She had also undergone a Cesarean section, myomectomy, and InterStim stage II sacral nerve implant. She is sexually active with her husband.

Upon admission, her vital signs were stable. Her abdomen was soft, non-distended, and tender to palpation in the right and left lower quadrants, with voluntary guarding but no rebound tenderness or organomegaly. She also had tenderness in her lower back. Her urine test results were normal.

During her hospital stay, her pain was managed with toradol, gabapentin, tizanidine, nortriptyline, and a lidocaine patch. She was transitioned from IV Dilaudid to PO Dilaudid taper. Her pain improved on this new medication regimen, although she continued to experience early morning pain.

Her medications upon admission were gabapentin 300 TID, tylenol#3 as needed, elmiron 100 TID, colace, omeprazole 20', tramadol 50 TID as needed, nortriptyline 10 QHS, celexa 40', estradiol patch weekly, and tizanidine ?4mg QHS. During her stay, several changes were made to her medication regimen. Celexa was discontinued as nortriptyline was increased. Gabapentin was increased to 600 TID. Tramadol was held inpatient and resumed after 2 days of dilaudid taper. A Lidoderm patch was started inpatient, to be used around the clock. Tizanidine was added in the morning and continued at the current pm dose. Tylenol#3 was continued after 2 days of dilaudid taper completed, along with elmiron 100 3x/day, colace, and omeprazole 20'. She also received IV dilaudid and toradol inpatient and was discharged with 2 days of dilaudid taper and 2 days of intranasal toradol.

The patient was discharged with a diagnosis of endometriosis and interstitial cystitis. She was alert, interactive, and ambulatory at the time of discharge. She was instructed to follow up for a TENS trial and is considering a diagnostic laparoscopy to assess for other etiologies and progression of her endometriosis. Her family history was non-contributory.

The patient, a female with a history of endometriosis and interstitial cystitis, was admitted due to worsening chronic pelvic pain. She had been previously hospitalized for a 7-day inpatient stay for pain control, during which her medications were adjusted and she was seen by the chronic pain service. However, she reported little resolution of symptoms. She described severe spastic bladder pain and had been taking oral dilaudid, which she reported made her feel depressed. 

Her past medical history includes endometriosis, for which she has been on Lupron, and a history of ASCUS pap, which was never followed up for colposcopy. She also has a history of depression, irritable bowel syndrome, gastritis, interstitial cystitis, endometriosis, and leukopenia. Her gynecological history includes a laparoscopic supracervical hysterectomy and left salpingo-oophorectomy with transcervical morcellation. She has also had a cesarean section, myomectomy, and InterStim stage II sacral nerve implant. Her obstetric history includes one spontaneous vaginal delivery, one cesarean section, three therapeutic abortions, and one spontaneous abortion.

Upon physical examination, she was afebrile with vital signs stable. Her abdomen was soft, non-distended, with tenderness to palpation in the right and left lower quadrants, and voluntary guarding, but no rebound tenderness or organomegaly. She also had tenderness to palpation in the lower back. Her pelvic exam was deferred. 

Her lab results showed a white blood cell count of 3.9*, red blood cell count of 4.16*, hematocrit of 34.4*, and mean corpuscular hemoglobin concentration of 35.1*#. 

During her hospital stay, she continued to experience pelvic and lower back pain, most severe in the early morning and interfering with sleep. She was started on anti-spasmodic agents levsin and flexeril, which provided some improvement in pain. A foley catheter was placed and provided some relief. She was seen by psychiatry due to her report of suicidal ideation with dilaudid, who felt she was not depressed. 

Upon admission, she was on gabapentin, tylenol#3, elmiron, colace, omeprazole, tramadol, nortriptyline, estradiol patch, and tizanidine. 

Her discharge diagnosis included interstitial cystitis, endometriosis, and chronic pelvic pain. She was discharged home in a clear and coherent mental state, alert and interactive, and independently ambulatory.

The patient, a female, was admitted to the OB/GYN service due to an exacerbation of her chronic interstitial cystitis, which she described as 'bladder spasms'. She has a history of severe endometriosis and interstitial cystitis. She denied experiencing fever, chills, or any other symptoms inconsistent with an interstitial cystitis exacerbation. 

Her past medical history includes endometriosis, for which she has been on Lupron, depression, irritable bowel syndrome, gastritis, interstitial cystitis, and leukopenia. She has no history of sexually transmitted infections. She had an ASCUS pap in the past but never followed up for colposcopy. She underwent a laparoscopic supracervical hysterectomy and left salpingo-oophorectomy with transcervical morcellation. She also had a Cesarean section, myomectomy, and InterStim stage II sacral nerve implant. Her obstetric history includes G6 P2, with one spontaneous vaginal delivery, one Cesarean section, three therapeutic abortions, and one spontaneous abortion.

Upon admission, her vital signs were stable and her physical examination was unremarkable, except for tenderness to touch in the lower quadrants. She declined a pelvic exam, stating that it exacerbates her pain. Her blood work showed a white blood cell count of 3.5* and neutrophils at 38.3*, both of which are below the normal range. Her lymphocytes were at 52.3*, which is above the normal range.

Her home pain regimen includes tramadol, gabapentin 600 TID, lidocaine patches, elmiron TID, and tizanidine. During her hospital stay, she was given two doses of IV morphine 1mg and IV toradol, percocet every three hours, pyridium (elmiron was held), continued on lidocaine patches, and flexeril. On the third day of her hospital stay, she reported a significant improvement in her pain and was discharged home with instructions to follow up.

Her medications upon admission included Lupron, an estradiol patch (which she had not used for several weeks due to cost), gabapentin 600, tizanidine 4mg at bedtime, tramadol 50 several times per day as needed, lidocaine patches, elmiron 100, celexa 40, and omeprazole 20. Her family history was non-contributory.

The patient, a female with a history of endometriosis, interstitial cystitis, depression, irritable bowel syndrome, gastritis, and leukopenia, was admitted due to lower abdominal and bladder pain. She has a gynecological history of endometriosis, for which she has been on Lupron therapy, and has undergone a laparoscopic supracervical hysterectomy and left salpingo-oophorectomy with transcervical morcellation. She also has an InterStim stage II sacral nerve implant for her interstitial cystitis. She has no significant family history.

The patient's chief complaint was worsening bladder spasms and lower back pain, which she described as similar to her usual interstitial cystitis flare-ups. She also reported rectal incontinence, which began two months ago, stopped, and then recurred a few times in the past few weeks. 

Upon admission, the patient was on several medications including Gabapentin, Acetaminophen with Codeine, Citalopram, Estradiol Transdermal Patch, Leuprolide Acetate, Lidocaine Patch, Pantoprazole, Elmiron, Tizanidine, and Tramadol. 

During her hospital stay, her pain was managed with her home medications, with an increase in her Gabapentin, Tizanidine, and Tramadol dosages. She was also given Hyoscyamine, Pyridium, and a prescription for a Valium/Baclofen/Lidocaine suppository. She initially required IV Morphine and Toradol as well. However, the Hyoscyamine was stopped prior to discharge due to concerns of worsening urinary retention. 

The patient's physical examination upon discharge revealed mild diffuse lower abdominal pain but was otherwise normal. She was discharged with a diagnosis of an interstitial cystitis flare-up. Her mental status was clear and coherent, and she was alert, interactive, and independently ambulatory.

The patient, a female under the OBSTETRICS/GYNECOLOGY service, was admitted due to urinary retention and abdominal pain. She has a history of interstitial cystitis, endometriosis, and irritable bowel syndrome (IBS). On the day of admission, she experienced acutely worsened bladder pain and was unable to urinate since morning despite the urge to do so. The pain was described as a dull ache with intermittent sharp bouts, similar to her usual interstitial cystitis pain. She had her last bowel movement the previous day and reported no nausea, vomiting, fever, chills, or back pain.

Her past medical history includes depression, gastritis, leukopenia, and a history of sexually transmitted infection. She has been on Lupron for endometriosis. She had an ASCUS pap that was never followed up for colposcopy. She underwent a laparoscopic supracervical hysterectomy and left salpingo-oophorectomy with transcervical morcellation. She also had a Cesarean section, myomectomy, and InterStim stage II sacral nerve implant. Her obstetric history includes G6 P2, SVD x1, C-section x1, TAB x3, SAB x1.

Upon admission, her physical examination showed a temperature of 98.2, BP 118/71, HR 66, RR 16. Her abdomen was soft, with no rebound or guarding, but very tender supra-pubically. No edema or tenderness was noted in her extremities.

During her hospital stay, a foley catheter was placed due to urinary retention, which resulted in approximately 400cc of urine output. She was managed with IV morphine for breakthrough pain and continued her home medications. Her pain improved by the second day of her hospital stay, and she was discharged with the foley catheter in place.

Her medications upon admission included Acetaminophen w/Codeine, Citalopram, Elmiron, Gabapentin, Lidocaine 5% Patch, Tizanidine, and TraMADOL. She has allergies to Penicillins, Compazine, Tigan, Azithromycin, and Amoxicillin. Her family history was reported as non-contributory.

Upon discharge, her mental status was clear and coherent, and she was alert, interactive, and independently ambulatory. Her discharge diagnosis included interstitial cystitis and endometriosis. She was instructed to follow up with her doctor the next day to discuss catheter removal.

The patient, a female with a history of interstitial cystitis, endometriosis, irritable bowel syndrome (IBS), depression, gastritis, chronic pelvic pain, leukopenia, history of bulimia, H/pylori gastritis, osteopenia, history of colonic polyps, and a history of several surgical procedures including cesarean section, myomectomy, laparoscopic supracervical hysterectomy and LSO, and InterStim stage II sacral nerve implant, was admitted due to a rash and fevers. She had been receiving monthly leuprolide injections for her endometriosis. The patient reported severe bilateral leg cramps, and the development of itchy and painful bumps under her chin that spread to her face, arms, legs, chest, and back. She also reported chills and night sweats. She has a history of cold sores and has taken valacyclovir in the past. She denied any new medications, detergents, or foods, and no one else at home was sick. She has never had chicken pox. 

Upon admission, her vitals were: 103.1 113 114/71 16 99%. She had vesicular lesions with crusting on her face, back, and arms. Labs showed leukopenia of 2.7 with 49% neutrophils. Her baseline leukopenia is around 3.5. Other labs were unremarkable with Hb12 and Cr 0.6. 

Admission labs showed abnormal WBC-2.7*, RBC-4.19*, Hct-34.8*, MCHC-35.3*, Neuts-49*, Baso-3*, and Cl-109*. ALT-162* and AST-159* were also elevated. Interval labs showed ALT-119* and AST-47*. Discharge labs showed abnormal RBC-4.14*, Hct-35.1*, Neuts-35.5*, Lymphs-54.1*, and ALT-81*. 

Imaging results from a CXR and MRI of the head were normal. Microbiology results showed no growth in blood cultures and VZV culture. VZV IgG was negative, VZV DFA was positive, and HSV DFA was negative. CSF Gram Stain was negative, fluid culture showed no growth, and viral culture is pending. CSF VZV PCR is also pending. 

During her hospital stay, she was diagnosed with primary Varicella Zoster infection, neutropenia, headache, and transaminitis. Her varicella was treated with oral acyclovir. She developed severe headaches while receiving acyclovir, which were controlled with toradol, oxycodone, and fioricet. Her LP and MRI brain were obtained to evaluate for cause of headache and were negative. Her headaches improved at the time of discharge. Her neutropenia was thought to be secondary to her current viral infection and she was no longer neutropenic at the time of discharge. Her transaminitis was thought to be secondary to viral infection and was trending down at time of discharge. 

Her medications upon admission included Gabapentin, Tizanidine, Estradiol Transdermal Patch, Acetaminophen w/Codeine, Escitalopram Oxalate, pentosan polysulfate sodium, and Leuprolide Acetate. 

The patient's mother has a history of hypertension and her uncle has a skin condition. The patient was discharged with a diagnosis of Primary Disseminated Varicella Zooster. Her mental status was clear and coherent, and she was alert and interactive. She was ambulatory and independent upon discharge.

The patient, a female with a history of chronic pelvic pain, endometriosis, and painful bladder syndrome, was admitted due to an acute flare of her painful bladder syndrome. She has previously undergone a cesarean section, myomectomy, laparoscopic supracervical hysterectomy, and LSO, and has an InterStim stage II sacral nerve implant. Her other past medical conditions include depression, irritable bowel syndrome, gastritis, leukopenia, and osteopenia. She has been on a regimen of Gabapentin, Escitalopram Oxalate, Elmiron, Tizanidine, Acetaminophen with Codeine, Emoquette, and Lidocaine patch.

Upon admission, the patient appeared uncomfortable and was in pain. Her abdomen was soft and tender to touch diffusely, but there was no rebound, guarding, or masses. She denied vaginal discharge but noted two days of vaginal spotting. She also noted a slight urinary retention but was able to void. Her urine test results were normal, but her blood test showed abnormal levels of neutrophils (44.2), lymphocytes (48.7), and RBC (4.02), and HCT (34.2), and Chloride (110).

During her hospital stay, she was managed for her painful bladder syndrome, which was likely triggered by increased caffeine intake. She was given IV morphine for breakthrough pain, in addition to her home regimen for pain control. Her pain improved by the second day of her hospital stay, and she was discharged in good condition. She was advised to follow up with her primary GYN to discuss optimization of treatment for her endometriosis and painful bladder syndrome.

The patient's family history includes her mother with hypertension and an uncle with a skin condition. She has allergies to Penicillins, Compazine, Tigan, Azithromycin, and Amoxicillin.

The patient, a female with a history of endometriosis and interstitial cystitis, was admitted due to two days of pelvic pain. She has a history of chronic pelvic pain from these conditions, which she usually manages with a combination of medication, Interstim, and pelvic floor therapy. Her medications upon admission included Lupron, gabapentin, Elmiron, tizanidine, and Tylenol 3. She also has a history of depression, irritable bowel syndrome, gastritis, leukopenia, and osteopenia. Her surgical history includes a cesarean section, myomectomy, laparoscopic supracervical hysterectomy, and LSO, and InterStim stage II sacral nerve implant. 

The patient described her pain as constant, throbbing, and more intense on the right side of her pelvis. The pain radiated to her sacrum and down her thighs. She also reported a sore throat, cough, and fever for the last two days, but no nausea or vomiting. She had some dark brown vaginal spotting the day before, which she has experienced in the past since her hysterectomy. She reported normal bowel movements and no recent constipation or diarrhea. She had a colonoscopy recently which revealed internal hemorrhoids but no other abnormalities. 

The patient's mother was recently diagnosed with bladder cancer and her ex-husband's mother recently passed away. She reported feeling frustrated at being unwell as she wanted to support her mother more. 

Upon physical examination, the patient appeared fatigued and in pain. Her abdomen was soft with mild tenderness in both lower quadrants. Her pelvic exam revealed normal female external genitalia, diffuse tenderness on bimanual exam including CMT, and no palpable adnexal masses or fullness. 

Lab results showed an elevated neutrophil count. All other results, including glucose, urea nitrogen, creatinine, sodium, potassium, chloride, total CO2, anion gap, calcium, phosphate, magnesium, WBC, RBC, HGB, HCT, MCV, MCH, MCHC, RDW, monocytes, eosinophils, basophils, and platelet count were within normal ranges. Urine tests were also normal. 

During her hospital stay, the patient's pelvic pain was managed with her home regimen of pain medications and toradol. She was able to void spontaneously and had a viral upper respiratory infection which was treated with hydration and supportive care. By the second day of her hospital stay, she was tolerating a regular diet, ambulating independently, and her pain was controlled with oral medications. She was discharged in stable condition.

The patient, a female, was admitted to the Obstetrics/Gynecology service due to pelvic pain.
