His admission labs showed abnormal values for RBC (2.93), Hgb (8.2), Hct (26.6), MCHC (30.8), RDWSD (51.1), Glucose (290), UreaN (65), Creat (5.6), cTropnT (0.30), proBNP (3084), Albumin (2.4), Phos (5.6), Mg (1.5), pO2 (51), pCO2 (46), and pH (7.34). His microbiology results were negative for C. difficile DNA amplification assay, urine culture, blood cultures, and respiratory viral screen & culture. His diagnostics showed diffuse, bilateral peribronchial consolidative nodular opacities on CT chest, saccular structure within the left popliteal fossa on left lower leg U/S, and regular supraventricular tachycardia on EKG. His TTE showed normal left ventricular wall thickness, cavity size, and regional/global systolic function (LVEF >55%).

The patient's hospital course was complicated by hypoxemic respiratory failure secondary to aspiration pneumonia, acute on chronic kidney injury, supraventricular tachycardia, positive PPD, and nutrition deficits on PO intake. He also had insulin-dependent diabetes mellitus, elevated troponin, anemia, and hypertension. He eventually refused hemodialysis despite understanding that he will die without it. He was transitioned to comfort measures only and passed away.

The patient, a female, was admitted to the hospital due to chest pain and shortness of breath. She has a history of coronary artery disease (CAD) and has previously undergone percutaneous coronary intervention (PCI) to the right coronary artery (RCA) complicated by in-stent restenosis (ISR). She has had two instances of ISR, the most recent of which was treated with a drug-eluting stent (DES). The patient reported an episode of acute, deep, pressure-like substernal chest pain that woke her from sleep. Upon presentation to the emergency department, her chest pain had resolved. Her troponin levels were negative twice and her EKG showed no changes from prior. 

The patient has been on propranolol for angina and migraines, but her heart rate of 55-60 made it difficult to up titrate. She had previously tried nitrates for angina but could not tolerate them due to headaches. She was started on amlodipine for angina. The patient also takes carbamazepine, which has effectively controlled her seizures over the years. 

Her lab results showed abnormal levels of red blood cell count (3.47), hemoglobin (9.8), hematocrit (30.9), and mean corpuscular hemoglobin concentration (31.7). Her alkaline phosphatase level was also elevated (110). 

Upon admission, the patient was on several medications including amlodipine, carbamazepine, clonazepam, diclofenac sodium, vitamin D, ezetimibe, fluticasone propionate, furosemide, nitroglycerin, propranolol, rosuvastatin calcium, ticagrelor, aspirin, cetirizine, senna, polyethylene glycol, and docusate sodium. 

The patient was discharged with a diagnosis of stable angina. Her mental status was clear and coherent, and she was alert, interactive, and independently ambulatory. She was instructed to continue all of her pre-admission medications and to follow up with her cardiologist if her chest pain worsens.

The patient, a female with a history of obesity, epilepsy, and hyperlipidemia, was admitted due to chest pain. She reported experiencing exertional chest pressure for approximately six months, which had worsened in frequency and severity over the past several weeks. The symptoms were associated with severe chest pressure, left arm pain, jaw pain, radiation to the back, diaphoresis, dyspnea, and intermittent nausea. The symptoms began occurring at rest and were almost continuous, worsening with anxiety or physical exertion. She had been brought to the hospital by ambulance a week prior due to developing severe pain while walking. At that time, EKG, lab work, CTA, and CXR were reassuring and cardiac enzymes were negative. She underwent a pMIBI with normal LV contractile function and equivocal evidence of mild lateral wall ischemia. She was discharged with a recommendation to follow up with a cardiologist as an outpatient to receive a catheterization. 

Upon her readmission, her initial vitals were BP: 133/80; HR: 60; T: 97.8; RA: 97%. EKG showed T wave inversions in AvR and V1, unchanged from prior. Labs were notable for negative trop x2, Chem-7; CBC was unremarkable. She was given ASA 325, Nitro 0.4, and Tylenol. 

Her past medical history includes dyslipidemia, smoking, migraines, obesity, arthritis, and spondylosis. Her family history includes a brother and father with DVTs, a father with coronary artery disease and a possible myocardial infarction later in life, and a mother who had a stroke. 

During her hospital stay, she underwent a coronary angiography with PCI, which revealed single vessel coronary artery disease and successful PCI with a drug-eluting stent of the RCA coronary artery. Her discharge labs showed a decrease in RBC count (3.81). 

Her medications upon admission were CarBAMazepine 200 mg PO TID, Propranolol 20 mg PO TID, and ClonazePAM 0.5 mg PO TID. 

The patient was diagnosed with unstable angina, chest pain, and coronary artery disease. She was discharged with instructions to take all prescribed medications, particularly her new aspirin/Plavix to prevent clots from forming in her stent, and to attend all follow-up appointments.

The patient, a female with a past medical history of obesity, hyperlipidemia, and coronary artery disease (CAD) post drug-eluting stent (DES) to the right coronary artery (RCA), presented with recurrent chest pain. She reported experiencing crushing, substernal chest pain that radiated across her chest and to her right ear, associated with exertion, diaphoresis, dizziness, nausea, and extreme fatigue. The pain was similar to what she experienced prior to her first stent placement. 

Her past medical history also includes dyslipidemia, smoking, migraines, obesity, arthritis, and spondylosis. She has no history of coronary artery bypass graft (CABG), percutaneous coronary interventions, or pacing/implantable cardioverter-defibrillator (ICD). Her family history includes her brother and father having deep vein thrombosis (DVTs), her father having CAD with a possible myocardial infarction later in life and a clotting factor problem, and her mother having a stroke.

Upon physical examination, she was found to have a new bruise on her right hand where an IV was placed and her right arm was more swollen than her left. Her veins were more visible in her left arm than her right. 

Her lab results showed abnormal red blood cell count (3.62*) and red cell distribution width standard deviation (47.5*). Her cardiac troponin T levels were also elevated (0.13*, 0.14*, 0.06*, 0.03*), and her proBNP was 349*. 

She underwent a percutaneous coronary intervention (PCI) on her RCA due to severe in-stent restenosis. The procedure was successful with no residual stenosis, TIMI-3 flow in the distal vessel, and no evidence of residual dissection, embolization, or thrombus. A transthoracic echocardiogram (TTE) showed normal biventricular cavity sizes and regional/global biventricular systolic function, no valvular pathology or pathologic flow, normal estimated pulmonary artery systolic pressure, and mild aortic regurgitation with mildly thickened leaflets.

Her medications upon admission included carbamazepine, clonazepam, propranolol, aspirin, clopidogrel, nitroglycerin, and rosuvastatin. During her hospital stay, she was switched from clopidogrel to ticagrelor and her rosuvastatin dosage was increased. 

She was diagnosed with in-stent re-stenosis and non-ST elevation myocardial infarction (NSTEMI). Her mental status upon discharge was clear and coherent, and she was alert and interactive. She was advised to take her medications as prescribed, ice and elevate her arm to help with the mild swelling and pain, call her primary care physician if she experiences chest pain again, and follow a heart-healthy diet.

The patient, a female with a past medical history of obesity, hyperlipidemia, coronary artery disease (CAD), migraines, arthritis, and spondylosis, was admitted due to recurrent chest pain. She had previously undergone a drug-eluting stent (DES) placement in the right coronary artery (RCA) due to a 70% stenosis. However, she was readmitted with worsening chest pain and was found to have a 90-95% restenosis of the RCA stent. This was treated with balloon angioplasty followed by a cutting balloon and intravascular ultrasound (IVUS), which showed a well-expanded stent. Her medication was switched from Plavix to ticagrelor and she was discharged without chest pain. 

On the day before her current admission, she experienced substernal heaviness, chest pain radiating to her bilateral clavicles and neck, shortness of breath, dizziness, diaphoresis, paleness, and nausea. These symptoms were similar to those that led to her previous hospitalization. 

Her family history includes a brother and father with deep vein thrombosis (DVTs), a father with CAD and a possible myocardial infarction later in life, and a mother who had a stroke. 

Upon admission, her physical exam was largely normal, with the exception of swelling at the right radial access site. Her admission labs showed abnormal red blood cell (RBC) count of 3.62 and red cell distribution width standard deviation (RDWSD) of 47.5. Her cardiac troponin T (cTropnT) was also elevated at 0.07. A coronary angiogram showed no angiographically apparent coronary artery disease and a widely patent RCA stent. 

Her discharge labs showed a decreased RBC count of 3.42, hemoglobin (Hgb) of 10.4, hematocrit (Hct) of 32.1, and an elevated phosphate level of 4.7. 

During her hospital stay, she was diagnosed with non-ST elevated myocardial infarction and her symptoms were believed to be attributable to coronary spasm or microvascular showering from the prior catheterization. Her pain was managed with Tylenol and a lidocaine patch. She was medically optimized with the addition of ezetimibe to her medication regimen, which also included aspirin, carbamazepine, clonazepam, nitroglycerin, propranolol, ticagrelor, and rosuvastatin. 

Upon discharge, she was alert, interactive, and able to ambulate independently. She was instructed to continue taking her medications as prescribed and to seek medical attention if she experienced new or concerning symptoms such as chest pain, shortness of breath, or severe abdominal pain.

The patient, a female with a past medical history of obesity, hyperlipidemia, coronary artery disease (CAD), migraine, arthritis, and spondylosis, was admitted due to chest pain. She had previously undergone a drug-eluting stent (DES) placement in the right coronary artery (RCA) and a balloon angioplasty for stent restenosis of the RCA DES. Upon admission, she presented with continuous, non-exertional, midline, retrosternal chest pain, and left groin pain since her most recent catheterization. Initial findings included an EKG that showed interval progression in T-wave inversions in inferior leads and a troponin peak of 0.14 before downtrending. She underwent a coronary angiogram which showed a widely patent DES with no other significant coronary artery disease. Her symptoms were attributed to coronary spasm or microvascular showering from prior catheterization. She also experienced neuropathy radiating down her leg post-catheterization, likely related to nerve injury.

The patient's physical exam was notable for small volume hemoptysis, tenderness to touch in the lower abdomen, and a left groin site without bruit, nonpulsatile, no edema/erythema, but very tender to touch. Lab results showed a negative troponin x 2 and a D-dimer of 1660. Studies showed sinus bradycardia T-wave inversion in III and aVF on EKG. A CT scan showed no evidence of an aortic dissection or pulmonary embolism, but there was apparent thickening/soft tissue involving the proximal aorta and pulmonary arteries, which measured slightly hyperdense. The differential for this includes trace hemopericardium, versus inflammation of the aortic arch and pulmonary arteries. Bilateral lower lobe peribronchial nodularity was nonspecific but could reflect aspiration or atypical infection. There was hemorrhage surrounding the left external iliac artery, likely postprocedural, and a probable small right pseudoaneurysm which is 5 mm in length with a neck measuring 3 mm.

The patient was given ASA, ticagrelor, zetia, carbamazepine, clonazepam, and propranolol. She reported progressive joint pains, worse in the past few years, and joint stiffness upon waking for 30 minutes. She also reported not being able to bear weight on her left leg, such as after walking down the block to the bank or to grocery shop. Her level of physical activity has been significantly hampered, leading her to gain weight. She also reported hair loss and a facial rash, with redness especially around her nose. She has no family history of lupus or rheumatoid arthritis, but several family members have multiple sclerosis on both her father and mother's side. She also reported epigastric pain, which came on in the ED after having apple juice. It is also exacerbated when she eats nothing. She denies it is reflux. On further review of systems, she denies fevers or chills, any prior history of stroke, TIA, deep venous thrombosis, pulmonary embolism, bleeding at the time of surgery, myalgias, joint pains, cough, hemoptysis, black stools or red stools. She denies exertional buttock or calf pain. All of the other review of systems were negative.

Her family history includes a brother and father with deep vein thrombosis (DVTs), a father with CAD with a possible myocardial infarction later in life and a clotting factor problem, and a mother who had a stroke. Her admission labs showed abnormal values for RBC (3.18), Hgb (9.8), Hct (30.0), and RDWSD (46.5). Her discharge labs showed abnormal values for RBC (3.29), Hgb (10.2), Hct (31.4), and RDWSD (47.2). Her CRP was also elevated at 76.3.

The patient is a female with a history of coronary artery disease (CAD) post percutaneous coronary intervention (PCI) to the right coronary artery (RCA), epilepsy, migraines, dyslipidemia, arthritis, and spondylosis. She has had multiple recent admissions due to recurrent chest pain. She presented with intermittent low-grade chest pain that developed into severe chest pressure radiating to her right shoulder, right ear, and left shoulder blade. In the emergency department, her ECG showed sinus bradycardia with Q wave in III, late R wave progression, and T wave inversion in III and aVF. Troponins were negative twice and other labs were unremarkable. 

Upon admission, her medications included aspirin, carbamazepine, clonazepam, ezetimibe, nitroglycerin, propranolol, rosuvastatin calcium, and ticagrelor. Her family history includes her father having a myocardial infarction in his later years, while her siblings and children are all healthy. 

Her physical exam upon admission and discharge were largely unremarkable, with no significant findings. However, her admission labs showed abnormal values for RBC (3.27), Hgb (9.9), Hct (31.4), MCHC (31.5), RDWSD (46.5), and Phos (4.7), and CRP (8.7). Her discharge labs showed abnormal values for RBC (3.54), Hgb (10.7), Hct (33.7), MCHC (31.8), and RDWSD (47.2).

During her hospital stay, she underwent a stress echo and a chest MRA. The stress echo showed average functional exercise capacity with atypical angina symptoms and non-specific ECG changes without 2D echocardiographic evidence of inducible ischemia. The chest MRA showed mild but abnormal wall thickening of the ascending aorta and arch. 

Her chest pain was evaluated for noncardiac/noncoronary causes, and the differential diagnosis included coronary vasospasm vs. cardiac syndrome X. She was started on amlodipine for empiric therapy. Rheumatology was consulted due to concerns about a vasculitic process, but they agreed that it was unlikely to be causing her pain. She was discharged with instructions to continue her medications and to call the heartline or return to the ED if her pain worsened or occurred with exertion.

The patient, a female with a history of coronary artery disease (CAD) post percutaneous coronary intervention (PCI) to the right coronary artery (RCA), epilepsy, migraine, arthritis, and spondylosis, was admitted due to chest pain. She has had multiple hospitalizations for chest pain and a recent unremarkable stress test. The patient reported worsening chest pain, similar to previous episodes, with radiation to the back and down the arms, as well as a headache. The pain was not relieved with nitroglycerin and was associated with shortness of breath. 

During her stay, she underwent coronary angiography which revealed a 90% restenosis of the proximal RCA stent. A 3.5x18mm Onyx drug-eluting stent (DES) was placed. The patient's left ventricular end-diastolic pressure (LVEDP) was found to be elevated and she was started on low dose furosemide. 

The patient's family history includes her father having a myocardial infarction (MI) in his later years. Her siblings and children are all healthy. 

Upon admission, the patient was on aspirin, carbamazepine, clonazepam, ezetimibe, nitroglycerin, propranolol, rosuvastatin calcium, ticagrelor, and amlodipine. During her stay, furosemide was added to her medication regimen. 

The patient's physical exam upon admission and discharge was largely unremarkable. Her lab results upon admission and discharge were not provided in the note. A chest X-ray showed no acute intrathoracic process. 

The patient has had multiple recent admissions for chest pain. During her previous hospitalizations, she had a stress test that showed average functional exercise capacity with atypical angina symptoms and ECG changes without 2D echocardiographic evidence of inducible ischemia. A magnetic resonance angiogram (MRA) confirmed thickening and showed mild hyperintensity of the root of the aorta. 

The patient was discharged home with instructions to continue her medications and to follow up with her primary care provider. The discharge diagnosis was chest pain and restenosis.

The patient, a female with a history of coronary artery disease (CAD) post percutaneous coronary intervention (PCI) to the right coronary artery (RCA), presented with worsening cough, central chest discomfort, and mild shortness of breath three days after PCI. She described her chest discomfort as different from her prior chest pain associated with in-stent restenosis (ISR), which was described as substernal crushing pain. The patient's cough was worsening, and she began to feel chest tightness that was limiting her ability to breathe. She has been unable to lie flat due to chest discomfort for a long time. She has a history of epilepsy, migraine, arthritis, and spondylosis. Her family history includes her father having a myocardial infarction in his later years.

Upon admission, the patient was given Aspirin 243mg. Initial vital signs were T 98.1, HR 71, BP 117/68, RR 17, 98% RA. The EKG showed new T-wave inversions in V3 and V4. Lab results showed a hemoglobin level of 10.8, Trop <0.01, and proBNP 39. The chest X-ray did not show any acute processes. 

The patient's past medical history includes dyslipidemia and CAD post PCI to RCA with in-stent restenosis post plain old balloon angioplasty (POBA). The patient's medications upon admission included amlodipine, aspirin, carbamazepine, clonazepam, ezetimibe, nitroglycerin, propranolol, rosuvastatin, ticagrelor, and furosemide.

Abnormal lab results upon admission included RBC-3.60*, Hgb-10.8*, Hct-33.8*, and Phos-5.2*. Discharge labs showed RBC-3.40*, Hgb-10.3*, Hct-32.6*, and MCHC-31.6*, and Phos-5.1*. 

The patient underwent an exercise stress test which showed no significant ST changes noted during exercise or recovery. The patient's coronary status showed 90% ISR to RCA and an ejection fraction of 60% during stress echo. 

The patient's chest pain resolved on discharge. She was continued on her home medications including aspirin, ticagrelor, propranolol, amlodipine, rosuvastatin, ezetimibe, and furosemide. The patient also complained of her abdominal pulse being more noticeable recently, which was very apparent on physical exam. 

The patient's discharge diagnoses included chest pressure, epilepsy, migraine, and coronary artery disease. The patient was discharged home in a clear and coherent mental state, alert and interactive, and ambulatory - independent.

The patient, a female, was admitted due to right flank pain. She has a history of Ulcerative Colitis, Hypothyroidism, Hypertension, Hyperlipidemia, Anxiety, and Nephrolithiasis. She reported having passed a brown stone in her urine and was experiencing severe right groin pain. She also reported shortness of breath, 'arthritic type chest pain', chills, and multiple episodes of non-bilious, non-bloody vomiting. An EKG showed possible new ST changes and she was given Aspirin 325mg. Her cardiac enzymes were negative. Upon arrival to the floor, she reported feeling better, but was developing a migraine and had right flank pain. She also reported having chills overnight and hematuria. She suspected a colitis flare but denied bloody stools. She reported increased loose yellow stools and abdominal cramping. She has chronic arthritic joint pain, particularly in her right hip/knee.

A ureteroscopy and stent placement in the right ureter were performed. An abdominal CT showed a right proximal ureteral stone with mild right hydro and mild perinephric stranding. She was treated with IVF, Cipro, Toradol, and Zofran. 

Her lab results showed several abnormal findings. Her RBC count was 3.40*, Hgb was 10.0*, Hct was 29.7*, WBC was 13.8*#, Neuts were 85.6* and 93.1*, Lymphs were 8.7* and 5.3*, Monos were 4.8 and 0.2*, Glucose was 135*, Calcium was 7.7*, and Phos was 2.0*. Her urine and blood cultures showed the presence of E. coli. 

A CT of her abdomen/pelvis showed a 4-mm right proximal ureteral calculus, with mild right hydronephrosis and perinephric fat stranding. A CTA of her chest showed no pulmonary embolism or acute aortic pathology, but did show small-to-moderate bilateral pleural effusions, with adjacent focal atelectasis, and small focal airspace consolidation in the right lower lobe. 

She was diagnosed with Acute Pyelonephritis and Proximal Ureterolithiasis on the right side, complicated by E. coli Bacteremia. She was treated with Ceftriaxone for a total 14 day course and underwent ureteroscopy and stent placement in the right ureter. She was ruled out for MI and Acute diastolic CHF. 

Her medications upon admission included Aspirin 325mg daily, Levothyroxine 100mcg daily, Omeprazole 20mg daily, Propanolol 20mg BID, Sulindac 200mg BID, and Tramadol 50-100mg q6hr prn. 

Her family history includes a genetic disorder and CAD. She was discharged to home with service. Her discharge diagnoses included Nephrolithiasis, Pyelonephritis, and Hematuria.

The patient, a female with a history of Schizoaffective disorder, was brought to the ED after being found walking topless outside her apartment. She reported feeling 'on top of the world' for a couple of days, having lots of energy, and not sleeping the night before admission. Initial vital signs were 98.5, 144/72, 116, 16, 98% on RA. She was noted to be laughing inappropriately. Serum and urine tox were negative. CT Head was unremarkable. She was evaluated by Psychiatry who felt her presentation was typical of a maniform psychosis. She had a recent admission for Pyleonephritis due to Ureterolithiasis complicated by E coli bacteremia after Right uretheral stenting. 

Her past medical history includes benign hypertension, hyperlipidemia, ulcerative colitis, hypothyroidism, hematuria, nephrolithiasis, GERD, and osteoarthritis. She has a family history of CAD. 

Upon arrival to the wards, she reported feeling 'heavy,' but would not elaborate further. She denied pain or dyspnea, fever, chills, nightsweats, flank pain. She did report recent diarrhea. No suicidal or homicidal ideation was noted. 

Abnormal lab results include WBC-11.7*, RBC-3.46*, Hgb-10.4*, Hct-30.8*, Neuts-76.5*, Glucose-103*, ALT-44*, VitB12-1011*, TSH-4.7*, Hgb-10.6*, RBC->1000*, WBC-382*, and CastHy-14*. 

The patient was diagnosed with acute psychosis, complicated urinary tract infection, right ureteral stenting, mild transaminitis, anemia, hypothyroidism, and GERD. 

Upon admission, the patient was on aspirin 81 mg Cap, Propranolol 20 mg Tab, Clinoril 200 mg Tab, Omeprazole 20 mg Cap, Ultram 50 mg Tab, Synthroid ___ mcg Tab, and Lidocaine 5 % (700 mg/patch). 

The patient was discharged with the diagnosis of resolved psychosis NOS, unspecified mood disorder, hematuria, pyelonephritis, ureterolithiasis, E coli bacteremia, hypothyroidism, anemia, and gastroesophageal reflux disease.

The patient, a female with a history of ulcerative colitis (UC), diverticulitis, hematuria, hyperlipidemia (HL), hypertension (HTN), hypothyroidism, gastroesophageal reflux disease (GERD), osteoarthritis, and nephrolithiasis, presented with three weeks of diarrhea and mucoid/bloody stool, consistent with prior UC flares. She also reported diffuse abdominal pain, chills, difficulty eating solid foods due to nausea and pain, dizziness, dyspnea on exertion, occasional migraines, and a 16 lb weight loss. She denied fevers and melena. She had a recent episode of nephrolithiasis post lithotripsy and stent placement, and an emergency department visit when she was diagnosed with diverticulitis. 

Upon admission, her medications included aspirin, diphenoxylate-atropine, levothyroxine, omeprazole, oxycodone, propranolol, sulindac, and tramadol. She was given Ciprofloxacin, Flagyl, Morphine Sulfate, and Ondansetron in the ER. 

Physical examination revealed no apparent distress, regular heart rate and rhythm, clear lungs, soft and non-tender abdomen, and alert and oriented neurological status. Abnormal lab results included a lactate level of 3.3 and a platelet count of 473. A CT scan of the abdomen and pelvis showed colon wall thickening consistent with colitis, improved mild right hydronephrosis and more homogenous nephrogram, unchanged mild CBD and intrahepatic biliary dilatation, and incomplete contrast opacification of the origin of the celiac axis. 

During her hospital stay, she was initially suspected to have a UC flare and was put on IV antibiotics. However, stool samples tested positive for clostridium difficile toxin, leading to a change in treatment to IV and then oral Flagyl. She responded well to this treatment and was discharged home once she became asymptomatic. 

The patient has a family history of coronary artery disease. She is allergic to Indocin, Zyprexa, and Neoprene Elbow Support.

The patient, a middle-aged female, was admitted due to ongoing and worsening diarrhea. She has a history of ulcerative colitis, benign hypertension, hyperlipidemia, hypothyroidism, hematuria, nephrolithiasis, gastroesophageal reflux disease (GERD), and osteoarthritis. She has had multiple emergency department visits and hospital admissions over the past several months, including a recent admission for C. diff colitis, which was treated inpatient with IV flagyl for 5 days and discharged on a 14-day oral flagyl course. 

Upon admission, the patient reported continued/worsening diffuse lower abdominal pain and diarrhea, which improved but never fully resolved while on flagyl, and then worsened once the flagyl course was completed. She also reported feeling worse for the past few days, with symptoms including nausea, vomiting, fatigue/weakness, abdominal pain especially in the left lower quadrant, and 9 bowel movements the previous day. Stools were described as brownish fluid/mucoid gel with occasional blood. 

Physical examination revealed tachycardia and tenderness in bilateral lower quadrants without rebound or guarding. Lab results showed an elevated white blood cell count of 15.3, elevated neutrophils at 67.0, lymphocytes at 16.2, eosinophils at 12.1, and platelet count at 541. The patient's glucose level was also elevated at 105, and her sedimentation rate was high at 60. 

Stool cultures were negative for Salmonella, Shigella, Campylobacter, Cyclospora, Microsporidium, E.Coli 0157:H7, and Yersinia. However, the patient tested positive for C. difficile toxin. 

The patient's medications upon admission included aspirin, hydrocortisone acetate, levothyroxine, omeprazole, propranolol, sulindac, tramadol, and vitamin D3. 

The patient's family history includes a syndrome and coronary artery disease. 

During her hospital stay, the patient's diarrhea was attributed to recurrent C. Diff. She was treated with IV flagyl and oral vancomycin, which led to a significant improvement in her symptoms. She was noted to have a mild thrombocytosis during her admission, which was likely reactive. This should be rechecked when she follows-up with her primary care provider. 

Upon discharge, the patient was diagnosed with C. diff colitis. Her mental status was clear and coherent, and she was alert, interactive, and independently ambulatory.

The patient, a female with a history of hypertension, hyperlipidemia, ulcerative colitis, hypothyroidism, hematuria, nephrolithiasis, GERD, and osteoarthritis, was admitted to the hospital due to blood-tinged diarrhea and acute shortness of breath. She was found to be hypoxic and hypotensive upon arrival, and her heart rate had decreased significantly. She was intubated and started on dobutamine, and given 6 mg of epinephrine and 6 mg of atropine for her heart rate. She had a return of spontaneous circulation (ROSC) after about 30 minutes of CPR. A bedside echo showed right ventricular (RV) strain. She was empirically given tPA and started on IV heparin, as well as vanc/cefepime. Her oxygenation and blood pressure improved transiently, but she became bradycardic shortly thereafter and lost pulses, leading to another PEA arrest code. She was transferred to the MICU for further care.

Upon physical examination, the patient was found to be intubated and sedated. Her pupils were fixed and dilated to 8cm, with no corneal reflex. Her lungs were clear to auscultation bilaterally, and her heart was tachycardic. Her abdomen was mildly distended, with bowel sounds present and no rebound tenderness or guarding. Her extremities were cool, with difficult to appreciate peripheral pulses. She had no withdrawal to pain.

Lab results showed a glucose level of 343, AST of 41, AlkPhos of 118, cTropnT of 0.03, Calcium of 7.4, Phos of 8.3, and D-Dimer of 4990. Post-arrest labs showed WBC of 20.9, RBC of 2.91, Hgb of 8.8, Hct of 28.8, MCV of 99, MCHC of 30.4, PTT of 150, Glucose of 346, K of 6.3, Cl of 114, HCO3 of 16, ALT of 356, AST of 532, LD(LDH) of 976, Albumin of 1.8, Calcium of 7.6, Phos of 6.1, pCO2 of 87, pH of 6.83, calTCO2 of 16, Glucose of 323, and Lactate of 12.6.

Imaging showed an endotracheal tube terminating approximately 4.9 cm above the level of the carina, no focal consolidation, no large pleural effusion or pneumothorax, and a top-normal cardiac silhouette. An EKG showed sinus tachycardia, a new RBB with normal axis, and a widened QRS. A TEE showed right ventricular dilation, free wall hypokinesis, severe tricuspid regurgitation with non coapting tricuspid valve leaflets, and no pericardial effusion.

The patient's medications upon admission included Levothyroxine Sodium, Aspirin, Omeprazole, Sulindac, TraMADOL, Multivitamins, Propranolol, and Lidocaine. The patient passed away during her hospital stay. The cause of her PEA arrest is unknown, but it is presumed to be due to a pulmonary embolism.

The patient, a male, was admitted due to pain following an aborted laparoscopic hiatal hernia repair. He has a history of recurrent hiatal hernia and depression. His surgical history includes lap Nissen fundoplication and port site hernia repair twice. He presented to the emergency department with pleuritic left chest pain and right posterior shoulder/scapular pain. He had taken oxycodone, which did not improve his pain. He reported no nausea, vomiting, or difficulty breathing. 

Upon physical examination, his vitals were stable and he was alert and oriented. He had subcutaneous emphysema in the left lower chest. His abdomen was soft, non-tender, and non-distended with positive bowel sounds. His extremities were warm, well-perfused, with 2+ pulses. 

Lab results showed elevated WBC at 11.3, low Hgb at 13.3, and elevated glucose at 122. His ALT was also elevated at 53. A CT scan of the chest and abdomen showed no evidence of pulmonary embolism or aortic dissection. There was no oral contrast extravasation from the esophagus to suggest esophageal perforation, but an occult leak could not be excluded due to the degree of pneumomediastinum and pneumoperitoneum. There was left thoracic wall subcutaneous air likely related to trocar placement and a large hiatal hernia was noted. 

During his hospital stay, the patient was started on IV dilaudid for pain control, which was later transitioned to oral medication. He was also started on a regular diet which he tolerated well.
