An upper GI study was negative for a leak, therefore, the diet was advanced sequentially to a Bariatric Stage 3 diet, which was well tolerated. His liver function tests remained within normal limits. There were no signs of infection or bleeding.

Abnormal lab results include RBC-4.55*, RDWSD-51.6*, Glucose-103*, UreaN-21*, Creat-1.3*, TotBili-3.0*, DirBili-0.6*, IndBili-2.4, Phos-2.1*, tacroFK-4.2*, WBC-10.8*#, RDW-15.6*, RDWSD-52.1*, Glucose-106*, UreaN-26*, Creat-1.5*, ALT-53*, TotBili-3.3*, and tacroFK-3.8*.

Upon admission, the patient was on Amlodipine, Aspirin, Citalopram, Hydrochlorothiazide, Lisinopril, Metoprolol Succinate XL, Pravastatin, Tacrolimus, Testosterone Cypionate, Zolpidem, Calcarb 600 With Vitamin D, and Multivitamins.

The patient, a female of tall stature and thin build, presented to the emergency department with a four-day history of orthostatic headaches, which were worse when standing and improved when lying flat. The headaches were sometimes accompanied by nausea or a feeling of dysequilibrium when walking, and a 'muffled' sensation in her left ear. She had been previously diagnosed with a 'sinus headache' at an Urgent Care clinic and was prescribed Augmentin and Ibuprofen, which did not alleviate her symptoms. A head CT scan revealed a >10mm pituitary mass, likely a macroadenoma. The patient denied any prior history of headaches or endocrine symptoms such as menstrual irregularities, weight gain/loss, heat/cold intolerance, etc. She had a past medical history of an L4-5 bulging disk, for which she had received several epidural steroid injections. There was no family history of brain tumors, endocrine abnormalities, or migraines.

Upon admission, the patient was not on any medications. Her physical exam was largely unremarkable, with normal vitals and no signs of meningismus, edema, or rashes. Neurological examination was also normal, with no evidence of apraxia, neglect, or focal weakness. However, she did exhibit non-extinguishing horizontal nystagmus on endgaze bilaterally. 

Lab results showed abnormal values for Hgb (10.9), Hct (34.1), MCV (77), MCH (24.4), and LH (<1.0), and Cortisol (23.1). A pituitary protocol MRI recommended nonemergent evaluation for a high attenuation lesion within the sella turcica measuring 12 x 10 x 13 mm, likely representing a pituitary adenoma or other neoplasm. The differential included a Rathke's cleft cyst.

During her hospital stay, the patient's headache improved significantly following IV fluid resuscitation, and she was able to sit up, stand, and ambulate without severe headache pain. She was discharged home with instructions to maintain hydration, complete a course of antibiotics for presumed sinus disease, and follow up in the pituitary tumor clinic and ophthalmology for formal visual field testing. The primary discharge diagnosis was a headache secondary to dehydration and sinus disease, with the inability to rule out intracranial hypotension due to a CSF leak. A secondary diagnosis was a likely pituitary macroadenoma vs Rathke's cyst.

The patient is a left-handed male with a history of hypertension, hyperlipidemia, obstructive sleep apnea, chest pain, migraines, asthma, irritable bowel syndrome, hemorrhagic colitis, gastroesophageal reflux disease, Schatzki's ring, and eczema. He has also undergone an appendectomy. He presented to the emergency department with blurred vision, left arm and leg heaviness, abdominal pain, and difficulty opening his cell phone. His stroke scale score was 0, indicating no stroke symptoms. His physical examination was largely normal, with no evidence of apraxia or neglect. His neurological examination showed normal bulk and tone throughout, with no pronator drift, tremor, or asterixis noted. He had giveway weakness in his left fingers and decreased velocity of finger tap on the left side compared to the right. His sensory examination was intact.

His family history includes hypertension, hyperlipidemia, diabetes, and cardiovascular disease. There is no family history of stroke, cancer, intracerebral aneurysms/arteriovenous malformations, connective tissue disorders, autoimmune disorders, or inflammatory disorders.

Upon admission, the patient was taking albuterol sulfate, esomeprazole magnesium, Combivent, lisinopril, simvastatin, aspirin, a multivitamin, and omega-3 fatty acids.

His lab results showed abnormal RBC count (3.93), hemoglobin (13.2), hematocrit (38.0), MCH (33.6), RBC count (4.39), MCH (32.1), neutrophils (83.5), lymphocytes (12.8), PTT (20.7), glucose (131), cholesterol (211), triglycerides (193), and cholesterol (204). His urine tested positive for cocaine.

His CT scan of the head showed no acute intracranial abnormality, but his MRI showed an early subacute infarct involving the junction of the left putamen and the lateral aspect of the posterior limb of the internal capsule. His MRA was unremarkable. His TTE and TEE showed no cardiac source of embolism, preserved global and regional biventricular systolic function, and no significant valvular abnormality.

During his hospital stay, the patient was evaluated for possible stroke. His CT scan did not show acute bleeding, but his MRI showed an early subacute infarct. The most likely mechanism of stroke was thought to be microvascular. His blood alcohol level was 191 and his urine tested positive for cocaine. He was advised to never use cocaine again. His lipid profile showed high triglycerides and his HbA1c was 5.7. His aspirin was stopped and he was started on Plavix. His dose of simvastatin was increased from 20 mg to 40 mg/day and he was started on verapamil for control of migraines.

The male patient was admitted to the CARDIOTHORACIC service due to left chest pain and shortness of breath (SOB). He has a history of two previous episodes of left pneumothorax (PTX) within the last three months, with the last episode requiring chest tube placement. He was discharged two days after the last episode with plans to follow up with a thoracic surgeon, which he has yet to do. The patient reported recurrent symptoms of left PTX, including dyspnea on exertion (DOE), SOB, and left chest pain. He denied any recent illnesses and his review of systems (ROS) was otherwise negative. 

During his hospital stay, the patient underwent a left pleural pigtail catheter placement and a left thoracoscopy with bleb stapling and abrasive pleurodesis. His physical examination was largely normal, with the exception of decreased breath sounds in the left lung. His laboratory results showed an elevated white blood cell count (WBC-13.1*) and elevated absolute neutrophil (AbsNeut-8.16*) and absolute monocyte (AbsMono-1.17*) counts. His chest X-ray revealed a small left apical pneumothorax, which remained unchanged even after the removal of the left pleural drainage catheter. There was also mild left pleural thickening, but no pleural effusion. 

The patient's hospital course was stable. He tolerated the surgical procedures well and his pain was controlled with IV Dilaudid and Tylenol. His chest tube had no air leak and remained on suction. His pain medication was later changed to oral Dilaudid and Gabapentin was added. His wounds were healing well and his oxygen was weaned off with room air saturations of 97%. His chest tube was eventually removed and his post pull chest x-ray revealed a stable left apical pneumothorax. He was using his incentive spirometer with much encouragement and was ambulating independently. 

Upon admission, the patient was taking Chantix (varenicline) 1 mg orally daily. There is no family history of any known illnesses. The patient was discharged with a diagnosis of recurrent left pneumothorax. His mental status was clear and coherent, and he was alert, interactive, and independently ambulatory. He was instructed to follow up with Dr. ___ in 2 weeks.

The patient, a female, was admitted due to acute onset back pain. She has a history of chronic abdominal and back pain for several years, which is attributed to previous surgeries. The pain was accompanied by profuse vomiting. Upon examination at the emergency department, a Type B aortic dissection was identified through a CT scan. Her systolic blood pressure was over 200 upon arrival, but responded to nitroprusside and esmolol. She continued to experience abdominal and back pain, although the severity decreased with the administration of narcotics.

Her past medical history includes myocardial infarction, chronic obstructive pulmonary disease, cerebrovascular accident, prolonged QT, rectocele, hypertension, and conversion disorder presenting with signs and symptoms of cerebrovascular accident. She has undergone cholecystectomy, total abdominal hysterectomy/bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy, and appendectomy. She lives at home and continues to smoke. Her son has schizoaffective disorder and her daughter has bipolar disorder.

Physical examination revealed mild distress, clear lungs, regular rate and rhythm in the cardiovascular system, and a soft, distended, and obese abdomen with diffuse tenderness, particularly in the epigastric region. No palpable masses were found. Her lower extremities had scabs but no edema or cyanosis. 

Lab results showed abnormal calcium levels at 8.1, and a CT angiogram confirmed the Type B aortic dissection, starting just past the takeoff of the left subclavian artery and extending down to above the iliac bifurcation. There was a discrepancy in kidney perfusion, with the right kidney receiving less blood than the left. The right kidney also showed signs of an evolving infarct.

During her hospital stay, the patient was monitored in the ICU for strict blood pressure control. She showed no signs of mesenteric or peripheral limb ischemia. Despite an infarcted right kidney, her creatinine levels remained stable. She was found to have a urinary tract infection on the third day of her hospital stay and was started on a three-day course of ciprofloxacin. She experienced intermittent confusion, which was investigated and resolved. Her abdominal and back pain improved over the course of her stay.

Upon admission, her medications included Lactulose, Fosamax, Caltrate 600, Bentyl, Albuterol, Ativan, Aspirin, Hydrochlorothiazide, Cyclobenzaprine, Vicodin, Cymbalta, and Fioricet.

The patient, a female, was admitted due to sudden onset of midback pain radiating to the front, similar to a previous episode. She had been diagnosed with a Type B aortic dissection in the past and was managed non-operatively with strict blood pressure control. She was discharged on Hydrochlorothiazide 25mg BID, Losartan 50mg BID, and Toprol 100mg daily. The patient also reported nausea, vomiting, fatigue, shortness of breath with exertion, and leg weakness. Her systolic blood pressure was found to be in the 190s. A CTA showed extension of the dissection, leading to her admission to the vascular surgery service.

Her past medical history includes MI, COPD, CVA, prolonged QT, rectocele, HTN, and conversion disorder presenting with signs/symptoms of CVA. She has undergone CCY, TAH/BSO, and appy. She has allergies to Amoxicillin, Lipitor, ketorolac, Sporanox, Latex, Lasix, Amitriptyline, Benadryl, Sulfa (Sulfonamide Antibiotics), Erythromycin Base, and Haldol. Her family history is non-contributory.

Upon physical examination, her vitals were T: 98.9 degrees Fahrenheit, BP: 150s-160s/70s, HR 75 bpm, RR 16 bpm, O2: 91% on RA. Her general appearance was pleasant and calm. No abnormalities were noted in her HEENT, neck, CV, lungs, abdomen, and extremities. Neurologically, she was alert and oriented, with grossly intact cranial nerves and strength in all extremities.

Abnormal lab results included PLT COUNT-786, NEUTS-76.8, LYMPHS-16.9, WBC-11.4, RBC-3.47, HGB-10.5, HCT-30.7, GLUCOSE-109, SODIUM-131, and CHLORIDE-93. A CTA of the torso showed a contained aortic rupture at the proximal descending thoracic aorta with a small periaortic mediastinal hematoma, aneurysmal descending thoracic aorta up to 4.4 cm, interval progression of type B aortic dissection with compromised perfusion of the right kidney, and bilateral pulmonary granulomas and sub-4-mm nodules.

During her hospital stay, her blood pressure was monitored strictly and controlled with an IV nitroglycerin drip. Despite uptitration of her metoprolol and losartan, she remained hypertensive in the 160s. Her medications were switched to labetalol and lisinopril, which resulted in excellent control. She experienced recurrent abdominal pain after eating, which resolved after a bowel movement. At discharge, her blood pressure had been well controlled for 2 days, she was tolerating a regular diet without pain, and she was ambulating without difficulty.

Her medications upon admission were Famotidine 20 mg BID, Duloxetine 60 mg DAILY, Cyclobenzaprine 10 mg TID, Albuterol sulfate 2.5 mg /3 mL (0.083 %) One puff Q4H as needed for SOB/wheezing, Dicyclomine 20 mg QID as needed for GI spasms, Tramadol 50 mg Q4H as needed for pain, Hydrochlorothiazide 25 mg DAILY, Losartan 50 mg BID, and Toprol 100 mg DAILY.

The patient, a male with a history of Asperger's syndrome, Tourette's syndrome, and recurrent pneumothorax, was admitted due to a first-time seizure. He was found by his father in a state of altered mental status, shaking, and post-ictal confusion/weakness. The patient had no history of febrile seizures as a child, encephalitis, or meningitis. He had undergone surgeries for recurrent pneumothorax. 

The patient's symptoms included right arm soreness, tongue biting, and generalized tonic-clonic movements. He was given 2 mg IV ativan after a second seizure episode. He was somnolent following the medication administration. 

Upon physical examination, the patient was thin, pale, and appeared younger than his stated age. He was somnolent, arousable to touch and noxious stimuli. Neurological examination revealed that he was unable to maintain wakefulness for a long time and unable to complete a full evaluation due to his current mental status. 

Lab results showed abnormal glucose levels at 107, chloride levels at 110, total CO2 at 20, AST(SGOT) at 49, LD(LDH) at 299, phosphate at 2.2, WBC at 12.1, RBC at 4.27, HGB at 13.0, HCT at 37.2, NEUTS at 88.0, LYMPHS at 6.9, and PTT at 21.3. MRI results showed no intracranial abnormality, specifically normal signal intensity in the bilateral mesial temporal regions. EEG and spinal fluid cytology results were pending. 

During his hospital stay, the patient was loaded with Keppra, initially at 750mg BID, which was increased to 1000mg BID after additional events. EEG monitoring showed no epileptiform activity while on Keppra. CSF obtained by lumbar puncture was unremarkable for infection or other abnormalities. The patient was also maintained on Guanfacine for his Tourette's motor tics. His mood and personality improved over hospital day #2. 

The patient was pancultured for an initial temperature of 100.3 at the outside hospital. Urinalysis and Gram Stain of the CSF were unremarkable. The patient's LFT were slightly elevated with an AST of 49 and LDH of 299. 

Upon admission, the patient was on Guanfacine 1 mg TID and Duocal 1 scoop QID. The patient was discharged with a diagnosis of generalized seizures. His mental status was clear and coherent, and he was alert and interactive. He was instructed to continue the Keppra for seizure control and follow up with appointments.

The patient, a female with a history of cryptogenic cirrhosis, was admitted due to gastrointestinal bleeding and thrombocytopenia. She reported seeing bright red blood in her stool for one day. She had a similar episode several years ago but has not recurred since. She was initially presented to an OSH, where she was noted to have a Hct of 28.5 with platelets of 33. She was transfused one unit of platelets and transferred for further evaluation. She denies abdominal pain, nausea, vomiting but does note some slight fatigue. She also notes intermittent diarrhea over the last month. 

Her past medical history includes cryptogenic cirrhosis with documented esophageal varices, splenectomy of unknown cause, type 2 diabetes mellitus, colonic polyps, pulmonary hypertension, hypertension, left rotator cuff injury, chronic kidney disease with baseline creatinine of 1.4, appendectomy, macular degeneration, congestive heart failure, gait disturbance, paraproteinemia, and total abdominal hysterectomy. Her family history includes her mother dying of a blood clot and her father dying of diphtheria. She has two deceased siblings, one from coronary artery disease.

Upon physical examination, she was alert, oriented, and in no acute distress. Her vitals were stable and her physical exam was largely unremarkable. Her labs showed a Hct of 28.1, platelets of 85, and creatinine of 1.6. She received pantoprazole 40mg IV x 1. 

Her abdominal ultrasound suggested a liver mass in segment V/VIII and showed a heterogeneous liver with slightly decreased portal venous flow consistent with cirrhosis. However, the MRI of the abdomen did not detect any focal hepatic lesions. The MRI also showed a few cystic lesions of the pancreas measuring up to 14 mm, renal cysts, and cholelithiasis. A TTE showed mild aortic valve stenosis, minimal mitral stenosis, mild pulmonary artery systolic hypertension, and normal biventricular cavity sizes with preserved global and regional biventricular systolic function. 

Her CBC showed consistently low RBC, Hgb, Hct, and platelet count. Her chemistry panel showed elevated urea nitrogen and creatinine, and low potassium. Her LFTs were largely normal, except for an elevated LDH. Her D-dimer was elevated, but her fibrinogen, haptoglobin, and LDH were normal. Her hepatitis serology was negative. 

She underwent an EGD and colonoscopy, which showed diverticulosis, rectal varices, and internal hemorrhoids, but no active bleeding. Her C. diff test was negative. Her thrombocytopenia is thought to be related to her hepatic disease, and she was transfused a total of 3 bags of platelets during her admission. Her medications upon admission included Protonix, Nadolol, MVI, Feosol, Glyburide, Calcium/Vitamin D, Colace, Lasix, and Lisinopril.

The patient is a female with a history of cryptogenic cirrhosis, thrombocytopenia, chronic kidney disease (CKD), type 2 diabetes mellitus (DM2), congestive heart failure (CHF), paraproteinemia, gait disturbance, and left rotator cuff injury. She was admitted due to sudden onset altered mental status (AMS) including confusion, sensation of heat, lightheadedness, slurred speech, and left hand weakness. This occurred after being discharged from a previous hospitalization for evaluation of anemia. 

Upon admission, her blood pressure was 212/84 and her blood glucose was 36. She was given D50 and became more alert, but was still not back to her normal self. Labs were notable for hematocrit of 26, platelets of 74K, and INR of 1.1. A non-contrast head CT showed a linear pontine left greater than right hemorrhage. 

Her physical exam revealed that she was awake, cooperative, and not in acute distress. She had normal bulk and tone throughout, with no pronator drift bilaterally. Her sensory exam showed intact light touch and cold sensation, but impaired vibratory sense bilaterally to the ankle and impaired proprioception at the great toes bilaterally. 

Abnormal lab results included RBC of 3.02*, Hgb of 9.0*, Hct of 27.4*, RDW of 15.9*, and Plt Ct of 58*. Her glucose was 106*, UreaN was 37*, and Creat was 1.3*. LD(LDH) was 266*. Calcium was 8.3*. 

A CT scan showed an unchanged pontine focal hyperdensity concerning for acute hemorrhage. An MRI of the brain without contrast revealed a chronic small left pontine hemorrhage. A chest X-ray showed no evidence of pneumonia but small bilateral pleural effusions were present. 

The patient's medications upon admission included nadolol, multivitamin, ferrous sulfate, glyburide, calcium + D, colace, furosemide, lisinopril, and pantoprazole. 

The patient's family history includes hypertension and coronary artery disease/peripheral vascular disease. There is no family history of cerebrovascular accident/transient ischemic attack, intracerebral aneurysms/arteriovenous malformation, connective tissue disorders, or autoimmune disorders. 

The patient's altered mental status was likely due to a hypoglycemic episode. Seizure was less likely given no loss of consciousness and subcortical symptoms, but still possible. The hyperdensity's etiology is uncertain, possibly calcification in chronic cirrhosis versus hypertensive bleed. The patient was neurologically stable at the time. 

During her hospital stay, she had an episode of hypoglycemia to 46. Her insulin sliding scale was discontinued and she was encouraged to eat all her meals. She was restarted on her glyburide. She was also started on PredniSONE 60 mg daily due to her history of thrombocytopenia. Prior to discharge, her platelet count increased to 74. 

At discharge, her neurologic exam was significant for being at her baseline mental status and able to ambulate with assistance. Her discharge diagnoses included pontine hemorrhage (chronic), thrombocytopenia (unknown cause), chronic kidney disease, anemia, hypertension, and diabetes.

The patient, a female with a history of diabetes mellitus, congestive heart failure, cryptogenic cirrhosis as noted by CT/US, status post splenectomy, and thrombocytopenia, presented to the emergency department from the clinic for gastrointestinal bleeding. She had recently been admitted for gastrointestinal bleeding and thrombocytopenia. During that admission, her colonoscopy showed sigmoid diverticulosis, large rectal varices without signs of recent bleeding, and grade 1 internal hemorrhoids. She was also evaluated for thrombocytopenia, with the most likely causes thought to be idiopathic thrombocytopenic purpura (ITP) and cirrhosis. 

On the morning of admission, she had a large loose bowel movement with bright red blood mixed with dark colored stool. She reported having loose stools once to twice daily over the past several months, which were always dark due to her iron supplement. She also reported feeling more drained in the past week with increased fatigue and mild dyspnea on exertion and occasional lightheadedness. 

Upon physical examination, her rectal exam was notable for maroon stools, guaiac positive. Her labs showed leukocytosis (WBC 20.2), low hematocrit (23.4), platelet count of 103, INR 0.9, and elevated BNP (3645). She was given pantoprazole 40mg IV and started on octreotide given her history of rectal varices. 

In the ICU, a flexible sigmoidoscopy showed large rectal varices without bleeding, and ethiodol injection with hemostasis in the rectal varices was performed. Her hematocrit remained stable and she received 1 unit of packed red blood cells. Her anti-hypertensives were held overnight and then resumed, with furosemide 40 mg being held currently. 

Her past medical history also includes anemia possibly related to chronic renal failure, hypertension, pulmonary hypertension, mild aortic stenosis, mild mitral stenosis, mild mitral regurgitation on echo, chronic kidney disease with a baseline creatinine of 1.4, paraproteinemia, left rotator cuff injury status post motor vehicle accident, status post appendectomy, left macular degeneration, history of gait disturbance, and status post total abdominal hysterectomy and unilateral oophorectomy. 

Her family history is positive for hypertension and coronary artery disease/peripheral vascular disease, but negative for cerebrovascular accident/transient ischemic attack, intracerebral aneurysms/arteriovenous malformations, connective tissue disorders, and autoimmune disorders. 

Abnormal lab results on admission included elevated WBC (20.2), low RBC (2.47), low hemoglobin (7.2), low hematocrit (23.4), low MCHC (30.6), elevated neutrophils (86.9), low lymphocytes (8.7), elevated urea nitrogen (66), elevated creatinine (1.5), and elevated total protein (6.3). Abnormal lab results on discharge included low RBC (3.11), low hemoglobin (8.8), low hematocrit (27.7), elevated RDW (16.2), elevated glucose (183), elevated urea nitrogen (60), elevated creatinine (1.3), low chloride (109), and low calcium (7.7). 

Her chest x-ray showed increasing prominence of interstitial markings consistent with developing pulmonary edema and small pleural effusions. A CT of the head without contrast showed a focal hyperdensity within the midline pons, unchanged in appearance compared to a prior study. 

Her medications upon admission included ferrous sulfate, prednisone, nadolol, glyburide, furosemide, lisinopril, pantoprazole, amlodipine, colace, and calcium + D.

The patient is a male with minimal past medical history who presented with left-sided chest pain that radiated up to his left shoulder blade. The pain was worse with deep breaths, felt sharp, and was accompanied by shortness of breath. The pain did not go away over the course of the day and had no relationship to movement or exertion. The patient denied any diaphoresis, presyncope, syncope, nausea, or abdominal pain. There was no recent history of congestion, cough, or viral syndrome. The patient had previously been seen in the ED for atypical left-sided abdominal pain and was discharged on famotidine after a benign CT abdomen. 

Upon admission, the patient was given 80 mg enoxaparin SC, 325 mg ASA, started on a nitroglycerin drip, and morphine *1. His pain improved dramatically with these interventions. In the ED, his vitals were T 98.6, P 74, BP 140/70, O2 Sat 100% on 2L. He was continued on NTG drip. His pain got worse briefly and he was given one dose of morphine 4 mg IV with improvement. Cardiology evaluated his ECG and were unimpressed. 

The patient's lab results showed a D-DIMER of 223, GLUCOSE of 100, UREA N of 16, CREAT of 1.0, SODIUM of 138, POTASSIUM of 4.6, CHLORIDE of 101, TOTAL CO2 of 28, ANION GAP of 14, CK(CPK) of 153, cTropnT of <0.01, CK-MB of 3, WBC of 8.5#, RBC of 4.91, HGB of 15.0, HCT of 42.8, MCV of 87, MCH of 30.5, MCHC of 34.9, RDW of 13.3, PLT COUNT of 216, NEUTS of 59.8, MONOS of 4.7, EOS of 2.6, and BASOS of 1.1. 

A chest x-ray revealed a linear structure at the right lung apex, which was confirmed to be a right apical pneumothorax. However, a subsequent PA/LAT CXR showed that the lungs were fully expanded and clear, with no pneumothorax or pleural effusion. 

The patient's chest pain was likely caused by a spontaneous primary pneumothorax (PSP). He had no risk factors for PSP, including no family history of PSP, was a lifetime nonsmoker, and had no history of any genetic disorders. ACS and PE were ruled out due to poor story for angina, no EKG changes, negative enzymes, no risk factors, and a negative D-dimer. The patient was discharged with a diagnosis of right apical pneumothorax, in a stable, afebrile, chest pain-free condition. His medication upon admission was famotidine OTC PRN.

The patient, a female, was admitted due to a long-standing history of various symptoms including headache, inattention, generalized fatigue, sleep disturbance, memory loss, anxiety, OCD-like behavior, arthralgias, and myalgias. These symptoms began after an febrile illness associated with extreme fatigue, diagnosed as Mono, around the same time her parents were diagnosed with a condition based on a classic EM rash and history of tick bite. The patient was not treated despite similar exposure. She has seen many medical providers and been admitted multiple times in the past. Her symptoms have become more persistent and consistent, leading to her inability to obtain a job after completing esthetician school. She can perform ADL's, but has difficulty with iADL's, so she has moved back with her parents. All her prior workup had been negative, and her symptoms were attributed to psychosomatic, and she has been on many psychiatric medications. 

During her stay, she underwent a lumbar puncture and was started on high-dose CTX. The lumbar puncture showed no WBC's, normal protein, and normal glucose. She also received 2gm IV ceftriaxone. Her other routine labs were normal. A PET-CT head scan showed mild relative hypometabolism in the anteromedial temporal lobes bilaterally. 

She denies fevers, night sweats, focal weakness, numbness or tingling but endorses a 10 lb weight loss over a vague duration. She also notes lots of stretch marks on her skin, but did have intentional weight loss of 100 lbs in the past, after putting on 100 lbs due to side effects of Prozac. She has also developed a tic of her jaw, which causes significant discomfort at her left TMJ. She denies any bowel or bladder incontinence. Her parents report a withdrawn personality, which is a dramatic difference from her childhood personality and has worsened with time.

Her past medical history includes anxiety, depression, and migraines. She has no known allergies or adverse drug reactions. Upon admission, she was on Topiramate (Topamax) 100 mg PO DAILY, Amphetamine-Dextroamphetamine 15 mg PO QAM:PRN fatigue, Venlafaxine XR 37.5 mg PO DAILY, ClonazePAM ___ mg PO QHS:PRN sleep, and Naltrexone Dose is Unknown  PO Frequency is Unknown. 

Her physical exam showed no nuchal rigidity, no murmur, no crackles, no edema, and no erythematous rashes. She had many linear stretch marks / striae on her abdomen, back, and all extremities. She was alert and oriented, with no asterixis, and fluent speech. She appeared mildly anxious and easily frustrated. 

The patient was discharged to extended care with a diagnosis of headache and fatigue. Her mental status was clear and coherent, and she was alert and interactive. She was ambulatory and independent.

The patient, a male with a history of depression and irritable bowel syndrome, was admitted due to acute onset chest pain and pressure. He reported that the pain was constant, non-radiating, and did not worsen with exertion. He also experienced lightheadedness, blurred vision, shortness of breath, and palpitations, which have since resolved. He was found to have a diastolic heart murmur and aortic dissection extending to the iliac bifurcation. He has no known family history of aortic dissection. He was taken to the operating room for emergency surgery.

His past medical history includes depression and irritable bowel syndrome. He has had a cyst removed from his back and knee surgery for a congenital bone abnormality. His family history does not include premature coronary artery disease or aortic dissection.

Upon admission, his vitals were T 97.8, HR 58, BP 96/52, RR 13, O2 98ra. His physical exam revealed a diastolic murmur and decreased right carotid pulse compared to the left. His discharge exam showed stable vitals and no edema.

His admission labs showed abnormal values for WBC (12.5*), glucose (151*), urea nitrogen (24*), creatinine (1.3*), total CO2 (21*), and lactate (2.9*). His discharge labs showed abnormal values for WBC (12.5*), RBC (2.78*), Hgb (8.5*), Hct (25.7*), and urea nitrogen (26*).

Imaging revealed an extensive aortic dissection extending from the aortic root to the aortic bifurcation, a dilated aortic root and ascending aorta, and a left common iliac artery aneurysm. Echocardiography showed a moderately dilated aorta at sinus level, ascending aorta, and mildly dilated descending aorta. There was also severe aortic regurgitation and a hyperdynamic left ventricular ejection fraction of >75%.

During his hospital stay, he underwent emergency repair of type A aortic dissection with Bentall procedure using a 25 mm mechanical composite graft, and ascending aorta hemi arch replacement using a 28 mm Gelweave graft. He had significant bleeding issues intraoperatively and required multiple units of red cells, platelets, and fresh frozen plasma. He was chemically paralyzed and sedated with an open chest for recovery and invasive monitoring. He was extubated on postoperative day 5 and was transferred to the telemetry floor for further recovery on postoperative day 8. He was discharged home in good condition on postoperative day 12.

His medications upon admission were aspirin 325mg tablet prn headaches and bupropion 100mg tablet daily. His discharge medications included aspirin, atorvastatin, bupropion, clonidine, docusate sodium, furosemide, hydromorphone, lisinopril, metoprolol tartrate, potassium chloride, and warfarin.

The patient, a female with a history of right basilic deep vein thrombosis (DVT) on Coumadin, uterine fibroids, dysfunctional uterine bleeding, asthma, and eczema, presented with abdominal pain, nausea, vomiting, and diarrhea. She reported feeling sick to her stomach, with the pain being predominantly epigastric and sharp in quality. The pain was intermittent and she also reported subjective fevers and chills. She had more than 10 bowel movements that were watery brown and also reported nausea and vomiting. She was given 2L of IVF, zofran and promethazine for nausea, and morphine for pain in the ED. Her vomiting improved but she continued to feel lightheaded and her abdominal pain returned with rapid movement or standing. She was admitted to medicine for further workup and evaluation. 

The patient's family history includes her father having a history of myocardial infarction, her brother having a history of chest cancer (type unknown), and her grandmother also having a history of myocardial infarction. 

Upon physical examination, the patient was found to be mildly tachycardic with tender palpation in the epigastric region. Gross blood was noted on rectal examination. 

Lab results showed abnormal RBC count at 6.28, Hct at 48.4, MCV at 77, MCH at 24.2, Neuts at 84.4, Lymphs at 11.4, and Glucose at 116. AlkPhos was also elevated at 123. 

The patient's abdominal pain, nausea, and vomiting were most likely due to viral gastroenteritis. She did not have evidence of fever or leukocytosis, and her abdominal exam was significant for only mild discomfort in the epigastric region. Liver function tests and lipase were within normal limits, therefore, biliary or pancreatic pathology was unlikely. 

The patient's history of fibroids and dysfunctional uterine bleeding was managed by continuing her previously started 14-day course of Provera. Her history of right upper extremity DVT was managed by maintaining her therapeutic INR and continuing her home Coumadin dose. Her asthma was managed by continuing her home medications. 

Upon admission, the patient was on several medications including Albuterol Sulfate, Betamethasone, Cromolyn, Mometasone, Orphenadrine Citrate, Tramadol, Warfarin, and Provera. 

The patient was discharged with a diagnosis of viral gastroenteritis. Her mental status was clear and coherent, and she was alert and interactive. She was ambulatory and independent.

The patient, a female with a high body mass index of 46.43 kg/(m^2), was admitted to the OB/GYN service due to heavy and irregular vaginal bleeding. She has a history of fibroid uterus, which was recently diagnosed via ultrasound. An attempt at Novasure was unsuccessful due to the size of the uterus. An EM biopsy showed menstrual endometrium without cellular abnormalities. The patient's uterus was measured at 15x9cm with the largest fibroid being 7x7cm. 

The patient's past medical history includes a clot in the shoulder, for which she is not currently on anticoagulation, and morbid obesity. She has no history of STDs and her last pap smear was negative. She has no past surgical history. 

The patient's family history includes a brother with throat cancer and a father with a heart disorder. There is no family history of problems with anesthesia, blood clotting disorders, pulmonary embolism, or bleeding disorders. 

Upon physical examination, the patient was found to be well developed with no skin lesions or lymphatic adenopathy. Her thyroid was non-tender and not enlarged. Her heart had a regular rate and rhythm with no murmur, rub, or gallop. Her abdomen was soft and non-tender with no masses or palpable hepatosplenomegaly. Her uterus had an irregular contour and was difficult to outline, measuring the size of 12 weeks. 

During her hospital stay, the patient underwent a supracervical hysterectomy, right salpingo-oophorectomy, and partial left salpingo-oophorectomy. Her post-operative course was uncomplicated. Her pain was managed with IV dilaudid and toradol. She was given subcutaneous heparin three times a day for prophylaxis due to her history of an unprovoked upper extremity deep venous thrombosis. By post-operative day 3, she was tolerating a regular diet, voiding spontaneously, ambulating independently, and her pain was controlled with oral medications. 

Upon admission, the patient was taking acetaminophen-codeine 300-30mg as needed for pain and ibuprofen 400mg, one tablet every 6 hours as needed for pain. 

Upon discharge, the patient was diagnosed with a fibroid uterus. Her mental status was clear and coherent, and she was alert and interactive. She was ambulatory and independent.

The patient, a female, was admitted due to right knee pain. She has a history of right knee osteoarthritis that was refractory to conservative management. She underwent a right total knee replacement procedure during her stay. Her past medical history includes a clot in her shoulder, for which she is not currently on anticoagulation, and morbid obesity. She has no past surgical history. Her family history includes a brother with throat cancer and a father with a heart disorder. There is no family history of problems with anesthesia, blood clotting disorders, pulmonary embolism, or bleeding disorders.

Upon physical examination, the patient was well-appearing and in no acute distress. Her pain was well-controlled and her vital signs were stable. Her incision was healing well with staples and covered with Silverlon. There was scant serosanguinous drainage, but her thigh was full yet soft with no calf tenderness. Her strength was noted as normal and her toes were warm.

Her lab results showed several abnormal findings. Her RBC count was 3.88*, Hgb was 9.7*, Hct was 29.9*, MCV was 77*, and MCH was 25.0*. Her WBC count was 11.8*#, Hgb was 11.3*, Hct was 35.0*, MCV was 76*, and MCH was 24.6*. Her glucose levels were also abnormal at 104* and 142*.

During her hospital stay, she was managed by the orthopedic surgery service. Her surgery was uncomplicated and she tolerated the procedure well. She received perioperative IV antibiotics. Her pain was initially managed with a PCA and then transitioned to oral pain medications on the first postoperative day. She received Lovenox for DVT prophylaxis starting on the morning of the first postoperative day. Her surgical dressing was changed on the second postoperative day and found to be clean and intact. She was seen daily by physical therapy and her labs were checked and repleted accordingly.

Upon admission, her medications included Albuterol Inhaler, Citalopram, Clobetasol Propionate Cream, Fluticasone Propionate, Mometasone, LOPERamide, Loratadine, and DiphenhydrAMINE.

The patient was diagnosed with right knee osteoarthritis and was discharged to rehab in stable condition. Her mental status was clear and coherent, and she was alert and interactive. She was ambulatory but required assistance or aid.

The patient, a female, was admitted to the orthopedic service due to right knee pain and swelling. She had a history of a clot in her shoulder, for which she is not currently on anticoagulation, and morbid obesity. She had no major surgical or invasive procedures. Her gynecological history was unremarkable, with regular and heavy menses, no history of STDs, and a negative last pap smear. She was using long-term contraception. 

Upon physical examination, she appeared well and was not in acute distress. Her vital signs were stable and she was afebrile. Her pain was well-controlled. Her respiratory, cardiovascular, gastrointestinal, genitourinary, neurologic, and psychiatric exams were all normal. Examination of her lower extremity revealed a healing incision with staples, scant serosanguinous drainage, a full but soft thigh, no calf tenderness, and warm toes. 

Lab results showed abnormal values for RBC (3.77), Hgb (9.1), Hct (29.3), MCV (78), MCH (24.2), MCHC (31.2), and PTT (22.7). Her CRP was also elevated at 84.7. However, her ESR/CRP were within normal limits for a post-operative patient. 

During her hospital stay, she remained afebrile and her incision was clean and intact without erythema or abnormal drainage. She was seen daily by physical therapy and labs were checked and repleted accordingly. She was tolerating a regular diet and feeling well at the time of discharge. Her hematocrit was acceptable and her pain was adequately controlled on an oral regimen. Her operative extremity was neurovascularly intact and the wound was benign. 

Her medications upon admission included Albuterol Inhaler, Betamethasone Dipro, Citalopram, Clobetasol Propionate, Flovent HFA, Ketoconazole, Mometasone, Loratadine, and LOPERamide. 

She was discharged to rehab in stable condition. Her discharge diagnosis was right knee swelling. Her mental status was clear and coherent, and she was alert and interactive. She was ambulatory but required assistance or aid. 

Her discharge instructions included returning to the emergency department or notifying her physician if she experienced severe pain not relieved by medication, increased swelling, decreased sensation, difficulty with movement, fevers greater than 101.5, shaking chills, increasing redness or drainage from the incision site, chest pain, shortness of breath or any other concerns. She was also instructed to follow up with her primary physician regarding this admission and any new medications and refills, and to resume her home medications unless otherwise instructed.

The patient, a female, was admitted due to stiffness in her right knee, approximately 2.5 months after her right total knee replacement. She has been attending physical therapy sessions diligently for the past month but continues to experience stiffness due to pain. Her past medical history includes a history of clot in the shoulder, morbid obesity, asthma, and rashes. She has no current anticoagulation treatment. Her past surgical history includes a right total knee arthroplasty. 

During her hospital stay, she underwent a right knee manipulation under anesthesia. Post-operation, she was given a dilaudid PCA for pain and was transitioned to oral narcotics the following day. She was also given aspirin for DVT prophylaxis. Physical therapy worked with her to maintain the mobility gained from the procedure. By the second day post-operation, she was voiding, tolerating a regular diet, her pain was controlled, and her vital signs were normal. She expressed readiness for discharge.

Upon admission, her medications included Loperamide for loose stools, Diphenhydramine for itchiness and insomnia, Oxycodone-Acetaminophen for pain, Citalopram, Albuterol Sulfate for wheezing, and Fluticasone Propionate. 

The patient was discharged with a diagnosis of right knee arthrofibrosis. Her mental status was clear and coherent, and she was alert and interactive. She was able to ambulate independently. Her discharge instructions included weight-bearing as tolerated on the right knee, daily exercises as prescribed by physical therapy, taking narcotic medication as needed for pain, taking a stool softener with the medication, and taking aspirin once a day for 2 weeks to avoid the formation of blood clots in her lower extremities. She was discharged to her home.

The patient, a female, was admitted to the orthopedic surgery service due to tricompartmental osteoarthritis of the left knee and a prior vertical fracture of the patella, which was treated nonoperatively. She underwent a left total knee arthroplasty. Her past medical history includes asthma, depression, deep vein thrombosis (DVT), eczema, hyperlipidemia (HLD), hypertension (HTN), right total knee arthroplasty, and right shoulder arthroscopic SLAP repair. She has no significant family history. 

Upon physical examination, she was in no acute distress, afebrile with stable vital signs, and her pain was well-controlled. Her respiratory, cardiovascular, gastrointestinal, genitourinary, neurologic, and psychiatric conditions were all normal. However, her musculoskeletal lower extremity showed an incision with overlying silverlon, scant serosanguinous drainage, a full but soft thigh, no calf tenderness, and warm toes.

Her lab results showed several abnormal findings. Her white blood cell count was elevated at 11.1, and her red blood cell count was low at 3.56. Her hemoglobin was low at 8.6, her hematocrit was low at 28.2, and her mean corpuscular volume was low at 79. Her mean corpuscular hemoglobin was low at 24.2, and her mean corpuscular hemoglobin concentration was low at 30.5. Her glucose level was also elevated at 141.

During her hospital stay, she received perioperative IV antibiotics. Her postoperative course was remarkable for an elevated white count at the time of discharge, but she remained afebrile. Her pain was controlled with a combination of IV and oral pain medications. She received lovenox for DVT prophylaxis. The patient was voiding independently after the removal of the foley. The overlying surgical dressing was changed and found to be clean and intact without erythema or abnormal drainage. She was seen daily by physical therapy and labs were checked throughout the hospital course and repleted accordingly. At the time of discharge, she was tolerating a regular diet and feeling well. 

Upon admission, she was on several medications including Albuterol Inhaler, Betamethasone Dipro, Citalopram, Clindamycin, Clobetasol Propionate, Fluticasone Propionate, Ibuprofen, Ketoconazole, mometasone, OxycoDONE, Acetaminophen, DiphenhydrAMINE, LOPERamide, and Loratadine. 

The patient was discharged to home with services in stable condition. Her discharge diagnosis was left knee osteoarthritis. Her mental status was clear and coherent, and she was alert and interactive. She was ambulatory but required assistance or aid.

The patient, a female, was admitted due to stiffness and reduced range of motion in her left knee following a total knee arthroplasty. She underwent a knee manipulation under anesthesia to address this issue. Her past medical history includes obstructive sleep apnea, dyslipidemia, deep vein thrombosis in the right arm, hypertension, asthma, allergic rhinitis, arthritis, chronic right shoulder pain, headaches treated with mometasone, obesity with a BMI over 40, anemia, pre-diabetes with an A1C of 6.3%, extreme anxiety, depression, and eczema. She has also had an EKG showing sinus rhythm at 62 bpm with non-specific T wave changes. Her surgical history includes a left total knee arthroplasty, endometrial ablation, supracervical hysterectomy with right salpingo oophorectomy, right knee arthroscopy, lateral release, right total knee replacement, and right knee manipulation under anesthesia.

Upon admission, the patient was on several medications including Amlodipine, Clobetasol Propionate, Ketoconazole, mometasone, OxycoDONE, Acetaminophen, DiphenhydrAMINE, LOPERamide, Loratadine, Albuterol Inhaler, Betamethasone Dipro, Clindamycin, diclofenac sodium, and Fluticasone Propionate.

During her hospital stay, the patient underwent the aforementioned procedure without complications and tolerated it well. She refused to work with physical therapy during her stay but will return to outpatient physical therapy upon discharge. She also refused the continuous passive motion (CPM) machine at discharge due to cost. Her labs were monitored and adjusted as needed. At discharge, she was tolerating a regular diet, was afebrile with stable vital signs, and her hematocrit was acceptable. Her pain was adequately controlled with oral medication, and the operative extremity was neurovascularly intact with a benign wound.

Abnormal lab results included RBC count of 5.22, MCV of 77, MCH of 22.8, MCHC of 29.5, RDW of 17.1, RDWSD of 47.6, and glucose of 150. The patient was discharged with a diagnosis of left knee stiffness and reduced range of motion. Her mental status was clear and coherent, and she was alert and interactive but required assistance or aid for ambulation.

The patient, a middle-aged male, was admitted due to cellulitis in his right lower leg (RLE) which had been ongoing for a week. This condition developed after a biking accident where he got a large splinter embedded in his shin from a broken branch. Despite taking Keflex for a day, the redness and swelling in his leg continued to expand. He was previously admitted to another hospital where he received two doses of vancomycin and zosyn. He was discharged with ciprofloxacin, which he continued to take until his current admission due to lack of improvement. 

Upon admission, the patient was ambulatory and denied any fever, chills, night sweats, nausea, or vomiting. He had a past medical history of intracranial hemorrhage (ICH) following a previous biking accident. His family history includes his father having a cerebrovascular accident (CVA). 

Physical examination revealed a healed black eschar along the anterior shin of his RLE, surrounded by a large area of erythema, warmth, and 2+ pitting edema. There was increased swelling in the area adjacent to the initial injury. No calf swelling or tenderness was noted, and peripheral pulses were 2+ and symmetrical. 

Lab results showed abnormal urea nitrogen levels at 21, sodium levels at 146, red blood cell count at 4.56, and hemoglobin at 13.5. An ultrasound study revealed a linear foreign body in the area of clinical concern, either on the surface of or just within the anterior tibialis muscle. Edema consistent with cellulitis was noted, without evidence of focal fluid collection to suggest abscess. 

During his hospital stay, the patient underwent a surgical procedure to remove the foreign body (a 1 cm splinter of wood) from his RLE. He tolerated the procedure well and was ready for discharge the next day after the wound was examined by the surgical team and the primary dressing changed. 

Upon admission, the patient was on ciprofloxacin 250mg twice a day and ibuprofen as needed.

The patient, a female with a history of Crohn's disease, C. difficile, congenital hypothyroidism, depression, ADHD, anxiety, menstrual-related ocular migraines, preeclampsia, and rhinoplasty, was admitted due to abdominal pain and bloody diarrhea. She had been diagnosed with Ulcerative Colitis, which was later diagnosed as Crohn's disease. She had been relatively symptom-free for a period but was hospitalized for Crohn's in the past. She had not taken any medications for a while due to lack of insurance coverage. 

She began experiencing nausea, vomiting, sharp periumbilical pain, fecal urgency, and incontinence. She also had bloody malodorous loose stools, followed by a brown mucus discharge, bright red blood, and tenesmus. She developed a headache and felt lightheaded as her oral intake decreased. She reported weight loss, a messy divorce, and sick contacts. Her symptoms were consistent with prior Crohn's flare-ups, but this presentation was more severe than prior episodes. 

In the ER, her vitals were stable and labs were unremarkable. An abdominal exam revealed a soft but diffusely tender abdomen. A CT scan showed colonic wall thickening, extending from the splenic flexure to the distal sigmoid, and a second shorter segment of abnormal thickening at the hepatic flexure, consistent with an IBD flare. She was given IV fluids and cipro/flagyl for the presumptive IBD flare. However, the cipro was discontinued due to a reported sensation of 'throat closing' and mild shortness of breath. Unasyn was recommended in place of cipro. 

On the wards, she reported feeling slightly better but still experienced nausea and persistent lower abdominal cramping. A flexible sigmoidoscopy showed continuous ulceration, friability, and erythema with contact bleeding, more in the descending colon and minimally in the sigmoid colon, compatible with Crohn's colitis. Biopsies were performed for histology. 

Her TSH was measured at 42 with a free T4 of 0.97. Her endocrinologist suggested that her elevated TSH levels were due to the fact that she was taking her synthroid with other meds (Ca+2) and/or food. A CT demonstrated the presence of a nonstandard IUD position, which was removed by OB/GYN. 

Her medications upon admission included Synthroid, Celexa, Concerta, Mirena IUD, TUMS, and Fioricet. Her family history includes her maternal grandmother who had cancer and died of pancreatic/ovarian cancer, and her mother who has stage 4 breast cancer.

The patient is a female with a history of Crohn's disease, C. difficile, congenital hypothyroidism, depression, ADHD, anxiety, menstrual-related ocular migraines, preeclampsia, and rhinoplasty. She presented with abdominal pain, tenesmus, diarrhea, and nausea, symptoms similar to those that led to her recent hospital admission for a Crohn's flare. Despite being discharged with Augmentin, Flagyl, and 5ASA, her symptoms persisted and possibly worsened, leading her to return to the hospital for IV steroids and symptom management. 

During her previous admission, she experienced nausea, vomiting, sharp periumbicial pain, fecal urgency, incontinence, and a headache. A CT scan showed colonic wall thickening, consistent with an IBD flare. She was given IV fluids and cipro/flagyl, but the cipro was discontinued due to a reported sensation of 'throat closing' and mild shortness of breath. 

Upon her current admission, her vitals were stable and her CBC/CHEM7 and U/A were unremarkable. She was treated with IV fluids, morphine, zofran, and a dose of decadron. She reported feeling slightly better, but still experienced nausea, abdominal pain, and frequent diarrhea. 

Her family history includes a maternal grandmother who died of pancreatic/ovarian cancer and a mother with stage 4 breast cancer. 

On physical exam, she was awake and alert, with no signs of airway compromise or wheezing. Her abdomen was soft and tender to light palpation in the RLQ and LLQ, with no guarding or rebound tenderness. Her bowel sounds were hypoactive. 

Lab results showed a slightly elevated MCHC at 35.8. Her potassium was low due to persistent diarrhea and was repleted PRN. 

Her medications upon admission included Citalopram, Mesalamine, Pantoprazole, Levothyroxine, Metronidazole, Augmentin, Percocet, Sodium Chloride, and Pseudoephedrine HCl. 

Her symptoms were thought to be due to a persistent Crohn's flare, not amenable to treatment without steroids and antibiotics. She was admitted for IV steroids and IV unasyn/flagyl, which improved her symptoms. She was seen by the gastroenterology team, who ordered screening tests including a PPD and CXR, both of which were negative. 

Her hypothyroidism was managed with her home dose of synthroid. She was seen by social workers for her anxiety, depression, and ADHD, and continued on her citalopram and ativan. She was initially dehydrated and received IV fluids, but was eventually able to meet her fluid needs through oral intake. She had a nutrition consult to explain a low residue diet. 

Upon discharge, her diagnosis was Crohn's colitis and a vaginal yeast infection. She was afebrile, hemodynamically stable, and having less than 3 bowel movements per day. She was instructed to stop Asacol and start Prednisone, Augmentin, and Metronidazole. She was advised to call her doctor or return to the emergency room if she experienced any concerning symptoms.

The patient is a female with a history of Crohn's disease, C. difficile, congenital hypothyroidism, depression, ADHD, anxiety, ocular migraines, preeclampsia, and rhinoplasty. She has been recently admitted twice for abdominal pain and flares related to her Crohn's disease. She was admitted again after a presyncopal episode in the clinic. She reported ongoing nausea, dizziness, and a feeling of uncomfortable fullness in her epigastrium after eating. She also reported having watery bowel movements, but no mucus, blood, or fevers. She denied chest pain, shortness of breath, palpitations, and lightheadedness. 

During her visit to her primary care physician and gastroenterologist, she experienced nausea and lightheadedness while having labs drawn, almost passing out. She also reported difficulty in finding her veins while being phlebotomized. She has been going through a tough time at home due to a divorce and seeing her two children for the first time in two weeks. 

Her family history includes her maternal grandmother who had cancer and died of pancreatic/ovarian cancer, and her mother who has stage 4 breast cancer. 

Upon physical examination, she was found to be pleasant, awake, alert, and not in acute distress. Her mucous membranes were slightly dry. Her abdomen was soft, non-distended, and diffusely tender to light palpation throughout. 

Her admission labs showed an elevated white blood cell count (16.7), neutrophils (70.1), urea nitrogen (21), and TSH (9.3). Her free T4 was also elevated (2.0). Her urine culture showed yeast with 10,000-100,000 organisms/mL. Her discharge labs showed a persistent leukocytosis (13.8). 

During her hospital stay, she had a presyncopal event at her primary care physician's office during a blood draw. She did not lose consciousness and described nausea and lightheadedness prior to the event. An ECG did not show any abnormalities. It was thought that she had a vasovagal reaction during phlebotomy. 

She was continued on her home dose of Prednisone, Flagyl, Amoxicillin, and Mesalamine/Asacol. It was determined that she would begin Remicade as an outpatient the week after discharge. 

She had a persistent leukocytosis during her admission, which was thought to be likely secondary to steroid use. Her elevated TSH on admission improved from her prior values, and her T4 was slightly elevated. She was continued on her home dose of Levothyroxine 175 mg daily. 

She was dealing with many life stressors and was continued on her home dose of Citalopram. She often appeared very tearful and had a depressed affect. 

Her medications upon admission included Synthroid, Citalopram, Pantoprazole, Prednisone, Flagyl, Augmentin, Asacol, and Mesalamine enema.

The patient is a female with a history of Crohn's disease, depression, anxiety, hypothyroidism, GERD, C. difficile, ADHD, ocular migraines, preeclampsia, and rhinoplasty. She is currently on prednisone and remicaide for her Crohn's disease. She presented with acute onset flu-like symptoms including fevers, chills, myalgias, fatigue, sore throat, cough, sinus congestion, and dysphagia/odynophagia. She reported no acute flare of her Crohn's disease and her abdominal pain was at baseline. 

Upon examination in the ED, she was initially tachycardic with a saturation of 103. She was given 1L IVF due to suspected hypovolemia. Her breath sounds were coarse with wheezing, but her epiglottis appeared normal. Her chest x-ray was clear, rapid flu test was negative, and urine and blood cultures were sent. 

Her lab results showed an elevated neutrophil count of 82.0* and a decreased lymphocyte count of 13.8*. Her PO2 was elevated at 113* and her pH was also elevated at 7.46*. 

Her family history includes a maternal grandmother who died of pancreatic/ovarian cancer and a mother with stage 4 breast cancer. 

Upon admission, her medications included citalopram, synthroid, ergocalciferol, levothyroxine, pantoprazole, and prednisone. 

During her hospital stay, she was treated for flu-like symptoms with tamiflu, despite a negative rapid test, due to her immunocompromised state. She also showed signs of reactive airway disease and was given albuterol inhalers. Her Crohn's disease was managed with infliximab and prednisone, and she was given osteoporosis prophylaxis with daily calcium and vitamin D. Her hypothyroidism was managed with synthroid, and her GERD was managed with protonix. Her depression was managed with citalopram. 

She was ruled out for influenza B and mononucleosis, as her direct influenza B antigen test and monospot test were both negative. Her chest x-ray was also negative. 

Upon discharge, her condition was stable and comfortable on room air. She was instructed to stay at home and wear a mask until her PCP or the state lab contacts her with results from her flu tests.

The patient, a female with a history of indeterminate colitis with some functional bowel disorder, depression, and hypothyroidism, was admitted due to a week of periumbilical crampy abdominal pain, rectal bleeding, and fever. She also reported anorexia and intermittent nausea. She was referred to the ED by her GI. She denied any recent travel, sick contact, new diet, or recent antibiotics. Upon arrival to the ED, her vitals were stable and a rectal exam revealed brown, guaiac-positive stools. Labs were unremarkable. She was given ondansetron for nausea and hydromorphone for abdominal pain. 

Her past medical history includes indeterminate colitis with functional bowel disorder, hypothyroidism, GERD, C. difficile, depression/anxiety, ADHD, ocular migraines, history of preeclampsia, and a rhinoplasty procedure. Her family history includes a maternal grandmother who had colon cancer and died of pancreatic and ovarian cancer, and a mother who had breast cancer.

Upon physical examination, she was found to be in no acute distress. Her abdomen was soft, diffusely tender but greatest at epigastrum and umbilical area, not distended, no peritoneal sign, bowel sounds present. No other significant findings were noted.

Admission labs showed abnormal MCV and MCH values, and an elevated potassium level. Discharge labs showed decreased Hgb, Hct, MCV, and MCH values. A CT scan of her abdomen showed minimal bowel wall thickening and collapse of segments of the descending colon and sigmoid colon with minimal adjacent fat stranding. A colonoscopy revealed chronic and mild active colitis. Stool for CDIFF was negative.

During her hospital stay, her diarrhea resolved after admission with observation. She continued her outpatient levothyroxine for hypothyroidism and Citalopram for depression/anxiety. Her medications upon admission included calcium + vit D, certolizumab, citalopram 40 mg qday, hyoscyamine 0.125-0.250 mg tid, levothyroxine 300 mcg qday, and Bifidobacterium infantis 1 capsule qday. 

Upon discharge, her primary diagnosis was indeterminate colitis, with secondary diagnoses of hypothyroidism and depression. She was discharged home in a clear and coherent mental state, alert and interactive, and ambulatory - independent.

The patient, a female with a history of indeterminate colitis with functional bowel disorder, hypothyroidism, GERD, C. difficile, depression/anxiety, ADHD, ocular migraines, preeclampsia, and a history of rhinoplasty, was admitted due to abdominal pain. She has been managing her colitis with Cimzia injections twice monthly, but the most recent injection did not provide the usual relief. She presented with upper abdominal pain, low-grade fever, and watery diarrhea, with occasional small amounts of blood in her stool, though none in the past four days. 

Her family history includes a maternal grandmother who had colon cancer and died of pancreatic and ovarian cancer, and a mother who had breast cancer. On physical examination, she was found to have tenderness in the epigastrium and both upper quadrants, but no tenderness in the lower quadrants. 

Lab results showed a urea nitrogen level of 21*, MCV of 79*, and MCH of 25.9*. All other lab results were within normal ranges. 

During her hospital stay, her abdominal pain, consistent with her known colitis, improved with bowel rest. A colonoscopy revealed grade one active inflammation at multiple foci. Stool studies were negative. She was recommended a 14-day course of prednisone, with follow-up within that period to monitor and discuss dosing and continuation or cessation of prednisone. 

Upon admission, her medications included calcium and vitamin D, Celexa 40 mg daily, T4 200 mcg daily, and Zofran as needed. At discharge, her condition was stable, with clear mental status, alert and interactive consciousness, and independent ambulation. Her abdominal pain was minimal, and she was tolerating oral intake with no nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, or fevers.

The patient is a female with a history of indeterminate colitis with functional bowel disorder, hypothyroidism, GERD, C. difficile, depression/anxiety, ADHD, ocular migraines related to menses, preeclampsia, and a history of rhinoplasty. She was admitted due to nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, and abdominal pain. She had recently been admitted for an IBD flare with similar symptoms. During her last admission, a colonoscopy was performed, revealing chronic active colitis, and she was discharged on prednisone 40 mg daily. 

Upon admission, she was given dilaudid for pain and zofran for nausea. Her vitals were stable. Her past medical treatments include sulfasalazine, mesalamine, prednisone, infliximab, and monthly certolizumab. Her family history includes a maternal grandmother with colon cancer who died of pancreatic and ovarian cancer, and a mother with breast cancer.

The patient underwent an EGD, which showed normal mucosa in the stomach and duodenum, a small diverticulum in the second portion of the duodenum, and a small hiatal hernia. An MR Enterography showed no evidence of inflammatory change within the bowel, subcentimeter right renal cysts, and a 2 cm left uterine mural fibroid.

Her lab results showed abnormal MCV and MCH values, as well as an elevated UreaN and TSH. Her WBC count remained in the high end of normal, and the differential had a mild lymphocytosis. Infectious etiologies were ruled out via stool samples. 

The patient's symptoms were initially thought to be due to uncontrolled IBD symptoms, but given the lack of evidence of active inflammatory disease, it was determined her symptoms were most likely caused by her IBS, a viral gastroenteritis, or a combination of the two. 

The patient also has a history of congenital hypothyroidism, and her TSH was found to be 12 with a free T4 of 1.6. She was continued on her current levothyroxine regimen. She also has a history of depression and anxiety and was treated in the hospital with her normal regimen of Celexa 40 mg PO daily.

Upon admission, her medications included Citalopram, Levothyroxine, Ondansetron, Prednisone, Cholecalciferol (Vitamin D3), and Calcium Carbonate.

The patient, a female, was admitted due to diffuse abdominal pain and diarrhea. She has a history of indeterminate colitis, which is managed with twice-monthly cimzia. She has had multiple prior admissions for flares. Her most recent admissions for similar symptoms were not specified. A colonoscopy revealed inflammation in the sigmoid, descending colon, and hepatic flexure, and pathology consistent with active colitis. She was placed on oral prednisone 40mg for 2 weeks and noted only minimal improvement in symptoms. Upon tapering to 35mg daily, she reported that her colitis 'erupted.' She briefly increased the dose back to 40mg daily, but upon consulting gastroenterology, she went back to 35mg. 

In the ED, she complained of diffuse cramping abdominal pain, tenesmus with unspecified bowel movements per day. She reported some bright red blood per rectum earlier in the week, but stools have mostly been brown with mucus. Stools were reported as guiac negative in ED. She also complained of dry heaves. She denied emesis, fevers, or chills. No lightheadedness or orthostatic symptoms were reported. She had been seen as an outpatient for this and had stool studies performed 3 days prior to admission which were negative for infectious etiology. 

Her past medical history includes indeterminate colitis with functional bowel disorder, hypothyroidism, GERD, C. difficile, depression/anxiety, ADHD, ocular migraines, history of preeclampsia, and post-rhinoplasty. Her family history includes a maternal grandmother who had colon cancer and died of pancreatic and ovarian cancer, and a mother who had breast cancer.

Upon admission, her medications included calcium/vitamin D, certolizumab pegol (Cimzia), citalopram, hyoscyamine sulfate, levothyroxine, omeprazole, ondansetron HCL (Zofran), and prednisone. 

Abnormal lab results upon admission included MCV (77), MCH (25.4), and RDW (15.6). Discharge labs showed abnormal MCV (77), MCH (25.7), and glucose (191). 

The patient's discharge diagnosis was indeterminate colitis. Her mental status was clear and coherent, and she was alert and interactive. She was ambulatory and independent. Her discharge instructions included increasing her hyoscyamine dose to 0.25 3xs/day, continuing 35mg prednisone daily until told otherwise by her GI doctor, starting an antibiotic as prophylaxis because she has been on high doses of prednisone for a long time, and using Tylenol as needed for pain. She was also given a prescription for 10 tablets of oxycodone to use when the pain is still severe after Tylenol.

The patient, a female, was admitted due to abdominal pain, nausea, vomiting, and diarrhea. She was referred to the ED by her gastroenterologist. 

The patient's symptoms began with crampy abdominal pain associated with loose stools and increased stool frequency with bright red blood. She reported urgency, almost had fecal incontinence, and tenesmus. Her pain was partially relieved by the passage of stool, but she felt a sensation of incomplete evacuation. She also reported nausea and two episodes of non-bloody emesis. She had no significant changes in diet, no sick contacts or recent travel, no recent antibiotics, and no other medication changes. She had been prescribed vicodin and flexeril for back pain but had not needed these recently. She had lost a few pounds. 

The patient's physical exam revealed tenderness throughout the abdomen, normal bowel sounds, and external hemorrhoids with no stool or blood. Her vitals were stable. Her lab results showed abnormal Hgb, MCV, MCH, Neuts, and Lymphs. Her stool cultures were negative for Salmonella, Shigella, Campylobacter, Ova and Parasites, E.Coli 0157:H7, and C.difficile toxin A & B. A CT scan showed mildly thickened walls in the distal loops of the descending and sigmoid colon, likely representing chronic changes related to her inflammatory bowel disease. No acute Crohn's flare or bowel obstruction was noted. A sigmoidoscopy revealed abnormal vascularity in the sigmoid colon and erythema in the rectum, but no evidence of active colitis or etiology of diarrhea and bleeding could be seen. 

The patient's family history revealed no inflammatory bowel disease or irritable bowel syndrome. Her maternal grandmother had colon cancer and died of pancreatic and ovarian cancer. Her mother had breast cancer. 

The patient's medications upon admission included citalopram, Cimzia, lorazepam, Omeprazole, Synthroid, Hyoscyamine SR, Flovent, diphenhydramine, and Epinephrine. 

During her hospital stay, the patient was worked up extensively. Her white count, exam, and guiac were normal. Her CT abdomen was negative for any acute findings, as were her CRP and ESR. Her stool cultures, including c diff toxin, were also negative. GI was consulted for a sigmoidoscopy, which revealed normal mucosa, although biopsy results are pending. GI and the primary team's assessment was that IBD flare, infection were ruled out and that her symptoms were most consistent with an IBS flare. The patient took some comfort in this reassurance and was started on dicyclomine; unfortunately, this caused throat tightness and was discontinued. Social work was consulted to provide some support as it seemed marital pressures with her ex-husband triggered some of her IBS symptoms. She was discharged on her home hyocyamine which she was not taking due to misunderstanding its use (this was reexplained to her), and given a script for lomotil to address acute IBS flare in the future. Follow up was arranged with her primary gastroenterologist the following week after discharge.
