Physical examination on admission showed a soft, non-distended abdomen tender to palpation in all quadrants without rebound or guarding. Discharge examination showed a soft, non-distended abdomen with diffuse tenderness to palpation throughout, reportedly worse over the epigastrium, with no rebound or guarding. 

Admission labs showed abnormal RBC count of 5.39* and MCV of 81*. Discharge labs showed abnormal RBC count of 5.73* and Hct of 46.7*, and Mg of 2.7*. 

Imaging included a previous EGD showing an esophageal hiatal hernia, normal mucosa in the whole stomach, a diverticulum in the second part of the duodenum, and erosion in the duodenal bulb. A CT scan showed prominent wall thickening and narrowing of the gastroduodenal junction without gastric outlet obstruction, a small hiatal hernia, and a dominant 5.6 cm left anterior uterine fibroid. A colonoscopy showed localized discontinuous erythema and granularity in the sigmoid colon that was biopsied, two sessile non-bleeding polyps of benign appearance in the sigmoid colon post-polypectomy, and normal mucosa in the whole colon. 

The patient's hospital course included a presentation of significant abdominal pain, nausea, vomiting, and diarrhea. Her symptoms were likely due to acute gastroenteritis or an IBS flare in the setting of acute stressors. She underwent a colonoscopy that showed erythema and granularity in the sigmoid colon that was biopsied, and two sessile non-bleeding polyps of benign appearance post-polypectomy. She was started on rifaxamin and dicyclomine, which she was discharged on. 

The patient's medications upon admission included ondansetron, diazepam, omeprazole, venlafaxine XR, lidocaine patch, vedolizumab, levothyroxine sodium, albuterol inhaler, acetaminophen, and Viactiv. 

The patient was discharged with a diagnosis of IBS and bacterial overgrowth. Her mental status was clear and coherent, and she was alert and interactive. She was ambulatory and independent.

The patient, a male with a history of intravenous heroin and occasional cocaine use, was admitted due to acute hepatitis. He also reported testicular pain, raising concerns about a potential testicular abscess. However, no major surgical or invasive procedures were performed during his stay. The patient's family history includes a sister diagnosed with Hepatitis C.

Upon admission, the patient was restless and yawning, with dilated and reactive pupils, and scleral icterus. His abdomen was soft, non-tender, and non-distended, with no organomegaly. His extremities were warm and well-perfused, with no cyanosis, clubbing, or edema. Neurologically, he was alert and oriented, with no asterixis. His left testicle was tender to touch but without palpable lesions.

The patient's admission labs showed several abnormal results, including elevated WBC (18.2), RBC (4.14), Hgb (11.5), Hct (36.4), Neuts (76.1), Lymphs (16.4), AbsNeut (13.85), AbsMono (1.09), Glucose (129), ALT (1048), AST (640), AlkPhos (345), TotBili (7.3), and Albumin (3.3). His hepatitis workup revealed a positive HCV Ab and HCV VL (7.7). His urine test was positive for cocaine. 

Imaging studies showed severe diffuse gallbladder wall thickening and mild prominence of the portal triads, likely secondary to acute hepatitis. A 2mm gallbladder polyp was also noted. Scrotal ultrasound revealed a simple cyst in the left epididymal head measuring up to 0.9 cm, with no evidence of abscess or other acute abnormalities in the testes.

Throughout his hospital stay, the patient remained clinically stable with no encephalopathy or liver failure. His discharge labs showed persistently elevated WBC (12.3), RBC (4.54), Hgb (13.1), Hct (38.9), Glucose (146), ALT (1348), AST (709), AlkPhos (330), and TotBili (8.6). 

The patient was diagnosed with acute hepatitis C, opioid use disorder with intravenous drug use, opioid withdrawal, cocaine use disorder, and a testicular cyst. He was highly motivated to quit drug use and was provided with outpatient addiction service referrals. He was also counseled on safe use and provided with Narcan and symptomatic treatments for withdrawal. 

Upon admission, the patient was taking ProAir HFA (albuterol sulfate) 90 mcg/actuation inhalation as needed. His mental status was clear and coherent, and he was alert, interactive, and ambulatory at the time of discharge.

The patient is a male with a history of intravenous heroin and occasional cocaine use. He was recently admitted for acute hepatitis, which was found to be due to Hepatitis C (HCV). His recent hospital course was notable for positive HCV antibody and viral load (VL), with other viral and toxin screens being negative. His labs at discharge showed elevated ALT, AST, AP, and TBili. He also had an elevated HCV VL and IgM. Since discharge, he reported two episodes of clear non-bloody, non-bilious vomiting and was found to have increasing jaundice with worsening liver function tests (LFTs) and bilirubin. He reported ongoing intravenous drug use and denied any symptoms such as fevers, chills, confusion, chest pain, shortness of breath, abdominal pain, weakness, diarrhea, constipation, lower extremity swelling, or easy bruising. He also denied taking Tylenol or ibuprofen containing medications since discharge, and denied taking any dietary supplements or herbal medications in the time leading up to his initial admission or in the time since discharge.

On examination, he was found to be jaundiced with scleral icterus, alert and oriented, with no asterixis, and a benign abdominal exam. His labs were significant for elevated WBC, Hgb, Hct, ALT, AST, AP, LDH, and TBili. His serum and urine toxicology screens were negative. Imaging showed a patent main portal vein, no ascites, persistent gallbladder wall thickening improved from prior, and mild splenomegaly. 

His past medical history includes intravenous heroin use for over six months and occasional cocaine use. His family history is notable for a sister with HCV due to a blood transfusion as a child. 

His admission labs showed abnormal WBC, RBC, Hgb, Hct, RDW, AbsNeut, AbsLymp, AbsMono, AbsEos, Na, ALT, AST, LDH, AlkPhos, TotBili, DirBili, IndBili, and PTT. His LFTs and chem trend showed persistently elevated ALT, AST, LDH, AlkPhos, TotBili, and low Na and glucose. His CBC/Coag trend showed persistently low RBC, Hgb, Hct, and elevated RDW. His pertinent labs showed high HCV VL, positive smooth muscle antibody, elevated IgM, and positive HSV1 IgG. 

During his hospital course, he was monitored for signs of withdrawal and provided symptomatic relief. He was also found to have hyponatremia on admission, which improved with fluid boluses. He was prescribed a nicotine patch for tobacco use. He was given the first vaccine of Hep B series while inpatient and needs Hep A and B vaccinations. He was discharged with a plan to recheck labs and follow up with his primary care physician and hepatologist. His discharge medications included Clonidine and Hydroxyzine. His discharge diagnoses were acute hepatitis and Hepatitis C.

The patient, a male with no significant past medical history, was admitted due to a jaw fracture. The injury occurred during an altercation at a party, where he was punched in the face, lost consciousness for 30 seconds, and fell to the ground. Upon regaining consciousness, he experienced left jaw pain and blood oozing from his mouth. He presented to the emergency department with left jaw swelling and looseness of left lower medial and lateral teeth. He denied any other injuries, fevers/chills, headaches/dizziness, nausea/vomiting, chest pain/shortness of breath, abdominal pain, or changes in bowel or urinary habits. 

The patient's past surgical history includes a tonsillectomy in childhood and wisdom teeth removal. He has an allergy to penicillins. He was not on any medications upon admission. 

Physical examination revealed left lower and middle third facial edema, bilateral V3 parasthesia, and left-sided soft tissue swelling. The patient's head CT was negative, but a CT of the mandible showed two fractures. The left mandibular ramus fracture was minimally comminuted with one bone width posterior and medial displacement. The second fracture was nondisplaced, extending through the symphysis to the right mandibular body and midline. No dental fractures were evident. 

The patient underwent a transoral open reduction and internal fixation of the left angle and symphysis mandible fractures. Post-operative X-rays showed a plate and screws transfixing the left mandibular rami fracture and two plates and interlocking screws transfixing the right symphyseal fracture. No evidence of early hardware failure was noted. 

Lab results showed elevated white blood cell count (12.6), neutrophils (86.3), lymphocytes (8.1), monocytes (4.9), eosinophils (0.0), absolute neutrophils (10.91), and absolute eosinophils (0.00). 

The patient's hospital course was uneventful. He tolerated a full liquid diet, his pain was well controlled, and he was ambulating and voiding without assistance. He was discharged home in a clear and coherent mental state, alert and interactive, and able to ambulate independently. His discharge diagnosis was left angle and symphysis mandible fracture.

The patient, a female, was admitted due to an infection in her left foot. She has a significant past medical history including pancreatic adenocarcinoma, for which she underwent a subtotal pancreatectomy, and bilateral deep vein thrombosis (DVTs) for which she is on Lovenox. She also has a history of chronic back pain, benign hypertension, and glucose intolerance. Her surgical history includes an appendectomy, distal pancreatectomy, and splenectomy. She has allergies to Demerol and Floxin. 

The patient reported bumping her left foot and subsequently noticed progressive redness and swelling. She was given Keflex, which initially improved her symptoms. However, she started to have fevers despite two days of antibiotics. She was recommended to go to the ED for admission due to worsening swelling, pain, and erythema. She also has a chronic cough, which she attributes to allergens in her home. 

Upon admission, her vitals were stable and physical examination showed blanchable erythema and swelling of the left foot dorsum through the calf. Lab results showed a WBC count of 3.0, HCT of 33.9, and lactate of 1.4. She was given vancomycin 1g IV. 

Her family history is significant for a grandmother with stomach cancer. Her mother died from a myocardial infarction and her father died from diabetes mellitus. 

During her stay, her cellulitis improved with IV vancomycin and was later switched to oral amoxicillin and doxycycline. Her bilateral DVTs were managed with home Lovenox. Her hypertension was stable and managed with home metoprolol tartrate. She was also started on an insulin sliding scale for prandial coverage for her secondary diabetes. 

Upon discharge, her vitals were stable and her left foot showed minimal erythema with no warmth. Her discharge medications included enoxaparin, senna, simvastatin, omeprazole, lactulose, metoprolol tartrate, doxycycline hyclate, and amoxicillin. She was instructed to continue her oral antibiotics at home.

The patient, a female with a history of pancreatic cancer, hypertension, hyperlipidemia, chronic back pain, and glucose intolerance, was admitted due to fevers and left lower extremity cellulitis. She had previously undergone a distal pancreatectomy and splenectomy for her pancreatic cancer and was on a clinical trial with Gemcitabine. She also had a recent deep vein thrombosis (DVT) for which she was on Enoxaparin. 

The patient reported that her cellulitis started after she bumped her left foot, which then became progressively red and swollen. She was initially treated with Keflex, Vanco, and Zosyn, and then switched to Amoxicillin and Doxycycline. The cellulitis improved and was completely resolved by the time of admission. However, she developed fevers up to 101.5F and reported feeling unwell. She denied any other symptoms except for chronic rhinorhea, cough, and early satiety. 

A CT scan showed a fluid collection in the region of the head of the pancreas, which had increased in size and was causing early satiety due to extrinsic compression of the stomach. The patient underwent an exploratory laparotomy, abdominal wash-out, and drain placement, as well as CT-guided abscess drainage. 

Lab results showed an elevated WBC count of 14.2, Hgb of 10.6, Hct of 34.6, and Plt of 1146. Other abnormal findings included RBC of 3.47*, Hgb of 9.6*, Hct of 31.5*, MCHC of 30.6*, RDW of 15.7*, Glucose of 120*, and UreaN of 4*. Fluid culture showed moderate growth of Klebsiella pneumoniae. 

The patient's family history is significant for a grandmother with stomach cancer. Her mother died from a myocardial infarction and her father died from diabetes mellitus. 

Upon admission, the patient was on Enoxaparin, Senna, Simvastatin, Tums, Metoprolol tartrate, Doxycycline hyclate, Amoxicillin, and Colace. 

During her hospital stay, the patient's fever and fluid collection were managed with antibiotics and surgical debridement. Her DVT was managed with Lovenox and Heparin. Her hypertension and hyperlipidemia were managed with Metoprolol and Simvastatin, respectively. Her pancreatic cancer treatment was postponed due to her cellulitis and will be continued after recovery from surgery. 

The patient was discharged in a stable condition, with clear mental status, alert and interactive consciousness, and independent ambulation. Her discharge diagnoses included left foot cellulitis, fevers, metastatic pancreatic carcinoma, and recent DVT.

The patient is a female with a significant past medical history of pancreatic cancer, for which she underwent a Whipple procedure and is currently on gemcitabine. She also has a history of deep vein thrombosis (DVT), hypertension (HTN), and hyperlipidemia (HLD). She was admitted due to abdominal pain and pancreatitis. Prior to this admission, she had presented to an outside hospital (OSH) with redness and swelling of the right leg, which was diagnosed as cellulitis and treated with augmentin. 

During that time, she also experienced right upper quadrant (RUQ) and epigastric abdominal pain, which she did not report during her initial emergency department (ED) visit. She was referred back to the ED by her oncologist due to these symptoms. A CT scan at the OSH showed hypodense lesions in the liver and new fluid and stranding around the pancreas, suggestive of acute pancreatitis. Her liver function tests (LFTs) were within normal limits (WNL) with a lipase of 8. 

Upon arrival to the current facility, her vital signs were stable and her labs were consistent with OSH values. She reported pain in the RUQ and epigastrium that was non-radiating and worse with palpation and movement. She also reported a headache but denied any other complaints. 

Her past medical history also includes chronic back pain due to arthritis from a horseback riding injury, glucose intolerance managed by diet, hypercholesterolemia, a history of gallstones, and an appendectomy. She also had a DVT diagnosed previously. 

Her family history is significant for a grandmother with stomach cancer, a mother who died from a myocardial infarction, and a father who died from diabetes mellitus. There are multiple family members with cancer on her mother's side of the family, including kidney and breast cancer.

Abnormal lab results included a white blood cell count of 3.5*, red blood cell count of 3.04*, hemoglobin of 10.2*, hematocrit of 30.5*, mean corpuscular volume of 100*, mean corpuscular hemoglobin of 33.7*, red cell distribution width of 18.9*, metamyelocytes of 2*, and glucose of 103*. Her alanine aminotransferase was also elevated at 43*.

During her hospital stay, her abdominal pain was likely related to mild pancreatitis as seen on OSH imaging. She was evaluated by the transplant surgery team who reviewed her imaging and felt that there was no acute surgical issue. Her pain improved and she was discharged home. She was encouraged to continue a clear liquid diet for a few days until her pain had resolved. She will follow up with her primary oncologist and primary care physician next week.

Her medications upon admission included metoprolol tartrate, ranitidine, simvastatin, and ondansetron.

The patient, a female with a history of pancreatic adenocarcinoma, was admitted due to a fever of 102 degrees. She had previously undergone a Whipple procedure and was on her second cycle of paclitaxel and gemcitabine. She reported feeling fatigued, experiencing chills, nausea, mild headache, sore throat, and mild rhinorrhea. She denied having a cough, shortness of breath, sputum, neck stiffness, dysuria, diarrhea, vomiting, photophobia, phonophobia, myalgia, or arthralgia. She also noted chronic lower extremity swelling with some erythema. Upon examination, she was found to have bilateral lower extremity erythema and swelling. A rash was also noted on the back of her head. 

Her past medical history includes chronic back pain due to an arthritis from a horseback riding injury, hypertension, diet-controlled glucose intolerance, hypercholesterolemia, a history of gallstones, and a deep vein thrombosis (DVT). She has also had an appendectomy. Her family history is significant for a grandmother with stomach cancer, a mother who died from a myocardial infarction, and a father who died from diabetes mellitus. There are multiple instances of cancer on her mother's side of the family, including kidney and breast cancer.

Abnormal lab results include a decreased red blood cell count, hemoglobin, hematocrit, and mean corpuscular volume. Her white blood cell count was elevated at one point. Her neutrophils were consistently high, while her lymphocytes and monocytes were consistently low. Her glucose levels were also elevated. Her aspartate aminotransferase (AST) was elevated at one point. 

A chest x-ray showed a likely small left pleural effusion but no pneumonia. During her hospital stay, a definitive source for her fevers was not found. The possibility of a viral infection was considered but not confirmed. She was started on vancomycin and her fevers improved. She was discharged with a diagnosis of cellulitis. 

Upon admission, her medications included Metoprolol Tartrate, Simvastatin, Fluconazole, Zenpep, Ondansetron, and Pyridoxine.

The patient is a female with a history of pancreatic cancer, which was confirmed through imaging and cytology. She underwent a distal subtotal pancreatectomy, splenectomy, and fiducial placement. The pathology revealed a moderately to focally poorly differentiated T3N1 (Stage IIB) adenocarcinoma with complex dissection off the portal vein and SMA. Two of 42 lymph nodes were involved. The tumor extended beyond the pancreas, but without involvement of the celiac axis of the SMA. She also has pancreatic intraepithelial neoplasia and chronic pancreatitis. Post-operatively, she was treated for a postoperative fluid collection at the distal pancreatectomy site with cipro/flagyl and was diagnosed with DVT. She was also treated for fluid collection infection with IV antibiotics and surgical debridement. She is currently on gemcitabine.

Her other past medical history includes chronic back pain due to arthritis from a horseback riding injury, hypertension, diet-controlled glucose intolerance, hypercholesterolemia, a history of gallstones, and an appendectomy. She has a family history of stomach cancer in her grandmother, and her mother died from an MI while her father died from DM. There are multiple family members with cancer on her mother's side of the family, including kidney and breast cancer.

The patient was admitted due to fevers. Her physical exam upon admission showed slightly dry MM, 2+ pitting edema pre-tibially b/l, and lower legs very TTP with overlying scale and erythema to mid shin b/l. Her discharge exam showed 1+ pitting edema pre-tibially b/l, lower legs very TTP with overlying scale and erythema to mid shin b/l, and webs between toes are slightly macerated.

Her admission labs showed abnormal WBC, RBC, Hgb, Hct, MCV, MCH, Neuts, Lymphs, PTT, Glucose, Na, AlkPhos, and Albumin. Her discharge labs showed abnormal RBC, Hgb, Hct, MCV, MCH, Neuts, Lymphs, Glucose, and Calcium. Her microbiology results showed mixed flora in the urine, consistent with contamination. All cultures were pending or negative. Imaging showed small bilateral pleural effusions, a diffusely heterogeneous and echogenic liver due to extensive fatty deposition, several small cysts in the left lobe of the liver, a round hypoechoic lesion in the periphery of the right lobe of the liver which has increased in size, and trace ascites, predominantly surrounding the liver.

The patient's fevers were likely due to tumor fever given recent elevation in tumor markers and increased size of hepatic lesion. She also had leukocytosis likely due to a Neulasta injection. She was discharged on indomethacin 25mg TID and omeprazole 20mg daily to treat her tumor fever. She also had a rash likely due to pseudocellulitis from gemcitabine chemotherapy and was continued on econazole topical treatment for superficial fungal infection of the skin. Her other chronic issues include a scalp rash and CAD, for which she remained clinically stable on her home metoprolol and statin. Her medications upon admission included Metoprolol Tartrate, Ondansetron, Simvastatin, Zenpep, Triamcinolone Acetonide, and econazole.

The patient, a female, was admitted due to bright red blood in her bowel movement and melena. She has a history of metastatic pancreatic cancer and is currently enrolled in a study for adults with advanced solid tumors. She reported feeling weak and having chronic diarrhea since starting the study medication. She also reported episodes of dark brown stool and bright red blood in the toilet bowl. In the emergency department, she was hemodynamically stable but had another episode of bright red blood per rectum (BRBPR) that was almost entirely blood. Her blood pressure began to decrease and she was given 2L IVF for resuscitation and a total of 4 units of packed red blood cells (pRBCs). A femoral line was placed for additional access. She underwent a CT angiogram which showed high density fluid in the jejunum and proximal ileum, suggesting hemorrhage in the mid small bowel. She was given Protonix 40mg IV x1. 

The patient's past medical history includes hypertension, glucose intolerance, hypercholesterolemia, appendectomy, Whipple's pancreaticoduodenectomy, and postoperative deep vein thrombosis (DVT). She has been treated for pancreatic adenocarcinoma and has undergone various therapies and surgeries. Her family history includes a sister with osteoarthritis, a mother who died of congestive heart failure, a father who died of diabetes mellitus and kidney disease, an uncle treated for renal cell carcinoma, an aunt treated for brain cancer, and two cousins treated for breast cancer.

Upon admission, the patient's labs showed abnormal values for RBC (2.52), HGB (8.9), HCT (27.1), MCV (108), MCH (35.2), NEUTS (73.1), LYMPHS (14.5), and GLUCOSE (116). Urine tests showed abnormal RBC (5) and WBC (6). Imaging showed significant progression of metastatic disease with innumerable pulmonary metastases, new bilateral pleural effusions, moderate amount of intra-abdominal ascites, and enlarged retroperitoneal lymph nodes. No definitive active extravasation was identified, however, there was higher density in a loop of distal jejunum, suggesting the possibility of hemorrhage or contrast.

During her hospital stay, the patient was diagnosed with acute blood loss anemia from a GI bleed. An EGD and colonoscopy showed a 2 cm gastric jejunal junction ulcer and a sigmoid mass at 20 cm. She was treated with a proton pump inhibitor (PPI). Her INR was elevated to 1.4-1.6 on admission and her platelet count was low. Her metastatic pancreatic cancer progressed according to CT. Her hypertension was managed with a beta blocker.

Upon discharge, the patient was instructed to continue taking the PPI, omeprazole, and her dose of metoprolol tartrate was changed to long acting metoprolol succinate XL 25mg daily. She was advised to follow up with her oncologist and the GI team for her biopsy results.

The patient, a female, was admitted due to pancreatic adenocarcinoma of the body and neck of the pancreas. She had a history of chronic back pain, hypertension, and glucose intolerance. She had previously undergone an appendectomy. During her stay, she underwent a diagnostic laparoscopy, an open distal subtotal pancreatectomy and splenectomy with complex dissection off of the portal vein and superior mesenteric artery, and placement of fiducials for Cyberknife postoperatively. 

Upon admission, her physical examination showed stable vital signs, regular heart rate and rhythm, clear lungs, and a soft, non-tender abdomen. Her wound was clean, dry, and intact. However, her lab results showed an elevated MCHC at 35.7, a high glucose level at 127, and an increased lactate level at 3.0. 

During her hospital stay, she tolerated the surgical procedures well and was transferred to the PACU in stable condition. A perioperative epidural was placed and removed when her pain was adequately controlled. She received post-splenectomy vaccinations on postoperative day 5. Her diet was slowly advanced, and her JP drain and staples were removed on hospital day 7, postoperative day 6. She was discharged with home physical therapy. 

Upon discharge, she was clear and coherent, alert and interactive, but required assistance or aid for ambulation. She was advised to resume all regular home medications, get plenty of rest, continue to ambulate several times per day, and drink adequate amounts of fluids. She was also instructed to avoid lifting weights greater than a certain limit until her follow-up with the surgeon. She was advised to avoid driving or operating heavy machinery while taking pain medications and to follow up with her surgeon and primary care provider as advised. 

The patient was discharged with a diagnosis of distal pancreatic adenocarcinoma. She was advised to call her doctor or nurse practitioner if she experienced increased pain, swelling, redness, or drainage from the incision site. She was also instructed to avoid swimming and baths until her follow-up appointment. She was allowed to shower and wash surgical incisions with a mild soap and warm water, and to gently pat the area dry. If she had steri-strips, she was told they would fall off on their own and to remove any remaining strips a certain number of days after surgery.

The patient, a female, was admitted due to abdominal pain and fever. She had previously undergone a distal subtotal pancreatectomy and splenectomy for T3N1 adenocarcinoma with complex dissection off of the portal vein and SMA with fiducial placement. Post-operation, she experienced a twenty-pound weight loss, and despite passing flatus, she had not had a bowel movement. She also reported increasing left flank pain radiating to the back, anorexia, and low-grade fevers. Her white blood cell count was elevated at 18,000. Imaging was obtained, including CXR, KUB, and CT A/P, which led to concerns for an intra-abdominal process, resulting in her transfer for further management.

Her past medical history includes chronic back pain, hypertension, glucose intolerance, appendectomy, distal pancreatectomy, and splenectomy. She has a known allergy to Demerol and Floxin. Her family history is significant for a grandmother with stomach cancer. 

Upon admission, her vitals were recorded as 99.4 99 142/67 16 93 RA. Her physical examination showed no significant abnormalities. However, her lab results showed several abnormalities: WBC-16.1*, RBC-3.29*, Hgb-9.6*, Hct-28.9*, and Glucose-145*. A CT scan revealed a postoperative collection at the distal pancreatectomy site measuring 2.6 x 6.2, stable subcentimeter hypodensities within the liver, attenuation of the proximal SMV by the collection, new bilateral pleural effusions with overlying atelectasis, and possible hemorrhage into a left ovarian cyst.

During her hospital stay, she was started on IV Ciprofloxacin and Flagyl and made NPO for possible drainage. However, after reviewing her CT scan, the decision was made to continue conservative treatment with antibiotics. Her condition improved with a decrease in WBC count, improved abdominal pain, and she was able to move her bowels. She was discharged in stable condition.

Her medications upon admission included reglan 10, lopressor 50, omeprazole 20, simvastatin 10, and colace 100. Her discharge diagnosis was postoperative intra-abdominal fluid collection.

The patient, a middle-aged woman, was admitted due to lower extremity swelling. She has a history of chronic back pain, hypertension, and glucose intolerance. She has undergone an appendectomy, as well as a distal subtotal pancreatectomy and splenectomy for T3N1 adenocarcinoma. The patient reported bilateral lower extremity swelling since being discharged home after her surgery. The swelling in her right lower extremity improved, but the left persisted. She also experienced twitching in her right leg and slight numbness/heaviness in the left leg and thigh. An urgent ultrasound showed occlusive thrombosis in the superficial femoral, popliteal, peroneal, and posterior tibial veins of the left lower extremity, and thrombosis in the peroneal vein of the right lower extremity. 

Upon admission, the patient was on Omeprazole 20 mg daily, Lopressor 25 mg twice daily, and Simvastatin 10 mg daily. Her physical examination revealed slight fatigue, edema in the right lower extremity, and 2+ edema in the left lower extremity. She also had crackles at the bases of her lungs. 

Lab results showed abnormal values for RBC (3.43), HGB (9.5), HCT (31.8), MCHC (30.0), NEUTS (75.4), LYMPHS (17.0), PLT COUNT (647), PTT (22.8), Glucose (156), and a second PTT (28.3). Imaging studies revealed occlusive thrombosis of the entire left lower extremity deep venous system, sparing one of the posterior tibial veins. There was no DVT in the right lower extremity. The patient's echocardiogram showed normal global and regional biventricular systolic function and no clinically significant valvular disease. A chest X-ray showed a small left pleural effusion and bibasilar atelectasis, with no evidence of pneumonia.

During her hospital stay, the patient was found to have bilateral deep vein thrombosis (DVT). She was started on BID Lovenox injections, which improved her pain and swelling. She was trained on self-administration of Lovenox prior to discharge. Her hypertension was managed with continued Lopressor, and her secondary diabetes was managed with an insulin sliding scale for prandial coverage. Her hyperlipidemia was managed with continued statin. 

The patient's family history is significant for a grandmother with stomach cancer. Her mother died from a myocardial infarction and her father died from diabetes mellitus. Upon discharge, the patient was clear and coherent, alert and interactive, and ambulatory-independent. Her discharge diagnosis was bilateral lower extremity deep venous thrombosis.

The patient, a male, was admitted to the neurosurgery service due to an L2 superior end plate fracture and L4-5 stenosis. His history of present illness includes chronic lower back pain, for which he had received spinal injections and narcotics. Despite these treatments, the patient reported no improvement in his pain. An MRI of his lumbar spine revealed severe lumbar stenosis and multiple lumbar disc bulges. The patient also reported worsening symptoms, including a fall due to back pain and leg weakness, bowel and bladder incontinence for over a month, and low back pain with right thigh pain that worsens with movement.

The patient's past medical history includes coronary artery disease, ankylosing spondylitis, colon cancer (polyp) with a partial colectomy and no recurrence, narcotics agreement, benign prostatic hypertrophy, borderline cholesterol, borderline hypertension, carotid stenosis, chronic sinusitis with sinus surgery, depression, fatigue, knee pain, obesity, shoulder dislocation, shoulder pain, temporomandibular joint dysfunction, ganglion cyst, osteoarthritis, peripheral neuropathy, and lumbar back pain.

During his hospital stay, the patient underwent a T12-L2 Laminectomy & Fusion, and L4-5 Laminectomy. Post-surgery, he was neurologically and hemodynamically stable. A TLSO brace was ordered for him and lumbar films showed good placement of screws. He was evaluated and recommended for discharge to acute rehab.

Upon admission, the patient was on several medications including Atorvastatin, Gabapentin, Metoprolol succ, Naproxen, Nitroglycerin, Percocet, Tamsulosin, Tramadol, Venlafaxine ER, Aspirin, Tylenol, and a multivitamin.

The patient's discharge diagnosis included L4-L5 Lumbar stenosis and L2 superior endplate fracture. His mental status was clear and coherent, and he was alert and interactive. His activity status was independent. The patient was instructed to wear a brace at all times when out of bed, avoid heavy lifting, running, climbing, or other strenuous exercise until his follow-up appointment, and not to smoke. He was also advised not to take any blood thinning or anti-inflammatory medications until cleared by the neurosurgeon.

The patient, a female, was admitted due to progressive pain in her right knee. The pain was diagnosed as being caused by osteoarthritis (OA) in the knee, as revealed by X-rays. Her past medical history includes borderline diabetes mellitus (DM) and gastritis. She has allergies to iodine, iodine-containing substances, silver, and mint. 

A major surgical procedure was performed on her right knee, specifically a total knee arthroplasty (TKA). The physical examination revealed a normal head, eyes, ears, nose, and throat (HEENT), clear lungs, regular rate and rhythm (RRR) in cardiovascular (CV) examination, and a soft, non-distended, non-tender abdomen with bowel sounds. However, her right knee was found to be varus with osteophytes.

Lab results showed several abnormal findings. Her white blood cell (WBC) count was elevated at 14.8, indicating a possible infection. Her red blood cell (RBC) count was low at 3.36, and her hemoglobin (HGB) and hematocrit (HCT) were also low at 10.2 and 30.6 respectively, suggesting anemia. 

During her stay, she was given prophylaxis for deep vein thrombosis (DVT) with boots and subcutaneous Lovenox. She also worked with a continuous passive motion (CPM) machine. Upon discharge, her condition was good and she was sent home with service. Her discharge medications included Oxycodone for pain, Docusate Sodium for potential constipation, and Enoxaparin for continued anticoagulation. 

The patient was instructed to engage in activity as tolerated, use a walker or crutches for ambulation, and take her prescribed medications. She was also advised to turn, cough, and deep breathe every two hours when awake. Her surgical incision was dressed with dry gauze and she was told she could leave it open to air.

The patient is a female with a history of hypertension, arthritis, uterine prolapse, dyspepsia, hyperlipidemia, pre-diabetes, urinary incontinence, gastroesophageal reflux, hiatal hernia, diverticulosis, shoulder pain, and a history of breast cysts. She presented with right-sided facial weakness. She has no major surgical or invasive procedures in her medical history. The patient's family history includes diabetes mellitus in her brother and colon cancer and esophageal rupture in other relatives.

Upon admission, the patient was on several medications including amlodipine, clotrimazole cream, ibuprofen, oxyquinoline-sodium lauryl sulfate, ranitidine, acetaminophen, calcium 600 + D(3), loratadine, multivitamins with minerals, psyllium wafer, riboflavin (Vitamin B-2), estradiol, and hydrocortisone-aloe-vits A,D,E topical. 

The patient reported waking up with a heavy feeling in her left eye and mouth, and noticed that the left side of her mouth was drooping. She also reported drooling from the right side of her mouth, but no asymmetrical changes in taste, sensory changes, or recent falls. She had no recent medication changes or notable travel history. 

Physical examination revealed slight left periorbital edema, decreased right eye blinking, and right lower-motor neuron pattern facial weakness. Neurological examination showed normal bulk and tone throughout, with no pronator drift or adventitious movements. Sensory examination showed no deficits to light touch, pinprick, temperature, or vibration. Reflexes were normal and coordination was intact. 

Lab results showed elevated glucose (129) and urea nitrogen (24) levels. Urine analysis showed moderate leukocytes and bacteria. Other labs including creatinine, sodium, potassium, chloride, total CO2, anion gap, and lactate were within normal limits. Imaging studies including CT head, carotid series, and CXR were unremarkable. MRI/MRA of the brain showed no evidence of acute infarction, hemorrhage or intracranial mass, but revealed few scattered white matter changes in the cerebral hemispheres bilaterally, likely sequela of chronic microangiopathy. TTE showed no definite structural cardiac source of embolism identified, trace to mild posterior mitral valve prolapse with trace mitral regurgitation.

The patient was diagnosed with Bell's palsy (right) and was started on prednisone 60 mg daily and valacyclovir 1,000 mg three times daily for 1 week. She was also prescribed lubricating eye drops to help keep her eye moist given right eye closure weakness. The patient was discharged home in a clear and coherent mental state, alert and interactive, and ambulatory - independent. She was advised to follow up with her primary care doctor within the next 2 weeks.

The patient, a female with no significant past medical history, was admitted due to a possible ingestion of bleach following a verbal dispute with her husband. She reported squirting toilet bowl cleaner ('bleach') in her mouth but spat it out immediately. She experienced a burning sensation on her lower lip and chin but denied swallowing any of the bleach. She denied any history of psychiatric problems, including depression and suicidal ideation, and did not report any physical abuse from her husband. The patient has no known allergies to drugs and was not on any medication upon admission.

During her physical examination, she was found to be alert and oriented, with no signs of acute distress. Her vitals were stable, and her lungs were clear to auscultation bilaterally. Her abdomen was soft, non-tender, and non-distended, with present bowel sounds. Her extremities were warm and well perfused, with no signs of clubbing, cyanosis, or edema. However, her chin appeared rough with hyperpigmentation, resembling a microvascular pattern of bruising.

Lab results showed elevated neutrophils (81.6*) and lymphocytes (14.2*), and a slightly high glucose level (106*). Her pO2 level was also elevated (125*). The exact chemical composition of the cleaning agent she ingested was not identified, and no interventions were conducted in the emergency department. She refused an EGD procedure to rule out more distal mucosal GI damage, acknowledging the risks of chemical burn, mucosal damage, stricture, perforation, and subsequent GI dysfunction, infection, and even death.

The patient denied suicidal ideations and was evaluated by psychiatry, who determined she was likely suffering from adjustment or depressive disorder due to being isolated from her family. They recommended outpatient follow-up and social work. The social worker deemed her safe to return home to her husband and children and provided her with multiple contact numbers for outpatient counseling. She was discharged with a primary diagnosis of chemical ingestion, in a clear and coherent mental state, and was ambulatory and independent.

The patient, a female, was admitted due to an infection in her left lower extremity. She reported having pain and swelling in her left leg and intermittent fever at home. She has a history of severe burns on both lower extremities, with the left being more severe than the right. She also has a history of Systemic Lupus Erythematosus (SLE) and Gastroesophageal Reflux Disease (GERD). She has no known history of infection. She was given Vancomycin at the previous hospital before being transferred. 

Upon physical examination, she was found to have old scars and skin deformities on both legs, with bullae and blisters on the medial calf of the left leg, accompanied by severe erythema from the mid-foot to the knee. Her vitals were within normal limits, except for an elevated white blood cell count. 

She underwent an incision, drainage, and biopsy of the skin of her left lower leg. The procedure was performed due to a suspicion of necrotizing fasciitis. However, this condition was ruled out during the operation. 

Her lab results showed several abnormalities: WBC-26.8, RBC-3.92, HGB-9.5, HCT-28.9, MCV-74, MCH-24.2, MCHC-32.8, RDW-18.2, TSH-6.1, ALK PHOS-147, and CREAT-1.8. 

She was started on Vancomycin, ciprofloxacin, and flagyl IV. Her blood cultures were negative for growth, and her wound culture was positive for peptostreptococcus, which was considered to be a contaminant. An MRI of the left lower extremity revealed no evidence of fluid collection, abscess, or osteomyelitis. She was switched to oral ciprofloxacin and clindamycin. 

Bilateral lower extremity dopplers were ordered to rule out Deep Vein Thrombosis (DVT), and the results were negative. 

Upon discharge, her diagnosis was cellulitis of the left lower extremity with ulceration. She was coherent, alert, and able to ambulate with assistance. She was instructed to follow up in the clinic and continue her oral antibiotics and as-needed pain medications at home.

The patient, a female with a history of hypertension (HTN), gastroesophageal reflux disease (GERD), and osteopenia, was admitted due to left leg pain. She had previously fallen from a ladder, which required open reduction and internal fixation (ORIF) of the left femur for fracture. She was recovering as expected, but experienced a fracture through the plate without new trauma. She reported increased pain without new injury and was sent for further evaluation and treatment after x-rays revealed the fracture. She had been following her weight-bearing instructions. 

During her hospital stay, she underwent a major surgical procedure for the removal of hardware and retrograde nail. Her past medical history includes HTN, GERD, osteopenia (for which she was on Fosamax), and an external fix for the initial injury followed by definitive fixation. She has no known bleeding or clotting disorders and no issues with anesthesia. Her family history is non-contributory.

Upon admission, her medications included Venlafaxine 37.5 mg PO BID, amLODIPine 5 mg PO DAILY, Omeprazole 20 mg PO BID, Loratadine 10 mg PO DAILY, Aspirin 81 mg PO DAILY, and Vitamin D 1000 UNIT PO DAILY. 

Abnormal lab results during her stay included low RBC (2.40*, 2.42*), low Hgb (7.4*, 7.3*), low Hct (22.4*, 22.4*), high WBC (12.9*, 10.8*), and high glucose (110*, 126*). 

During her hospital course, she was evaluated by the orthopedic surgery team and found to have a left distal femur fracture. She was taken to the operating room for left femur removal of hardware and retrograde IMN, which she tolerated well. She was initially given IV fluids and IV pain medications, and progressed to a regular diet and oral medications by post-operative day 1. She was given routine antibiotics and anticoagulation. Her home medications were continued throughout this hospitalization. She was discharged with her pain well controlled with oral medications, incisions clean/dry/intact, and voiding/moving bowels spontaneously. She was weight bearing as tolerated in the left lower extremity.

The patient, a female under the care of the neurosurgery department, was admitted due to an arteriovenous malformation (AVM) in the cerebellum. She had been experiencing intense headaches for three months, which was unusual for her. A CT scan, CTA, and MRI study revealed a 3-4 cm cerebellar AVM with irregular venous drainage, venous ectasia, and venous aneurysm. She underwent an angiogram for onyx embolization of the AVM. 

Her past medical history includes breast cancer. She has no known family history of any significant medical conditions. She is allergic to Tylenol and Theraflu Sinus & Cold. 

During her hospital stay, she developed thickened speech, diplopia, and projectile vomiting in the post-anesthesia care unit (PACU). A STAT CTA head scan was negative for hemorrhage or thrombus. She was given Aspirin 81mg and Dexamethasone 10mg IV x 1 for persistent symptoms with a plan for taper from 2mg TID. Her double vision and dysarthria improved somewhat and she was ambulating the halls with her husband by the evening. 

She continued to have mild thickened speech and reported clumsiness in the right hand which had not improved since the day prior. At the time of discharge, she was tolerating a regular diet, ambulating without difficulty, afebrile with stable vital signs. 

Her discharge medications included Aspirin 81 mg, Bisacodyl 10 mg, Cepacol (Sore Throat Lozenge), Dexamethasone 2 mg, Docusate Sodium 100 mg, Ibuprofen 400 mg, Omeprazole 20 mg, Scopolamine Patch, Senna 17.2 mg, and Sertraline 50 mg. 

The patient was discharged home with a diagnosis of cerebral arteriovenous malformation. Her mental status was clear and coherent, and she was alert and interactive. She was ambulatory and independent. 

The patient was instructed to gradually return to her normal activities, avoid strenuous exercise for ten days, and not to go swimming or submerge herself in water for five days after her procedure. She was also given instructions on the care of the puncture site and when to call her doctor.

The patient, a female, was admitted for the elective stage II embolization of a known cerebellar arteriovenous malformation (AVM). She had previously undergone stage I embolization on the right. The patient consented to the procedure after the risks and benefits were discussed. The patient's past medical history includes breast cancer. She has no significant family history. 

Upon admission, the patient was on Fioricet 50/325/40 as needed, sertraline 75 mg daily, Tylenol as needed, ASA 81 mg daily (last dose was a week prior), and a multivitamin. She has allergies to Theraflu Sinus & Cold, Gadavist, MRI contrast, and Tylenol.

The patient underwent Onyx embolization of the cerebellar AVM. Post-procedure, she was monitored in the PACU and then transferred to the NIMU. She remained neurologically intact, was ambulating independently, voiding, and tolerating a diet. The arterial line and foley catheter were discontinued. Her incision site in the groin was covered with a dry, sterile dressing with no evidence of hematoma. 

The patient's mental status was clear and coherent upon discharge. She was alert, interactive, and independently ambulatory. The patient was discharged home. Instructions were given to gradually return to normal activities, avoid strenuous exercise for ten days, and care for the puncture site. She was also advised on when to call the doctor for severe symptoms such as pain, fever, constipation, blood in stool or urine, nausea, vomiting, extreme sleepiness, severe headaches, seizures, vision problems, and weakness or changes in sensation in the face, arms, or legs.

The patient, a female with a history of eczema and six pregnancies, was admitted due to recurrent abdominal pain. She reported having intermittent abdominal pain in the past, but recent labs and abdominal ultrasound were unrevealing. Two weeks prior to admission, she experienced colicky right-sided abdominal pain, diarrhea, and low-grade fever. She also reported occasional night sweats. A pelvic ultrasound showed thickened loops of bowel, leading to her referral to the ED for potential appendicitis or colitis. 

Upon admission, her vital signs were stable and she was given Tylenol for pain. She reported improved right lower quadrant pain and no bowel movement on the day of admission. She denied any smoking history, nausea, vomiting, anorexia, or weight loss. She also reported no recent travel, exposure to healthcare facilities, or pets, and no close contacts with similar symptoms. 

Her family history revealed no instances of inflammatory bowel disease or autoimmune disease. Her mother died of gynecological cancer, her father died of coronary artery disease, her maternal aunt died of uterine cancer, and her maternal grandfather died of an unknown cause. 

A physical exam on discharge showed no acute distress, clear lungs, regular heart rate and rhythm, mild tenderness in the right lower quadrant of the abdomen, and no rash. 

Lab results on admission showed abnormal RBC count (3.97), neutrophils (84.7), and lymphocytes (10.8). Inflammation labs showed elevated ESR (85) and CRP (89.6). Discharge labs showed decreased RBC count (3.70), hemoglobin (11.4), and hematocrit (33.2). Microbiology tests for various pathogens were all negative. 

A CT scan showed marked bowel wall thickening involving the terminal ileum extending into the ileocecal valve, most compatible with inflammatory bowel disease, specifically Crohn's disease. A colonoscopy revealed inflammation with bleeding at the ileo-cecal valve, blood in the cecum coming from the IC valve, and grade 1 internal hemorrhoids. Multiple attempts at intubating the terminal ileum were unsuccessful due to congestion and edema at the IC valve. 

The patient was not on any medications upon admission. During her hospital stay, she was started on Asacol and discharged on a 14-day course of Cipro/Flagyl. Her discharge diagnosis was inflammatory bowel disease, most likely Crohn's disease. She was advised to follow up as an outpatient for final pathology results.

The patient is an adult male who self-presented to the emergency department with his brother due to unusual statements and behaviors following recent substance use, including marijuana, cocaine, and LSD. He reported feeling as though he was 'brainwashed by his fears' and made vague statements about being pursued by the FBI, having a microchip, and experiencing 'bad vibrations.' He denied any suicidal or homicidal ideation, plans, or intent, but expressed feelings of grief due to his perceived self-brainwashing. He also denied believing he has special powers. He reported recent illicit substance use, including daily marijuana use for several years, occasional cocaine use, and two instances of LSD use. He reported feeling depressed in recent days and experiencing increased restlessness, decreased concentration, and memory issues. He denied any prior episodes of hypomania or mania, auditory or visual hallucinations, and ideas of reference.

The patient's brother provided collateral information, reporting that the patient had been making unusual statements about 'intuition' and being 'brainwashed,' and that his thoughts seemed scattered. He also reported that the patient had been making statements about his friends not being his friends and that he was glad his brother was on his side. The patient's roommate also provided collateral information, reporting that the patient had been acting unusually and had been using substances, including cocaine and LSD. The patient's mother reported that the patient had been acting erratically and paranoid over the phone, expressing fears about malware on his computer and stating that he had tried LSD.

The patient's past medical history is unremarkable, with no medical diagnoses. He reported seeing a therapist in high school for supportive counseling, but denied any prior psychiatric diagnoses, hospitalizations, or treatments. He denied any history of suicide attempts or harm to others, but reported a history of self-injurious behavior in the form of cutting in grade school. He denied any history of arrests, convictions, or pending legal issues. His family psychiatric history is also unremarkable, with no reported history of psychiatric disorders or suicide attempts, though there is a history of alcohol abuse and unknown pill use among his paternal siblings.

Physical examination revealed a thin man in no acute distress, with steady gait and normal stance and posture. His mood and affect were described as 'better,' with full range and appropriate to the situation. His thought process was grossly linear and goal oriented, with no circumstantiality noted. He denied suicidal or homicidal ideation, auditory or visual hallucinations, and thought insertion or broadcasting. His judgment and insight were described as impaired but improving. His attention, orientation, and executive function were intact, as was his memory. His speech was spontaneous, with regular rate, rhythm, and prosody, and his language was fluent without paraphasic errors.

Lab results revealed a slightly low hemoglobin level (13.1) and hematocrit (39.5), slightly elevated glucose (115), and a positive test for cocaine in the urine. Other lab results were within normal limits.

During his hospital stay, the patient was placed on 15-minute checks and remained unit restricted. He was started on Risperidone, which led to rapid improvement in his symptoms. He was provided with individual, group, and milieu therapy, and received psychoeducation on substance abuse. He was encouraged to participate in various groups and milieu therapy opportunities, and was appropriate with staff and social with select peers in the milieu. His mother was involved in aftercare planning. At the time of discharge, the patient was not considered to be at an acutely elevated risk of self-harm or danger to others. His prognosis was considered good.

The male patient was admitted due to erythema at a previously removed IV site, with concerns of cellulitis or thrombophlebitis, and neutropenia. He has a history of Acute Myelogenous Leukemia (AML) and is currently on clofarabine/ara-C. He had a line pulled from a previous IV site that had become erythematous. He reported no fever, recent infections, or fevers, and the site on his left arm was tender to touch but stable. He also reported a resolving rash, mainly located in the inferior part of his abdomen, and left lower extremity swelling with signs of petechiae. He had a recent admission for lower extremity DVT on the left and was found to have PE. He was on anticoagulation with lovenox but this was stopped due to low counts.

His past medical history includes AML, right cataract surgery, left cataract, hereditary hemochromatosis, and DVT. His family history includes a mother with cervical cancer, a sister with lung cancer and a brain tumor of unknown etiology, and multiple brothers who passed away from coronary disease.

Upon physical examination, he had a macular rash in the inferior portion of his abdomen, a nodule on his left upper extremity, and petechiae on his left lower extremity. His discharge physical exam showed improved left upper extremity erythema and induration.

His admission labs showed abnormal values for WBC, RBC, Hgb, Hct, MCH, MCHC, RDW, Plt Ct, Neuts, and Lymphs. His discharge labs showed abnormal values for WBC, RBC, Hgb, Hct, MCH, MCHC, Plt Ct, Neuts, Lymphs, Atyps, and Glucose. His imaging results showed findings compatible with superficial thrombophlebitis in the left antecubital fossa and enlargement of the cardiac silhouette without definite vascular congestion.

During his hospital stay, he was started on vanc/cefepime for presumed cellulitis. He spiked one low-grade temperature but remained afebrile since then. His cellulitis significantly improved during his stay, and he was switched to augmentin and levaquin. He was discharged on augmentin and cipro for 2 weeks. His medications upon admission included ondansetron, docusate sodium, multivitamin with minerals, omeprazole, and fluconazole.

His primary discharge diagnosis was cellulitis and recurrent Acute Myelogenous Leukemia, with a secondary diagnosis of cataracts. His mental status was clear and coherent, and he was alert, interactive, and independently ambulatory upon discharge.

The patient is a male with a history of relapsed Acute Myelogenous Leukemia (AML), now in remission after induction with clofarabine & ARA-C and one cycle of consolidation. He was admitted for an allogenic stem cell transplant (allo SCT). He reported feeling well with no acute complaints, except for slight soreness where his tunneled line was placed after admission. He had dental work done, fillings on 4 teeth, and has healed well from this. He denies pain, soreness, or trouble chewing/swallowing. He reported a good appetite and eating well. His lower extremity edema has resolved, and he is able to wear his regular shoes without difficulty. He continued on Lovenox injections to treat his Deep Vein Thrombosis (DVT) until the day before admission. 

His past medical history includes right cataract surgery, left cataract, hereditary hemochromatosis, DVT, Inferior Vena Cava (IVC) filter placement, and a history of syncopal episodes related to voiding vasovagal episodes. His family history includes a mother with cervical cancer, a sister with lung cancer and a brain tumor of unknown etiology, and multiple brothers who passed away from coronary disease.

Upon admission, his physical exam was normal except for 2+ pretibial bilateral edema, slightly greater on the left than the right. His labs showed abnormal values for WBC, RBC, Hgb, Hct, MCV, MCH, MCHC, RDW, and Plt. His glucose levels were also elevated, and he had abnormal ALT, AST, LD(LDH), and TotBili levels. His urine test showed the presence of RBCs.

During his hospital stay, he received his allo SCT without complications. His conditioning for the allo SCT included Fludarabine, busulfan, and ATG rabbit. He had a transient reaction to the first dose of ATG rabbit, but tolerated the remainder of his treatments well. He was transitioned from IV to PO cyclosporine without issue and was started on fluconazole, bactrim, acyclovir, and ursodiol prophylaxis prior to discharge.

His medications upon admission included amlodipine, enoxaparin, ondansetron, docusate sodium, a multivitamin, and omeprazole. His discharge diagnosis was Allogenic Stem Cell Transplant and AML. His mental status was clear and coherent, and he was alert, interactive, and independently ambulatory upon discharge.

The patient, a male with a history of Acute Myelogenous Leukemia (AML), was admitted due to abnormal liver function tests (LFTs). He had previously undergone a mini-allo stem cell transplant for relapsed AML. He reported low-grade fevers, decreased energy levels, and mild throat and sinus discomfort, which he attributed to seasonal allergies. He denied any alcohol use, abdominal pain, nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, rash, skin changes, shortness of breath, or mouth pain. 

His past medical history includes multiple rounds of chemotherapy, a diagnosis of hereditary hemochromatosis, a history of syncopal episodes related to voiding vasovagal episodes, and a history of deep vein thrombosis (DVT). He has also undergone right cataract surgery, inferior vena cava (IVC) filter placement, and endoscopy and colonoscopy procedures. His family history includes a mother with cervical cancer, a sister with lung cancer and a brain tumor of unknown etiology, and multiple brothers who passed away from coronary disease.

Upon admission, the patient underwent a liver biopsy and a bone marrow biopsy. His physical examination was largely unremarkable, with no acute distress noted. His vital signs were stable. 

Admission labs showed abnormal values for RBC (2.87), Hgb (10.3), Hct (31.2), MCV (109), MCH (35.8), Neuts (77), Lymphs (10), Atyps (1), Metas (1), Myelos (1), and PTT (39.3). His liver function tests were also abnormal, with elevated ALT (140), AST (116), LD (349), and AlkPhos (175). 

During his hospital stay, the patient was diagnosed with Post-Transplant Lymphoproliferative Disorder (PTLD), confirmed by liver biopsy. His immunosuppression drugs were decreased to allow his immune system to fight the virus causing PTLD. He also had a fever and was started on antibiotics. His LFTs improved and he was restarted on his home medications. 

Discharge labs showed continued abnormalities in RBC (2.44), Hgb (8.5), Hct (26.2), MCV (107), MCH (34.9), RDW (15.8), Neuts (42.9), Lymphs (48.0), PTT (40.2), ALT (78), LD (264), AlkPhos (213), and Calcium (8.3). His glucose was also elevated (114) and he tested positive for Smooth Muscle Antibodies. 

Imaging studies, including a liver ultrasound, chest x-ray, CT sinus, and PET scan, were largely unremarkable, with the exception of a mucous retention cyst in the left maxillary sinus noted on the CT sinus and multilevel FDG avid lymph nodes noted on the PET scan, which were compatible with PTLD. 

Upon discharge, the patient was instructed to follow up with his oncologist for ongoing management of his PTLD. His medications upon admission included acyclovir, amlodipine, cyclosporine, enoxaparin, fluconazole, folic acid, lorazepam, sulfamethoxazole-trimethoprim, ursodiol, magnesium oxide, and a multivitamin. His cyclosporin dosage was decreased during his hospital stay.

The patient, a male of undisclosed age, was admitted due to nausea, vomiting, abdominal distention, and anorexia. He has a history of relapsed Acute Myeloid Leukemia (AML) after treatment with clofarabine & ARA-C and one cycle of consolidation. He is now 100 days post an unrelated allogenic hematopoietic stem cell transplant. His post-transplant course has been complicated by Post-Transplant Lymphoproliferative Disorder (PTLD) diagnosed by a liver biopsy, a high EBV viral load, and marrow involvement on biopsy. His EBV viral load declined after Velcade/Rituxan treatment. He also has a history of hereditary hemochromatosis, Deep Vein Thrombosis (DVT), and micturational vasovagal syncope. He has undergone right cataract surgery and has a left cataract. 

The patient's family history includes a mother with cervical cancer, a sister with lung cancer and a brain tumor of unknown etiology, and multiple brothers who passed away from coronary disease. 

Upon admission, the patient was taking oxycodone 5mg as needed for back pain and amlodipine 5mg for hypertension. He also reported taking acyclovir, cyclosporine, fluconazole, folic acid, hydrocortisone, lorazepam, sulfameth/trimethoprim, ursodiol, docusate sodium, magnesium, and multivitamins with minerals. 

The patient's symptoms included constipation, abdominal distention, decreased appetite, nausea, and vomiting. He also experienced one episode of lightheadedness. He denied having fevers, chills, jaundice, peripheral edema, or headache. 

Abnormal lab results included elevated urea nitrogen and creatinine levels, indicating acute kidney injury likely due to pre-renal etiology. This responded to IV fluids and increased oral fluid intake. His creatinine level decreased from 1.7 on admission to 0.9 upon discharge. Other abnormal lab results included low red blood cell count, hemoglobin, hematocrit, and platelet count, as well as elevated neutrophils, monocytes, and myelocytes. 

Imaging revealed gaseous distention of some loops of large colon, consistent with ileus. There was no evidence of free intraperitoneal air. A CT scan of the abdomen showed mild distention of the large bowel with gas and no obstruction. 

The patient was diagnosed with acute colonic pseudo-obstruction, likely related to his chemotherapy treatment. He was treated with supportive care, including IV fluids and laxatives, and his condition resolved. He was also diagnosed with hypercalcemia, which downtrended with IV hydration. 

Upon discharge, the patient was clear and coherent, alert and interactive, and able to move independently. He was instructed to continue drinking plenty of water and fluids at home.

The patient is a male with a history of relapsed Acute Myelogenous Leukemia (AML) after clofarabine & ARA-C and one cycle of consolidation. He underwent a hematopoietic stem cell transplant and his post-transplant course has been complicated by Post-Transplant Lymphoproliferative Disorder (PTLD) confirmed on liver biopsy. He was previously admitted with colonic ileus thought to be secondary to Velcade therapy, which resolved with IVF and laxatives. He was found to be febrile during a clinic visit and was admitted for workup. He also has a history of hereditary hemochromatosis, Deep Vein Thrombosis (DVT), cataract surgery, and micturational vasovagal syncope.

His family history includes a mother with cervical cancer, a sister with lung cancer and a brain tumor of unknown etiology, and multiple brothers who passed away from coronary disease.

During this admission, he presented with a fever and an erythematous line insertion site. A CT chest showed new peripheral consolidations in the left upper lobe and left lower lobe consistent with multifocal pneumonia. He was started on vancomycin, cefepime, and azithromycin to cover atypical organisms. His workup for pneumococcus and legionellosis was negative. He remained afebrile, had no cough or chest pain, and maintained a good appetite and regular bowel movements.

His admission labs showed abnormal RBC, Hgb, Hct, MCV, MCH, MCHC, RDW, and LD(LDH). His discharge labs showed abnormal WBC, RBC, Hgb, Hct, MCV, MCH, MCHC, RDW, Neuts, Lymphs, LD(LDH), IgG, and IgA.

His medications upon admission included Acyclovir, Folic Acid, Lorazepam, Oxycodone, Sulfameth/Trimethoprim, Omeprazole, Ursodiol, Milk of Magnesia, Amlodipine, Docusate Sodium, Mg-Plus-Protein, Multivitamins, and CycloSPORINE.

His discharge diagnosis included pneumonia, acute myelogenous leukemia, and post-transplant lymphoproliferative disorder. His mental status was clear and coherent, and he was alert and interactive. He was ambulatory and independent. He was instructed to start levofloxacin, an antibiotic, for 7 more days of treatment.

The patient is a male with a history of Acute Myeloid Leukemia (AML) who underwent allogeneic Bone Marrow Transplant (BMT) and Donor Lymphocyte Infusion. He presented to the clinic with symptoms of headache, malaise, and rash. He had been feeling unwell for several weeks, experiencing intermittent headaches, trouble sleeping, decreased appetite, and a general feeling of being cold. He also reported a scratchy throat, nasal congestion, and some nausea. He had a rash on his chest and the soles of both feet, and some scattered red spots on both arms. The patient's past medical history includes right cataract surgery, left cataract, Hereditary Hemochromatosis, and two instances of Deep Vein Thrombosis (DVT). His family history includes a grandson with Acute Lymphoblastic Leukemia (ALL), a mother with cervical cancer, a sister with lung cancer and a brain tumor, and multiple brothers who died from Coronary Artery Disease (CAD).

Upon physical examination, the patient was found to have inspiratory and expiratory wheezing heard on the left, lower than the upper lobe, and expiratory wheeze heard on the right. He had a diffuse, erythematous, blanching rash on his chest and both plantar surfaces were erythematous and warm. His lab results showed abnormal values for RBC (4.21), MCH (34.0), Neuts (77.1), Lymphs (11.9), UreaN (23), and LD(LDH) (261). Imaging results showed no evidence of acute or subacute intracranial process, but mucosal thickening was noted in the left frontal and maxillary sinus, suggesting an ongoing inflammatory process. His CT scan showed chronic-appearing inflammatory disease involving the left maxillary sinus, with occlusion of its ostiomeatal complex, as well as the left fronto-ethmoidal recess, where there may be an acute component. 

The patient's hospital course was complicated by several issues. He was initially suspected to have viral meningitis due to his presenting symptoms, but his CSF studies were negative. He was empirically started on IV acyclovir, which was discontinued after two days when CSF studies came back negative for HSV. He was given a 7-day course of doxycycline for sinusitis seen on MRI and CT. His headaches resolved following this. He was also diagnosed with graft-versus-host disease (GVHD) of the skin and gut. His rash showed some improvement with initiation of IV methylprednisolone for gut GVHD and eventually resolved with GVHD therapy. His diarrhea did not improve with treatment for C. diff infection, and he was started on IV methylprednisolone, made NPO for bowel rest, and TPN initiated. He underwent upper endoscopy which showed extensive erosions from the stomach to the duodenum. Biopsies were consistent with GVHD. He was increased to 80mg BID methylprednisolone and also placed on 1500mg BID Cellcept, and 3mg TID oral budesonide. After several days of no effect on stool output, Etanercept was started. His stool output decreased to around 2L/day, however his abdomen became increasingly distended and firm. CT abdomen showed diffuse gut inflammation from esophagus to rectum. He also became hypoalbuminemic, hyponatremic, and developed a non-gap metabolic acidosis. He also had a rise in his SCr. These abnormalities were thought to be due to intravascular volume depletion evidenced by significant peripheral edema and urine Na<10. He had several episodes of hypotension which were temporarily fluid responsive. He also became tachypneic, thought to be secondary to diaphragm restriction from bowel edema vs fluid overload. Given his tenuous volume status and pending respiratory compromise, he was transferred to the ICU for further management. He was transferred back to the floor for further management of his GVHD. His stool output began to decrease, however he developed ascites and increasing bilirubin. He was started on cyclosporin 100 mg IV BID which was discontinued due to worsening kidney function. Etanercept was discontinued and he received rituxan. He also received pentostatin 2 mg daily. Steroids were slowly tapered as his stool output decreased. His immunosuppression was altered to include IV cellcept and pentastatin every two weeks. 

The patient also developed hyperbilirubinemia and ascites. His liver improved by holding the TPN and giving him medication. His GVHD slowly improved, and most of his immunosuppressants were weaned off and his diet was advanced. By discharge, his TPN was discontinued and his bowel movements had returned to normal. He was discharged to a rehabilitation facility for physical therapy given how deconditioned he was following this hospital stay. His discharge diagnosis included viral meningitis, acute graft-versus-host disease (gut and skin), Clostridium difficile associated diarrhea, and acute kidney injury. His medications upon admission included Acyclovir, Folic Acid, Omeprazole, Ondansetron, and Multivitamins.

The patient, a male of undisclosed age, was admitted due to a rapid weight gain of 17lbs in one week, abdominal distention, and lower extremity edema. His past medical history is significant for Acute Myeloid Leukemia (AML), for which he underwent Allogenic Stem Cell Transplant (AlloSCT). Post-transplant, he was diagnosed with gut and skin Graft Versus Host Disease (GVHD) and Post-Transplant Lymphoproliferative Disorder (PTLD) based on a liver biopsy. He also has a history of right cataract surgery, left cataract, hereditary hemochromatosis, and two instances of Deep Vein Thrombosis (DVT) for which an Inferior Vena Cava (IVC) filter was placed and later removed. 

The patient's family history includes a grandson with Acute Lymphoblastic Leukemia (ALL), a mother with cervical cancer, a sister with lung cancer and a brain tumor, and multiple brothers who died from Coronary Artery Disease (CAD). 

Upon admission, the patient's physical exam revealed tachycardia, abdominal distention, and a superficial hematoma on the left inner thigh. His lab results showed abnormal levels of Red Blood Cells (RBC), Hemoglobin (Hgb), Hematocrit (Hct), Mean Corpuscular Volume (MCV), Mean Corpuscular Hemoglobin (MCH), Red Cell Distribution Width (RDW), Platelet Count (Plt Ct), Neutrophils, Alanine Aminotransferase (ALT), Aspartate Aminotransferase (AST), Lactate Dehydrogenase (LDH), Alkaline Phosphatase (AlkPhos), Albumin, Urea Nitrogen (UreaN), Creatinine (Creat), and Immunoglobulin G (IgG). 

Radiology results showed mild dilation in the left atrium, aortic root, and ascending aorta, as well as mild symmetric left ventricular hypertrophy. There was also a nonocclusive thrombus in the left common femoral vein and an occlusive thrombus in the right common femoral vein extending to the superficial femoral and deep femoral veins as well as down to the popliteal vein. 

During his hospital stay, the patient was treated for abdominal distention, ileus, nausea/vomiting, nutrition issues, Deep Vein Thrombosis (DVT), and thrombocytopenia. His abdominal distention was likely due to ileus, and his thrombocytopenia was likely multifactorial, possibly related to GVHD, chemotherapy, drug-induced causes, or a hypocellular bone marrow. His acute drop in platelets was most likely related to consumption by the clot. 

Upon discharge, the patient's primary diagnoses were ileus, Inferior Vena Cava (IVC) thrombosis extending to lower extremities, chronic GVHD, thrombocytopenia, and poor oral intake requiring parenteral nutrition. His secondary diagnoses were Acute Myeloid Leukemia (AML) status-post allogenic stem cell transplant, Vitamin D deficiency, and depression. His discharge medications included Acyclovir, Azithromycin, Budesonide, Calcitriol, Lidocaine Jelly, Micafungin, Miconazole Powder, Nystatin Oral Suspension, Pantoprazole, Sertraline, Spironolactone, Sulfameth/Trimethoprim, Vitamin D, Enoxaparin Sodium, Simethicone, Folic Acid, Furosemide, Multivitamins, Promethazine, Methylprednisolone Sodium Succ, and Lorazepam.

His past medical and surgical history includes right cataract surgery, left cataract, Hereditary Hemochromatosis, and DVT with Inferior Vena Cava (IVC) filter placement. 

Family history includes Acute Lymphoblastic Leukemia (ALL) in his grandson, cervical cancer in his mother, lung cancer and brain tumor in his sister, and multiple brothers who died from Coronary Artery Disease (CAD). 

Physical examination revealed blood blisters on left and right hand with some bruising, chronic skin dimpling and sclerotic regions secondary to GVHD, and fragile skin. No acute changes were noted.
