The patient had been lethargic for three days prior to admission and was found to be hypoxic and hypotensive with runs of ventricular tachycardia. He was treated with amiodarone, norepinephrine, epinephrine, and vasopressin. He also received 4L of intravenous fluids, Zosyn, vancomycin, 100mg hydrocortisone, 2g of magnesium, 2g of calcium, and was paralyzed with vecuronium. He was also found to be hyperkalemic and was treated with calcium, bicarbonate, and insulin, which brought his potassium levels down to 3.2. 

Upon admission, his labs were notable for a white blood cell count of 15.7 with 21% bands, hematocrit of 39.1, platelet count of 159, INR of 1.0, potassium of 3.2, bicarbonate of 20, BUN of 30, creatinine of 2.0, glucose of 435, anion gap of 16, AST of 59, ALT of 8, alkaline phosphatase of 141, total bilirubin of 0.7, albumin of 2.8, troponin T of 0.08, and lactate of 6.1. His urinalysis showed >182 white blood cells, large leukocytes, large blood, negative nitrite, no ketones, and moderate bacteria. 

Imaging showed multifocal consolidations in his lungs, worst in the right upper lobe, and multifocal pneumonia with aspiration, secretions within the trachea and esophagus. There was no acute process in the head CT, no fracture in the CT of the cervical spine, and no definitive pleural effusion on the left, but a small one may be present on the right. 

Upon arrival to the MICU, the patient was intubated, sedated, and unresponsive to commands. His medication record from the nursing home indicated that he was not to be resuscitated, intubated, or receive a feeding tube or dialysis. 

During his hospital stay, the patient was diagnosed with septic shock likely from aspiration pneumonia and a urinary tract infection. He also had hypoxic respiratory failure from acute respiratory distress syndrome (ARDS), altered mental status of unclear etiology, and non-ST elevation myocardial infarction (NSTEMI). Given his significant illness with poor prognosis and overall poor state of health, a decision was made to transition care to comfort based care. The patient was subsequently extubated and later found to be unresponsive and not breathing. His time of death was recorded as 1635. 

The patient's preadmission medications included melatonin, trazodone, docusate sodium, guaifenesin, senna, carbidopa-levodopa CR, polyethylene glycol, loratadine, acetaminophen, milk of magnesia, bisacodyl, Fleet enema, citalopram, furosemide, multivitamins, and vitamin D.

The patient, a male with a history of hypertension, hyperlipidemia, and pancreatitis, presented with abdominal pain and fever. He reported a previous episode of pancreatitis, for which he was admitted overnight to a hospital and underwent endoscopy. He also reported a history of alcohol consumption, which he has since reduced. His symptoms began with vomiting and fevers, which improved but then recurred along with right epigastric abdominal pain. Initial vital signs were stable and a CT scan of the abdomen showed inflammation in the retroperitoneum that appeared to originate from the pancreas. Lab results revealed mild transaminitis with a lipase level of 185. He was administered levo/flagyl and admitted for further management.

The patient's physical exam was largely unremarkable, with the exception of tenderness to touch in the right epigastric region. Lab results showed a glucose level of 119, chloride level of 95, ALT of 73, AST of 79, and lipase of 185, all of which were elevated. The patient's RBC count was 3.73, HGB was 11.4, and HCT was 34.8, all of which were low. The patient also had elevated bands at 16 and lymphs at 12, as well as metas at 1. 

Imaging studies, including a CT scan and MRCP, showed extensive pancreatitis with a small to moderate region of hemorrhagic necrosis in the pancreatic neck and head, as well as significant duodenitis secondary to the pancreatitis. There was also persistent mild right hydronephrosis, likely due to involvement of the right ureter by the inflammatory process. 

The patient's hospital course was notable for acute pancreatitis with a prolonged course, including hemorrhagic necrosis on imaging. He was treated with antibiotics and slowly began to tolerate a regular diet. He also experienced a fever while hospitalized, which was likely due to pancreatitis. His hypertension was managed with atenolol and his hyperlipidemia was managed with simvastatin. He also had anemia and reactive thrombocytosis, which were monitored. Additionally, he had a gout flare in his left knee, which was confirmed by fluid analysis and treated with indomethacin and vicodin for breakthrough pain. 

Upon admission, the patient was taking atenolol, aspirin, and simvastatin. His family history was non-contributory and he had no known drug allergies. The patient was discharged in good condition with instructions to avoid alcohol and to use indomethacin as the primary treatment for his gout, with vicodin for breakthrough pain.

The patient, a male with a history of alcoholism, was admitted due to recurrent pancreatitis, presenting with fever and abdominal pain. His first episode of pancreatitis occurred years ago, with recurrences in the past and most recently three weeks prior to this admission. His past medical history includes hypertension, hyperlipidemia, and a previous appendectomy. He has no significant family history. Upon admission, he was taking atenolol, a statin, celexa, and omeprazole. 

During his hospital stay, he underwent several surgical procedures including an open cholecystectomy with cholangiogram, a pancreatic pseudocyst jejunostomy (Roux-en-Y), and debridement of the tail of the pancreas with external drainage. His hospital course was complicated by post-operative hyperglycemia, which was managed with insulin. He was also monitored for infection, with home Zosyn prescribed for a Streptococcus anginosus infection. 

Abnormal lab results included elevated white blood cell counts (WBC-17.0*#, WBC-15.2*), decreased red blood cell counts (RBC-4.39*, RBC-4.06*), decreased hemoglobin (Hgb-13.0*, Hgb-12.4*), decreased hematocrit (Hct-37.5*, Hct-36.4*), elevated amylase (Amylase-267*, Amylase-288*), elevated lipase (Lipase-102*, Lipase-116*, Lipase-126*), and decreased calcium (Calcium-8.0*). A CT scan revealed pancreatic necrosis and large diffuse fluid collections with apparent fistulous connections to the colon and duodenum. Tissue culture from a right lower quadrant abscess showed moderate growth of Streptococcus anginosus. 

Upon discharge, the patient was hemodynamically stable, tolerating oral intake, and his pain was controlled with an oral regimen. His discharge diagnoses included an infected pancreatic pseudocyst, necrotic pancreas, gallstones, pancreatic leak, and post-op hyperglycemia. He was instructed to keep his incision clean and dry, monitor for signs of infection or other complications, and to continue with dressing changes to the incision. He was also advised to resume all regular home medications and to follow up with his doctor.

The patient, a right-handed male with a history of hypertension, hyperlipidemia, anxiety, alcohol abuse, recurrent pancreatitis caused by a complex infected pseudocyst, and gout, was admitted due to an episode suggestive of a seizure. His mother found him in his bedroom with his whole body shaking and unresponsive. He had blood coming out of his mouth but no urinary incontinence. He was disoriented after the episode but regained full consciousness within ten minutes. He reported no aura-like signs before the episode. He also mentioned a recent pedestrian/SUV accident where he was hit in the chest and thrown several feet backwards. He denied having a headache after the accident and has been taking 400mg of Advil three times a day for a week since the accident. He quit alcohol four weeks ago and has not experienced any withdrawal symptoms. 

The patient's family history includes hypertension, hyperlipidemia, coronary artery disease, and cancer (skin, testicular, throat, and lung). His physical examination revealed a heart rate of 136, blood pressure of 135/93, and oxygen saturation of 99%. He had an old laceration on the volar tongue surface, no bleeding lacerations, and his neck was in a cervical collar. His left shoulder was immobile with edema and echymoses at the left shoulder and left elbow. 

Lab results showed abnormal values for RBC (3.38*), Hgb (10.7*), Hct (30.7*), Glucose (141*), CK(CPK) (2495*), ALT (77*), AST (134*), LD(LDH) (413*), and CK-MB (18*). Urine test showed positive for opiates. CSF test showed RBC (1*). 

Imaging results showed a small frontal hyperdensity on the CT scan, which could be either artifactual or hemorrhage. MRI showed no signs of hemorrhage and no cause for the seizure. EEG was unremarkable. X-ray showed a fracture of the proximal humerus predominantly involving the greater tuberosity which is comminuted. 

The patient underwent open reduction/internal fixation of the left shoulder. He had a second seizure prior to the procedure and was therefore started on oxcarbazepine for seizure control. He was instructed not to drive for 6 months. He also had a fever during admission, but lumbar puncture and cultures were negative. His atenolol was increased to 50mg daily for blood pressure control. 

Upon admission, the patient was taking atenolol (dosing unknown), citalopram 40mg daily, simvastatin 80mg daily, and omeprazole 20mg daily.

The patient, a male of undisclosed age, was admitted with a chief complaint of esophageal perforation. He has a past medical history of mitral valve prolapse, aortic dilation which is monitored annually via echocardiogram, a surgically repaired detached retina, and possible newly diagnosed reflux. His family history includes a variant of a genetic disorder. 

The patient presented with pneumomediastinum, likely due to esophageal perforation. He reported having undergone an esophagogastroduodenoscopy (EGD) for dilation of a Schatski ring earlier in the day. After the procedure, he consumed some broth and liquids, which was followed by right upper quadrant (RUQ) pain radiating to the back. The pain was exacerbated by eructation, coughing, and deep inspiration. A chest CT scan revealed pneumomediastinum tracking along the right distal esophagus. The patient was treated with ceftriaxone, Dilaudid, and Phenergan before being transferred for further evaluation and treatment. 

The patient also reported a history of intermittent dysphagia for meats, which had worsened over the past few years and recently resulted in regurgitation of chicken. A barium swallow test was performed prior to the EGD, which, according to the patient, was normal. 

Upon physical examination, the patient was found to be in no apparent distress. His vital signs were stable, and his physical exam was largely unremarkable. 

Lab results revealed abnormal RBC count of 4.38, Hct of 39.0, WBC count of 19.7, Cl of 110, and glucose level of 113. An esophagram showed no evidence of extraluminal contrast, suggesting no ongoing esophageal leak. A small distal esophageal diverticulum was noted. A chest X-ray showed no evidence of pneumomediastinum or pneumothorax, with normal cardiomedistinal contours and clear lungs. 

During his hospital stay, the patient was kept NPO and started on IV antibiotics. His condition remained stable with serial CBCs and CXRs. He was later started on a soft solid diet without difficulty swallowing, and his antibiotics were converted to oral administration. 

Upon admission, the patient was on aspirin 81 mg daily, niacin 250 mg three times a day, atenolol 100 mg daily, and topiramate 25 mg twice a day. The patient was discharged in stable condition with a diagnosis of pneumomediastinum. His discharge instructions included calling his doctor if he experienced fever, increased cough or chest pain, hoarseness, or difficulty swallowing, and to continue a soft solid diet until his follow-up appointment in one week.

The patient, a male, was admitted due to experiencing five episodes of bright red bleeding per rectum. He reported a sudden urge to defecate and evacuation of bright red blood, which he estimated to be about a cup in volume. This was followed by three smaller episodes before arriving at the emergency department. He also reported mild lightheadedness with standing and mild weakness. He denied having fevers, chills, nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, chest pain, shortness of breath, abdominal or rectal pain, and hematuria. He also denied any indigestion-like pain or hematemesis. 

The patient has a history of aortic dilation, esophageal perforation, atrial fibrillation managed with Pradaxa, pemphigus managed with prednisone and Cellcept, gastritis, and prostate cancer treated with radiation. He also has a history of benign adenomas and diverticular disease, as revealed by a colonoscopy. He had a schatzkis ring which was dilated. He also has a history of prostate cancer treated with external beam radiation. His last EGD showed gastritis and no ulcers or varices. He has been diagnosed with pemphigus vulgaris in the past year and has been on prednisone and cellcept. 

Upon examination, he was found to have tenderness of lower left ribs with overlying healing ecchymosis, and bright red blood on rectal exam, guiaic positive. His initial vital signs in the ED were: 98.3 63 164/84 18 99% RA. 

Lab results showed abnormal values for WBC (12.8), RBC (4.15), Hgb (13.0), Hct (39.5), Neuts (93.3), Lymphs (3.1), Monos (2.3), Eos (0.1), Baso (0.2), AbsNeut (11.91), AbsLymp (0.40), AbsEos (0.01), Glucose (131), HCO3 (19), AnGap (22), UreaN (22), Na (147), and K (3.4). 

The patient's medications upon admission included PredniSONE, Losartan Potassium, Atenolol, Dabigatran Etexilate, Escitalopram Oxalate, Mycophenolate Mofetil, Tamsulosin, Vitamin D, and Pantoprazole. 

The patient has a family history of a variant of a genetic disorder that affects the body's connective tissue. 

Conditions and symptoms that have been ruled out include fevers, chills, nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, chest pain, shortness of breath, abdominal or rectal pain, hematuria, indigestion-like pain, and hematemesis.

The patient, a male of undisclosed age, was admitted to the hospital after experiencing an episode of dizziness. He reported feeling severe congestion, which he occasionally experiences, and while walking across a room, he felt his ears pop, followed by sudden dizziness and a feeling of impending collapse. He also felt nauseated and clammy, but these symptoms dissipated after a few minutes of lying down. He denied experiencing loss of consciousness, numbness, weakness, tingling, difficulty talking, smiling, vision blurriness, vision loss, or a sensation that the room was spinning. He was brought to the emergency department (ED) by ambulance, during which he experienced a migraine aura without a headache. 

Upon evaluation in the ED, his symptoms had completely resolved. A neurologist evaluated him and found no evidence of orthostasis, suggesting the possibility of vestibular neuropathy, but also considering a cardiac cause. His INR was therapeutic and a CT scan was normal. The patient was transferred to the floor with no complaints and reported feeling 'completely fine'. 

The patient's past medical history includes a prosthetic heart valve following surgery for an aneurysm of the thoracic aorta and a benign tremor. His family history includes a father who died of a stroke, a mother with dementia living in an assisted living facility, and a brother with a severe resting tremor. 

Upon admission, the patient's physical exam was largely normal, with a noted mild tremor on finger-to-nose testing bilaterally and very mild cogwheeling at the elbows bilaterally. His admission labs showed abnormal values for RBC (3.99), Hgb (13.4), Hct (37.9), MCH (33.6), MCHC (35.3), Neuts (84.3), Lymphs (11.6), and Glucose (108). 

Imaging studies included a CT scan of the head without contrast, which showed no acute intracranial process, and an echocardiogram, which showed a moderately dilated aortic root, a severely dilated ascending aorta, and a well-seated mechanical aortic valve prosthesis with normal leaflet/disc motion and transvalvular gradients. 

The patient's dizziness was thought to be likely related to an inner ear process, given the timing of ear popping and the onset of disequilibrium. However, due to his mechanical aortic valve, a transient ischemic attack was also considered, though deemed less likely given his therapeutic INR upon admission. The patient's echocardiogram showed a well-functioning valve with no clots, but also revealed aortic root dilatation that will require surgical follow-up. 

The patient's medications upon admission included warfarin and multiple vitamins including L-carnitine, fish oil, and selenium. He was discharged with a primary diagnosis of dizziness and a secondary diagnosis of history of mechanical aortic valve replacement. His mental status was clear and coherent, and he was alert, interactive, and independently ambulatory upon discharge.

The patient, a male, was admitted due to a thoracic aortic aneurysm. He has a history of thoracic aortic dissection with graft repair and mechanical aortic valve replacement. The patient was asymptomatic, but an echocardiogram during a recent admission for dizziness showed increased dilation of the aneurysm from 4.0 to 5.5 cm. Further evaluation with CT and CTA confirmed the enlargement of the aneurysm, as well as an ulcer and dissection along the anterior portion. The patient was admitted for IV heparin bridging while off his coumadin prior to upcoming procedures, due to his mechanical aortic valve. 

The patient denies any prior history of stroke, TIA, deep venous thrombosis, pulmonary embolism, bleeding at the time of surgery, myalgias, joint pains, cough, hemoptysis, black stools or red stools. He also denies recent fevers, chills or rigors, exertional buttock or calf pain, chest pain, dyspnea on exertion, paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnea, orthopnea, ankle edema, palpitations, syncope or presyncope. 

His past medical history includes ascending thoracic aortic aneurysm with conduit graft and mechanical AV replacement, Hepatitis C (positive by ab in the past, however negative since), Hyperlipidemia, Hemorrhoids, Benign tremor, and Seasonal Allergies. The patient's family history includes a brother who also has dextrocardia but no genetic testing done, a father who died of a stroke, a mother with dementia, and another brother with severe resting tremor.

Upon physical examination, the patient was found to be in no apparent distress, with stable vital signs. His cardiac review was notable for the absence of chest pain, dyspnea on exertion, paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnea, orthopnea, ankle edema, palpitations, syncope or presyncope. 

Lab results on admission showed abnormal RBC count (4.12), Hemoglobin (13.7), Hematocrit (39.4), MCH (33.2), and Glucose (188). Phosphorus was also found to be abnormal (2.4). Imaging showed a large aneurysmal segment of native aortic root surrounding the aortic root graft. The coronary 'buttons' appear to have dehisced from the insertion position on the sides of the endograft. This has led to a 'pseudoaneurysm' surrounding the graft with multiple communications between the tube graft. 

During his hospital stay, the patient underwent an uncomplicated left heart catheterization with no coronary artery disease found, but a small left groin hematoma resulted which was stable and of no hemodynamic significance. He was bridged with a heparin drip to therapeutic INR. 

Upon admission, the patient was on warfarin, Zyrtec, and Sudafed as needed. The patient was discharged in stable condition with plans for thoracic aneurysm revision in the near future. His primary diagnoses upon discharge were thoracic aortic aneurysm and mechanical aortic valve.

The patient, a white male, was admitted due to a pseudoaneurysm of the aortic root. He had previously undergone a Bental procedure with a Bjo valve, but recently developed dizziness. An echocardiogram and catheterization revealed dilation of the root and a pseudoaneurysm of the aortic root, respectively. His past medical history includes aortic valve replacement, Hepatitis C, hyperlipidemia, hemorrhoids, benign tremor, and seasonal allergies. He has never been tested for a certain genetic disorder, but it is suspected due to physical characteristics. His family history includes a brother with similar physical characteristics and dextrocardia, a father who died of a stroke, a mother with dementia, and another brother with a severe resting tremor.

Upon admission, the patient was on several medications including Warfarin, Cetirizine, a multivitamin, Omega-3 fatty acids with Vitamin E, Phospatidyl Coline, and Pseudoephedrine HCL. His physical examination was largely normal, with no acute distress, dry and intact skin, healed surgical scars, and normal heart and lung function. However, his right groin was noted to be soft and ecchymotic.

An echocardiography report revealed an aortic disruption near the right coronary cusp with a fluid collection consistent with a pseudoaneurysm. The mechanical aortic valve prosthesis was functioning normally. The right coronary artery was not easily identified, leading to the belief that the right coronary button (ostium) was the location of the aortic disruption. This was confirmed intraoperatively. His blood work showed abnormal RBC, Hgb, and Hct levels, as well as an elevated PTT.

During his hospital stay, the patient underwent a reoperation involving a redo sternotomy, resection of the pseudoaneurysm, and reimplantation of the right coronary artery. He tolerated the procedure well and was transferred to the CVICU in a stable condition. He was extubated without incident and began taking beta blockers, Lasix, and Amlodipine for hypertension control. He was also started on Coumadin. A right pleural effusion was noted on a CT scan. He was transfused multiple units for a dropping hematocrit and was taken back to the OR for re-exploration and washout. His postoperative course was largely uneventful, and he was discharged in a stable condition.

The patient, a male, was admitted for the removal of sternal wire. He had previously undergone a redo sternotomy, resection of pseudoaneurysm, and reimplantation of the right coronary artery. He presented with severe pain in the lower half of his sternum, which was intense even with light touch. Despite a normal physical exam and chest x-ray showing no changes and intact sternum and sternal wires, the patient opted for conservative management with pain medication. However, he returned still complaining of pain, especially over two sternal wires. He was admitted for Heparin bridge with Coumadin stopped in anticipation of surgery.

His past medical history includes a Bentall Procedure with Bjork aortic valve replacement, Hepatitis C, Hyperlipidemia, Hemorrhoids, Benign tremor, Seasonal Allergies, and hyperlipidemia. He has never been tested for a certain genetic disorder. His family history includes a brother who also has physical characteristics suggestive of the same genetic disorder and dextrocardia, but no genetic testing has been done. His father died of a stroke, his mother has dementia and lives in an assisted living facility, and his brother has a severe resting tremor.

Upon admission, the patient was on Warfarin, Zyrtec, Multivitamins, Fish Oil (Omega 3), and Pseudoephedrine. His physical exam was normal, with a well-healed sternotomy scar, clear lungs, and a soft, non-distended, non-tender abdomen. His pulse rates were normal.

Abnormal lab results included RBC-4.08*, HGB-13.3*, HCT-39.0*, MCH-32.5*, and PTT-95.9*. A chest radiology report showed interval removal of the sternal wires, no pneumothorax or other complication, mildly hyperinflated but clear lungs, no pleural effusion, and an unremarkable cardiomediastinal silhouette.

During his hospital stay, the patient underwent sternal wire removal and tolerated the procedure well. He was extubated immediately post-operatively and transferred to the stepdown unit for post-operative management. His pre-operative medication management was resumed, and a heparin drip was initiated as a bridge to coumadin management for his prosthetic valve. By the time of discharge, the patient was ambulating freely, the wound was healing, and pain was controlled with oral analgesics. He was discharged to home in good condition with appropriate follow-up instructions.

The patient, a male, was admitted due to an abnormal aortagram. He has a history of aortic valve replacement (AVR) and resection of aortic root pseudoaneurysm. A recent chest CT showed a thrombus within the graft, which led to the need for further evaluation via aortagram. His past medical history includes Hepatitis C, hyperlipidemia, hemorrhoids, benign tremor, and seasonal allergies. He has never been tested for a certain genetic disorder, despite showing some physical characteristics. 

The patient's family history includes a brother who also exhibits similar physical characteristics and has dextrocardia, but no genetic testing has been done. Their father passed away from a stroke, and their mother is currently living in an assisted living facility due to dementia. Another brother has a severe resting tremor.

Upon admission, the patient's physical exam showed a thin man, speaking in full sentences, with aortic mechanical valve, and no signs of edema or stasis dermatitis. His discharge physical exam showed similar results. 

Admission labs showed abnormal levels of glucose (128), RBC (3.93), HGB (13.0), HCT (35.7), MCH (33.2), MCHC (36.5), and PTT (37.3). Discharge labs showed abnormal levels of RBC (4.09), Hgb (13.4), Hct (37.3), and MCH (32.8). A chest X-ray showed no acute cardiopulmonary process.

During his hospital stay, the patient was transferred to the cardiology service due to weekend staffing. A catheterization showed a large ascending aortic aneurysm. His coumadin was initially stopped in anticipation of surgery and he was placed on a heparin drip. However, the decision was later made to have surgery as an outpatient, so coumadin was restarted and he was discharged with a lovenox bridge until coumadin was therapeutic.

The patient's medications upon admission included Warfarin, Fish Oil (Omega 3), Loratadine for allergies, and Multivitamins. 

The patient was discharged with a diagnosis of pseudoaneurysm and thrombus, with secondary anticoagulation for aortic valve. His mental status was clear and coherent, and he was alert, interactive, and independently ambulatory. He was instructed to stop taking his coumadin on a specified date in preparation for his upcoming surgery.

The patient, a male, was admitted due to fatigue and dyspnea on exertion. He has a history of a Bentall procedure with a mechanical valve, resection of aortic root pseudoaneurysm, Hepatitis C, hyperlipidemia, hemorrhoids, benign tremor, and seasonal allergies. He has never been tested for a genetic disorder that may cause physical features similar to his and his brother's. His brother also has dextrocardia but has not undergone genetic testing. Their father passed away from a stroke and their mother is currently living with dementia in an assisted living facility. 

The patient's chief complaint was increasing shortness of breath over the past few months, to the point where he could not climb three flights of stairs. However, he did not report any chest pain, nausea/vomiting, or diaphoresis. He was seen by the cardiac surgery service and accepted for a redo Bentall procedure. Pre-op cardiac cath revealed a large ascending aorta pseudoaneurysm with proximity to the right coronary artery (RCA) take off. He was discharged to home on Lovenox and returned for a heparin bridge and redo sternotomy Bentall and coronary artery bypass graft (CABG) procedure. 

Upon admission, his vitals were stable and physical exam was largely unremarkable. His admission labs showed abnormal RBC count (3.89*), HGB (12.6*), HCT (36.0*), MCH (32.4*), and glucose (118*). His echocardiogram showed a mildly dilated right ventricular cavity with borderline normal systolic function, focal calcifications in the ascending aorta, mildly thickened mitral valve leaflets with moderate thickening of the chordae, and a pseudoaneurysm identified below the ascending aorta at the location of RCA implantation. His chest radiology report showed persistent right pleural effusion and likely right lower lobe atelectasis, unchanged right pleural effusion, improving left loculated pleural effusion, stable cardiomegaly, and prosthetic cardiac valve. 

During his hospital stay, he underwent repair of his pseudoaneurysm and tolerated the procedure well. He was extubated within several hours of arrival in the CVICU and weaned off of inotropic support on post-op day 1. His post-op course was largely uneventful and he was discharged to home on post-op day 7. 

Upon admission, he was on warfarin, fish oil, loratadine, multivitamin, and phosphatidyl choline.

The patient, a male of unspecified age, was admitted due to fatigue. He has a history of aortic pseudoaneurysm, benign tremor, hemorrhoids, Hepatitis C, hyperlipidemia, and seasonal allergies. He has never been tested for a genetic disorder that is characterized by features such as tall stature, long limbs, and cardiovascular abnormalities. His family history includes a brother with similar physical characteristics and dextrocardia, a father who died of a stroke, and a mother with dementia.

The patient has undergone multiple surgical procedures, including a Bentall procedure with a Bjork-Shiley valve, resection of aortic root pseudoaneurysm and reimplantation of the right coronary artery, and removal of a salivary stone. On the day of admission, he experienced disorientation, shortness of breath, nausea, and diaphoresis, symptoms similar to those he experienced when he developed a pseudoaneurysm in the past. A CTA revealed an aortic pseudoaneurysm.

Upon admission, his heart rate was 65, blood pressure was 155/88 on the right and 155/81 on the left, respiratory rate was 14, and oxygen saturation was 94% on room air. His physical exam was largely unremarkable. Lab results showed abnormal RBC count (2.84, 2.81, 4.32), hemoglobin (8.2, 8.1, 12.6), hematocrit (25.9, 25.9, 37.7), MCHC (31.7, 31.3), RDWSD (50.4, 51.2, 51.8), and glucose (117). His PTT was also elevated at times (37.7, 66.0, 73.8, 44.3).

During his hospital stay, he underwent a redo sternotomy, aortic pseudoaneurysm repair, and coronary artery bypass graft. Post-operatively, he was stable but required nitroglycerin for hypertension and high flow oxygen for 48 hours. He was evaluated by physical therapy for strength and mobility assistance. His warfarin was resumed for his mechanical valve. By the time of discharge, he was ambulating freely, his wound was healing, and his pain was controlled with oral analgesics.

His medications upon admission included Metoprolol Tartrate, Warfarin, Fish Oil, Loratadine, Multivitamins, and phosphatidylcholine.

The patient, a male, was admitted to the Cardiac Thoracic unit due to fatigue. He has a history of multiple redosternotomies, aortic pseudoaneurysm repairs, and a Bentall AVR. He also underwent resection of aortic root pseudoaneurysm and patch of Dacron graft and reimplantation of the right coronary artery. He has a history of benign tremor, hemorrhoids, Hepatitis C, hyperlipidemia, and seasonal allergies. He has never been tested for a certain genetic disorder, despite having symptoms suggestive of it. 

The patient's brother also has physical characteristics suggestive of the same genetic disorder and has dextrocardia, but no genetic testing has been done. Their father died of a stroke and their mother is currently living in an assisted living facility due to dementia. 

Upon admission, the patient was on several medications including fluticasone, metoprolol succinate, warfarin, aspirin, cetirizine, guaifenesin, and a multivitamin. He reported a cough that started two days prior to admission, which was occasionally productive of loose clear phlegm. He denied having a fever. A chest X-ray revealed a retro-sternal fluid collection. 

During his hospital stay, a CT scan and echocardiogram were performed. The CT scan revealed a new 11.2 x 2.8 cm thickly rim enhancing air and fluid containing retrosternal collection in close association with the right lower lobe bronchi, which raised concerns for a bronchomediastinal fistula. Foci of air adjacent to the ascending aorta were also noted, which were atypical for postsurgical change given the 2 month time interval, raising concerns for infection. However, there was no evidence of a new pseudoaneurysm or aortic dissection. A partially calcified left renal artery aneurysm measuring 1.5 x 1.2 cm was also noted. 

The echocardiogram showed normal left ventricular systolic function, a well-seated mechanical aortic prosthesis without significant aortic regurgitation, and no pericardial effusion. A mobile density was seen either adjacent to and within the descending aorta which likely represents artifact. 

The patient was maintained on Coumadin for his mechanical aortic valve replacement. No surgical or invasive procedures were performed during this admission. The patient was discharged home in a stable condition with instructions for wound care and activity restrictions.

The patient is a male with a history of atrial fibrillation, benign prostatic hypertrophy (BPH), prostate cancer, and diffuse large B-cell lymphoma. He was admitted for ICE chemotherapy due to a large chest wall mass. His oncologic history includes initial development of splenomegaly, worsening splenomegaly prompting splenectomy, remission, enlarged left axillary lymph node, worsening left axillary node adenopathy per CT, repeat left axillary lymph node biopsy, identification of splenectomy-bed disease progression, and development of a left sternal mass. He has also sought alternative therapies and was hospitalized due to recurrent disease with extensive osseus and paraspinal tumor masses seen on MRI. 

The patient denies any specific complaints but notes a recent upper respiratory infection and urinary retention, for which a foley was placed. He denies any recent dysuria or hematuria, and any pain. He is eating well but his appetite is down and his weight has decreased. He denies any chest pain, shortness of breath, or cough, but notes a 'pulling sensation' around his sternal mass with certain movements. He denies any rashes or skin changes, except for some erythema inferior to the sternal mass. He denies any swelling in his arms or face. He has chronic varicositis of his legs and continues to wear compression stockings. He denies any numbness or tingling in his hands or his feet and denies developing any neuropathy from previous treatments.

His family history includes his mother who died of pneumonia and CHF, his father who had a history of both prostate and rectal cancer, and a brother who had a nonmalignant tumor removed from his pituitary. His maternal grandmother had CAD and his maternal grandfather died of liver cancer in the setting of cirrhosis. His paternal grandfather had diabetes. His paternal aunt also had cancer of an unknown type.

Upon physical examination, the patient was found to have a large sternal mass, not warm or ecchymotic, with mild blanching erythema inferior to the mass. There is marked vascular congestion at the base of his ribs bilaterally. His extremities showed mild edema with greyish patchy discoloration bilaterally and purple, swollen fingers. 

Abnormal lab results include a platelet count of 643, WBC of 13.3, RDW of 16.5, LDH of 539, potassium of 5.3, neutrophils of 88.9, lymphocytes of 5.9, glucose of 106, urea nitrogen of 24, RBC of 4.35, hemoglobin of 12.9, and LDH of 427. Urine culture showed Neisseria species with 10,000-100,000 organisms/mL.

During his hospital stay, the patient was monitored for the development of tumor lysis syndrome while undergoing ICE chemotherapy. He was started on IVFs and given allopurinol prior to starting ICE. Tumor lysis labs were carefully monitored and remained within normal limits. The patient tolerated IVFs as well as ICE without complication. He was asked to continue taking allopurinal and acyclovir for prophylaxis of TLS and shingles as well as phenergen for prevention of delayed nausea. He was also given a 10 day supply of filgrastim and instructed on how to administer the medication subcutaneously at home prior to discharge. 

The patient's medications upon admission included acyclovir, allopurinol, digoxin, ondansetron HCL, prochlorperazine maleate, and tamsulosin.

The patient is a middle-aged male with a history of diffuse large B cell lymphoma, currently pending enrollment in a clinical trial. He presented with symptoms of early satiety and acholic stools for a week. He also reported a 7 lb weight loss, increased fatigue, upset stomach, and decreased appetite. He denied experiencing pain, fever, chills, night sweats, headache, vision changes, chest pain or tightness, palpitations, cough, shortness of breath, wheezes, urinary frequency or burning, and rashes or skin breakdown. 

His oncologic history includes initial development of splenomegaly, worsening splenomegaly prompting splenectomy, remission, enlarged left axillary lymph node, worsening left axillary node adenopathy per CT, repeat left axillary lymph node biopsy, identification of splenectomy-bed disease progression, and recurrent disease with extensive osseus and paraspinal tumor masses. He also has a history of BPH, prostate cancer, osteoporosis, atrial fibrillation, and CHF. 

His family history includes his mother's death due to pneumonia and CHF, his father's history of prostate and rectal cancer, one brother's nonmalignant tumor removed from his pituitary, maternal grandmother's CAD, maternal grandfather's liver cancer in the setting of cirrhosis, paternal grandfather's diabetes, and paternal aunt's cancer of an unknown type. 

Upon physical examination, he was found to have a small round, mobile mass to the right of his umbilicus. His lab results showed abnormal values for Lipase (631), LDH (296), Tprotein (5.5), RBC (4.20), HGB (12.1), HCT (37.4), and RDW (16.5). Imaging revealed bibasilar opacities and small bilateral pleural effusions in his chest XRAY, a large deep ulcer in the fundus in his EGD US, a 4 cm ill-defined mass in the head/uncinate pancreas in his EUS, and progression of metastatic disease in his CT abdomen. 

During his hospital stay, he was diagnosed with a pancreatic mass and gastric ulcer. His lipase trended down to 437--> 182 and he was able to eat a light bland breakfast the day of discharge. He was also found to be in atrial fibrillation and continued to take his home dose digoxin (0.25 mg Q day). His medications upon admission included Digoxin, Cod Liver Oil, and Omega-3 Fatty Acids.

The patient, a male with a history of metastatic large B cell lymphoma, was referred to the ER following the discovery of bilateral lower lobar pulmonary embolisms on a scheduled CT scan. This scan was conducted to evaluate the progression of his lymphoma in preparation for possible enrollment into a research study. The patient has a history of extensive metastases and had recently undergone radiation therapy to an anterior mediastinal mass causing destruction of the sternum and a soft tissue mass in the sacrum encasing the sacral nerve roots. He also had a history of a perforated gastric ulcer, which he refused intervention for at the time. The CT scan revealed multiple masses in the lung bases, throughout the mesentery, retroperitoneum, and a 3.5 x 2.7 cm mass in the uncinate process of the pancreas. An EGD showed a large deep ulcer in the fundus of the stomach with a 4 cm ill-defined mass in the uncinate process with biopsy consistent with lymphoma.

The patient's past medical history includes BPH, untreated prostate cancer, osteoporosis, atrial fibrillation (not anticoagulated), and well-compensated CHF. His oncologic history is extensive, with initial development of splenomegaly, worsening splenomegaly prompting splenectomy, remission, enlarged left axillary lymph node, worsening left axillary node adenopathy per CT, repeat left axillary lymph node biopsy, identification of splenectomy-bed disease progression, and recurrent disease with extensive osseus and paraspinal tumor masses seen on MRI. He has also sought alternative therapies and has been hospitalized multiple times due to his condition.

The patient's family history includes his mother's death from pneumonia and CHF, his father's history of both prostate and rectal cancer, and his brother's nonmalignant tumor removed from his pituitary. His maternal grandmother had CAD and his maternal grandfather died of liver cancer in the setting of cirrhosis. His paternal grandfather had diabetes. His paternal aunt also had cancer of an unknown type.

Upon admission, the patient was on Digoxin 250 mcg daily and Omeprazole 40 mg daily. His physical exam was largely unremarkable, with the exception of a palpable subcutaneous nodule on the right lateral to the umbilicus. His lab results showed abnormal WBC, RBC, Hgb, Hct, RDW, Plt Ct, Neuts, Monos, Metas, Lipase, b2micro, AST, LD, and Glucose. Imaging revealed new multiple pulmonary emboli, multifocal progression of lymphoma, multiple severe thoracic vertebral compression fractures, large left paraspinal mass at the level of C3-C4, enlarged level II node on the left, large anterior mediastinal soft tissue mass surrounding sternum, large right pleural effusion, and non-occlusive deep venous thrombosis of the distal right popliteal vein.

During his hospital stay, the patient was initially started on a heparin drip to treat his PEs and DVT. He had an IVC filter placed by interventional radiology. He also had an upper extremity DVT, melena and blood tinged stool, cough attributed to previous hospitalization, known perforated gastric ulcer, hyperbilirubinemia, hypotension, hyponatremia, and anasarca. His disease continued to progress with obvious enlargement of palpable nodules on physical exam in his arm and abdomen as well as on serial CT scan. He was known to have extensive metastases in his mediastinum, abdomen, and pancreas. His family decided to make him DNR/DNI and to convert to comfort measures only. He was discharged to an extended care facility.

The patient is a male with a history of polysubstance abuse (including alcohol, cocaine, heroin, and marijuana), hepatitis C, pancreatitis, and a genetic disorder. He presented with bright red blood per rectum, anemia, hypotension, tachycardia, and syncope. He was found to have a bleeding duodenal ulceration which required several procedures and multiple transfusions. An EGD showed a large adherent clot in the second portion of the duodenum, but no active bleeding was seen. The patient was started on a protonix gtt and his H/H was monitored. He had a brief episode of Afib with RVR and became hypotensive, requiring transfusions. 

The patient's vital signs upon transfer were T98.4, HR 103, RR 18, BP 96/79, 100% Ra. His physical exam was notable for tenderness in the epigastric and periumbilical region. Abnormal lab results included WBC 13.9, Hb 7.7, Na 134, K 3.1, BUN 6, Cr 0.48, INR 1.35, and Lipase 78. Imaging showed a 1 cm sliding hiatus hernia and fresh blood and clots in the gastric fundus. There was no evidence of ulcerations throughout the fundus, body, or antrum. The duodenal mucosa appeared erythematous in the bulb. Fresh blood was noted in the duodenal bulb and second portion of the duodenum. A large adherent clot with active bleeding in the second portion of the duodenum was found. Temporary hemostasis was achieved with injection of 12 mL of epinephrine. 

The patient's past medical history includes alcohol abuse, history of heroin, marijuana, ETOH and cocaine abuse, hepatitis C, pancreatitis, scoliosis, depression, and chronic fatigue syndrome. There is no known family history of GI or liver disease, heart disease, or cancer. 

Upon arrival, the patient was alert and oriented, complaining only of fatigue. He received IVF, 7 units of pRBC and 2 units of FFP, Phenobarb gtt, and Pantoprazole gtt. His urine tested positive for both cannabinoids and cocaine. 

Admission labs showed elevated WBC, RBC, Hgb, Hct, Neuts, AbsNeut, AbsMono, AbsEos, AbsBaso, Glucose, AnGap, TotBili, Albumin, Calcium, Phos, and Lipase. His TSH was also elevated. Imaging showed echogenic liver consistent with steatosis, trace ascites, mildly enlarged periportal lymph nodes, and no large volume free intraperitoneal air. 

The patient's hospital course was complicated by duodenal ulcer bleed, abdominal pain, hepatitis C, alcohol dependence, polysubstance dependence, reduced cardiac EF, and atrial fibrillation. He was not taking any preadmission medications. His discharge diagnoses included duodenal ulceration, gastroduodenal branch pseudoaneurysm, alcohol abuse, and steatosis of the liver.

The patient, a female with a history of multiple aortic aneurysms, aortic dissection, sick sinus syndrome, pacemaker, ventral hernia, cardiomyopathy, and aortic valve replacement, was admitted due to difficulty swallowing, nausea, vomiting, dizziness, and a right internal carotid artery (ICA) pseudoaneurysm. She had undergone multiple surgical procedures including angiogram with carotid occlusion, ICA coiling, direct repair of the femoral artery, tracheostomy and PEG placement, and evacuation of a right thigh hematoma with repair of the right femoral artery.

Upon admission, she was on Coumadin and presented with dysphagia and a lump in her throat, as well as dizziness, nausea, and vomiting. A neck CTA showed an enlarging right carotid aneurysm. She experienced respiratory distress and hypotension after a choking episode, which was managed with Albumin. She developed a full body rash, worsening hypotension, and respiratory distress with hypoxia, leading to intubation. Her INR of 3 was reversed for a potential vascular procedure with 4 units of FFP and 3 doses of Vitamin K 10mg IV. She was started on Dexamethasone for possible airway edema and a heparin drip after it was decided she would not undergo a procedure.

Her admission physical exam revealed a large right neck pulsatile mass and her mental status was intact. She was intubated, restless, and oriented to self and hospital. Her cranial nerves were largely intact, with normal bulk and tone bilaterally, and good strength in all extremities. Her discharge physical exam showed her to be an ill-appearing elderly female, with a tracheostomy in place, decreased breath sounds over the right lung fields, and a blister on her right foot.

Her admission labs showed abnormal RBC count (3.69), MCHC (31.7), RDWSD (50.9), PTT (60.0), glucose (142), sodium (148), and calcium (7.9). Her discharge labs showed abnormal RBC count (2.60), Hgb (8.0), Hct (27.1), MCV (104), MCHC (29.5), RDW (17.2), RDWSD (65.4), PTT (72.8), glucose (147), urea nitrogen (53), chloride (93), bicarbonate (34), anion gap (20), and phosphorus (5.0). Her cTropnT was elevated at 0.02. Her calTIBC (257), ferritin (503), and haptoglobin (286) were also elevated.

Imaging showed no evidence of acute intracranial hemorrhage or large vascular territorial infarction, but did reveal dissection flaps involving the right brachiocephalic artery, proximal to mid right common carotid artery, and proximal left common carotid artery. There was severe ectasia of the bilateral internal carotid and vertebral arteries, with areas of focal stenosis and post stenotic dilation. A large focal dilation versus aneurysm/pseudoaneurysm of the distal right ICA was noted. There was also a large heterogeneous right medial groin/thigh hematoma without evidence of pseudoaneurysm or active extravasation, severe cardiomegaly with pulmonary edema and left lower lobe atelectasis/collapse, and nonobstructing left nephrolithiasis and bilateral renal cysts.

Microbiology results showed moderate growth of Pseudomonas aeruginosa in her sputum, which was sensitive to several antibiotics including cefepime, ceftazidime, ciprofloxacin, gentamicin, meropenem, and tobramycin.

During her hospital stay, she experienced cardiogenic shock, heart failure with reduced ejection fraction, acute hypoxemic respiratory failure requiring tracheostomy, Pseudomonas pneumonia, monomorphic and polymorphic ventricular tachycardia, right femoral hematoma, blood loss anemia, GI bleed, and bacteriuria. She was treated with a variety of medications including aspirin, bisacodyl, heparin, lansoprazole, lorazepam, multivitamins with minerals, polyethylene glycol, pravastatin, senna, acetaminophen, metoprolol tartrate, oxycodone, torsemide, and losartan potassium. She was discharged to extended care.

The patient is an elderly female with a complex medical history including abdominal and thoracic aneurysms, aortic dissection with subsequent aortic replacement and mechanical aortic valve repair, sick sinus syndrome with pacemaker placement, atrial fibrillation managed with Coumadin, and heart failure with reduced ejection fraction (HFrEF). She was transferred for management of a right carotid aneurysm. Her course has been complicated by a right groin hematoma requiring transfusions and wound vac, shock requiring pressors, polymorphic ventricular fibrillation, non-sustained monomorphic ventricular tachycardia, respiratory failure requiring tracheostomy and PEG, pseudomonas pneumonia treated with meropenem, and volume overload with HFrEF. She was readmitted for oozing from the right thigh after the wound vac was not replaced after transport.

She has undergone multiple surgical procedures including aortic replacement, aortic valve replacement, transverse arch and thoracic descending aortic replacement, thoracic aneurysm repair, thoracoabdominal aneurysm repair, and dual chamber pacemaker placement. She also has a history of stroke with residual right facial numbness.

Upon admission, she was found to have pseudomonas bacteremia, likely secondary to the right thigh wound. She was treated with Ceftolozane-Tazobactam for 4 weeks. Her right femoral hematoma was managed by vascular surgery with wound vac changes every few days. She required intermittent blood transfusions due to persistent slow bleeding into her wound given her anticoagulation. Her anticoagulation was restarted prior to discharge with wound vac in place and blood counts were stable.

Her admission labs showed abnormal values for RBC (2.60), Hgb (8.0), Hct (27.1), MCV (104), MCHC (29.5), RDW (17.2), RDWSD (65.4), Neuts (79.8), Lymphs (9.0), Eos (0.4), NRBC (0.2), AbsNeut (6.79), AbsLymp (0.77), AbsEos (0.03), PTT (72.8), Glucose (147), UreaN (53), Cl (93), HCO3 (34), AnGap (20), Phos (5.0), and Mg (2.7). Her discharge labs showed abnormal values for RBC (2.49), Hgb (7.9), Hct (26.1), MCV (105), MCHC (30.3), RDW (20.1), RDWSD (73.5), PTT (64.1), Glucose (133), Creat (0.3), Cl (92), HCO3 (37), and Calcium (8.0). All blood cultures showed no growth.

Imaging studies showed stable appearance of aortic root repair with multifocal aortic aneurysm and chronic dissections, left upper lobe/lingular consolidation likely due to infection, and postsurgical changes within the anterior soft tissues of the right thigh. Her left atrium was dilated and overall left ventricular systolic function was mildly depressed (LVEF= 40-45 %). A mechanical aortic valve prosthesis was present with no masses or vegetations seen on the aortic valve.

Her medications upon admission included Aspirin, Bisacodyl, Lansoprazole, Lorazepam, Multivitamins with minerals, Polyethylene Glycol, Pravastatin, Senna, Acetaminophen, Metoprolol Tartrate, Oxycodone, Torsemide, Warfarin, Albuterol, Amitriptyline, Brimonidine Tartrate, Gabapentin, Latanoprost, Losartan Potassium, and Timolol Maleate.

The patient, a female, was admitted due to hemoptysis. She has a complex medical history including abdominal and aortic aneurysms, aortic dissection with aortic valve replacement (AVR) on Coumadin, sick sinus syndrome (SSS) with a pacemaker, atrial fibrillation (Afib), congestive heart failure (CHF), right carotid pseudoaneurysm post coiling, polymorphic ventricular fibrillation and ventricular tachycardia, and chronic respiratory failure requiring a tracheostomy and percutaneous endoscopic gastrostomy (PEG). She also has a history of multiple aortic aneurysm repairs, ventral hernia, heart failure with reduced ejection fraction (HFrEF), ascending aortic replacement, transverse arch and thoracic descending aortic replacement, thoracic aneurysm repair, thoracoabdominal aneurysm repair, and dual chamber pacemaker with replacements. She has a possible history of stroke with residual right facial numbness. 

Upon admission, she was on several medications including acetaminophen, albuterol, amitriptyline, aspirin, bisacodyl, brimonidine tartrate, gabapentin, latanoprost, lorazepam, losartan potassium, metoprolol tartrate, multivitamins with minerals, polyethylene glycol, senna, timolol maleate, oxycodone liquid, scopolamine patch, torsemide, pantoprazole, warfarin, atorvastatin, and ceftolozane-tazobactam. 

The patient presented with three days of bleeding from her trach, with intermittent hypotensive episodes requiring fluid bolus. She also had recurrent episodes of bleeding from the trach. She denied chest pain, nausea, vomiting, and lightheadedness. She had a bronchoscopy performed during her stay. 

Lab results showed an INR of 2.4, hemoglobin of 7.8, sodium of 149, and WBC of 8.1. Other abnormal results included neutrophils at 82.4, lymphocytes at 5.9, absolute neutrophils at 6.69, absolute lymphocytes at 0.48, creatinine at 0.2, total CO2 at 38, cTropnT at 0.04, proBNP at 6670, CK(CPK) at 14, PO2 at 82, PCO2 at 70, and total CO2 at 49. 

Imaging did not show any changes in the innominate artery fistula. The patient's right internal carotid artery (ICA) pseudoaneurysm was stable post coiling. She also had a right femoral hematoma post evacuation. 

The patient's hemoptysis was likely multifactorial related to aspiration, epistaxis, and friable mucosa from frequent suctioning. She also had acute on chronic hypoxic respiratory failure with increased oxygen requirement, which improved with diuresis and removal of a large mucus plug via bronchoscopy. Her hypernatremia was treated with free water flushes. Her preload was managed with boluses of lasix, and her home medication was held due to normotension. Her home metoprolol was continued for rate control of Afib. Her target INR was adjusted to 2.5-3.0 due to frequent small volume bleeding. Her tube feeds were held while the possibility of procedure awaited, and were resumed when she was asymptomatic with no signs of bleeding. 

Upon discharge, the patient was diagnosed with hemoptysis, heart failure with preserved ejection fraction (HFpEF), history of mechanical mitral valve replacement, and hematoma post evacuation. She was discharged to extended care.

The patient, a female, was admitted due to hemoptysis. She has a complex medical history including multiple aortic aneurysms, aortic dissection with aortic valve replacement (AVR) on Coumadin, sick sinus syndrome (SSS) with a pacemaker, atrial fibrillation (Afib), congestive heart failure (CHF), right carotid pseudoaneurysm with coiling, polymorphic ventricular fibrillation and tachycardia, and chronic respiratory failure requiring a tracheostomy and percutaneous endoscopic gastrostomy (PEG). She also has a history of ventral hernia and possible stroke with residual right facial numbness. 

Upon admission, she was on several medications including amitriptyline, aspirin, atorvastatin, brimonidine tartrate, gabapentin, latanoprost, metoprolol tartrate, multivitamins with minerals, scopolamine patch, senna, timolol maleate, acetaminophen, pantoprazole, spironolactone, torsemide, potassium chloride, guaifenesin, enoxaparin sodium, lorazepam, and oxycodone. 

During her stay, she experienced no further episodes of hemoptysis. She was found to have a urinary tract infection (UTI) and was given ceftriaxone. Her troponin was elevated at 0.05, and her hemoglobin and hematocrit were at baseline. Her labs showed a white blood cell count of 7.5, hemoglobin of 7.0, platelets of 352, sodium of 148, bicarbonate of 37, INR of 1.3, PTT of 39.4, and a lactate of 1.4. Her chest X-ray showed cardiomegaly and congestion with moderate pulmonary edema. A CT angiogram of her chest showed no pulmonary embolism or active bleeding. 

She was given ceftriaxone and IV morphine. She reported no further episodes of hemoptysis and felt well at her baseline, which includes chronic lower extremity pain managed with oxycodone. She had a 6-beat run of ventricular tachycardia which self-resolved and was asymptomatic. 

Her labs showed several abnormal findings including a red blood cell count of 2.34, hemoglobin of 7.0, hematocrit of 25.1, mean corpuscular volume of 107, mean corpuscular hemoglobin concentration of 27.9, red cell distribution width of 18.5, neutrophils of 82.3, lymphocytes of 8.6, nucleated red blood cells of 0.4, absolute neutrophils of 6.20, absolute lymphocytes of 0.65, PTT of 39.4, glucose of 123, urea nitrogen of 31, sodium of 148, bicarbonate of 37, troponin T of 0.05, partial pressure of oxygen of 37, partial pressure of carbon dioxide of 82, calculated total carbon dioxide of 47, and lactate of 1.4. Her urine showed large leukocytes, 300 protein, trace blood, and her blood culture was not grown to date. Her sputum culture showed gram-negative rods, gram-positive rods, and gram-negative diplococci. 

Her discharge diagnoses were hemoptysis, acute kidney injury, acute on chronic respiratory failure, and hypernatremia. Her secondary diagnosis was mechanical aortic valve replacement. Her discharge medications included a heparin IV per weight-based dosing protocol, acetaminophen, amitriptyline, aspirin, atorvastatin, brimonidine tartrate, chlorhexidine gluconate oral rinse, guaifenesin, latanoprost, lorazepam, metoprolol tartrate, multivitamins with minerals, oxycodone, pantoprazole, scopolamine patch, senna, and timolol maleate. Gabapentin, potassium chloride, spironolactone, and torsemide were held. She was discharged to extended care.

The patient, a female, was admitted due to a dislodged G-tube. Her past medical history includes gastroesophageal reflux disease (GERD) by aortic dissection, mechanical aortic valve replacement (AVR) on warfarin, abdominal aortic aneurysm (AAA) repair, bilateral carotid artery dissection, right internal carotid artery aneurysm status post coiling, congestive heart failure, atrial fibrillation on Coumadin, and sick sinus syndrome status post pacemaker. She also has a history of stroke with residual right facial numbness. 

The patient developed pain near the G-tube site two days prior to admission. A KUB revealed displacement of the G-tube tip subcutaneously. She was referred to the emergency department for G-tube evaluation. She denies fevers, chills, chest pain, shortness of breath, and worsening leg pain. 

She was previously admitted to an outside facility for respiratory failure complicated by hemoptysis. She had difficulty with her worsening heart failure and was transferred. An echo showed mild concentric left ventricular hypertrophy with impaired LV systolic function. Ejection fraction was measured at 35%, lower than her previous estimates of 40-45%. She was treated for septic arthritis of the knee. She received vancomycin and oxacillin, which was transitioned to cefazolin. Her warfarin was initially held due to concern the patient might need a right knee procedure, but this was thought to be nonoperative and it was resumed. She is now being bridged with heparin due to her history of mechanical aortic valve.

Upon arrival to the MICU, the patient was on the ventilator. She communicated by mouthing words. She stated she is tired, and she is having abdominal pain around her PEG site. The pain has been there for about 2 months, and it has gotten worse.

Physical examination revealed redness around the G-tube site but no pus. The patient was admitted to the ICU for monitoring. The G-tube was replaced. 

Lab results showed abnormal values for RBC (2.38*), Hgb (6.9*), Hct (24.0*), MCV (101*), MCHC (28.8*), RDW (17.8*), RDWSD (65.1*), Neuts (78.1*), Lymphs (11.0*), AbsNeut (6.19*), AbsLymp (0.87*), PTT (150*), UreaN (29*), Na (134*), K (5.3), Cl (91*), and AlkPhos (143*). 

The patient's medications upon admission included Atorvastatin, Latanoprost, Metoprolol Tartrate, Multivitamins W/minerals, Potassium Chloride, Gabapentin, Scopolamine Patch, Warfarin, Heparin, CeFAZolin, Lidocaine, Amitriptyline, Brimonidine Tartrate, Digoxin, Losartan Potassium, Famotidine, OxyCODONE, and Furosemide. 

The patient was discharged to an extended care facility with the diagnosis of a dislodged G tube. Her mental status was clear and coherent, and she was alert and interactive, but bedbound.

The patient is a female with a history of hypogammaglobulinemia, chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD), gastroesophageal reflux disease (GERD), and a history of tension pneumothorax with surgical bleb removal. She also has osteoarthritis, fibromyalgia, osteoporosis, and depression. She presented with a two-week history of epigastric/right upper quadrant abdominal pain associated with nausea. The pain has been constant and worsening, with no radiation, and may be associated with food consumption. She has not been able to vomit since her fundoplication surgery and has no diarrhea. She has been experiencing fevers, chills, and sweats that have been getting worse over the few days prior to presentation. 

The patient underwent an endoscopic retrograde cholangiopancreatography (ERCP) and cholecystectomy. An ultrasound showed a gallbladder stone but no signs of inflammation or dilation. A repeat ultrasound showed a stone, 12mm common bile duct (CBD), and gallbladder wall thickening. Her labs were notable for elevated liver function tests with aspartate aminotransferase (AST) 139, alanine aminotransferase (ALT) 177, alkaline phosphatase 420, white blood cell count 12, total bilirubin 5.2, and direct bilirubin 4.5. 

Abnormal lab results included a low red blood cell count (3.00*), low hemoglobin (8.5*), low hematocrit (27.2*), low mean corpuscular hemoglobin concentration (31.3*), and elevated glucose (104*). The patient's bicarbonate level was also elevated (34*). Her alanine aminotransferase (ALT) and alkaline phosphatase were elevated at 70* and 322* respectively. 

Imaging showed streaky opacities at the lung bases most likely representing atelectasis. An MRCP showed acute calculous cholecystitis without perforation or an abscess, choledocholithiasis, especially within the distal CBD causing obstruction and mild intrahepatic biliary ductal dilation, and a low insertion of the cystic duct. There was no evidence of cholangitis or intrahepatic abscesses. 

The patient's hospital course included an ERCP with removal of CBD stone and sludge, and an open cholecystectomy. Her post-operative course was stable, and her incisional pain was controlled with intravenous analgesia. Her white blood cell count and liver function tests were normalizing. 

The patient's medications upon admission included immune globulin intravenous (human) unknown IV 1X/MONTH, rosuvastatin calcium 20 mg PO QPM, NexIUM (esomeprazole magnesium) 40 mg oral DAILY, ranitidine 300 mg PO QHS, Symbicort (budesonide-formoterol) 160-4.5 mcg/actuation inhalation BID, albuterol inhaler ___ PUFF IH Q4H:PRN SOB, Forteo (teriparatide) 20 mcg/dose - 600 mcg/2.4 mL subcutaneous ___, and venlafaxine XR 75 mg PO DAILY. 

The patient has a family history of immunodeficiency. She was discharged with a diagnosis of acute cholecystitis and choledocholithiasis, along with secondary diagnoses of hypogammaglobulinemia, depression, fibromyalgia, chronic obstructive pulmonary disease, and gastroesophageal reflux disease.

The patient is a female with a history of dilated aortic root, well-controlled asthma, restrictive lung disease, type 2 diabetes, hyperlipidemia, gastroesophageal reflux disease, non-alcoholic steatohepatitis, and depression. She presented with shortness of breath on exertion and chest pain. The patient reported intermittent chest pain, shortness of breath, and left arm pain which began at the shoulder and radiated down the arm. She also reported sharp, burning midsternal chest pain, radiating down her arm. The pain was worse with exertion, and sitting upright. 

Upon examination, the patient was not in respiratory distress and her airway was intact. Her heart rate was normal and rhythm regular. She denied chest pain and there was no pedal edema. Her abdomen was not tender and bowel sounds were normal. 

Lab results showed a negative troponin, PTT of 24.3, lactate of 1.6, AST of 70, proBNP of 25, WBC of 11.8, ANC of 6.12, and a urine glucose of 1000. An EKG showed a normal sinus rhythm rate, normal axis, q wave and inverted t in lead 3. A CT scan showed no evidence of aortic dissection, no central or lobar pulmonary embolus, no acute process in the abdomen and pelvis, and extensive posterior spinal fusion hardware and degenerative changes without hardware complication detected. No fracture was detected.

The patient was given Atenolol 25 mg, Aspirin 81 mg, Cetirizine 10 mg, Escitalopram Oxalate 30 mg, Montelukast 10 mg, Omeprazole 20 mg, GuaiFENesin ER 600 mg, and Insulin 2 Units. 

The patient has a history of cough and dyspnea since a respiratory infection. Allergy workup with RAST testing showed sensitivity to dust mites, but PFT's and exam were not classic for poorly controlled asthma. The patient has responded to prednisone and inhaled steroids in the past. A CT scan showed no concerning findings to explain cough, but with possible elevated pressure in the pulmonary arteries. 

The patient's family history includes depression, glaucoma, seasonal allergies, high blood pressure, high cholesterol, osteoarthritis in her mother. Her father had type 1 diabetes, heart attack, high blood pressure, high cholesterol, and osteoarthritis. Her brother had seasonal allergies and high blood pressure. 

Upon discharge, the patient's physical exam showed no significant changes from admission. Her discharge labs showed a WBC of 7.5, Hgb of 10.8, MCV of 79, MCH of 23.0, MCHC of 29.0, RDW of 18.1, RDWSD of 51.1, PTT of 24.8, and glucose of 134. A urine culture showed mixed bacterial flora consistent with skin and/or genital contamination. 

The patient's chest pain was ruled out as acute coronary syndrome, pulmonary embolism, and aortic dissection based on CTA chest, EKG, and labs. Her chest pain had resolved by discharge. Her shortness of breath was likely multifactorial including deconditioning from limited movement since use of braces, anemia contributing to decreased O2 carrying capacity, and possible contribution from pulmonary hypertension. The patient was scheduled for a TTE next week, a sleep study, and consideration of CPET pending further discussion with her pulmonologist. 

The patient's chronic conditions include type 2 diabetes, hyperlipidemia, gastroesophageal reflux disease, non-alcoholic steatohepatitis, depression, and endometriosis. Her medications upon discharge included Albuterol Inhaler, Aspirin, Atenolol, Cetirizine, Escitalopram Oxalate, Ferrous Sulfate, Fluticasone-Salmeterol Diskus, GuaiFENesin ER, Montelukast, Omeprazole, Simvastatin, and Vitamin D. 

The patient was discharged home with instructions to see her primary care physician, lung doctor, and to get her echo done.

The patient, a female of unspecified age, was admitted for observation due to increased aortic root dilation. She was pregnant at 33+2 weeks at the time of admission. Her chief complaint was aortic root dilation. She has a history of aortic root dilation/aneurysm. She underwent a Cesarean section during her stay. 

The patient's family history is significant for aortic complications. Her father had aortic repair at an unspecified age. Four of her paternal aunts and uncles had aortic complications, two of whom had aortic surgery. Her paternal grandmother also had aortic surgery. The patient's sister has mild aortic dilation and had back surgery for scoliosis.

Upon admission, the patient was taking prenatal vitamins. During her hospital stay, she was closely monitored due to her aortic root dilation/aneurysm. Her systolic blood pressure goal was kept below 130. She was administered metoprolol tartrate 6.25mg every 6 hours and received two doses of IV hydralazine 10mg for transient hypertension. She experienced one episode of vagal-mediated bradycardia and hypotension while pumping, which resolved spontaneously. 

Postpartum, she had severe range blood pressure and received labetalol 10mg IV. She remained asymptomatic throughout these hypertensive episodes. Her medication was adjusted throughout her postpartum course to a dose of hydralazine 50mg every 6 hours and labetalol 400mg three times a day. Her pain was managed with oral medications. By postpartum day 5, she was tolerating a regular diet, ambulating independently, and her pain was controlled with oral medications. She was afebrile with stable vital signs. 

The patient's discharge diagnoses included Cesarean section, Ehlers Danlos, dilated aorta, and pre-eclampsia with severe features. Her mental status was clear and coherent, and she was alert and interactive. She was discharged home in stable condition with postpartum outpatient follow-up scheduled.

The patient, a female with a history of depression and 'extra heart beats', presented with symptoms of sweating, chills, dizziness, shortness of breath, cough, lightheadedness, blurry vision, and weight gain. She also reported edema at her ankles that worsened at night. She denied experiencing chest pain, palpitations, falls, loss of consciousness, and urinary or stool incontinence. She reported feeling 'out of it' and was noted to be hypoxic to 70%. Initial vital signs in the ED were 97.3 82 110/71 14 100%. Labs were notable for Hct 34 and DDimer 1025. She was started on a heparin drip empirically for PE as she could not get a CTA due to an allergy. 

Upon review of systems, she reported urinary incontinence (dribbling throughout the day), mild back pain, and throat congestion. She denied fever, headache, vision changes, rhinorrhea, congestion, sore throat, cough, shortness of breath, chest pain, abdominal pain, nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, constipation, bowel incontinence, BRBPR, melena, hematochezia, dysuria, and hematuria. 

Her family history includes her mother with elevated cholesterol and depression, her father with MI, CABG, dilated cardiomyopathy, kidney problems, and diabetes. Several paternal uncles had cardiac problems. There is also a family history of pelvic cancer, diabetes, and CVD - stroke. 

On physical exam, she was found to have faint rales at the bases of her lungs, good air movement, and no accessory muscle use. Her heart had a non-displaced PMI, RRR, no MRG, and normal S1-S2. Her abdomen was soft/NT/ND, with no masses or HSM, and no rebound/guarding. Her extremities had WWP, no c/c/e, and 2+ peripheral pulses (radials, DPs). 

Abnormal lab results included Hgb-10.9*, Hct-34.6*, MCV-80*, MCH-25.1*, Neuts-80.5*, Lymphs-16.4*, and Glucose-103*. Her D-Dimer was also elevated at 1025*. 

Imaging studies included a chest x-ray which showed a normal-sized heart with no signs of CHF or pneumonia, no effusion or pneumothorax, and intact bony structures. A transthoracic echocardiogram showed normal left ventricular cavity size and wall thickness with preserved global and regional biventricular systolic function. No clinically significant valvular disease was found and the pulmonary artery systolic pressure was normal. 

During her hospital stay, the etiology of her presyncope remained unclear but was consistent with a vagal episode. Her hypoxemia and dyspnea were investigated, but her O2 sats were consistently in the 97-100% range upon arrival. Her elevated D-dimer led to the start of a heparin drip, but this was stopped as her Wells score was 0. Her history of weight gain, lower extremity edema, and improved oxygenation with lasix was concerning for CHF, but chest x-ray and clinical exam were not consistent with fluid overload. An echocardiogram did not reveal any valvular disease, systolic dysfunction, and actually showed decreased aortic root dilation. 

Her medications upon admission included Aripiprazole 30 mg PO DAILY, traZODONE 100 mg PO HS (hold for sedation), and Metoprolol Tartrate 12.5 mg PO DAILY (hold for sbp < 100 and hr < 60).

The patient, a male with a history of aortic dissection requiring repair resulting in paraplegia, depression with a history of suicide attempts, and aortic valve replacement with a St. ___ valve, was transferred from an outside hospital due to concerns of an innominate artery dissection. The patient had overdosed on oxycodone and oxycontin (~79 tabs) due to feeling 'frustrated and overwhelmed' by his pain, medical problems, and family life. He was found unresponsive by a friend who called EMS. At the outside hospital, his tox screen was positive for opiates, cocaine, and marijuana. His INR was 6.5 on admission. He received narcan but then had an episode of hematemesis and complained of some neck pain. A subsequent CT of the neck demonstrated dissection extending into the innominate artery, leading to his transfer for further evaluation. 

Upon arrival at the current facility, his vitals were stable and labs were normal. Cardiac surgery and vascular were consulted who obtained old records which showed chronic nature of dissections so no indication for surgical intervention. The patient was observed overnight in the ED for pain control and received 2.5mg IV vitamin K with INR 5.7 -> 2.3. He was subsequently admitted to medicine for bridging to coumadin prior to psychiatric placement. 

The patient has a history of hypertension, spinal paralysis, poly substance abuse, chronic indwelling foley with UTIs, depression with prior suicide attempts, and pressure ulcer of ankle. His surgical history includes ascending aorta/arch replacement/St. ___ mechanical valve, repair of descending aortic dissection resulting in paraplegia, repair of pectus excavatum, and total hip replacement. His family history does not include any history of CAD or SCD. His mother and maternal grandmother had breast cancer.

On physical examination, the patient was thin, well-appearing man in no acute distress. He had a foley in place, lumbar scoliosis, and decreased sensation from the nipple line down though he could feel pressure everywhere. His strength in upper extremities was intact. 

His admission labs showed abnormal Hgb-13.6, PTT-58.7, Creat-0.4, and LD(LDH)-328. His discharge labs showed abnormal RBC-4.04, Hgb-12.2, Hct-37.0, and PTT-49.9. His urine culture showed mixed bacterial flora consistent with skin and/or genital contamination. His CTA chest showed dissection involving brachiocephalic trunk extending into proximal right subclavian and the common carotid arteries, post-surgical changes related to aortic valve replacement and bypass graft involving the descending aorta feeding intercostal arteries, and focal dissection of the intra-abdominal aorta at the level of SMA spanning approximately 1.5 cm segment.

The patient was discharged with a diagnosis of suicide attempt, supratherapeutic INR, and chronic arterial dissections. His mental status was clear and coherent, and he was alert and interactive. His activity status was out of bed with assistance to chair or wheelchair. His medications on admission included Fluoxetine 20 mg PO DAILY, OxyCODONE SR (OxyconTIN) 20 mg PO Q12H, OxycoDONE (Immediate Release) 20 mg PO Q6H:PRN pain, and Warfarin 12.5 mg PO DAILY16.
